Mini metro is an absolutely fantastic game. The first thing to say about it is that it is so elegant. It's clean and not overcrowded with features or unnecersary complexity. Yet there is still time for a few cool features - such as the ability to output .gifs of your game.  It's also highly additcive and can be played for hours. This is because there are many cities and three game modes (normal, endless, and extreme). To unlock cities you need to complete milestones in normal mode (eg. transport 1000 passengers) and to unlock cities in extreme mode you need to complete even tougher challenges (eg. transport 1000 passengers using only 1 tunnel, transport 1600 passengers with no more than 1 locomotive per line).   In normal mode you can move lines to respond to new stations, in extreme mode they are fixed. In normal and extreme mode you recieve investment (eg. a new line, tunnels, trains or carriages - you can choose) at the end of each week and you loose as soon as a station overcrowds. In endless mode you recieve investment based on efficiency milestones and stations do not overcrowd.  This game is 100% worth the money.

This is just my personal opinion, but as someone with about 450 hours into Civ 5, I feel like this game is inferior in just about every way. I can still to this day play a game of Civ 5 and get completely sucked in for hours on end. I'm about 4 hours into 1 game in Civ 6 and I'm already bored to tears. It just doesn't have that same feel to it.  First off the cartoonish art style is a HUGE turnoff for me. I just can't get past it. It looks as though it was designed for a MUCH younger audience on mobile platforms and not for pc. Very disappointing.  The UI is clunky and not very intuitive, the combat animations are poorly done and a huge step back from Civ 5 imo. The AI interaction from my experience so far is very boring, with them randomly repeating the same things over and over again. Not to mention the way they're animated looks very Disneyish. Yet another step back from Civ 5.  With that said, the game itself isn't bad. I was just hoping for a bit more after Civ 5, which I still love to this day. But hey, at least they have Sean Bean narrating. That's about the only saving grace I've found so far.

I am an old-school gamer. From my humble Commodore 128 (bootable into -64- mode!) I've played: Bards Tale 1-3, Questron, RiverRaid, Movie Mogul, and many Public Domain titles. Next up was my IBM Clone 286 which was quickly upgraded to an i386-dx40 just to play Lucasarts' X-Wing, TIE Fighter, etc! Skipping ahead past several generations and finding parenthood has demanded much of my spare time, late at night I would fire up Balder's Gate I and II, Icewind Dale I & II, NWN 1 & 2, etc, and so forth. I followed the early development of this game and though I was unable to be a backer I recently purchased it and I have found it a great way to become immersed in a world, though new is eerily familiar and worlds apart from our own! I highly recommend this title, kudos and a sincere hats off to the team of developers, writers, graphic artists and engineers who have done something awesome yet so far removed from the controller-twitching console madness that permeates the current electronic game landscapes. A true deep-story game combining strategy & RPG elements into something great. I would type more but I would rather play now - a great purchase at any price!!

I've played many a piloting simulation since the late 90's. I've flown the Sci-Fi and Real Life versions. From Microsoft's Flight Simulator to X-Wing, Tie Fighter and even X-Wing vs Tie Fighter, I've flown every Wingman game out there, Privateer, etc etc etc.   You needed to know that to understand my review a bit better. You'll see the low amount of hours played and tell yourself, usually rightously so, that this dude doesn't know what he's talking about. He hasn't played it enough. Normally, you're right on the spot, but this time, not so much. If not for my extensive past and decades of flight sticks behind me, this wouldn't be as accurate.   Disclaimer given, this game blows in some areas and innovates in other places. Unfortunately, there isn't enough innovation to outweigh what's completely craptastic. I'll start with what's to love before I start about what's to dislike.  Good Stuff: 1. Realistic landing and launching skills can be developed and honed. Training missions help with that long before you go into game if you need it. Also, if that is something you wish to avoid, you can use in game credits, cheap too, to get a docking computer to do the work for you. Kudos there.   2. Gravitational mass affecting speeds and the ability to 'jump' into hyperspace or supercruise. Well done.   3. Cockpit instruments aren't eye candy and actually reflect everything that is happening. Superbly done.   4. You have to earn your advancements. Everything isn't handed to you right away. No instant gratification. That's the best part.  Bad Stuff:  1. Evil MOB Ship spawns can severely outclass your starting ship in the starting area. You can get shot out of the sky in 3 seconds flat because you checked out a 'weak' signal source that's only 200Ls from your starting base. In most games, you start out in a beginning sector, you come up against mobs that are maybe one or two steps above you. Not here. My Sidewinder, the starter ship, won't survive an attack from a Viper, which is an upper tier vessel. Out of the three Sidewinder mobs I've faced, the other 15 Viper/Eagle based ships were a pain in the butt. There is no underdog here. Go into any other game and be mde to face a level 20 versus your level 1 most the time, you're not going to enjoy the game. And no, I was not being camped or griefed by other higher level players. These were mobs, game generated. I do know how to tell the difference.   2. Board Missions are an insult to the intelligence. If you take the kill assignment, you have to hunt the named ship in a huge sector by stopping at the signal sources, and I was given 5 hours to loacte my target. Never found him. I checked every Unidentified Signal I could find in system provided, and I was onel-shot-killed a handful of times by various NPC pirates in ships I would haven't had any hope of winning against. One of those kill shots came from a torpedo... I was two hours into my next assassination job before abadoning it. I have patience. I like earning things the hard way. There is hard, and there is ridiculous. This is more on the ridiculous side.   3. Other starter board missions, like deliveries and the such, are such a low payout, they aren't worth your time. I earned more in killing the three 'wanted' mob ships in bounties paid out in those few hours than half a day in deliveries. Getting blown up and all by the other dozen plus higher tier ships on top of it...  4. Flight stick set up. The axis assignments are completely loopy. I had to manually set every dead-zone and even teach the system to recognize the throttle slide. My Sidwinder 3D Pro has always been easily configured for every game, but not this one. I can't even assign any of my other firing buttons beyond one and two in the fire-groups. Even the unfinished game Star Citizen used my stick far better than this. I would love to assign buttons 7, 8 and 9 for Chaff, Warrant Scan and Mining Laser use, but nope, not possible. I have to make three or four different firing groups and stick with only buttons 1 and 2 and cycle between them all on the fly... yuck.   5. AFK players sitting on the launch pads, forcing me to restart the game in 'solo' mode so I can dock and refuel. That alone should explain why this isn't an ideal system...  6. 'Roid Rage. The mining system in Elite Dangerous is akin to pulling your teeth out with red-hot pliers. There is no low-end dedicated mining ship you can earn. Either mine in your Sidewinder, or set up an Eagle when you can earn one up, but you will never truly appreciate true 'roid rage until your 'Limpets' actually use up the very same cargo space your refined materiels drop into, which you have to rebuy limpets... becuase if you skip using them, you get to cut and chase down the rocks into a cargo scoop with the aid of a targeting camera... which is an insult to Sci-Fi everywhere. I watched many a mining video and even read a few mining guides for this game. Nude sunbathing on a beach of broken glass is more productive and less painful.   7. Scanning Systems and selling data is a serious waste of time. The big problem, you have to sell it more than 20 light years from where to scanned it. Fly for hours. Discover with your discovery scanner. Find more than what you expected by activating it manually. Get paid literally nothing for hours of flight time. My highest sale of undiscovered data was 1,200 credits, for a system where I discovered 15 new Astrological Locations... bleck.  Did I mention the bad items outnumbered the good? I'm stopping here. If you play this and get used to the painful parts, you'll find the good parts some measure of fun. Can I recommend this piloting game? Sorry, no. I wish I never wasted the money on it.

It does too many same mistakes that Dark Souls 2 did. Throws a bunch of mobs and or multiple bosses at you in the boss fights, far too many times. Cheap ♥♥♥♥ like that doesnt make a game challenging in a fun way, its lazy and literally the easiest way to make a game 'hard'. Its like they forgot what made Demons Souls so good.  Then again they have used up all their ideas as it is and this is more or less a desperate attempt at trying to get that one last cash grab off the license.  The game can be fun, its largely well designed, the world is incredibly detailed and spooky, but the few things it does wrong are such major ♥♥♥♥ups that it turns the game into a frustrating mess. Demons Souls and Dark Souls 1 were challenging (we dont talk about DS2, its like the deus ex: invisible war of souls games) and yes they ♥♥♥♥♥♥ me off at times too when something was really tricky, but it was a good kind of a frustration, getting through the frustratingly hard part felt great.  DS3 really just leaves a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ taste in my mouth even after clearing a hard part, partially because I keep dying of stupid ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t. Enemies can hit through walls, no matter how thick they are, lockon is erratic and has a mind of its own, enemies have massive hitboxes even when they are small dudes and they can bodyblock you in the many tight corners of the areas which naturally causes the camera to go crazy. Getting stuck and the camera preventing you from seeing anything, then dying. Yeah thats fun. Camera was always an issue, but in this game it somehow becomes a problem far too often. Its not an ocasional little annoyance, its a near constant thing no matter what camera setting you choose.  As for PvP, oh boy have they done goof'd, but everyone already knows that.  Invasions are extremely irritating this time around too. Its a constant bombardment of invasions from various kinds of spirits no matter where you are so basically you will only use the mememode right before you enter a boss room. Otherwise you will be invaded nonstop. Get rid of one invader, another one appears. There is supposed to be a cooldown for that ♥♥♥♥, but it sure as hell doesnt seem to be working.  It was never this common in the previous games which made invades actually kind of exciting. You never expected it and it was a surprise. Now its something that happens like clockwork whenever you use an ember. Its messed up. Theres no anticipation, no surprise to it. You just know that its going to happen very quickly after you use an ember, with a 100% certainty.  All in all its a 'good game' with some very big design flaws that they can never fix. Worth picking up from a sale, but if you spend 60shekels to this game, you will feel incredibly salty about your purchase soon after buying it.

TLDR: I love this game, not enough in the game to buy it, seem unfinished  Don’t get me wrong I love this game. I fell in love form the first few moments. The world proved to be immersive colourful playful. The first few moments of the game are the best part, you’re getting to feel your way around the world, finding out limits wondering what you can find and make. You feel that you are on an alien world due to the fantastic character and world design. However, about an hour or two in it falls in to the trap of being repetitive (now to me this is not a turn off I bought this game for this exact reason, I love the grind and exploration aspects, I’m just warning you if you’re not into that). Soon I found that the blatant lack of depth in the crafting system smacking me across the face (I’ll explain later). I thought to myself I’m willing to look past this. I found that I soon forgot about it as soon as i discovered a new island with a totally different biome and animals to fight and capture. But this faded quickly and my problem with the crafting emerged again. There is no need to craft anything past the extractor, I never found myself crafting anything past the first special ammo. Let’s skip forward to the last island. it was to the end of this game is where I got mad, I bought this game thinking it was complete as it recently came out of early access. Yet so much of it seemed... lacking, there was too much that seemed small and that needed expansion including the game length. It is because I cannot recommend this game. Because as much as i loved those small six hours I don’t think it’s worth it. If you want this game then wait, either for a sale or for the developers to expand on what is currently there.

A good old game ruined by being an awful port.   Don't get me wrong, the game still holds up well. The jokes are still funny, the characters are still charming, the story is fine. If you grab this game and never played it before, you'd probably enjoy it. I don't regret buying it, especially since my PSP died few years ago and I wanted to replay/own it on PC. The game has lots of contents that I guess can argue for its price tag, even though the game is 13 years old.  That said, my old PSP ran the game better than my PC. I can see some effects causing a brief lag. Buttons are not even properly labelled unless you use a mod. Not to mention few crashes here making it annoying sometimes. Are these gaming breakingly bad? Not really. Are these disappointing? Yes, 13 years old game that ran fine on my old PSP shouldn't be this finnicky on PC.  I am not sure whether I can recommend the PC version when other versions run fine compared to this.   Not to mention, the game doesn't add anything new. I remember ports adding new things to the game. PSP version added Etna mode, whereas PC version just seem to be direct port of PSP version without adding anything new (i.e. one of the user mod I think was adding the contents that were added on DS version).   So they charge $20 for a 13 year old game without adding anything new to the game. Newer fans would probably enjoy this game and older fan would probably 'enjoy' the game, as it's just the same game from 2003. That said, am I the only one who is thinking, NIS could've done far better than this?   Anyways, all in all, a good old game, an awful port, leaves much to be desired for next port if there is going to be any.

The only gripes (minor at that) I had was despite the game being 'open world', It's open in the sense you could wander around, but there's not much to do and the fact that there's sooooo much driving. In addition to the timers, you have to get to a specified point with the timer running after you finish the mission. I eventually gave up playing both dlc's because of how annoying this superfluous addition was and have not finished either. The main game is still great, but avoid the dlc's.

Returning to DOOM 3 after twelve (!) years of other first person shooters is simultaneously refreshing and frustrating.  Pacing is generally fantastic, and the game is still oh so atmospheric. Audio logs are fantastically voiced, the environmental storytelling is great, generally speaking it does a fantastic job at creating the impression of a real working facility full of scared and frustrated workers. The dark industrial sci-fi theme is well realised and consistent, everything fits together nicely and the demon designs are my favourite of the series. Though I will admit they look more alienish than demonic, I still think they fit well for this particular game.  DOOM 3 is from a time before mission markers, so if you're not careful you can easily get lost. I found I adjusted fairly quickly to this, and became better at overcoming obstacles without guidance after a few hours. It can be frustrating, but the lack of any assistance does enhance the feeling of isolation and claustraphobia, and gives the game a nice survival horror esq aftertaste. Exploration feels more natural because of the lack of hand-holding, in my opinion. It's not nearly as bad as DOOM II, which had me searching for hours on some levels for that last bloody switch.  Some of DOOM 3 hasn't aged well, though, in my opinion. There's a distinct lack of agility in the player character, you'll rarely be able to dodge enemy attacks up close even by dashing backwards or to the sides. This can leave combat feeling like attrition, more a numbers game than a test of skill. Especially when you factor in DOOM 3's love of monster closets and spawning enemies behind you. The oppressive darkness doesn't help, it's literally pitch black in places which makes picking out targets an utter ballache. You can switch to the flashlight, but most of the time you'll be shooting one enemy whilst another approaches in total darkness from his flank.  Overall, though, I think DOOM 3 still does more right than wrong. It feels a little conflicted at times, like it's unsure whether it wants to be a horror game or an action game, but I think it does both well enough to be consistently enteraining.   It's like a time capsule, the embodiment of design sentiments from a time before Halo and Call of Duty really changed the scene.

Tomb Raider - Overall 6.5 out 10. A play through movie with a bit of camping  The graphics , music and realism of character's in this movie game are great. On one hand this is one of the best 3rd person adventure games, and the other hand; a really bad button mashing game between cut scene's.   WARNING : There is a scene early in game where you will be forsed to watch Lara being forsefully felt up, you can not skip, gives you 2 seconds to try to work out what to do, 4 or 5 times, if you get one button wrong, repeat again and again. Honestly the frustration of stupid button mashing with such a sensitive scene, ruined the whole mood of the game for me personally.  Once you get passed that you can camp up, kill animals, level up, go treasure hunting, some interesting and historic artifacts, and more. Lap it up plenty more cut scene's to go.  The main aim of the game (apart from trying to make it a shoot em up, survival game) Is to set up Lara Croft's origins as a young woman turning into the great treasure hunter. Which in one sense they do a great job if you see the game for what it is ... A play through movie with a bit of camping. Was very impressed with the detail of character's, lighting voice acting and smooth game play.  True fans of Tomb Raider may not like the new direction they were going with this game, but if your new or not really a fan of Tomb raider, It may surprise you.  PRO's = Graphic's , gameplay, music, lighting, charaters = 8.5 MID GROUND = Storyline = 5.5 (It's there but something feels like it's missing) CON's = Too much button mashing between cut scene's.

It has the dark, dystopic feel of Half-Life 2, the numerous objective approaches of Deus Ex, the mission looting system of Bioshock, and the superb AI awareness of Hitman. Thank you Arkane Studios and Bethesda!  Now where is Dishonored 2?

Star Conflict is one of my favorite games! For a brief period about a year ago, I was reading tons of science fiction, and I wanted to get in on some space battles. This game is PERFECT! It's like a 3rd Person Shooter, except you're a space ship. Run through on like 10v10+ TDM's or Capture the Beacon (like domination). The best part about this game is that it's FREE! Similar to league, you CAN buy stuff on there, but you can save up in-game credits and buy all the same stuff. Some of the ships are premium ships, like you have to use the actually paid for credits to purchase them, but they don't provide any advantage. I beat kids in premie ships all day.  The ships are very customizeable, offering a nice range of weapons to choose from. You get one main weapon (obv) and then you can load your missile bays with all kinds of different missiles, from homing missiles to armor piercing and everything in between. You also get active modules that you can equip your ship with. You can equip shield boosters, or like tractor-beams, you can put IR Flares into one module, and whenever someone sends a missle at you, just pop them bad boys and you're able to get away.   There are fighting/command ships, Dagger/assassin-style ships, and there are huge slow moving tank/frigates to choose from. You end up gaining enough experience and move through the different tiers of ships. So you fully upgrade and customize your ship, then get enough experience with it, and buy the next one higher (always better and worth it).   There's also 3 different factions in the game. There's Federation, Empire, and Jericho, and they all compete for sectors of space near where the FIRST alien artifact was found. There's a bunch of different PVP game modes, and there's also some PVE missions that are very dope and fun as well. This is a very awesome game and I just can't say enough good things about it. Oh yeah, did I mention that it's FREE! lol.

Considering every hit tends to land where it's aimed, not only is this imbalanced, but the more it happens the more jarring it is. After going through all the effort to make it realistic, it then suffers from LOL, halflife style play where your sword is super heavy, but you can move your hitboxes out of the way via rotation. Wow pvp style arena points, which causes people to steal your kills and in the process usually screw everyone over Toxic community: Feint? Do well? Tk someone who hit you first? Votekick a tker? It all boils down to how many friends you have: might makes right. Unfortunatly, with a lack of decent servers, this can literally spell the end of fun while you wait for them to go away. Mutes don't carry across games Trying to find a good server at bad times will either end with you in a server with toxic people and no exp or kicked for ping. Why can't we go back to the scrub servers? Why can't we have a couple higher level ranked servers? Server owners are anal, which seems to be the case with every medieval fanatasy fan base. (why are these people into this fandom when they have no honor or history?) if you don't know them you're insta out. FFA servers suffer from friends teaming up. Objectives are either won or you're forced to watch bad players.  You can kick, you can't vote ban. You can avoid kicks. Sometimes TKing is encouraged by your team. (!!!) Your team, with FF on and an imperitive to kill steal, will jump in front of you and prevent you from helping or making you so frustrated you swing through them, losing 10 points.  Broken, inbalanced mechanics that with recent announcements leads one to believe this game has already peaked and won't recieve proper love to fix the problems, so don't expect help.   6 dollars for a single skin? I REALLY wanted to like Daylight. I really did. It had alot of cool things going for it, like randomly generated levels, showing off the 4th version of the Unreal Engine, and Actually being a horror game with ghosts in it (count the amount of horror games with ghosts in them. I can only count them on one hand.) but it turned out to be another disappointment. Why??? Well, ill attempt to explain why.   THE GOOD  One of the good things going for Daylight is the lighting. Daylight has some great lighting, and it shows it off with the use of the different items in the game like the flares. Another cool thing is most of the environments. From a sewer, to the outside, alot of the areas look pretty good and creepy. The only areas that aren't very good are the first two but besides that, they are very well made environments. One last thing is the sound. Not all of the sound is good and the way most of the sound is used is rather crappy, but many other SFXs are great, and are fitting with the setting. The sound that tells you a ghost is nearby and most of the old, worn out doors are very creepy, and set the mood perfectly.   Also, the way Twitch (Livestreaming) is integrated is neat as well.    THE BAD Here's where it all goes downhill. one thing to make your game scary is be crafty and paced well. Daylight fails at this. Here are daylights 'regular' scares.  - cheap jumps scares  -doors opening by themselves (SPOOOOOKY)  -Objects getting flung around  -and loud screams that get old about the third time they are used  Sure, most people will find this scary, but I don't. Jump scares can be scary if done right, but ones that are thrown out of nowhere with no buildup are not scary. Daylight does that so many times its not even funny. As for doors and objects magicly opening or get thrown, its such a done to death cliche I roll my eyes instead of being scared. And the loud screams are pretty self explanatory. The worst part is the gameplay. Here is the entirety of daylights gameplay. 1. Enter level. 2.collect 6 pages. 3.go to the key. 4. Go to exit. 5.rinse and repeat. Sound familiar, doesn't it? Yes, this is pretty much a rip-off of the smash hit horror game, Slender: The Eight Pages (which I don't like either). Not only that, but it gets extremely repetitive later in the game. Another thing that is bad is the lack of enemy variety. The entire game, you're being chased by ONE ghost the whole game, and its design is very bland. Because of this, it adds on to the repetition and bore-factor of the game. One last thing is the story and characters. The story is told through notes and slight (and I mean slight) exposition between levels. I would have been fine with this if not for the fact that it is very bland and uninteresting from start to finish. Most of the notes are either ZOMG I SAW SOMEONE RUN AROUND WHERE I WAS AND IVE NEVER SEEN HIM IN MY LIFE! To ZOMG SOMEONE DIED! It got to the point where reading them felt like a chore. Another thing is that the protagonist is SOOO annoying. I give props for not using the 'silent character' approach, but id rather prefer that then this. The entire game Sarah will say 'Is anyone there' to 'What was that?' And it gets annoying real fast because of how many voice clips are used over and over again.  THE VERDICT What a disappointment. It had alot of potential, but wound up being boring and uninspired. 3/10.   For more reviews, check out our True Reviewers  Group:http://steamcommunity.com/groups/truereview   I cant believe i bought this instead of The Amazing Spider-man 2. Hey! Dont judge!

I wrote this review as a means of restoring some balance on some of the reviews below. A lot are negative, as are a number of threads in the discussions page.  I don't agree - this is a great game which reminds me of some of the best parts of the Hitman series. Gone is the linear approach of Absolution, where the freedom of your approach was curtailed. This Hitman is pure sandbox, with huge levels and many different approaches to the target. It's an approach that lends itself to mastering the levels, coming back to them over and over again.  I was somewhat skeptical of the ''episodic'' approach to releasing levels a bit at a time. So far I dont mind although i prefer to play through a game rather than a drip feed approach. The latest level, Sapienza, is a joy to play - and has all the usual elements of the series - hidden gems, humour, and freedom of approach.   In terms of bugs - There are a few that are noticeable (the odd leviatating knife), but Ive had nothing like the bugs complained to in the discussion threads. Overall, this is a solid game that plays to the fans of the series. I'd recommend you give it a go.

My graphics card had decided to die on me and as I was waiting for my new one to come in the mail I decided to get my gaming fix from a little game called 'The Cat Lady'. I like cats, and I joke about becoming a cat lady one day. It was supposed to be strange and dark which i'm never opposed to, and the graphics were at the level I needed them to be so I figured why not? What started out as a half-joke became one of the best gaming experiences i've ever had.   If you are used to high end graphics you might initially be put off by the visuals of the game, but trust me when I say you will get over that very quickly. I, in fact, became fond of the dark 2D visuals. The voice acting was of a quality that is difficult to find in even blockbuster games; very believable and genuine. I was honestly impressed with the way they were able to make these characters come to life and provide authenticity to the story.  The soundtrack was immersive and lulled you into the events appropriately. It was never jarring or detracted from the experience, but in fact enhanced it (i'm one of those gamers who is sensitive to soundtracks, the music chosen for a game is very important to me to fully realize the experience so this is a big plus).  The gameplay involved the expected puzzle solving sequences and other tasks (like performing investigative jobs, eliminating subjects and completing day-to-day activities), but it also involved a lot of decision making, often relating to some very powerful subject matter. Life, death, depression, abuse, suicide, and more. I found myself completely wrapped up with the impactful dialogue and accompanying visuals, this was truly the highlight of the game. Although it was satisfying to take out the 'parasites' in this game, I was much more concerned with the welfare and communications of the main protagonists, and very curious about the introduced supporting characters. You will care about Susan and Mitzi. You will actually want these 2D images to survive, to get stronger, to get better. You will wonder who Joe Davis is and what his story and motivations are. You will want to adopt a cat. Most importantly, you will be given some powerful insight into the nature of depression and how isolating it can be. It truly is a very unique experience. Highly recommended, even to the most hardened headshot-junkie gamer.

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: I don't usually play war games because they tend to be tactical and strategy based, but this caught my eye. The graphics, the stories, the characters. By some sheer luck, a friend had a copy of the game and gifted it to me. While some have complained about Uplay, it's just one small hitch. Putting that aside, I immensely enjoyed this game, and cried buckets as I played.  The game mechanics and puzzles seem easy, but actually take a few tries at certain parts. It all adds up to the urgency of the game. There have been times when I've to replay a scene a few times because either my timing was wrong, or just to get a feel of the pattern. Then there is the characters and stories behind it. I like that it has proper history behind it and you can read about them as it goes on. It makes so much sense when a specific character does certain actions or why that route is taken. As all the four characters (and the dog)'s story unfolds, they crush my heart. At times nail-biting, other times heart-wrenching. You begin to feel for them, and in a way, they represent the many facets of all those involved during the Great War.  Valiant Hearts really echoes with me, and I highly recommend this game to everyone. It's also educational and engaging. I find myself playing it just to unveil the events, and in the end find it a satisfying, yet entirely bittersweet. This game really throws you into the horrors of war, but also the bravery and self-sacrifice of it all: Emile, Freddie, Karl, Anna, Walt, and even the side characters. They all play an important part. Perhaps I should have expected that ending (I knew not all endings are happy), but it still made me shed tears.  The only thing I wish this game has is a DLC of the soundtrack. Because honestly, I'll buy it if there's one. All in all, get this game! I promise you won't regret it.

Took days to learn how to smile again.  Second season? Easily one of the most soul-crushingly depressing anythings. Which means it's on par with season 1, except even more horribly depressing and disheartening.

app_name: Unearthed: Trail of Ibn Battuta - Episode 1 - Gold Edition
review_text: Unearthed: Trail of Ibn Battuta - Episode 1 is not a great game. As is often the case, its inherent awfulness has been exaggerated by us hack video game writers in an attempt to deliver an amusing bash review, but it's still consistently awful in every area it wants to touch. And, because it has an unnatural hard on to be indie Uncharted, it wants to do a little slice at everything. So, for a brief glance at bad driving, rail shooting, melee combat, exploration, puzzle solving, parkour, platforming, cover-based gunfights and stealth, Unearthed is a fair case study.   Full review -  http://www.honestgamers.com/12173/pc/unearthed-trail-of-ibn-battuta-episode-1/review.html

Age of Mythology: EE is a reprise of the oldschool AoM: Titans, incorporating many online features thanks to the Steam platform the game manages to come back to life easing out the task of playing with other players around the world which is always a refreshing thing to do when it comes to replaying really oldschool games.  Graphically the improvs of the game aren't as shocking as the multiple previews we had through the years this was being announced, but its something, and its nice, decent.  The game on its core is better balanced, and the gameplay has some nifty improvements to offer.  The main issue is the performance.   Somehow the game manages to run poorly on most systems, with huge frame drops, and the games unability to run from certain common graphic cards, and only from integrated graphics. Which drops the framerate and overall responsiveness even more. To the point that is actually hard to enjoy.  Even if there aren't much changes graphically/gameplay wise to be called 'Extended Edition' its a shame such cool online features are being hindered by the inability to correctly address the performance issues that were promised to be tackled since the launch itself.  If you really enjoy RTS games, and you love AoM, and you're sure your system's performance will overcome the game issues then you will have a good time.  Otherwise. Stay Away.

And now that it's out and i've played it, i can tell you that this game can really fuel your imaginations and take your time away from you. It's immersive and amazing that we can just go visit any planet of choice and go to any solar system. It's really open to any play-style and let's you do whatever you feel like doing with it. You can name planets, creatures and plants with whatever you want, now what other game allows that? And the size for this game is not crazy high like some games and yet this game has an infinite amount of planets and solar systems to explore. Not to mention, when you die, you don't just get a boring loading screen but you have quotes. And speaking about loading screens, we don't even have loading screens when we leave or enter a planet except when we die.  Some people complain about it being repetitive but that is what exploration games mean, like Don’t Starve and other hack and slash or shooters. And the lags and fps drops will be fixed anyways so why not be a little patient?  9/10 – cause minor lag P.S - My play time is short because I play offline as I'm always in school :l

This game can be pretty difficult. In the beginning you will die. A Lot. You will die to bosses many times and think it's impossible to win. And then you will learn the ways of the dodge roll. You need to dodge roll. Eventually you will start flawlessing the first boss and then die immediately to the second. Rinse repeat getting good, then die to a normal bullet kin. It's a very fun game but it has a steep-ish learning curve. It isn't very fair to compare it to The Binding of Isaac in any way because it is much faster and doesn't focus as much on synergies, only skill. However it isn't always fair. Many of the guns in this gun game are worse than your starting gun and some which are fun run out of ammo to fast. The drops are still being balanced and they have fixed a key dropping issue where you would get almost no keys, but many times you will get a useless trinket or useless gun. Don't be fooled it can be very difficult at first. Sometimes it can be very grindy so maybe play it in bursts. I have only been playing for around 80 hours and it is easy to get caught up it, but just know you probably aren't going to get good items most of the time. This game requires more skill compared to other games like Isaac that require good items and some skill. The bosses are the real bullet hell though. It is worth noting that you may have bad luck and will spend up to 2 minutes on the first boss since you need good guns and that bosses are bullet sponges. The first floor will always have a chest with a weapon and a chest with a passive item. May RNJesus be on your side. Just watch out for dirty rats.       TL:DR It's still being balanced but is a fun and grindy bullet-hell game where you need RNG and skill  Tl:DR;DR Don't be a scrub and git gud

Republique is a brilliant stealth game from a small development team with an  amazing  story. If you don't cry a little at the end of Chapter 3, well, you may not have a soul. The story is just that good.  The simple 'point-and-click' controls are reminiscent of later Kings Quest games which means that your grandmother could play this. But don't think that means Republique will be a walk in the park! The guards are smart, the puzzles are intriguing, and there are several places off the beaten path to explore. BRILLIANT voice acting by Rena Strober, Jennifer Hale, David Hayter, Khary Payton, and Dwight Schultz make this a must buy for me. (I'm a sucker for good voice acting, what can I say?)   I played this on my Android, and I had a blast, but I want you to understand: This is no mere phone port. The graphics have been upgraded to take full advantage of the Unity 5 engine, and it shows. The new cinematics are amazing, and the environments really benefit from the new lighting and textures. I was happy to see that this came to PC, but wasn't sure about the price. But I took a risk, and I'm glad I did. It was totally worth it. I can't wait for chapters 4 and 5 to come out so I can see what happens next.   5/5 stars. A+++ would recommend.

I cannot recommend this game because it looks dull, has no real gameplay and the story isn't interesting enough to make that up and it's short as hell. New players:   This game is challenging, but u can make it even harder yourself in the game. For new players of the total war series i suggest starting on normal and try out all the factions / races first cause they all play very different, with some u can rush the campaign map and with others u need to build up economy and some army first and just conquer a province. Start with diplomacy from the start and try to check back on it some turns later to see if u discovered new factions or situations have changed with your current relations and can be improved.  Tip to new and veteran players:   Once u picked a campaign difficulty, u can then adjust the battle difficulty in the game at the menu, this due to the fact that above normal difficulty the enemy's do not only play better but the units become stronger.  Conclusion:  The future is looking very bright for this game even for people who want to play singleplayer only or a CO-UP vs AI This due to the fact that excisting factions and the capaign map will get new units, hero's, lords and more territory/landscapes with new races and army's in the future, even now there are allot of DLC's and one Expansion.  Do keep a track of time cause this game sucks u in and u easily lose track of time. Cause u have diplomacy, defense, attack, quests, economy, technology and skill trees and much more progress in the game that take your attention and can sometimes be game changing.  Personal opinion:  I like history and all that but fun wise, this is the best Total War game so far, the massive numbers of different races and units, the different battle maps u fight in and all the different ways they fight and have special effects in battle makes this a timeless strategy game with everything u need or could think of and its only getting better.

The game itself can be enjoyable, however there are a bunch of things holding it back:  - The camera control is super clunky and sluggish, no way to rotate too.  - No speed-up time feature, you have to watch the ball slowly make its way to the end every time, if you failed too bad you're gonna waste your time just sitting there watching the ball again.  - Every time you place something you gotta click on inventory to open it then click on the thing, over and over, doesn't matter if you're gonna place the same kind of gadget. It sounds minor, but gets irratating after playing for like 15 minutes.  - Sometimes the ball would stop moving for no reason, and you have to reset the level and watch it slowly make its progress again.  - Gadgets glitch out and you can't click on them after reseting the ball, so what you need to do is reset the whole level and place every gadged again.  - The ball is constantly slow-ish, no matter if it goes through speed boosts.  - There is no indicator of how far the ball will be thrown by some of the gadgets, so you're left experimenting until you get it right, not because the puzzle is hard, but because the game failes to give you useful information.  - First 30 or so puzzle were really easy, don't know if they become hard at some point, but I personally don't have the patience to play anymore.  That being said, the game can be enjayable for those with patience and high tolerance. I personally won't play much more and can't recommend it because of the reasons stated above.

A masterpiece of game design.  The online coop is the best I've ever seen. It's designed in such a way as to compel you to play it cooperatively. Try to go it alone and you'll be overrun by one of the specials. The zombies are also balanced perfectly for 4 players. If one player goes down it becomes much more difficult. This means that it is always better to share your supplies with your team mates rather than horde them for yourself. This further promotes a positive cooperative atmosphere to the game. One final component of the excellent coop design is that you are credited on screen whenever you do something that helps the group. Again, this rewards and encourages cooperation and camaraderie between players.  The weapons have enough varieity to support all kinds of play styles, and not one of them feels unnecessary. All of them have their own merits and you'll find yourself constantly trying different ones rather than sticking to one favourite.  The supply drops are placed perfectly. They encourage exploration of the levels and are sparse enough that they almost always feel valuable when you find one.  The level design itself is closed without feeling closed. I'm not sure how they managed it but valve created the levels in a way that naturally draws players to their conclusions. I very rarely felt myself lost or stuck and didn't feel constricted by the linearity of the game. There is enough breathing room in the play space that makes them feel open.  The mission design is varied so that you won't feel bored playing through the campaign multiple times.  The graphics are timeless even years after release. The only thing I found strange about them was the film grain effect but you can disable that.  Ultimately I find it very difficult to find bad things to say about this game so I have to give it a perfect 10

Rule of thumb for popular multiplayer games!  6 months after release = Servers strong, newbies and confused people plentiful. [Big Patch Event/DLC Content] 1 year after release = Servers plummeted in numbers, much better experienced players populate them. 2 years after release = Servers very small BUT still provided by devs. Hackers begin to surface. 2 years, six months after release = Player made servers non-exsistant. 1 in 40 lobbies are hacked. [Big Patch Event/DLC Content in attempt to bring players back] 3 years after release = Handful of servers populated by extremely competative professionals. [Sequel to game release announced/utter abandonment of game and possibly servers]  So in conclusion, LFD2 is an excellent game but has been out for a while. Expect people to know maps inside out, exploit glitches and problems and be nothing less than the best and get angry at you for not keeping up (or being psychic). If you have some buddies LFD2 is an excellent timewaster with its mutated campaign gamemodes.

If you're looking for a review, you've come to the wrong place but heres a recipe for a cinnamon apple pie instead: Ingredients: 1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter 2. 1/2 cup white sugar 3. 1/2 cup brown sugar 4. 1/4 cup water 5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon 6. 1 pinch salt 7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced 8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie   Directions: 1. Preheat oven to 425 degrees F (220 degrees C). 2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside. 3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices. 4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal. 5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust. 6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C), and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.

Gunpoint is a puzzle game with some stealth elements where you play as a freelance spy working for competing companies while also trying to protect yourself from being framed for a murder. It is equal parts hilarious and smart, with a script that manages to both tell a serious story while allowing your character to be completely snarky and witty the entire time. I was laughing at nearly every exchange of dialogue between the characters. The game itself plays very well, with a multitude of gadgets and quality level design that allows you to beat the levels through many different tactics, and a possibly branching narrative that encourages a second or third playthrough. The game is short, maybe 3 hours long, but entirely worth its $10 price tag.

app_name: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition
review_text: The Witcher 2 grabs ones attention with it's stunning graphics and well designed environments but it's the compelling story lines which keeps the player coming back for more. What makes The Witcher 2 unique from other RPGs is the ability for the player's actions to have a real effect on the progression of the story lines and similarly the world around them. Even the side quests are interesting elements of this game and can also make a difference on how things pan out. Although there are some linear sections sprinkled throughout the game, the developers have done a good job ensuring these don't feel too constraining to the player. For gamers who enjoy well written and in depth story lines, The Witcher 2 is definitely worth a play through; however for those who aren't interested in complex story lines and prefer more focus on hacking and slashing this might not be the game for you.

Hotline Miami. Where do I start? This game is spectacular! The music is spot on and the gameplay is just perfect. Other people might say this game is repetitive but it's not, really. You get rewarded for doing different types of killing... Sounds bad, I know.   There's no other word to put it. What's frequent in this game is.. you die... a lot. That's normal considering the difficulty of this game. When you get killed by an enemy, you start again where you spawned. It's kind of hard, especially in the later levels. In this game, you start off in the tutorial as most games do. After that, you start the level and you then put on a mask. You then kill the Russians in the building either by beating them up or using a weapon to kill them in one hit. That's kind of what you do in the game. In some levels, there are bosses which you need to kill with the weapons you pick up either a gun or a melee weapon. As you progress through the game, you collect more masks and weapons. The masks give you special abilities but the first one you receive doesn't give you any type of special ability whatsoever.   In my opinion, this game is pretty awesome and you should buy it whether or not its on sale. I can't wait for Hotline Miami 2. I hope that game is as good as the first one. Unlike my older reviews, I'm not going to give a number rating on this. Instead, I'm going to say that it is awesome. Get it now.

app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: What do you say about a game that already has nearly 10 thousand positive reviews?  When I was young and naive, I had no idea how to PC game. The first game that changed that was Fallout 1, the iso-RPG predecessor to this game. When Fallout 3 was announced, I admit I was skeptical. A first-person shooter? Trying to catch the imagination, care, precision, and solid (yet challenging) gameplay that the first two games had? Impossible!  I was right. In many ways, more care was put into recreating the mythos in 3D. And by 3D, I don't mean the Van Buren demo ;). No, this game's world was rich! Deep! Intricate, yet approachable. They did a lot of research going into this game and it shows through the environment's rough and rugged textures. Buildings that look nuked is a particularly nice touch.  But I'm sure you could have learned all of this from one of those other ten thousand or so positive reviews!

Everything is balanced, from perks, to guns, to leveling, and even the enemies, although the cazadors are a bit hard to kill. The new locations really show off the beauty of the engine, and while there are some bugs, a patch came out today that's really working quite well. The voice acting is great and the characters, simply charismatic. Enjoy your stay.

It may have been overshadowed by the scope of its contemporary, Deus Ex, and the streamlined accessibility of its spiritual successor, BioShock, but System Shock 2 was unique in its day and still holds its own today as a classic First-Person Action RPG, providing an experience that, unfortunately, still hasn't quite been reproduced in any other game since.  The original System Shock was somewhat ahead of its time (consequently it suffered from questionable execution of some of its ideas such as with its controls) whereas Shock 2 arrived at just the right time to help define the Immersive Sim genre with an arguably more refined take on the cyberpunk horror concept.   Created from a collaboration between Looking Glass Studios and Irrational Games, Shock 2 has many similar traits to other games from the school of immersive sims such as Thief and the aforementioned Deus Ex. Atmosphere, world-building and multi-solution problems - all pillars of the genre - are to be found here in abundance.  With deep customization trees that allow you to use all kinds of weapons and tools and develop exotic powers, Shock 2 allows you to approach the game in a variety of ways, and then return to its creepy hallways with a new character build for a different experience. Enlist with the Marines to wield the heavy-duty military weapons found scattered across the Von Braun; or spend some time in deep space with the Navy and learn to use highly technical engineering tools such as hacking and energy-based weapons; or maybe you'd rather get in touch with your inner zen and gain mastery in the powers of the mind. Shock 2 might make you work to earn each and every skill point, but it rewards investment into specialisations.  Shock 2's story is told through an impressive number of different narrative devices, each serving to slowly reveal the mysterious ship, its previous owners, and its new inhabitants. Clear plot points and objectives keep you moving forward through the numerous decks of the ship. While wireless transmissions, audio logs and emails are the norm in today's games, they were still new and innovative when Shock 2 made use of them. The game steers clear from technical hurdles that would shatter your immersion - there are no awkward NPCs to draw you out of the experience - instead, the game emphasizes isolation and loneliness, and the characters come to life through their voices, whether logging their last moments or speaking into your receiver to instruct you with dubious intent.   In fact, the sound design may be Shock 2’s crowning achievement. The doom-ambiance of the cargo bays will be an enduring memory for those who have gone through that harrowing ordeal. The multitude of enemies identify themselves through their terrifying sounds, such as the tragi-horrific moans from grunts of the Many, urging you to kill them, and the ear-splitting screeches of the psi-enhanced monkeys. The iconic antagonist SHODAN is memorably characterized by her evilly seductive and corrupted speech, echoing throughout the ship and reverberating within your skull.  Shock 2 won’t be considered a looker today - it certainly wasn't at the time either - but there are a few community-built mods available to help make it more palatable to the eye. By and large, you’ll still have to develop a taste for its neon-green cyberpunk aesthetics and hard-hitting techno soundtrack, but if you do, you’ll find plenty of interesting enemies and well-realised locations as you explore. The layout of the ship follows a believable structure, with different decks for different purposes such as the Medical wing that you start out in, and the Recreational deck complete with a swimming pool and basketball court.   The HUD successfully feels like a cool sci-fi neural interface, allowing you to manage your inventory, browse through your logs and emails, upgrade your software and even conduct research on unknown materials. Not to mention the inclusion of a mini game console, complete with game titles to collect like Overworld Zero, a roguelike to help you unwind during your downtime or to kill the minutes as a security alarm ticks down. The combat might feel rather stiff and clumsy owing to the game’s dated engine, but if you approach every scenario with careful preparation and strategic thinking, you’ll overcome the odds without much fumbling.  All of which is to say that there is so much to System Shock 2 that is worth your time. It’s not easy to get into, but it’s almost impossible to let go of. It rewards exploration of its mechanics, environments and story. It is a classic that endures the test of time and continues to influence games and gamers, a decade on.

Post-June update: The new GUI is crap. A lot of people are pointing that out. But, what I am noticing is the play of the games has changed. The gravity simulation feels different. The nudging does not have the same bump action pre-update. And the tables are glitching a lot more. I always play on DX11 on a PC.  Gorgar for instance. I can shoot the plunger, set the ball down the C lane, and it center drains 60% of the time. Flippers that used to seem strong now react spongy, like there is dirt gumming up the flipper post. Also, sometimes the rubbers on the playfield spring like you would expect, other times they are dead like they are actual dried out rubber that need to be replaced. Also, the scoreboard graphic that is in the upper left corner is unreadable. The score sits behind the ball counter graphic.  Flight 2000, Firepower, Black Knight, Centaur and even Star Trek: The Next Generation all have similar issues. I do not know if the game mechanics have been tweaked, or if the gravity engine has changed but, something is different and it is very frustrating. I personally own a Haunted House and a Terminator 2. I have restored them and play them on an almost daily basis. I know the angles and the shots. The reason why I have bought all five pro-packs for me and my Son was because of how realistic the play was on this simulator. Also, finding any of these machines in this game in working order where we can play for hours is hard to come by.   We started playing this game on the PS3 and it was because you are modelling all of these old classic pins that now, as time goes on, if you want to buy one, it costs thousands of dollars to get one. When we saw it was on Steam, we did not hesitate to purchase every season again, and in duplicate. The play on the PS3 was very poor sometimes but, we love pinball.  I do recommend this game but, something has changed since the patch. And it needs to be fixed. If you are looking to buy this for the first time, maybe hold off until all the bugs have been chased out of the outlanes.

The best thing about playing Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor is that it makes me feel over-the-top powerful without sacrificing the fear of defeat. It’s got great free-flowing combat and a good-sized, good-looking open world full of Lord of the Rings lore to find, but what makes it special is what’s going on in the background: an intriguing hierarchy of enemies that gives every victory and defeat extra meaning.  A brief and shocking opening scene sets a dark and brutal tone as Talion, a former Ranger of Gondor, is ritually executed along with his family. Talion’s spirit is then bound to an amnesiac elf ghost and returned to Middle-earth for vengeance against Sauron’s forces. It’s a story that doesn’t make total sense for hardcore Tolkien scholars, but it’s well-acted. It makes decent use of Gollum, and there are a couple of memorable new characters - particularly Ratbag the comic-relief uruk, who guides you through the process of infiltrating the enemy army.  My interest in the plot dwindled toward the end, but it does a fair job of explaining why Talion has such sweet supernatural powers with his sword, dagger, and bow. Much like in the Batman: Arkham games, you can choose to stealthily pick off enemies one at a time, or dive head-first into a brawl with dozens of opponents and beat the crap out of them with a smoothly animated series of attacks and counters. Unlike Batman, of course, Mordor’s involve great, gory decapitation and stabbing animations, and outside of the scripted story missions, there are few distinctly separated stealth and combat areas. It’s enticingly freeform.  Acrobatically slaughtering Sauron’s ugly minions feels as easy as it appears to be for Legolas in Peter Jackson’s movies, and it’s more fun to do than to watch. Sword combat feels pretty much just as good here as brawling does in Batman – which is to say it’s amazing. Mordor’s take on building up hit streaks to power up lethal takedowns is a bit more forgiving, in that you’re almost never locked into an animation. If you push the counter button, Talion will drop what he’s doing and counter instantly. It’s extremely responsive. A time-slowing power makes scoring a couple of headshots with the bow easy, whether in stealth or in the heat of battle, though it’s kept in check by ammo constraints and a limited (but upgradable) supply of time-slowing juice. And even though stealth gameplay is pretty basic, it’s great to have the option to thin the herd a bit before they know you’re there.

review_text: You wouldn't know that hidden within Typing of the Dead: Extended Cut is a another absolutley brilliant and fun FPS rail style game called House of the Dead Overkill: Extended Cut. The story is highly entertaining, The characters are brilliantly concieved, corny and sufficiently angry and the main female character Varla Guns is without a doubt the hottest femaile/stripper in video game history. I would spend my life savings watchiung her dance. Issac Washington is a black detective teamed up with and FBI Agent. Washinton is a f-bomb factory and knows how to talk about women in a mannert that gives new meaning to sexist pig. And they are what makes the games so fun to play. The dialouge is never ending from level to level. This is the only rail type FPS shooter where each room/screen is cleared before moving to next. Sufficiently difficult and at times very hard. I wish Steam would break it out from Typing of the Dead. House of the Dead: Overkill is the one Steam forgot. Flatout forgot.

My first try with this game was 13 hours, so for the tl;dr people I recommend this game with all of my heart!  I have to admit that I had forgotten how much I like city builder games. This thing is near perfection, though there are some things that could be improved. This game has endless possibilities and I'm pretty sure it's going to be a classic that the userbased modding will keep 'alive' for years.   Rating - 9/10      Free and realistic city planning:   You can create your city the way you want and it will react very realistically to it. Especially when the city grows into a megapolis, even the smallest changes can have catastrophic results. The zoning is a great way to plan what is build where.     The difficulty:   The game teaches you basics at the beginning and then lefts you loose to do what ever you want. It doesn't hold your hand but this can leave you some times bit baffled on what you are supposed to do. The great thing about these situations is that you can usually think your self out of the issues logically.     Public transportation and traffic:   It's quite obvious that the creators previous games have been traffic simulators. Creating well flowing streets is challenging in a good way as your city dwellers aren't the smartest kind when they hop on a car. (Another very realistic point of this game) The public transportation system is great but I would like to get better line builder. The current system is good and intuitive, but when your city grows into a massive scale, it becomes very difficult to keep track of your bus lines etc. Extra points for possibility to choose own colors for each line.     So much to do!   There is a lot to do in this game. Usually I get bored with the city builders after I get the city running, but this game keeps you busy as it should. There seems to be endless possibilities on expanding your city. The lack of natural disasters haven't yet bothered me but I'm sure some one will make a mod for it.      Improvement ideas:  * Seasons and day/night cycle * Better public transport management -tools

I don't usually like indie games, but this one, however, is quite nice. I like the fact that your character gets stats upgrades throughout the run and you don't really have to upgrade  a  weapon. Even though, I dislike a bit the fact that the weapons in the shop are a bit expensive. You probably have to buy them when they are 'on sale'(random in-game shop discounts) and just ring around the rosie with the zombies untill you get enough money(I have only played for one hour or so, so I don't know what strategies are to be followed). You may be able to upgrade your defense system, but the offensive one will probably be worse. Also, I don't know why the money, temporary stats upgrades and ammo dropped by the zombies disappear so fast. I believe they should stick around a bit more. For example, at the end of the night(or round), when the character stats upgrade menu pops up, it freezes you and you cannot pick anymore items left on the ground.. which sucks. Lastly, the optimization is not so well done. I don't know if it's capped or something, but I can't go above 30fps for some reason. It just.. doesn't go further. Despite this being more of a rant than a positive feedback, I do recommend it because it's really fun, both singleplayer and multiplayer.

Worth the Movie Money*.   * Movie Money is the amount of time and enjoyment I could get at the movies for the same amount of money I paid for the game. I valuate if a game is worth purchasing based on this metric.

Turn, and run. I played nearly all the predecessors from the series and, assuming this would be another iterative improvement with a new engine and better UI, got sucked into a pre-order. This is not the X game you were looking for. It doesn't even seem to be an X game, just a console port that was never accepted for its console of choice, and so was sadly pushed into a PC launch wayyy before it was finished. Ubiquitous bugs aside, the game just isn't done. Maybe another year of development could have turned it into something special, but then it would have to compete with Elite and Star Citizen, something I sadly cannot see this game ever doing. You can see what they were trying to do, but oh my did they miss the target, every time. It just feels like a poorly implemented console game with none of the richness and depth that made the X series what it is (was). Maybe in a year with a heap of community mods this will be worth 5 bucks on sale, but I hesitate to even hope that much. You can get WIng Commander Privateer 2 on GOG for 5 bucks now.

My choice for the hidden gem category!  And finally, a game that treats you like a grown up: You can save literally anywhere, as many times as you like. Its 'real-time with pause' gameplay allows you think your moves and lets you walk away to your fridge or pick up the phone, as you like.  The characters at some points may seem a bit cheesy, still all in all a well made 70s setting.  The gameplay is basically clicking paths for your different characters. Then at every node of that path you can attach one or multiple commands for the respective skill of the character, like putting down a guard or crawling through an air vent. So it's up to you, how you solve achieving the goal set out by your boss, like stealing a diamond or freeing your buddies from prison.  A great game! ...unfortunately it hasn't gotten the attention it deserves! Just watch the two trailer videos on the store page ;-)

I've been playing all of the computer Magic games since they first game out. While this game has some issues I think it's overall good, has plenty of potential and is far better than the horrible version of their last release.   While the game can be slightly buggy at times I believe the patches are slowly taking care of those issues. I think the deck colors are fairly balanced and the autobuilder is a good help, especially to those learning the game. While the computer does seem to 'cheat' at times it puts up a good challenge and is probably the best AI I've seen in a Magic game.  The player matches do drag. I'd like to see shorter timers and stiffer penalties for people who drop off matches.   I definitely disagree with the people whining about having to pay money to get good cards. I play sparingly, attempt to complete all of the challenges as they come in and have unlocked probably 15 packs, along with many rare cards along with 2 of 5 of the Planeswalkers.   As long as they provide fairly regular patches and content updates this should shape up to be a great game.

Tons of play time for only $25.00. Outstanding value for money if you're into this type of game. The time and effort spent on details, gameplay, and features really shows. This game is beautiful and it runs like butter on my very old 32-bit XP box (3GHz Athlon 64 X2 CPU, 4GB of DDR2 RAM), Radeon 6700-series video).  [Edit] Revision to the above. The game has crashed 4 times, once with an actual 'The game has crashed' dialog (creating a crash dump folder which I emailed to the developer), and 3 times it just locked up solid at various points., forcing me to Ctrl+Shift+Esc into Task Manager to kill the process. In fairness, It's possible that the problem may lie with my aging hardware.  Things I like: 1. Great combination of puzzle/strategy/simulation. Min/maxers will *love* this game! 2. Except for entering your company name and custom names for your saves (both of which are optional), game can be played entirely with the mouse. 3. So nice to have a 'sandbox' mode to just goof around in as well as create your own levels/puzzles. 4. Also nice to be able to build/remove/adjust while paused.  One nit: As with many such real-time simulation games, there can be lengthy periods of 'sit back, watch, and wait' but the visuals of your factory alleviate that somewhat.

Good puzzle-platformer. Trine reminds me of the old The Lost Vikings for its gameplay, you get three different characters and alternate in between them to get through the challenged on each stage.  Visually very pretty, Trine's narrative has a dose of humor and a very nice soundtrack that suits well each stage. It is fun to find every hidden item on the game, and at times some clever exploration is required.  The game itself is quite easy though, even on the hardest mode. Very little enemy variation (You basically kill a horde of skeletons), and it gets repetitive when you approach the end.  Anyway, it was a fun ride, and certainly more than I'd ask for the price of admission.  7/10

I was trying to be humorous with my initial review, but it wasn't clear enough.  First, if you're not sure this is the game for you, make sure you setup a Frontier account and link it to your Steam account before running the game because when the launcher is open, the game will appear to be running to Steam. Also, watch all the training videos on YouTube first instead of clicking the links in the game (which also send you to YouTube). The game itself doesn't teach you all the basics, so these are useful to watch. The videos total about an hour, so you could easily get 2 hours of playtime before you actually play the game. This is more of an issue with Steam than the game itself. You should at least be able to play the game long enough to get a sense of it...  The UI in this game is great and I like the fact that they tried to be realistic with the spacing of stars and planets. However, the main reason I cannot recommend this game is the implementation of supercruise. You spend a lot of time flying from point A to point B for trading, etc. It could easily take you 10 minutes in supercruise to fly in a straight line to another space port within a star system and unless you go slow on your approach - even though it slows you down automatically while you're approaching, there's a good chance you'll overshoot it and spend extra time flying back to it. You can't simply warp there and there doesn't seem to be an autopilot to do this for you. Most destinations in supercruise are a straight line, so you could step away from the computer, but you still have to pay attention and slow down well beforehand to avoid overshooting your destination. A good ship is expensive. To earn enough through trading to get a good ship and decent equipment would require a lot of trips like this.  Mining is also a chore. It involves carving half a dozen chunks of ore off an asteroid and chasing them down just to get enough ore to have 1 piece of refined ore and you can only gather/smelt one ore at a time (depending on your equipment). For the amount of time this takes, I'm not sure that it's worth it - especially if you could make more from trading in the same amount of time.  Having played NMS, I was encouraged by all the reviews comparing this game favorably to that. There is more substance to this game and space seems more realistic, but the implementation of supercruise makes it seem more tedious.  I recommend Rebel Galaxy instead for solo players. Travel takes much less time and it's much more eventful, among other things.
