As any review that has credibility, I'll point out the pros(+)/cons(-) of this game for the sake of impartiality so it's up to you decide if it's worth. In my view, the pros are greater that the cons, so that's why this is comming with a thumbs up.  In summary, SRS is a 2004 race game (this steam version was released in 2005) that features an underground import racing scene, on which the player's main objective is to gain respect on the streets and affection of various females in the city.  + This game has a killer idea of gameplay, in which you have 3 types of racing: 1. Crew Meets, 2. Street Challenges and 3. Random Encounter. In Crew Meets, you have 3 pre-requisites to meet in order to enter a racing series: Respect Amount, Ante Cash (to build the final prize) and the car horsepower. When these are attended, you may enter the series of 3 races. Before each one of these races, you decide whether to place or not a reserved bet on a specific racer to boost your money earnings. These bets are per race and not related to the total prize made by the ante cash of the 4 racers. The others two types are similar and simpler. To compete in them, you need the cash the rival is willing to bet. The higher his car specs are compared to yours, higher is the amount of cash required to race (for instance, if its a skyline versus a skyline, the bet is x thousand, but if it's a skyline versus a celica, the wager would go up to 3x thousand). The difference between 2 and 3 race types is in the way you trigger them. While 2 is in a specific spot within the map, 3 is triggered when you eventually find a racer in free roam and you flash your headlights at the rear of his car. The customization options require effort in tunning because all can be bought and installed in your car since the very beggining; you only need the cash to get it. But that is not probably the best idea bacause equipping your car with all that can be purchased may overpower the car, rendering it uncontrolable, easy to crash and slower than the competition as it burns tires for a quarter mile at the slightest touch on the throttle. A balanced car is needed to raise the win rate and finding the thereshold for power/control in each car is different and very rewarding for those who like motorsports (as myself). 'But where is the killer part in the gameplay until now?' 'All this I have seen in other racing games blabla...' Now just imagine all this I have said with these aspects: SRS features a car damage model that forces the player to drive carefully. Heavy damage impacts car performance and heavy repairs cost money to the player. Depending on the price of the car and the amount of destruction, fixing the ride will drain the most (if not all or even more than) of the earnings from their last race. All of this with no restart feature! That's right people NO RESTART! If you lose a race, you will lose the money and your girlfriend - if you had any riding with you - and that is genious. Weaklings don't survive on this hardcore simulation of life game, and I say that because in life you have only 1 shot at pretty much everything, and this games does just that, and it does beautifully, unlike any other from any other genre.  - For the weak spots, I will state the lack of polish and a deeper care for the excellent idea the game promotes. The game has a fair amount of cars and customization options (given the limitations of the platforms originally intended to run this game) but the carrer mode is very short and generic. There are only a handful of short cutscenes to give the player a feedback on the overall progress and the story itself is a little out of place. The driving feels different for each car as they have different wheel drive configurations, weight and engineering, but that isn't very convincing in this game. The physics lack trimming to convince the player the car is actually going through the surface, the graphics are dated and the city plays static and dead. In some regions is always night time and in others always day, the police A.I. is abnormally ♥♥♥♥♥♥ and you can count in your fingers the people found on the streets and sidewalks on the hole district.  With these comments stated, I finish this review giving a score of 8.5/10 to this game. I know the game looks dull, unfinshed and unpolished, but my score is this high because of the concepts this game offers. The removal of the restart feature makes you enjoy and live trough every corner of the game, and crashing makes you rage just like if you did in a real car. You know it can't be undone and it's going to cost a lot. With 14 hours of gameplay on this steam version and hundreds more in the XBOX version, I can safely assure you, seen that I have experienced countless other racing games from before and after SRS time, that this game is the best at it's genre, and I strongly recommend it to virtual racing and car tuning lovers just like myself.

After playing the first installment in the Pen and Paper series, I already had my eye on this game. More witty comments and terrible puns? I'm up for that. I happened to come across this game as part of a bundle, and started playing right away. To me, this game is everything good about KoPaP and then some. I'm already at about level 25 after a few days of play, but for the amount this game costs (about $9 CAD) you're getting some decent bang for your buck.  Story: 8/10. Silly and fun, but with plenty of lore. Reminds me of a more kid-friendly Borderlands.  Graphics: 8.5/10. Attack animations fit together smoothly in a classical 16-bit RPG style.  Gameplay: 7.5/10. While it's a lot of fun, grinding can get repetitive. Definitely a step up from the first game, though.  Music: 7/10. Nice area themes and battle themes, but it could have used a tad more diversity.   Overall: 8/10.  I'm currently having a lot of fun, and I really felt like trying to post a more in-depth review. Hopefully this help anyone considering to purchase the game. I'm currently holding off on my opinion of the Here Be Dragons DLC, as I have yet to buy it myself.

What happened here ? (for comparison I am able to play Crisis 2 on 1920 x 1080 with high detail on and get 50 -60 fps).  There are a lot of bugs, bugs and ton of bugs.... here are some of them:   Sometimes the sounds crashes and the only way to fix it is to restart the game. The server search engine can sometimes find servers like “Australian ones” (and you are in Europe :P ) but it is  unable to find European servers. The item drops sytem works....sometimes. It shows you acuierd an item but they dont appear in your inventory. The textures load slow and pop – up.  Sometimes textures or skins will not load.   Of course you can say it is still in early access and those bugs will be fixed, but...technicly it is SECOND TIME IN EARLY ACCESS ! ! ! (after all it is Primal Carnage uploaded on steam second time with changed title) so there is on esxuse.    The developers where not able to fix the bugs in “first” Primal Carnage, so what guarantee you have they will fix them this time ?! None.   The only think they did was to reales anather, identical “game” and force most of us to buy it....Again. And what do we get ? For paying twice for the same ?   A broken “game” that is still barly playable.   I will not be suprised if soon there will be an info about anather game called “Primal Carnege: RESURECION” or “Primal Carnage: REVANGE”   I bought the game only becouse I thought it will be unfair to write a comment without experiencing the “game” itself. This is.. okay. I mean I only paid like $0.50 for it on sale so it was well worth the cost. It kept me entertained for like 2 hours or so (not including idle time). I got to play a game, hear a story, and give myself something to do while waiting on progression on a roleplaying game on my other monitor. I'll recommend it if you need a time-waster and have some change laying around, but otherwise I think there are better games you could choose from.  Pros: +Story was interesting to see. It didn't suck. +Variety of puzzles so it didn't seem repetitive. +Appreciated the instructions for the puzzles upon opening the puzzle, without having to use a hint to know what kind of a puzzle it was so I didn't sit there forever doing the wrong thing.  Ehs: =Drawings were okay. They weren't as good as some of this style games are, but they weren't awful either. =Cannot review sound or any voice-overs (I'll assume there are some) since it was late so my speakers were off.  Cons: -Certain parts lacked subtitles. I couldn't turn on sound and I seem to have missed some parts because they didn't have subtitles unlike the rest of it. -The game was on advanced and still didn't seem hard at all. Either I'm really good at these games, or it was just too easy for an advanced mode. -I didn't like the handholding aspect of it. I'd click on something and it would say flat out at the top of the screen 'I could use a screwdriver for this.' Meh. Its like, 'Oh, well thank you game, I happen to have a screwdriver in my inventory. Rather than figure it out myself so it requires any effort on my part aside from clicking, you go ahead and tell me how to do it, mmk.' -Sometimes the bottom of the screen that flips up your inventory would not respond. It kept sending me back a room instead of opening the inventory. -When the inventory would respond, it had an annoying delay to flip open. The back button to go back a room was also delayed. I really didn't like the delays when having to travel back 10 rooms for a stone or something. -I was honestly tired of the back and forth. Its fine to find pieces in one room, go to another, go back, go to another, etc. But when your inv has a scroll bar because you are still collecting pieces from 7 rooms ago... thats excessive. I was starting to get annoyed having to walk back that far to finally open a chest for a piece that really could have been given further up in a different room.

my review:  'Elite: Dangerous is one of the best open-world space exploration game I've ever played, surpassing games such as PAYDAY 2 on my favorites list. With no hand-holding rules, and multiple things to do, I most definitely recommend this game to anyone who prefers the genre.'  First review I've seen:  'Elite: Dangerously Boring is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ game about flying through space. Sometimes you shoot other ships but mostly you don't.'  Not true. Actually having played for some time, I've been relatively successful NOT shooting any ships at all, because that is the beauty of this. You dont have to shoot other people. You can be an explorer, and sell data for profit, or you can be a trader if you want. E:D has no real limits on what you can do, and if you need any help, there's a wiki. a very detailed, helpful wiki.  Play to have fun, don't try to win a game that has no such reason to include such a system.  Rant over, have an actual review: + Excellent graphics. I would take notes, they hit the look & feel sweet spot. +Great sounds: nothing much to say, excellent sounds. + Smooth gameplay and control. only time that I've had a control problem: I was under stress and pressed the wrong button. - In game contract system! No. Contracts CAN be a bit tedious, and there are really only 4 or such types, and the payments are relatively small. yes, one contract can be worth 100,000 CR, but it's also somewhat of a challenge, usually with a hint of RNG luck mixed in with the fail condidion. Grab a weapon and kill a few wanted people, no need for the system.  But, due to the glory of the game, after starting out, you don't need to do these.  I've gotten more than my money's worth out of E:D. Probably one of, if not the best game I've ever played. Frontier Developments: Keep up the good work!

Quite a good game, I bought this as my Spore game by EA and Maxis no longer works - why I am clueless. The bright colours can be quite straining on your eyes when you first start playing but after about five minutes you get used to it. Honestly though, I expected more - much more - at the start of this game. There are a limited number of levels and only three different shapes you can be depending on your level - either a red, blue or green organism. There is not much to do other than follow simple tasks that don't ask much of the player, other than the odd few tasks that are extremely difficult to complete. There is no HP meter or health bar etc so you cannot die, meaning that the few enemies and one boss fight aren't challenging at all - more laborious. However, my deep affinity for evolution games and the empty hole left by Spore kept me playing this game, despite all that is wrong with it. Overall, I think it would be much more interesting if there was more freedom given to the player, e.g. the same evolution system but utilised in a different way, such as the nutrient points could be 'sold' for certain body parts you could place on your creature, wherever you wanted. Also, I like the idea of having your 'creator' looking in and talking to you, but maybe a deeper storyline would help. To sum it all up, I would only recommend this game to people who love evolution style games.

Another game in the clicking-genre - however not that too great of it.   To start off, there's no real satisfication or real point to this game. You click the girls/leave your team to attack, wait until you have enough spirits, reset, spend spirits on 'artifacts' (pets) that will increase your damage output, time on bosses, gold gained etc a small amount... then rinse and repeat. Unless I've missed out on something, it just goes on forever and the areas and enemies just repeat. There aren't any achievements and the trading cards/emotes/backgrounds are kind of disappointing. I'd love it if the backgrounds were the backgrounds in the game, but it's just one of the enemies to the side of your profile.  On the topic of trading cards, as this is a F2P game you can only get them by purchasing the DLC, which is just clothes, hairstyles and weapons for your main character. Sounds cool, right? Until you realise that all your character does is stand to the side of the screen, completly still and doing nothing. A lot of the DLC can only be accessed from the game (I counted around 21 pieces of DLC (clothes and weapons are seperate, hairstyles are in one pack) yet only 10 of these apparer on the front page. This may just be a bug with Steam however...? Again, none of this impacts your gameplay at all, unless you want to get aroused from the artwork of a girl covered in chocolate or wrapped up in bows while playing.  The only 'good' thing one could consider is the artwork, which from an artistic point of view is very good. The backgrounds and beautiful and  some  thought went into designing the 'enemies'... however that's it. The enemies are just generic female anime-styled characters dressed up as animals and such, most of which are sexualized in some way. It's kind of nice how there are different faces and hairstyles randomized though for the classes, so it's not the same enemy each time. Ignoring the sexual features, the designs are pretty interesting to be honest.  I will give credit for being able to mute things seperately - voices, effects and music. The music I find is pretty good and suits an old JRPG style, however gets kinda repetative. Effects are for the coin collecting, hit sounds, buying etc. However the 'voices' are just moans of  pleasure  pain in typical anime fashion. I have heard 'baka' and 'hentai' once or twice, so yes, it is intended to be rather sexual.   All-in-all, it's free and I can only complain about the time I've wasted (most of which is just leaving it in the background, forgetting it was open...). Get it out of curioisty if you're bored or want to compete with your friends to see who can get the furthest or something, and get the DLC if you'd like the support the devs - they do have other games in the visual novel style which are pretty good for the genre if you like it. I may edit some of this later if I've gotten anything wrong.

+ Gorgeous pixel art, with probably the best water I've ever seen in a pixel-style game + Fantastic atmosphere, sound design, lighting + Extremely minimalist, simple interface and mechanics. Gold serves as both currency and the player's HP, everything is interacted with by paying gold + A very artistic, zen-like experience, but at the same time an actual game with mechanics and objectives + Updated pretty frequently, Devs are responsive to player feedback  - Becomes very repetitive - Although learning what everything does is part of the experience, some mechanics are too obtuse for their own good, and even searching online won't help sometimes - Eventually your kingdom becomes so large that it takes an entire to day to ride from one side to the other, although   destroyed, rebuilt Greed portals can be used to teleport long distances for only 1 coin.     NOTE: New Lands comes with the original game Kingdom: Classic as well. Classic is more of an endless wave survival roguelike and has a single, large procedurally generated world, whereas NL has multiple persistent islands (afaik) and a clearer sense of progression. They're both worth a try although I think Classic is no longer updated.   TLDR: It's hard not to reccommend this game as the wonderful devs gifted it to owners of the original. It's a unique and artistic game with plenty of content for its price point.

If your looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if your looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.  Ingredients: 1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter 2. 1/2 cup white sugar 3. 1/2 cup brown sugar 4. 1/4 cup water 5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon 6. 1 pinch salt 7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced 8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie   Directions: 1. Preheat oven to 220 degrees C. 2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside. 3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices. 4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal. 5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust. 6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 175 degrees C, and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.  Like, comment and subscribe for more recipes.

Listen to this while reading this:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yVm_ljDSdwA   The most intriguing, thought-provoking, unique universe that exists in a video game. The most interesting and real characters you'll ever come across in a video game. You'll feel as if you know these characters personally. You'll be attached to them and by the third game, it'll hit you where it matters most.  It's amazing what Bioware created and it's the only video game where your decisions will carry over for an entire trilogy. It's quite possible that nothing like this will ever be recreated and that's perfectly okay. This particular game and the two sequels are going to be thought as of something incredibly special and it's a real shame some people won't ever experience it.  Unfortunately, the first game isn't very welcoming with it's endearing looks and gameplay. Despite this, I urge you to just push through because the story itself will get you hooked. The second and third game becomes much more accessible and in my opinion, much more fun. Each game is extremely memorable and has so many incredible moments backed by an amazing soundtrack. From synthesizers to heart drenching piano music, the score will never leave you with a dull moment and it accompanies the characters emotions to near perfection.  I highly recommend this game and the entire trilogy to anyone that wants to experience something truly special.

Wolfenstein: The New Order is a great game to play for an adrenaline rush and nazi killing.  +Awesome fun at times +Great gore +Near death experiences that makes you ♥♥♥♥ your pants +Scary ♥♥♥ metal dogs +Fast-paced gameplay +Moon Nazis +Cool level variation (in terms of location, not design) and outfits for the nazis +Main menu theme that makes you want to kill nazis   -Lack of weapon variety -Linear levels, could be improved with slight variety -Cliché potrayal of nazis (which would be great if the story wasn't an attempt to be inspiring like said below) -Decent graphics, but still runs pretty bad(I have GTX 770) -Terrible story with an attempt to sound inspiring, but in reality is just a massive cliché (not the good one like Just Cause 2, just a nasty inbetween zone like the uncanny valley) -Not enough stealth options -Too much corridor running and not enough sneak -Really, really ♥♥♥♥♥♥ dialouges and cutscenes -Not enough exploration of Germania and mostly just random military bases   8.5/10, awesome game but the story gave me a headache. Like I said I'm cool with cliché stories and stuff like in Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon, but this is just a nasty cliché main character + an attempt to make a serious story about rebellion. Should've just made the story like The A-Team or Blood Dragon in my opinion, now it's just cheesy. Blaskowitz is a really badass guy though.

So, it's kinda funny... 24.6 hours on record. The keywords being ON RECORD. I have literally spent a solid thousand hours playing this game on the Nintendo Gamecube since I was a little kid. I've dumped more of my life into this game than almost any other game, and for good reason -- This game is bliss. This game is what all these subpar modern 3D Sonic games should've strived to be. The action stages are incredible, the chao garden is a lot of fun (and has a really deep meta... No, really, google 'sonic adventure 2 chao garden' and look at a comprehensive guide. Chao Garden is practically its own game in the broad spectrum of things...) the graphics were EXCELLENT for 2002, and it all runs at a silky smooth (but unfortunately locked) frame rate on even my low-end laptop. If you have a weak system, a USB-connected controller, and you DON'T own Sonic Adventure 2, you are legitimately missing out on one of the best games your computer can run.  P.S., BUY THE BATTLE DLC, IT'S ONLY $3 AND ADDS PLENTY OF CONTENT TO THE GAME  P.S.S., Getting 180 emblems isn't hard... But if you're up for a real (but completely do-able) challenge, get 180 A ranks.

Insurgency.  After 40 hours of in-game time, I feel that I am eligible enough to write up some kind of review. I bought the game when it came out. It was my first time with Insurgency (It was a third-party mod, previously, as far as I know) for a relatively cheap price as it had a on-launch 50-60% off.  Now, when I first got in to it, it was already something different to enjoy for a while. There were few weapons, new - interesting, but not a revolutionary system for your weapon upgrades. There are few classes, each with it's own ammo count - currency you use to upgrade your load-out. Every game you start, you choose your load-out and upgrades, armor, grenades etc.  I played like 20 hours of the game, and somehow I got tired of it. At start, it was pretty much a pain to play, becasue I have recorded some serious hours on Counter-Strike games, I am used to quick rush in, shoot everything with pin-point (almost) accuracy. Insurgency is different story. Although it's based on Source engine, shooting is nothing like Counter-Strike. It's more releated to Red Orchestra, Day of Defeat, theese kind's of games - Recoil, supression, aiming down your sights, carefull observation of surroundings, cover to cover gameplay.  Now, if we return to my first 20 hours of this game, I played it, and I enjoyed it, after I realised, that rushing is not going to always work. So, I became professional camper and defender, and let the ones with more experience, take objectives and attacking. I was a passive player, becasue when I tried to activeley go with the ones, who knew the maps, knew how to shoot properly, I always got killed. At that time, there were only few maps, from which there were like 2 I really did not enjoy. I had a blast with it, but I got bored of it.  The last 20 hours I have played this game, I have fell in love with it again. There has been some serious ammount of updates and new additions to the game, including weapons, accesories and yes, the thing it really lacked - new maps. What I have understod, there are a low of game modes. But the one I enjoy the most - skirmish, makes me play the game again and again. I joined today some servers, which were running all the other modes (randomly) and I got tired of the game already. I don't know what it is, but one thing is clear - what this game does great in gameplay, also, ruins the joy of it. I still recommend it, this is one of the games, I don't regret purchasing, especially the price I got it. Wait for discounts and grab it, it's really fun.

I think zombies are boring, stupid, ugly, and bad-tippers. So why would I recommend Dead Pixels? It is a zombie-apocalypse 8-bit grindhouse shootemup with Survival RPG elements. Ugh..Gag me with a rusty spork. I'm surprised that there isn't any platforming, permadeath, and every other thing an indie game is required to have. But you know something? I don't mind it one bit. This is a game where every element works.  Basically the goal is to escape the zombie-infested streets. You're told everything you need to know in the first 30 seconds: Press a button to shoot, another to melee, abandoned buildings have free stuff, and there are shops for all the necessities. After that you're on your own. Surviving is a simple enough task, provided you have the ammo. For the most part however you'll only be killing whatever is in your way. Even then, it's usually better to just move around everything. Zombies are (aside from a few exceptions) as dumb & slow as ever. You stick, you move, you juke a little, and conserve that precious ammo.   But you need money too right? Abandoned buildings are always good for weapons and valuables, but that stuff gets heavy. Also, depending on the difficulty, shopkeepers can become very scarce. This results in more decision-making, because you can't carry everything. To add to the 'fun', once you reach a new street, you can't go back to the previous one. Yeah it's some BS about 'Oh No it's too zombie-infested!' It doesn't really matter though. The undead spawn at a constant rate, so all you should do is move forward.   Along with being all-around terrible people, zombies only get worse as you progress. New varieties start appearing, and they've got all sorts of nasty tricks. The worst are the huge shirtless guys. They move quickly and take so much punishment. Usually I just try and move around so the big ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s get stuck behing the slower zombies. The brain-chomping idiots tend to crowd together. It's nice because it makes them easier to manipulate, but if you're surrounded, then a Game Over is assured. Don't forget about the fireworks and adrenaline you have stowed in your backpack! They're easy to forget about on easy & normal, but the harder settings practically require that you take advantage of every possible resource.   Along with the main game, the 'Solution' and 'Last Stand' modes are nice additions. In the first mode, you select a prison in-mate. In order to earn a pardon, you have to set off an explosive. This is basically an arranged mode. You can order everything direct from the military, but your goods always arrive a couple streets away. There's no use holding onto junk, because there's no way to sell it. Also at the half-way point, you have to head back. So all those buildings you looted, probably won't replenish their stock, when you make your way back. Secondly there's the obligatory wave-based survival mode. Kill zombies, buy stuff, repeat until dead.   The mechanics of zombie killin' and dodgin' are pretty solid. The melee is pretty awful though. Even fully upgraded, it can still take several hits to put down one creature. Maybe if the survivors actually put some effort into their melee attacks, they'd be a little more effective. You can see it in-game. Their strikes are so weak-looking, they might as well be murmuring 'ehh' and 'weh' and 'uh' instead of yelling 'HAH!' 'KIYAA!' and 'RARRRRGHGHGHZZZ'. Otherwise this game gets the fundamentals right, which makes the more intricate aspects (resource-management) all the more rewarding.  Aside from that minor annoyance, this game is really good. The difficulties and modes are nicely balanced, and the weapons are pretty satisfying to use. Plus it makes survival aspects appealing, even to someone like myself. Usually I write a game off as soon as soon as I see the word 'conserve', but I like how it's handled here. For once it's fun to cross several zombie-filled streets and not fire a single bullet. The easier difficulties are fun in their own way, because it's much easier to load up on ammo and obliterate everything in sight. As good as Dead Pixels is, it's making the wait for the sequel absolutely brutal. I expect that Dead Pixels 2: Straight to Video will be the smash hit co-op sensation of 1991, or whenever it actually releases.

I played Baldur's Gate when it originally came out and didn't understand much because I didn't know D&D. Why are my characters dying after one hit? Why don't they hit anything? Why are lower armor class numbers better? And so on. So I put it aside after a brief time, disappointed.  Fast forward 2 years or so. Shortly before Baldur's Gate II was released, I gave it another try. And it clicked! Although to this day I'm not a fan of their (modified) D&D system, mainly because of the limited ways to develop your character, I discovered that there were a lot of other things to like in this game. The huge (for the time) open world to explore, the many interesting items to find, a competent story and challenging - if sometimes a little random - tactical battles.   Of course, I only fell really in love with the series when Baldur's Gate II - and later, Throne of Bhaal - came out but I guess this would be a topic for the Baldur's Gate II review. There are very few RPG series where you can play a character from a nobody to a god over the course of 2,5 games. The sense of power progression in this series is unmatched in my opinion. After initially dislilking Baldur's Gate, I've now played through the game four times and three times through the whole saga (fun fact: My first character was a paladin, maybe that's also a reason why I disliked it at first...).   This brings me to the 'Enhanced Edition' because the fourth playthrough which I finished earlier today was with this remastered version. I'd heard much about the issues with this 'improved' version but fortunately, I seem to have evaded the worst after playing the patched up version now. I had only some minor issues which didn't interrupt my enjoyment with the game at all. It's nice to play in widescreen in a native resolution. The engine is apparently updated to Baldur's Gate 2 level which means nicer graphical effects, a lot of UI improvements and gameplay conveniences. The two new companion characters, which are DLC content, integrate nicely into the game and are more fleshed out, similar to Baldurs Gate II companions.  If you have the time and patience, you can mod the old game to maybe even higher standards than the Enhanced Edition. But if you just want to play without much messing around, I can recommend this version. The Baldur's Gate saga is one of the all time greats and a true classic.

Review for Act 1  While playing video games growing up I've come to realize that the best games always seem to have something very specific in common; something that allows you get wrapped up in and immersed in a different world to escape reality for a short time and get lost in the beauty of it; and that would be that they all have a great story. A story first above all else; above crazy graphics, above gameplay, and even above music, etc. A story that lets you become a part of it.  There are so many games these days that seem to forget that you should always start with that good story first, with fully realized worlds and the characters that inhabit them. And the games that do realize what should come first, usually turn out to be amazing works of art; games that draw you in and never let go, and have a lasting impression. And Broken Age is an excellent example of this. The story is at the heart of the experience and you can feel it.  And even though this game might seem very simplistic in nature (given its gameplay style), it truly is just as satisfying to play as it would be to play a huge, multi-million dollar, RPG production made by a first-party studio by hundreds of people. But I think that's what makes this game so special: that it can be as great as it is even though it was made by just a handful of dedicated people who truly care about what they’re creating and why they’re creating it.  Double Fine has shown that you can have an exciting adventure that whisks you away to worlds full of challenges, laughter, and even emotionally-engaging moments, in the form of a point-and-click adventure that’s very accessible and easy to pick up and play. And the impact you feel at the end of Act 1 is an immensive one, that really gets you thinking. It was surprising to find out how much I cared about what happened to the two main characters and how they impacted the worlds around them.  I would definitely recommend this game to people who enjoy a great story, even if the gameplay style isn’t normally what they might be in to. You just might be surprised with how much you’ll enjoy it.  I absolutely cannot wait until Act II! I cannot wait to go back into Shay's and Vella's worlds to finally get some answers to some burning questions! And I have a feeling it's going to be a very satisfying conclusion regardless of how it ends.  :)  Congrats again to Double Fine for some amazing work so far!

awesome game with fast paced gameplay (id change the parry though, making it require tighter timing) that any fan of the hack and slash genre will love. nothing incredibly innovative in the gameplay department (zandatsu is fun but it doesnt really affect gameplay at all) but it feels tight and very responsive and with enough depth to satisfy fans of the genre (DMC3 beats it by a long shot in this regard (regarding depth i mean), but then again thats DMC3 were talking about :p)  storywise i didnt know what to expect (never played any metal gear games) so at first i thought it was the typical 'hey those other guys are bad and greedy so lets stop em'. and it seemed so at first and i was okay with that, after all, the main thing about this genre is the gameplay not the story. however, i was very pleasantly surprised to find out that it wasnt like that at all. the game touches a lot of sensitive themes that i dont even have any other game to compare it to (maybe specs op the line? didnt play that one yet). from the war on terror being just excuses to get oil and make war (and how obviously it was caused in purpose, like most wars) to stuff like homeless kids in third world countries dieing by the millions without anyone really caring. stuff i certainly wasnt expecting to find here.  the optional conversations (that im sure 95% of players didnt even get to see since you need to go to a separate UI and theyre not required to advance the story or anything) are pretty awesome. i mean obviously the game is extreme science fiction but its not like they just said 'okay bullets dont hurt you anymore, but swords do'. they go in-depth on most things, from the carbon nanotube armor to the high frequency blades. you can really see that the devs tried (and tried real hard) to make you believe in this world and it shows. i mean whoever made this all up certainly knew what he was talking about and wasnt just some random guy browsing wikipedia. the amount of technical data you can also get in those conversations, about even the little things present in the game, is astounding and sent me more than once to wikipedia to learn more  the characters are pretty awesome as well. raiden (the protagonist of the main game) at one point when confronted by sam (one of the main antagonists) about the hundreds of innocent soldiers he killed and how hes using the 'cause' as a way to shield himself from guilt, finally kinda breaks down and realizes that yea, hes not fighting for some ideals, he just loves to kill, and he just needed some excuse to keep the craziness in check. how often do you see the protagonist say something like that?  overall i liked sam DLC gameplay a little better than the main game, and also the unlockables in the main campaign are OP to the point of breaking the game. obviously you can choose not to use them but i felt the game would be better off without them (or at least make them punish your rating)  the game has 5 difficulties, tons of in game tittles (theyre a lot harder than the achievs), challenges outside of the campaigns (called VRs) and it has the classic S-A-B-C-D ratings to show how well youve done (obviously theres a tittle for getting S on all levels, on all difficulties :p). so the replayability? yea, its insane, which is awesome news if you love the gameplay (and theres no way you dont if you love the genre!)

app_name: Deus Ex: Human Revolution - Director's Cut
review_text: This game was a huge disappointment for me. A Deus Ex game should have delivered a great story with nefarious conspiracies, and game objectives that could be completed in multiple ways. In this game the story makes absolutely no sense, the biggest 'conspiracy' is that television lies, you barely discover anything about the organizations and the people behind the events, the whole augmentation debate is discussed on kindergarten-level, and the ending is mind-meltingly stupid. I could write an entire book about how badly written this game is. It's true that multiple playstyles are supported, but there is an optimal one that is heavily favored by the game rules: stay undetected, use only nonlethal melee takedowns (yes, this game is not a shooter), and hack everything. If you follow the optimal style, all weapons, ammo, and weapon mods are useless (take the laser rifle to the last boss though), all health-restoring items are useless, and there is no difference between the difficulty levels either, because every enemy goes down with a single button press. The only difficulty is finding the correct order of taking down the guards without alerting the remaining ones, but thanks to the rtarded patrol routes it's usually quite easy. I could list the flaws in the game mechanics for pages, but it's tl;dr anyway. The boss fights are often criticized for being out-of-place, but the real issue is that the boss characters have no part in the story other than appearing as an obstacle at a certain point. Overall, this game is not completely awful, for example, the dialog challenges are interesting, the hacking minigame is nice, the visual design is impressive, and I liked some of the side-quests. My biggest grief is that for lots of people this is The Deus Ex Game, while in reality both its predecessors were vastly superior in almost every way. Oh, and why does the DC version occupy twice the disk space as the original release?

Anybody who is new to a moba should play this game! Dragons and Titans doesn't feel overwhelming and its has just the right amount of pacing to get into it and have fun. There are no microtransactions for any player to feel frustrated and most of the items such as the runes, rune slots, the weapon packs, and dragon packs, can be purchased through in-game currency. However, the exeptions are that you can't purchase the dragon skins, the avatar riders, also the dragons and weapons individually. You can upgrade your weapons and the dragons you have so you can become stronger, faster and better. Players get to try five weapons and dragons each week as well. There are other ways to get free dragons but purchasing the Titans Pass can save you the hassle by getting all the weapons, all the dragons, the skins, the riders and all of the acts. When you do, the in-game currency gets an extra boost the more you play. Not to mention you recieve the latest weapon or dragon now and then. The Titans Pass only lasts for a year, but don't worry you still get to keep everything else except the currency boost. The only thing I can complain about are the Acts. If you do it for the story and free dragon they're okay but it does get tedious the more you play. I wish the makers of Dragons and Titans would be a little more creative on the gameplay for the acts. But everything else is very much worth it.

IMO this game is the BEST(!!!) RTS game ever released, and beeing a old school RTS gamer my favourites were the earliest of the C&C series, and the later ones such as Generals and Tiberium Wars, and SC1 was allways my childhood.   The guys with the bad and negative revies, you should all take a look here:  http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=514651893   This game is much more complicated then your average RTS. Cons; Quite a short game, I finished it in a couple of hours. The body horror and spooky settings were all but gone after the second chapter, making it less interesting. This game has had a bad history, but it is great now.  I have played this game since March 30, and I have enjoyed it all the way (It is only 190 hours because I have played this game on another account as well). Yeah, it was a bit tough at first I will admit, especially with all the bugs, hackers, etc., but ever since 1.4, things have gotten a lot better. I am very content on the fact that there are almost as many people in servers as there were in launch time. This game is similar to Destiny in it's looting sense. It has the dark zone, which is like the PVP area of the game, and that is a great way to find loot, and also a way to lose it... The DLC are nice, especially Survival so far. You have to survive in the snow of New York in the Survival DLC and try to find anti-virals. You must get through the snowstorm before it gets through you... Underground DLC is nice for looting as you get a lot of loot in doing them. The story however, is okay. The characters were a little blunt, and I hope that the developers improve the story later on in updates as the story has a lot of potential to it and it can be improved in that sense. The game has a lot of lore, such as random phone calls in different parts of New York showing what happened to people before the disease broke out and during it. Another cool thing is ECHO's, which where the Division agent can see what happened in the past in that certain area where an ECHO is available. These are pretty cool. The Division is a bit dark (emotionally), especially in one mission where criminals were brutally killing JTF (The most useless allies that you will ever get in a game XD). Just download the game itself and if you really enjoy it, then get the season pass as well, it is well worth it, as there is one more DLC left for year 1 and who knows what will be in year 2. The game is around 10 dollars cheaper than what it was at release time so I reccomend that you get it right now. The developers have worked really hard in improving this game and I really like that. Overall I think this is a great game, and unlike reviews months ago, I highly reccomend this. This has fast become one of my all-time favorite games. At first glance, this gem looks like a love letter to Earthbound; those aspects do exist, and the experience is richer for it, yet it is so much more.  Toby's proficiency in audio truly shines in this game, from the opening hum on the title screen drawing you into adventure, to the dramatic flair that plays during battles with glamorous bosses. The graphics tie so beautifully into the feel, giving that SNES experience of polished sprites with enough room for your imagination to let this world blossom in your imagination.  All of this, however, serves the singular purpose of telling a poignant story. I cannot stress this point enough. The script is truly phenomonal, and every character (no matter how minor) has a personality all their own sprinkled into it all. This is to say nothing of the journey you'll embark on through your playthrough, which manages to leave such an impact that I still stay up at night thinking about it.  This is a game that encourages you to grow during its intial stay of 4~6 hours, and that alone has made it the best $10 I've spent on a game in over five years. Yet this developer has made a world so beautiful, so powerful, I still can't bring myself to say goodbye to it all even after two completions.

While I recommend this game overall, unfortunately it is a huge step back from The Lost Crown and the previous two Dark Fall titles in a number of ways. While it is most similar to the first Dark Fall: The Journal in both tone and setting, with of course being a graphical leap over the original, it lacks the series and its spin off's(The Lost Crown) rich lore, notably in its lack of virtually any supporting flavor text which added so much to the series aside from scattered newspaper clippings. The other titles all had a novel's worth of extra materials that added to the stories. What's left is a point and click stumble through a rehash of the Dowerton Train Station and Hotel that we already explored long ago. And with the cumbersome method of panning and up and down, a very tedious stumble at that. Several times now I've had to check a guide on where I went wrong only to find I didn't pan up at one overly specific spot. In addition the Penumbra/Amnesia style mouse interaction with some puzzles is clunky to say the least, and not even casually hinted at. It can be quite difficult to intuit where exactly you are supposed to move the stupid mouse to get a reaction, or you find yourself clicking things to no avail only to find you were supposed to drag them to the side(spark plugs...) despite it not really making sense to do so.  Putting aside these design and technical issues, the game still stands as a great little horror adventure title to play through. It is atmospheric and has a few nice jump scare scenes and a continuation of the Dark Fall storyline is always a good thing.

This review is a little lengthy, so if you just want to know why I recommend this game skip the  Results  and read from there.   Valve produces one of the most rigorous zombie games ever created. This fantastic first person shooter offers co-operative play which puts you right in the apocalypse that really keeps you on the run.    Story:  Left 4 Dead 2 places you as a character in a movie: Louis, Zoey, Francis, Bill, Coach, Ellis, Nick or Rochelle. Each level is suppose to be a part of a movie which combines into a single story. There are two stories which involve the first four characters or the latter. Eventually the characters from each movie integrate and intermingle joining the two movies together as one. There is a clear start and finish to the story, but as you play the maps online the story gets conveluded and lost. But if you play the campaign in order you will notice connections between all the maps that drive the characters to a set objective. Left 4 Dead 2 is a movie and as such the characters are unrealistically attributed with super human strength, unrealistic accuracy, and cheesy lines all of which are similar to most action movie stars.   Campaign:  Left 4 Dead 2 drops you right into a zombie apocalypse. The campaign consists of both Left 4 Dead 2 and remastered Left 4 Dead levels. The included campaigns are: No Mercy, Crash Course, Death Toll, Dead Air, Blood Harvest, The Sacrifice, Dead Center, The Passing, Dark Carnival, Swamp Fever, Hard Rain, The Parish, and Cold Stream. This can be expanded upon with hundreds of maps created using the editor which sometimes are better constructed than the originals. The brilliance behind this game is that due to a basic AI instructor the zombie placement changes every time you play a level which produces high replay value.   Modding:  Not only can you replay the included levels a plethora of times without being bored, but the workshop allows you to expand upon that and download new maps and mods that make the game better with every adjustment. You can even go to certain websites and download maps created by the community there. I personally prefer to download my maps from community websites as they are easier to install from the website. If you like fast paced zombie games and have some friends to play with this is the game for you.    Gameplay:  Left 4 Dead 2 is a fast paced Zombie first person shooter that involves running between areas to safe houses. Each level has chapters which you must run through, these chapters can either be incredibly long or ridiculously short. You have the choice to take it slow and work your way through the levels in time, or go as fast as you can. Typically teams that go slow do worst because as time progresses they must face more enemies than quicker teams. Hordes of fast moving zombies spawn in ways that can seem endless. Like most zombie games a headshot is much more effective than a chest shot, but enemies can still be taken down either way.    Multiplayer:  One of the epic features about this series is the online multiplayer. In this mode you are pitted as teams undead or survivors. The undead spawn as special zombies and must collaborate to kill off the survivors. Survivors on the other hand must complete objectives or finish chapters without all being perished. The multiplayer is brilliant, although it can be a little less fun if you don’t have someone to play with as this is a heavily team based game.    Results:  If you love zombie games, you like co-op experiences, you understand how to install mods, you just want to be an action hero, or even you just want to play by yourself Left 4 Dead 2 is the game for you. The amazing AI director makes the same map different every time. The bots although not the smartest can make a single player experience fun, and with some mods you can easily switch between players and control the bots if you accidently die. The game is an amazing action thriller that keeps you on your toes and constantly wanting more.   Pros: +Four player co-op campaigns that last at most one hour each +Good graphics and smooth animations +Fantastic zombie feel without seeming too mind-numbing (making zombies intelligent and tactful) +Fun campaigns that blow away realism and just create action packed fun. +Workshop compatible allowing for large amounts of mods and customization +Basic AI so you can play with a single friend without having to join a game online  Cons: -Mods are not perfect and can cause the game to crash (so remember what you installed before each play-through in order to remove defective files) -Bots are essentially mentally handicapped even with Bot Mods -Bots will not use the defibrillator unit -When playing single player the human player cannot switch between bots in order to continue enjoying the game despite their own demise -Some weapons seem excessive and worthless (two different types of pump shotguns) -Poor servers in comparison to the Xbox 360 (I miss mod free servers)

A nice little puzzle game with a steep learning curve.   Pros: I personally liked the art style, which I think worked well with puzzles that remind me of optical illusions. The rewind mechanic is one of the more forgiving aspects of this game, similar to the Prince of Persia's dagger, but without a time limit. The puzzles were engaging enough that I remained invested in playing through to the finish.  Cons: Puzzles range from really simple to extremely difficult. I didn't mind using video walkthroughs for some of them, but while some puzzles were easy enough once you figured out the trick, others I wouldn't have figured out without the walkthrough (i.e. using momentum).  Bottom line: I would recommend playing through it once, but for me it doesn't hold a lot of replay value.

If you like taking part in large space battles as a one man fighter (or bomber or 'strike suit') this game is competent at delivering that experience. You get to do strafing runs on Cruisers or Frigates, and taking out their turrets or weak points is very satisfying. Using the strike suit is a blast once you get the hang of it. At rapid pace you can lock and then launch about 40 missiles at varying targets, either severely crippling a smaller capital ship or thinning out a fighter formation.  The story is somewhat interesting, but mostly only serves to tie together the levels. Checkpoints are plentiful towards the beginning of the game but become non-existant at the end of the game when you need them most to save yourself from having to redo sections that are cakewalks leading up to some very challenging sections.  Unfortunately for me personally the frustrating checkpoint system really hampered my enjoyment during key moments of the game. For instance in mission 13 of the standard campaign a character essentially just monologues a bunch of story explanation at you while you navigate some incredibly tight tunnels for about 7 minutes before reaching your destination. There are two major problems with this. First of all the game up until this point does not in any way prepare you for precision flying, with every other level taking place in a wide open space battle. Secondly there are absolutely no checkpoints on this mission and this means when you inevitably die on this level, you must hear the explanation monlogue again... and again... and keep redoing the same simple manuevers.  At it's best the game is a well put together space combat game. At it's worst it is an exercise in frustration. Overall I would say that it is slightly above average, and recommend it only to fans of the genre.

No More Room In Hell is a game that is more like Gmod; You play 10-30 minute games where you try to survive as long as you can from the zombies in a map, while completing objectives. If done correctly, someting comes to pick you up- However, if all players are killed, its game-over.  Starting with the graphics; Absolutely outstanding for a free to play game. Especially a ZOMBIE one. Next, i haven't seen any other good zombie game that is free to play. This game isn't at all P2W heavy, like Romero's Aftermath. The only thing that I don't like as a feature, is after you die. After you die, you sit there for 5-20 minutes spectating other players. They should add a mechanic to include dead players, like adding in player zombies. Other than this problem, this game is a 10/10 for me! HIGHLY suggest playing this. If you don't, your missing out on a great F2P Zombie Game.

It took me a bit to warm up to this game, given the fairly low production values and the controls. Once I got used to it though, and past the intro, I became compelled to complete this odd game. I'm not into violence and don't watch horror films, so, I guess maybe I found some of the graphic things described in the game more disturbing than people who actually enjoy the horror genre would. (Which is to say, I enjoyed it, but horror-buffs might find it all a bit tame for their liking, maybe?)   I found the ending (or at least the one I achieved) really cheesy. Also, there's one part that that I found waaaay too predictable, which completely ruined 'the feels' I was probably meant to have.   But don't get me wrong! This game had some absolutely fantastic moments which made it all very much worth it. It's the kind of thing I would buy again, and play again, if it were re-released on a bigger budget. It's not the art or music - it's the controls and gameplay itself that I'd like to see enhanced.  Overall, I'd recommend this game, even though it doesn't have many 'game' elements. It's a peaceful, interesting time killer, that I reckon will take a good 5 - 8 hours to get through.

Please note that I have played the game more than 7 Minutes, it's just that I began playing on 10/13, so the time played then did not register, neither any of the achievements I was supposed to obtain.  What I like about the games, is that it is the one true horror game we've been waiting for. The game gives the feeling that Resident Evil 4 Gave when it first came out, dark, twisted areas where you find yourself engulfed in the atmosphere the game presents, the evidence that everyone is under the control of a being of higher power, everything that Resident Evil 4 Had, is in this game. The gameplay is responsive, just the camera that gets in the way most of the times, but otherwise everything is perfectly fine where it is, I find myself most of the times sneaking around, waiting to pick off the enemies due to the scarce amount of ammo that you're presented, and other times I just go in guns blazing when there are lesser enemies about.  Some people complain that the graphics are outdated, and issues with the FPS, but what do we play games for? For pretty graphics? For frame rate that doesn't change gameplay much? I play games for the fun of it, and what they were created for, this game delivers what it was made for, the dark and blood filled areas to create a heavy atmosphere, and in the beginning of the game I had my heart running as I had to sneak around in the hospital. Games should be viewed as what they were intended to be, not some pretty game that puts a good front to hide all the crap behind it.   I recommend buying that game if you want the feeling of a classic horror game that involves survival, and a chance to fight back.

app_name: Navy Field 2 : Conqueror of the Ocean
review_text: Well, simply I just love warship games. I would like to play World Of Warships, but I dont have invitation code ;( . Kantai Collection is unreachable for me too. Only Battlestations left (but it's not on multi anyway), so for me only avaiable MMO warship game is Navy Field. I've played NF 1 long time ago, so now it's time to test number '2'. After first hours of gameplay I must admit that second NF is somehow odd for me. I mean game looks better in matter of animations and 3D view, unfortunately ships details are really badly drawed. Very poor compared to NF 1. Another thing is that game is more simplified than NF 1, by saying this I mean, there are much less modifications and items for ships unlike in NF 1, where was plenty of it. Well... automatic aiming is somehow better in version 2. Generally game is average, but for warship fan like me it looks quite more interesting. Anyway, I recommend for try. It's kinda funny.

Awsome realism tactical team gameplay. If you are sick of COD style shooters. You will like this.

Liked what I got but, true to human nature, I want more.  The only other gripe I have is the rampant grammatical/spelling errors.  YE GODS AJTILLEY, HAVE YOU NEVER HERAD OF SPELL CHECKER? HIRE A BLOODY PROOFREADER!  Hell, you can even PM me and I'll do it for you  FOR FREE   All in all I am glad I took the chance on this game and I'm looking forward to the release of the next two parts.

TL;DR Don't be afraid of the Mixed Reviews. This game is great, but I highly recommend getting the base game and Horizons especially when they're on sale. Things can get boring though, so play with a friend!   KEEP IN MIND: This game is not perfect and it's still in development and there are things being added, tweaked, and or fixed almost constantly. Also, playing this game with a stick and throttle will make your playtime much more enjoyable.  Pros:  1) I put in well over 200 hours into this game before the whole Steam page fix (back when the Horizon's pack was sold sepearately as it's own sepearate thing).  2) From smuggling, exploring, to collecting bounties, you name it and it's probably in this game (or at least added later on).  3) This game, honestly, is way more fun when playing with friends or with other players in general. I mean, I got to go canyon racing with a community of players. What more can you want?! 4) The Faction system is extremely interesting and has the potential to change the way you play the game (A buddy of mine almost got shot to shreds just by jumping into the wrong system controlled by an enemy faction) 5) This game is absolutely gorgeous especially when you're playing it with some sort of VR system  6) Gameplay is solid and pretty easy to get into and not overly complicated, especially if you have a stick and throttle.  Cons:  1) This game can get frustrating at times especially with the new AI and other players. It's part of the experience, but just like DayZ, you will encounter players who will stop at nothing until you're dead just for their own pleasure.  2) Not only that, space as you know is big. Really... Really... BIG! So at times, especially if you're a trader or explorer, this game can get dreadfully boring. This is known as Space Trucker Simulator for a reason.  3) The   biggest   con of this game is the fact that Horizons is sold as a separate season pass instead of being part of the actual game. I don't care what you have to say about it, but the fact that I have to pay an extra $30 to land and explore moons or some planetary bodies is utter bullsh*t. Fortunate for me though, I bought Horizons by itself last year, so I don't feel completely ripped off, but this reason alone is a huge reason why Elite Dangerous has Mixed Scores on this steam page. 4) There are still plenty of bugs in this game. Fortunately, this game has (in my experience at least) great support so any glitches, bugs, or issues you encounter will probably be fixed relatively soon.   Conclusion:  Bottom line, despite the problems, this game is a lot of fun and I highly recommend it whenever it goes on sale.

Undertale brought back something I hadn't seen for a long time, a good pixelated game. Most popular games you see today aim to have the best graphics, but Undertale doesn't need good graphics to be good. The different areas around the game all have their fun things. Hotland has a lot of funny minor characters, and other places like Waterfall have funny characters like Temmie. I like walking into Snowdin and talking to all the interesting people there. And I really like the different bosses of the game, from Greater Dog to Flowey to Undyne, I love the battles and the different ways in which you can end them. I especially love the main characters, like Sans and Papyrus, Undyne and Alphys, and Toriel and Asgore. They were all very interesting to meet and befriend. I like one great thing it provides, which is the different pathways. You can do whatever you want. You can kill, or spare, it's all up to you.   I feel that it teaches players a valuable lesson, which is for everyone to avoid conflict and to keep peace, to not encourage battle but instead try to end it without anyone getting hurt. It also talks to not only the character, but you. It breaks the 4th wall and refers to you, the player, when you thought it was just talking to your character. Even the music is good. The battle themes really fit for the battle and the personality of the character you are fighting. There were so many aspects of the game that were great.  If you play it, you might be like me, and go back for more. You might like the game enough to play it to the end and replay it, doing it different next time. If you want a unique gameplay, get Undertale and get playing.

Counter Strike Condition Zero is the sequel to the original Counter Strike and now it includes a Single Player Campaign, so let's take a look shall we ?  When I mentioned that this game has a single player campaign, I didn't mean a story mode. The single player campaign is a very short bundle of missions where you have few objectives during each missions. Like rescue hostages, defuse bombs and take out terrorists with a specific weapon.  The missions are incredibly short and you can finish all 12 within 2 hours.   The AI will make the hostage missions a living hell. Once the round starts the AI are on the hunt to find any enemy player on the field and will not help you so don't expect any help from the AI. Getting a hostage to a drop off, before your team kills the entire enemy team.  The gameplay is not much different from the original Counter Strike so don't expect a major change.  Now for the feature everyone cares. THE MULTIPLAYER. It's almost completely dead. I had trouble finding a match and when I did get into a full lobby match, I felt bored and it's not as good as the original Counter Strike.  This game is not good, but it's not horrible either. The campaign is short and not worth the money and the multiplayer is slowly dying out. In the end only get this if you're a hardcore fan, otherwise get any other game in the franchise.  Final Rating: 6/10 - Okay   If you liked this review please consider giving it a thumbs up and if you dislike post in the comments on what you dislike about the review.  For more reviews follow Snort's Review curator page  here  - follow for regular updates on reviews for other games!

Military Simulations  4/16/2013 Arma 3 is till in ALPHA stage not Beta but this isnt the arcade style that most of you kids play by those major company names like EA (Battlefield series is actually made by DICE in Europe) and Infinity Ward (Call of Duty).  This is suppose to be a SIMULATION in a way not just walking around and running on the ground but flying crafts and driving vehicles trying to give you that real enviornment feeling that comes down to a simulation game not arcade of Counter Strike fps type.

Arma 3 is an incredible game that truly lives up to the Arma name. It has a refined inventory UI, which is much more user friendly than that of the previous games. The setting is truly remarkable, with even the smallest map (Stratis) being pretty large. The new weapons are great, and can now be customized with any optics or other attachments that you may loot off the weapon of a dead enemy, or find laying around at base. Ragdoll physics have been implemented, and the look spectacular, as well as the new vehicle mechanics. Improved AI makes the enmy more fun yet more deadly, both friendly and enemy. The campaign is much better than that of previous Arma titles (my own personal opinion). It is longer and more in depth, and has several unique twists. Also, user made scenarios can be downloaded through the Steam workshop. I don't play with any mods, so I really won't pretend to.  Even if you have never played Arma a day in your life, i would recommend this game for the lengthy campaign, amazing graphics and gameplay, downloadable user scenarios, and spectacular multiplayer, not to mention mods. All of this adds up to countless hours of late night play in a game you just can't put down.

Plenty of vehicles/aircraft to get the job done and offer a challenge, even though the flight models are still somewhat arcade'ish/linear. The scale of the maps is just crazy, although everything kinda looks the same, it's all laid out very believably. There's a few game modes for MP that are worth giving a try, mainly king of the hill/wasteland and I guess altis life but I havent tried it more than 10 minutes. One thing though, in MP games there's not much you can do FPS wise when there's >60 players, you will get <25 FPS in cities, it doesn't even matter how good your computer is. The SP campaign is good but the controls for AI still need a lot of work and more often than not they just die without following orders. The developers are constantly updating this game everyday with frequent patches so with that I'd give a rating of 8/10 at $60

A great beginning to the game series that brought social normalcy to evesdropping on people. The game itself has a very interesting story which leaves you wanting to know more as you complete each chapter.   This does however trade off with the gameplay, which at times is monotonous and frustrating. The game does do a fantastic job of pumping up the adrenaline when you're being chased by guards by tuning in some intense music to your parkour endevors. But, this is sometimes hindered due to the guard's seemingly omnipresence in the cities, making escapes often quite difficult. The side missions feel like a copious set of chores in preparation to the quality main missions, but the story is worth it. Regardless of it's flaws, the game is a quality time to enjoy, and I reccommend it to anyone interested in the series.

This is a fantastic game if you like the Warhammer 40k universe. For me Dawn of war 2 was a first hated then loved departure from the first installment of the series. I was used to having a massive army and a base to defend or fall back to. DOW 2 allows for a very small and controllable force to go in and strategically defeat a much larger force. It is kind of like having a Special Forces squad instead of a standing army. The developers seemed to have re-imagined DOW 2 to be a much more personal experience. You now care if your devastator squad is getting overrun and try to defend them, unlike in the previous installment. If you like real time strategy but hated the resource gathering and loved Company of Heroes game play mechanics. Then this game was almost tailor made for you.

This almost seems like a lost Treasure game, something in the vein of Gunstar Heroes, Guardian Heroes, Silhouette Mirage, Alien Soldier etc. Huge and creative bosses, really fast action, neat extras, beautiful pixel art... This game has everything. I can't believe it was made by just one guy (Konjak) - it's kind of awesome to see an indie developer that is really trying to make awesome games (and that has a deep respect for classic game design). It just saddens me that hacks like Jonathan Blow and Phil Fish make each only one game (the deservingly nice Braid and the holy-s***-what-a-stupid-and-ugly-game Fez) and get treated like a messiah, while Konjak consistently makes creative and beautiful works and still don't get any awards, funding or media attention from the so-called indie scene.  I want my games awesome, and Konjak makes them so. Buy this if you like creativity and challenge in your games too.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: A very reluctant reccommendation. The game itself is awesome - challenging, fun and strangely beautiful. I really love the interconnected, vertical world design, feels almost like a metroidvania. The bosses are great too - the giant wolf with a giant sword was my favourite (its backstory was pretty depressing though). Sure, there are problems here and there - some clipping, a few annoying levels (a sewer, a swamp and THE ♥♥♥♥ING SLIPPERY ICE LEVEL WITH ♥♥♥♥ING INVISIBLE FLOOR AND BOTOMLESS PITS) - but they don't make the game bad.  What is bad though is the port. It's seriously one of the worst PC ports ever with very limited resolution choice, attrocious kb+m controls and a general 'we just don't give a ♥♥♥♥' vibe. I mean, the tutorial is even hardcoded to give you prompts for xbox controller no matter what you're actually using. DSFix is a must, but if you need mods to even play a game then something is seriously wrong.

Missing out on the alpha and beta stages over the past three years has been one of my biggest regrets in gaming. Late to the party, but still the life of it. I've stopped my four main games to play this almost full-time, which I think speaks for itself.  More than a dozen battleframes let you change class at almost any time, each with its independent level to allow you to follow your newer friends up with a lower frame while grinding a higher frame at your own time. A single shared inventory facilitates item transfer between battleframes e.g. for secondary weapons which are usable by all frames.  Incentive to max out a battleframe duly given with class-exclusive and class-unlocked perks. What happens when you max them all out? I don't know, but I'll race you there.  And it's legitimately free-to-play. Microtransactions are cosmetic or to cut travel time; paid currency itself can be traded up for very easily.  Definitely give it a shot. Even if it's not for you, I guarantee you'll have fun.

I wrote a review for this , that I then deleted because I thought perhaps I was being too harsh.   Nope I was right the first time.  True, this game does have some highlights, and it has some endearing qualities, no doubt about it, so it's not all bad.  I think the problem is that Stardock simply do not know how to program proper AI into their games. The ai is simply given stacked number bonuses of varying sorts, so that it becomes about beating numbers, rather than any kind of creative/clever/mindful strategy.   As such I cannot recommend this game.

At the time of writing, I have 41 Hours put into this, and still got about a third of the game left to complete. Loving every minute of it.  My belated game of the year for 2015. Gameplay's simple but extremely satisfying, combined with amazing post-apocalyptic atmosphere. HIGHLY RECOMMEND IT!  One of the only games I've ever played that makes me want to 100% it. Despite the fact that this game has the 'Ubisoftian' style of open world design, it's not like Assassin's Creed or Batman, where the world is filled with absolutely mind-numbingly boring activities. You drive around and visit decrepit locations, loot and collect scraps while sightseeing, beat up people with a satisfying Batman-like combat, maintain and upgrade your vehicle, run errands for Warlords and learn more and more about both Max and how the world turned into a wasteland. Everything is fluid, you go from one simple and satisfying activity to another. Not like AC or Batman where half the activities are clunky, time consuming and boring. Need scrap to upgrade car, drive around, find a camp, beat people up, reduce enemy presence in the region, take their scrap, upgrade car, need more scrap to upgrade further, rinse, repeat. Perfect and simple.  One single unsatisfying thing exists in the game, which is disarming minefields. Gigantic speed-bump. Very annoying. But not at all mandatory, it just triggers my OCD if I leave them undone.  Nobody hates the Ubisoftian design more than me. Seriously, I didn't even like Assassin's Creed 4. But Mad Max shown me that it can be done well. And I have to re-state how amazingly well done the atmosphere is. I will keep the studio behind this game on my radar, because it takes true talent to take the idea of a 'desert' and flesh it out into such a complete, detailed world. It's actually way better than the movie (Fury Road) in that aspect. Fury Road was just a desert. In this game, you go through dried out seabeds, an uncovered volcano, abandoned highways, decrepit buildings, an airport buried in sand... It's beautiful.  There's a host of cons, which would absolutely destroy a lesser game, but the fact that I don't care about them here is a testament to how good this game's core gameplay is.   Pros: -A simplistic, Ubisoftian game design actually being executed well for once. -Near-perfect PC port. The only thing I can nitpick is that Anti-Aliasing is just a simple 'Yes' or 'No', despite there being many other highly customizable graphical settings. -Long, if you want to do everything. -Photo Mode allows you to pause the game and take pictures while noclipping. There's also a selection of post-processing you can apply before taking the picture. -Highly detailed atmosphere and game world. -Good voice acting. -Very satisfying action. Vehicular combat is powerful and explosive. Melee combat feel powerful and requires you to pay just enough attention to make it interesting. It plays just like Batman, but the amount of handholding is taken down by a strong notch, making you have to pay attention to your positioning and timing. The sound design also makes your hits feel much more satisfying than in the Batman games. -Style. Not sure what else to call it. There's a bunch of little things that make me feel good. Like the way Max behaves while interacting with the environment, being able to use the Harpoon for more things than you'd expect, the chest-mounted flashlight actually having it's uses, etc. etc.  Cons: -The cars cannot be flipped. I can think of several good reasons why they made this decision, that go beyond the simple idea of 'handholding', but overall it really takes away from the game. There's no risk to making bad jumps or driving through uneven terrain. In the end they should have found a way to make it work. It looks goofy, too. -There's no enemy variety, which holds a really big threat to the game's replay value, but not a fatal issue. Not to me, anyways. Even the bosses are all the same. -The game is overall on the easy side, so long as you pay attention. -The gotdamned minefields are so stupid, I swear to god. Play the game to see what I mean. It can't be put into words. A huge break in the action. -Max has a shotgun and there's enemy Snipers littered all over the world, yet gunplay is not actually a part of the game. Nobody else carries any fire arms. It would have been hard to implement firefights into the game, but I hope they consider it, if there's a sequel.  -The soundscaping could have used some more work. The NPC that rides along with you constantly calls out events that aren't actually happening. 'THERE IT IS! THE MIGHTY DUSTER!' he tells us, warning us of a mini-tornado that is nowhere to be seen. 'YONDER! A WAR PARTY!' but not a single enemy unit anywhere. -The races are really dumb. Might have been an unfinished aspect of the gameplay that they didn't have time to flesh out. Or maybe it's an unwritten law of the Ubisoftian game design that your open world game needs some side-activities that nobody in their right mind would ever consider entertaining. Still, they're somewhat engaging at least and fast paced enough to not be a huge negative. Not to mention optional.

Lots of replay value, many ways to play with each of the four playable characters. It may seem difficult at first, but like the games of old you get better at it. It feels rewarding going back through and making light work of bosses because of your skill. The story is decent if you decide to look through it, but it's not necessary. The only gripe I had is the lack of a world map, until I learned what zone connect where. All in all, if you're into scrollers and like a challenge play this game.
