So I bought this as soon as it came out of early access and so far it has been great. Very cool cyberpunk atmosphere, an open world, good selection of abilities and equipment, and lots of other things that all add up to one excellent game.  I particularly like how every mission can be approached in multiple ways. For example say you have a mission to go steal a prototype piece of equipment, well you can just run in guns blazing and hope you can fight your way through waves of guards and steal it. If that isn't your style you could pay an informant that might lead you to an employee who will, with the right incentive, unlock a back way for you to fight through more easily. Heck, maybe you like stealth and would prefer not to fight anyone, well you can do that too.  I do however have a couple things that I am not the hugest fan of but in reality are relatively minor. My biggest gripe is that the game does not make it very clear as to how far or what the guards can see, and sometimes that can make stealth a bit of a pain. Also I find that often when entering and exiting stealth your selected unit will get confused if you try to give it a command immediately afterwards, which when you are short on time can lead to being spotted out by a guard.  All in all though, Satellite Reign is a unique experience and worth checking out. If you are on the fence and looking through reviews to see if you should buy this game, all I can say is that I have been having a blast so far and that it was money well spent.  I give it a 9/10.

Also the environment has to be taken into account for your strategy. Maps are farmable and can be played several times if you want to achieve the best rating. Not writing a lot of reviews but I know how those are important to indie devs.  This game got an unfair amount of bad reviews in my opinion.  It does one thing really bad, it fails to introduce progressively the player to its concepts. Thus at first when you start to play you get your ♥♥♥ handed to you and fail to understand why. So at first you're really faced by an intimidating learning curve.  However when you're passed this and bear with getting your butt kicked, it is a great game. I'm into my 4th game and I think I may be able to finish it (currently halfway). The choice of having 2 actions per turn is a bit weird but you get used to it.  The presentation of the game... it's just perfect. I really dig the art, makes me think of Geof Darrow... which is a good thing. Music is alright, sounds are good.   So if you're ready to get passed that initial kick in the groin delivered by this game, enjoy roguelikes and turn based tactical games, you will enjoy it.

Devil’s Bluff is a light hearted murder mystery game. It's a good party game with friends with built-in voice chat. The core aspect of the game to find the shortest path to victory by completing your scavenger hunt list first, capturing the Devil, or be the last man standing.  The setting takes place in an elaborate mansion with a top-down camera view, filled with hidden passages and booby traps. Players are given a clue in a form of a riddle, specifying the general location and item they are looking for.  The game requires a minimum of 6 players, with a maximum of 12 players, divided into 6 teams of two. With an additional 2 slots for spectators. One of the 12 players will be chosen by random to be the Devil, who has the capabilities of murdering all the other players. This means the game is on a spectrum between 6 teams playing against each other to 11 players hunting for 1 Devil. This fluctuation in team work is dynamic, as players will discover which path is the quickest to their victory.  When a player loses all their HP they are incapacitated, unless if you’re the Devil. If they are not revived by a fellow player within time, they will be KO’d for the remainder of the match. Additionally when a survivor dies, you can steal what items they have found to add to your scavenging progression. So, it might be to your advantage to let an opposing team die.  There are two objective for the survivors. -Be the first team to find all the items on your scavenger list -Capture the Devil  The Devil has an extra optional objective, -Kill all the survivors.  The voice chat in the game is considered as essential gameplay aspect as it adds to the suspense of deception and cooperation between players. However, do consider that there is no push-to-talk option in the game. So, if you do not want a live mic open all the time, you’ll have to manage it outside of the game.  The game also supports Twitch Chat which adds extra features in the game. Chat has abilities such as teleporting players together, or highlighting on the map where the Devil is. These features are exclusive to Twitch Chat as they poll the audience for the results.  The longevity of the game relies on the map and the clues. As players encounters the same clues, they will get significantly better at finding the items they need. A high level match can last between 5 to 8 minutes, while a match with new players can last between 10 to 20 minutes.  If you're looking for a party game with a very small learning curve, this is a good pick. Minimal mechanics, and enough variety to last a few hours.

Sickness  is a disturbing game; it’s dark, demented, bloody and can even be offensive at times. I loved every moment of playing this title.  This game is not for the faint of heart - if violence is not your thing then turn back now. While things don’t ever get too graphic or gory it’s still enough to turn some off. There is strong language as well as some creepy plot elements, but if you’re into visual novels of a different breed then please read on.  Our main character is Suoh, who has just killed his boss by mistake during an argument. Panicked, he reaches out to one of his co-workers who is involved in some shady business. This dangerous young gentleman, Markus, saves Suoh’s bacon but wants something in return, and he introduces Suoh to Kurasu, a mob boss. What unfolds next is our protagonist's descent into a world of  money, lies, violence, and ultimately murder . One fatal mistake sends Suoh’s life spiraling out of control.  The story continues as Suoh advances in Karasu’s criminal organization that Markus has dragged him into. Suoh has battles with himself, his actions and whatever set of morals he has left. Midst all the chaos,  Suoh has two shining beacons of hope , his twin sister Sara and a girl he meets in his new line of work named Misa. There are two endings in the game, one for Sara and one for Misa. As our “hero” progresses down either path, he is plagued by doubt that he can lead such a violent lifestyle and still have happiness.  Along with a gripping story, the game has mood setting music and well made artwork. The art has a few up and down moments in terms of quality but overall the game is visually appealing.  The CG scenes are what stand out the most , guiding readers along. The game does suffer from a few typos that should have been caught in post-production but they are few and far between and not enough to ruin the game.  When I started getting deeper into  Sickness  I personally found it to be a lot like the TV show  Dexter , a story about a man who is a serial killer trying to have a normal life and battle the cruel urge he names his “Dark Passenger.” Here, Souh names his “Sickness” and his mental state even brings him physical pain and delusions.  I loved the story, I loved how different it was from conventional slice-of-life visual novels  or overdone love stories, I was at the edge of my seat during each chapter wanting to know what happens next. Despite the dark nature of this title, I would wholeheartedly recommend  Sickness  to anyone that can handle it. After having played Contrast and wanting more I found LIMBO in the recommended game list. Other than them both being puzzle platformers the games aren't really comparable. I put LIMBO on my wishlist and waited for it to go on sale... it finally did.  I thoroughly enjoyed this game even through a couple of frustrating parts. The puzzles are not exceptionally difficult but did require some thought (which I really liked). Sometimes when you are having difficulty finding an answer you have to step back and look at the problem differently. Find out if you overlooked something. Question why a game that continuously leads you to the right would allow you to go back to the left.  You will die... alot! But you will also feel a sense of satisfaction when you overcome an obstacle.  There was only one 'puzzle' that frustrated me beyond belief. It was late, I was tired, and I looked up a video to see what I was missing. When I saw the video I felt ridiculously silly for not having seen onw of the most obvious answers.  There is not much I can say about the graphics and sound that has already been said in numerous other positive reviews.  Gameplay is very simple. With the keyboard it only takes 5 keys, the Arrow Kays to move and jump and the CTRL Key to pull objects, move levers or activate/deactivate switches. I didn't try using a controller as I am a more proficient keyboard jockey.  If you like platformers that require a little bit of thought and not so much of the rapidly quick precisely timed reflexes then this game is worth checking out.  btw, watch the video on the store page as it will give you a couple of hints.

There is no denying this is a very well put together HO style game. Instead of the usual 'mostly static locations you move about and click on things with a few puzzles' what you get is much more like an interactive cinematic experience (with some HO and other puzzles thrown in).  The reason for the -ve rating is mostly value for money. I completed the 1st episode in under 80mins, without skipping any of the cutscenes. There were two, maybe three HOPs, and only one other puzzle which took more than a few seconds to solve.  So while it's very well crafted, I feel it's way too short. Especially as there are some pretty decent HO games out there with far more content and replay value, despite the (slightly) less glossy presentation. The whole series (4 planned episodes atm) may well be better than any of the other HO games out there at the moment. But will cost at least four times as much. And I don't feel the extra gloss is worth it. If anything, it actually detracts from the puzzle-solving aspects.  If what you want is a well presented interactive story (which is very standard 'supernatural dectective' fare) this is almost certainly for you. If you want puzzles and good value casual gameplay; then probably less so.

An underrated game that features an good storyline ( the characters makes the story amusing) because of their troubled main antagonists. Their sarcastic views and comments makes this very mature story feels easier to digest for some people. The level design is simply astounding as you go from a california city, to a prison, to a tokyo dance club to a jungle. In other words the levels are varied. Althought the voice is not well executed (voice doesn't sync with the characters), the rest of thats being presented to you is well done.   Some weird thing I noticed. Kane is a mercenary so he has experience with weapons but the aiming mechanics is to shoot randomly in a cone of front of you on purpose so you miss intentionally. It's just like the game Freedom Fighters where the aiming mechanics is done in a way that your character has no experience whatsoever in shooting so the aiming is really bad. But this guy is a mercenary.  Put that aside and let yourself immerse in their world of chaos, revenge and redemption... you won't regret it.
All I can say is that this game is an absolutely MUST PLAY. The story is fantastic, the gameplay is varied fun, and the graphics are a real treat.

The city of colombia is beautiful highlighted by the giant statue and the interlocking grid of skyrails. Throughout the game you are accompanied by Elizabeth who assists you in combat by tossing you weapons or ammo. The many features of combat combined with the immense plot of the false shepard makes this game a 9/10 should play.

Capsized turned out to be far more fantastic than I initially gave it credit for.  There are plenty of physics-based games with transparent mechanics and floaty controls where the player is given the opportunity to mess around with objects and you can be as silly as possible, but in Capsized the physics take a back seat. Nevermind the fact that you can jetpack around lasso-ing objects and enemies alike to navigate- the world itself is the star. The environment is beautiful, full of detailed organic life and creatures and I don't even know what, and an equally awesome soundtrack echoes throughout giving this game an A+ for atmosphere in my book.  I played the game on Hard and it was a fun difficult challenge, when I encountered a few specific types of enemies. If you're looking for a surreal space adventure with some narrative and a bunch of weapons, this is a small-package game done right.

I haven't gotten too far in, but from what I've played, this is hands down one the best third-person shooters out there.  The gameplay is so far from repetetive, all the bullet-time and diving around all over the place never gets old. Max Payne 1 & 2 did a good job with shooting mechanics, but this just takes it to the next level. Showing 'killcams' with slow-motion bullets combined with the realistic physics and enemy reactions are just way too satisfying. Diving head first out of a window while picking off bad guys in slow motion never felt so good.   The story is average, but definitely engaging. Max's monologue throughout the game adds to the immersion, and the character development is spot-on.  Only negative I can really think of is the amount of cutscenes, they do a great job of telling a story in a movie-like fashion, but sometimes they can feel a bit unnecessary.

The story is somewhat weird but nevertheless, this game is really enjoyable. Though i would recommend you to be frustration-resistent ^^  You take over the control of the main protagonist from a top down perspective. Each Chapter consist of different levels (e.g. a hotel with different floors) which you need to clean from the bad guys. You can do so, by killing them with guns, melee attacks, slamming doors open, throwing objects etc. It is really satisfying when you get to clean a level, which you tried over and over again.  But even if you fail, you just hit a button, and start over again. The pacing is very high, so it doesn't get too frustrating (imho) as you can hop in right away and start over again quite fast.  At the beginning of each level, you can choose one mask which will give you a specific bonus (starting with a knife, moving faster, walking silent etc )  Really good 'old-school' game! If you can handle frustration and repeated attempts, and you're somewhat skilled then give it a try.

7.5/10  A quirky platformer where you play as a number of different coloured blocks with unique properties. Some can jump higher, one can float, and another can strangely hang upside down from walls.  One of the easiest games on Steam to 100% on Achievements. A 100 short and simple levels, easy to complete in 3 to 4 hours. Not much replayability as this is a simple puzzle platformer, but there is several hard to reach/hidden blocks that add to the achievement tally if missed the first time.   The best part has to be the comedic conversations between the different blocks, all told through a charming British Accent. More character development and tensions that most triple AAA games.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: In Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons you follow two siblings Ayi and Nayi on a quest to find a rare plant to save their dying father. The game features an interesting way to control both brothers: left joystick and the left shoulder button control the older brother, and right joystick and the right shoulder button control the younger brother, which are both controlled simultaneously to navigate through the game world and solve puzzles. Without any (understandable) words mentioned to the player, the game signals quest items through clever camera movement and interactions between the brothers. The game takes a comfortable 3-4 hours to complete and is very enjoyable for winding-down after playing a more stressful game.  Overall, the game is very enjoyable and worth its price. The art is beautiful, the music is amazing, the sound effects are well-done and the story is easy to follow.  TIP: If you regularly mix up both character controls, try to keep the older brother (Nayi) on the left of the younger brother (Ayi), since Nayi is controller with the left hand and Ayi is controlled with the right hand.

★★☆☆☆ - 'Average at best, Id give it a miss'   PROS: - Visually stunning art style - The sound is terrific, using the recommended preset for my headphones worked a treat and definitely adds suspense. - Multiple endings depending on how you play  CONS: - I fail to see where the two other endings originate from and at what part of the story. - Gameplay is made up of walking, opening doors, interacting with as limited objects as possible, learning walking patterns of a few enemies and pretty much working out how to run and avoid your character having an asthma attack. - The plot is as boring as the gameplay. - Whilst it was scary at first with creepy noises and jump scares, you soon become very bored and can't wait til its over. A short game could have quite easily been shorter due to lots of pointless fluff. - You run and your characters breathing goes unrealistically out of control, it gets annoying fast and detracts from immersion.  VERDICT?  A part of me wants to experience the two other endings but gameplay is so boring that I will probably won't, thats the games downfall unfortunately.

Okay, first of all, whoever came up with the 'sniper' achievement should burn at the stake, because that achievement is absolute bull, and it only gives you 400 XP, which is only pocket change, really. Now that THAT's out of the way, let's get on with the game. The gameplay itself consists chiefly of disassembling and then reassembling guns, which is a nice way of researching guns or training yourself on how to do gun maintinence, and has some shooting range levels thrown in. It's a nice concept, but where does the actual game begin? When reviewing a Free to play game, what is arguably the most important part of it, is the business model, and this model is corrupt, and almost certainly too far gone. Now, granted, the achievements can give a major boost in XP, but later on down the line the achievement awards for each gun altogether cannot 'pay for themselves', let alone turn a profit like they should be. Fiddling around with gun internals is nice and all, but even still, the amount of grinding you have to do is very, VERY evident of an obviously corrupt business model, it's basically the same concept as waiting 6 hours for a new floor to be added to your skyscraper, but with THIS one you actually have to put in the work to do it. Alternatively, you can put down some money for credits and bypass the grind, but I strongly reccomend that you don't pay a cent and leave now, chiefly because the money would be going into the hands of the sick and twisted developers of this game, who deserve to be starved out of cash. Overall, if you are obsessed with guns and can therefore justify throwing yourself at the grind or selling out for credits, then you probably can find a decent experience in this game.   This game also has an 'elite club' feature that requires you to invite 5 friends to the game, which, being the equivalent of a ponzi scheme, is just the developer's way of using you as a tool to rake in more hosts to suck blood(money) from. If you do play this game, I ask you don't pay the developers anything, AT ALL. They're not worth your money and they certainly don't deserve it.

I picked this game up on a whim, as JRPG type games aren't always my thing, and I was plesantly surprised by the ease with which picking up the game mechanics was while still providing a pretty decent challenge. With the resurgence of games like XCOM: Enemy Unknown back into the mainstream, this is a pleasant game with a little more backstory to it.  The battles can be quite long, but they balance it out with the storyline - cutscenes into the lives of those participating in the war - what every war comes down to, the men and women on the ground. It's definitely heavily JRPG invluenced, but combines aspects of Turn based strategy into it as well. And the graphics are amazing - I mean I enjoy just watching the detail of the artwork that was put into the game.  There are some glitches, sure, with the tank controls sometimes being finicky, but the pros of the artowrk, storyline, and combat challenge outweigh it in the end. And the music is pretty good as well, although the voice acting can be a little flat at times.  I would definitely recommend this game to those who are fans of XCOM and don't mind the occaisional step away from normal western video games to latch onto this JRPG-Strategy combination.

Trine 3: The Artifacts of Power is worth buying if you were a fan of the previous installments, but it may be too shallow for you than previously so it'd be best to wait for a sale. A satisfyingly more frantic take on the original formula. There's more multitasking to be had as the increased number of animatronics can come from three places versus two, and a Puppet character replaces Foxy as the mechanic to keep you invested in the camera instead of spamming the halls and mask. With multiple animatronics able to be around you at the same time, the game comes down to split-second decision-making in deciding whether or not to placate the Puppet, fend of an animatronic in the hallway via flashlight strobing, or abandon everything to duck into the Freddy mask and hide. Scarily enough, the latter decision will sometimes be made for you. The movement is nicely faster and lights turn off upon mouse release, almost making the original seem a bit clunky in comparison, and all of the micromanaging that must be done makes the game wonderfully hard as nails. Couple that with increased graphical fidelity and animation, and a sensibly expanded take on the series' lore that paints the animatronics in a new light, Five Nights at Freddy's 2 is a genuinely great sequel worth every penny!

I love this game....  And you know what's funny about that statement? The negative reviews tell why I love it.  I got this game for my birthday from a friend. I was iffy cause I saw all the reviews where people screamed 'IT'S SOOOOO EMPTY' and that seeing other players is slim.  One negative reviewer said Explorers are exempt from criticism because it's a solo thing to do and really it isn't for the money...this negative reviewer is right, in that what I am about to express may be negative to some but positive to others...like it is for me.   So let's get the TL;DR out of the way  Pros: -Vast Space -Open Ended Careers -Beautiful Graphics -Immersion off the charts -NPCs act like humans [read below for clarification on what I mean by this] -Open and Solo play -Great for Role-Play  Both: [which means depends on you if it's a pro or con] -Once you are hundreds of light years away...expect to see no players -Steep Learning Curve for some -Some stuff you just have to google [Game says nothing to help you] -Some careers pay dirt -Docking -'Watered down version of EVE'  Cons: -Lag in the starting system in Open Play -One miss fire and the Federation will hunt you down -Repeat of NPC names -Waiting to Dock at populated stations -Noobs who don't know that you have to request to dock and crash into you as they speed to what looks like, to them, an open landing pad...in fact...all people who don't do basic research in general are annoying  To be updated as I continue to play  Now for the written portion that will be kinda long  Elite: Dangerous has a huge universe. Some nerd told me 400 million systems or something like that. Majority of the criticism this game gets is that it after leaving the starting system it's nothing. Empty space with a few NPCs there and a station or two. Hardly any players can be found here.  To some this is a problem. To some like me it's awesome. Here is my reasoning why I find it so great.  The starting area is great for the Civilization feel. Stations, Federation Patrols, Resource Mines and a lot of players. Best way I found to make money is the same idea most people have. Go to a resource mine and kill pirates. I went out a few dozen systems and found it a lot easier in the starting  area...why?  Other players and Federation will attack the people or NPCs with bounties on their head. It comes to 'Whoever got the last shot'. Why is this fustrating? well sometimes you do 99.9% of the damage and then a player or an NPC will snag it from underneath you with a .1% hit. It is great tho cause if you go solo you are the only target...learned this the hard way. At the starting area you'll be lucky if your shields get dropped. Now you can avoid competeing with other plays by going into Solo mode instead of open but I feel...I dunno it doesn't feel right for me...kinda like cheating I guess.  So after you make about 50-100K you go to a Federation station and hope you don't die along the way because if you do...you lose this money. You have to cash in the certificates before payment...you die, you lose the certificates. Once at the dock you'll wait ten minutes for granted access, struggle with docking for your first few time and then you go to Star Port Services. You get the money,byou got a giant ego,byou are like LET'S GO 30 LIGHT YEARS AWAY and boom...empty.  I love these areas. No crowded docks, no players and no federation. This is hella dangerous. Pirates run these parts for the most part. SOMETIMES a Federation patrol will pass. I responded to a weak signal source and got ambushed by some pirates. In the starting area most signal sources were either Federation or waste floating about.    --I am all out of time to write...this is it for now. To Be Update--

This is by far the most realistic and intense tactical first person shooter on the market. It's has 11 maps at release, each one unique unto itself. Twenty different special operators each bringing there own skill set for great variety. This is easily THE FPS to play, better than the competition. It looks great, sounds great and handles great. It's a pull no punches approach to a genre that has left its roots in realism for something to science fiction as of late. The matches are tight and intense and at any moment can shift from winning to losing if you are careful. The maps are fully interactive with security cameras to get the drop on your opponents to breachable walls to create your own path. Plenty of room for a large variety of strategies on both sides, so whether your attack or defense you and your team can create a style all your own. So if you like a seriously intense game play rooted in reality answer the call and join Rainbow6!

I came in to this having read the reviews  'It's not exactly like Sid Meier's Pirates!, and it's really hard'  However they were on the whole mostly/overwhelmingly positive. I chose to reserve judgement (I'm usually more grumpy about games than others)  And on the whole I'd like to say:  They were right about the first part, and wrong about the second.  It's not Pirates! You don't get dancing, or swordfighting (or more annoying points like ageing/loot division). What you do get is way more factions, and way more interwoven organic storyline.  The first few quests are repetitive, but once you get in to it, it's really engaging. From the appearance of random ropes on your deck, to niggling weirdness, to inhaling incense and encountering a demon.  It's Pirates of the Carribean on steroids. They pumped it full of beastly lore then added more magic in to the mix.  And I'm happy to say that I really like it!  It's more than worth the £11 asking price  Now I'll explain the reasoning for why I said that they were wrong about the game being hard:  I picked the easy difficulty going in - I was in for a white knuckle ride, and wanted to ease off a bit.  What actually happened, was I took the starting ship (actually fairly good for what it is, a small fast vessel, with decent cargo space), and after 'restarting' a few times, because you know - I really wanted to do it RIGHT.  After all that, I pretty much figured out that death doesn't really cost all that much.  I mean, you lose some money, and cargo, but you even keep the ship(s) and all mods.  To make the start of this game ♥♥♥♥ easy, just build movement speed, and maneuverability, and you'll outclass most enemies you come accross, including much bigger ships.  Later in to the game, I even managed to sink one of the hardest ships by adding long reach in to the mix, and kiting my ♥♥♥ off.  The real thing is that it's a little unintuitive in the beginning what you CAN and CAN'T do.  Towns are NOT forts. Small ships should NOT fight much bigger ships, or god forbid go NEAR a fort.  A town will heal you for half a penny. A fort will blow you to smithereens if you warp in on the map right under its turrets (unless you happen to have the right flag, and then they're next to useless anyway).  There still seem to be bugs - In ship descriptions, you'll find 2 references of repair speed (hull) next to each other (29/8/2016), but honestly, it seems pretty complete to me.  The main storyline even does a great job of doubling up as a tutorial, while perfectly ramping up the difficulty curve, and not hand holding too much.  The only thing I haven't managed to figure out sadly is the boarding.  So far, rule of thumb is: Have your shooter crew maxed out. Have sails, and belowdecks levels maxed out. And you should be able to have 2 'fighters' (mid level crew). After that, destroy sails, and 95% enemy middle (crew) health with shrapnel (you can't kill them all). And then ONLY BOARD SHIPS WEAKER THAN YOU. If you board a ship with even 2 more shooters on deck than you have, you could still quite easily lose.  This might not be by design. There are many shipboard roles for combat. And they all look equally interesting.  BUT  They only go up in experience (not down), and the guide to combat is in the mouse over descriptions of the crew you have.  Having said that, there's a nice feature where if you do manage to board a ship, you can take it as part of a down-payment on that ship type (trophy ship = same as if you'd buy it in a city, and it becomes permanently accessible)  I'm about 40 hours in maybe, and am still finding new stuff, and still enjoying it quite a bit.  The game's pleasantly surprised me at almost every turn, and it's still keeping it fresh :)  Hope you'll enjoy the game too!

If you come into this game expecting Orcs Must Die! 3 or something like LoL or DOTA than your going to be dissapointed, that doesn't mean this game is bad by all means, it's a great game.   The general gameplay of seige mode PvP is really fun, the trap building is very cut down to a basic form compared to the other Orcs Must Die games but adds a level of strategy to dealing with incoming waves, this is understandable as your focus is on this push/hold gameplay style but to ultimately break through the other team's rift room. Jungeling in this is not really a thing, it's destroying caches and getting minion portal upgrades which is essential to win, and doing this playstyle means you need to know when to support your team during pushes.   Survival mode is the PvE gamemode where you defend your rift against waves of enemy minions, this mode feels lacking especially if you've played the other Orcs Must Die! games, most of the time if your team isn't dumb or your down a player you will win, games don't feel long enough or are not difficult once you've figured out how to force minions the long way. Survival does need improvements and hopefully later in development it gets better. Not saying it's not enjoyable just that gets bland after a 3-5 games in one play session.  Free to play means, pay to win right? No sure you can get a boost from paying for heros and chests but doesn't mean that if you don't buy you will have to grind to be good at the game. The grind isn't too bad for traps and minions which determine your strategy of winning, however for heros and other things will take longer to obtain, in my opinion buying the calssic hero pack is not worth it and the grind for them is find for free to plays.

This is a great game, you'll have lots of fun with it.  Pros: - a lot of freedom - riding coasters - it's challenging  Cons: - path creation can sometimes be a bit awkward - coaster creation was really hard for me at first. The autocomplete feature saved my life! - it's unclear how you level up as a player, to unlock new scenarios. You don't see what you are getting XP for.  That being said, I can appreciate how difficult it is to create intuitive tools. The path / coaster editing just needs a bit of getting used to. So don't be discouraged, it does get easier!  Regarding the bad reviews I read about this so far: - Lagging: I mostly don't experience this. I run the game at almost ultra settings, despite of being somewhere in the middle of minimal and recommended specs with my PC. HOWEVER, I noticed pending notifications (the ones you get about rides e.g.) slow down the game a LOT. Reading the notifications brings the game back to normal speed. Weird. - Not enough challenge: It is challenging to set the prices so that the rides don't compete with each too much. E.g. if you have only 2 rides, you can set their prices high because people have nowehere else to go. But as you create more rides, you must keep an eye on how popular / pricey the are relative to each other. I think it's challenging enough.

I never played Eve before this week, but I have heard tons about it over the past decade. I always assumed in order to play eve, you needed to take it on like a second job. I find this to be the complete opposite. I don't know if things have changed, but due to being time walled by skills you can play casually and grow with your character.  First thing I thought when I started playing eve was, the bigger the ship, the better the ship. It seems like every ship (or at least family of ships) in this game has a place and something it excels at.   I started as an Alpha player, and quickly got my PLEX to be an Omega. [Spoiler-Free Review]  The spiritual successor to Breath of Death VII, Cthulhu Saves the World turns it's parodial focus to Lovecraft's ultimate evil-doer and dares you to pull it's finger.  In addition to the much improved engine which brings the series more in line with it's 16-bit forefathers (bridges are now beautifully rendered), Cthulhu features a fleshed out campaign with an all-new cast of characters and plenty of post game bonus content for completionists everywhere. There's even a second quest with an all-new storyline once you've completed the first.  I'll admit I'm not very well-versed in Cthulhuian lore and felt the humour was a bit too forced and goofy at times as a result. Where Breath of Death was sharp and witty, Cthulhu was brazen, outlandish and downright silly. Like a teenager trying to impress his friends with useless and mundane 'skills'.  The music is still unconventionally memorable (hard rock cave music?) and nods to Zeboyd's history are strewn throughout the game. As with the first game, the optional developer's commentary is enlightening and entertaining and fits perfectly within a game unafraid to break the fourth wall.  Cthulhu Saves the World is a bit of an enigma however, while I enjoyed it less than Breath of Death VII, I'm willing to admit it's probably more to do with my lack of understanding of the source material. I ended up playing this game nearly two times longer than I did Breath of Death VII and enjoyed most of it. Chances are if you've played the first game, you'll want to help Cthulhu Save the World too.  Um... hurry?

Okay. So to start, this game is TERRIFYING. It is very unsettling to just be plodding along, trying to find supplies or the next objective when suddenly you stop moving, look down, and see a massive spike being driven through your chest.  This game though is awesome. The graphics are fantastic, the characters are realistic and the story keeps you wanting more. The soundtrack makes you feel on edge the entire time and just sounds overall amazing. But by far the best thing about this game is how it makes you feel the need to survive.   I'd recomend playing on the hardest difficulty, as it does make the gameplay much more immersive and interesting. You have to hide, you have to run, and your always scared. You can't really afford to fight anything so it makes the game feel very realistic. While there are many frustrating points in the game    like the comm area where (especially on hard) you feel like you cant leave the hiding spot to unlock any doors because if you do the Alien just pops back around the corner and charges at you   , it is well worth it when you get through them.  Even if your not a big fan of the Alien movies, this is still a great game. I'd highly recomend it to anyone who can stomach it.

PlanetSide 2 is an MMO. But, for the first time in human history, it's a GOOD MMO.  Indeed, this game is an inventive game, that brings elements from the Quick-Paced action of Call of Duty, the huge maps of Battlefield, and the awesome sci-fi gadgets and stuff from Halo, and then adds a choice between 4 huge maps. Finally, for a finishing touch, it drops down anywhere from a hundred to a couple thousand soldiers, splits them into 3 teams, and makes them fight to death using any means necessary.  In PlanetSide 2, the player can choose between 6 classes, each with a different purpose. Wanna fly around, landing in on a squad of helpless souls, only to end their suffering with a shotgun to the face? Knock yourself out. Wanna be an invisible assassin that could easily land a headshot from hundreds of yards away without them ever knowing you were the cause behind it? Go nuts! Wanna be a walking tank that dual-wields guns that are about the size of a small man? You go do that, and watch everybody cry for an actual tank to take you out.  In addition to the classes, their are 3 factions, all of which are fighting for control over the entire map. Each of the 3 faction has their own specialization, as well as a reason why they're fighting. From the people who wanna remain in control, the classic freedom-fighter faction, and the technologically advanced religious fanatics.  The Terran Republic is the original faction on Auraxis, and once had control over the entire planet. They are the reason why the human race exists after whatever happened to Earth (I forgot what happened exactly, but I'm running from a tank division anyways). Since they can afford it, they tend to shoot as much as they can, as fast as they can.   The Vanu Sovereignty are a bunch of religious fanatics founded by a bunch of rogue Terran scientist. Unsurprisingly, they worship an ancient entity called Vanu. Having access to the most technologically advanced tools and weapons, they are all about precision.  Lastly, their is the rebellious faction known as the New Conglomerate. They are your classic rag-tag army of rebels, armed with whatever they can get their hands on. Having neither having the endless coffers of cash of the TR, nor the stockpile of alien gadgets of the VS, everything they have is built to last. Even tanks, which are usually tough to take down normally, find themselves redone to have even more armor, so they can seem nigh-impossible to destroy unless their enemy is packing some serious firepower.  I keep mentioning tanks, because there is plenty of that, to. After all, how could an army last purely on man-power? Their are tanks, quad-bikes, jets, mobile bases, and more things that I can't be bothered to name. All 3 of the factions have their own unique Main Battle Tank (more commonly known as MBTs), and fighter jet.  Overall, PlanetSide 2 is truly a game of teamwork and cooperation. There is no way someone can take land for their Empire single-handedly. In this game, you'll really learn that you're just another gear in your Empire's war machine. Still, that extra man could turn the tide of a battle into your Empire's favor. You just never know.  10/10 - Would get blown up again.

DmC differs from its previous entries by attempting to modernise the story, setting and characters. It partially succeeds but can't avoid slipping into giggly cheap laughs from time-to-time that will make gamers who fall out of the teenager core-demographic roll their eyes (Austin Powers-style covering-up-genitals and 'I've got a bigger ♥♥♥♥' spring to mind).  But as a 'game' it's difficult to fault. The combat mechanics are pleasingly simple to master (use a controller though) and the pacing largely gives you room to breath after each encounter. The difficulty curb is also steady and forgiving, and although the bosses sit on the easy side once you get the hang of their routines, they still feel suitably epic and relatively satisfying, but nowhere near the standard set by previous entries such as DMC3.  On the subject of the previous games, hardcore DMC veterans will scoff at the difficulty (I am a seasoned hack-and-slash fan although no DMC master but I didn't suffer a single combat-related death in the game). There are ways of upping the challenge but don't expect a brutal DMC3 beatdown here.  Overall, it's a difficult one to judge. It's focus-grouped and demographic-orientated to the extreme, making it a pleasing and generally lukewarm experience that will never hit the heights set by games such as God of War 1 and 2, Devil May Cry 1 and 3 and perhaps Darksiders 2, but will still be fun and definitely worth playing once. Recommended.

Gone Home isn't a game. It is a story in game format. And it wouldn't work on any other medium. The story and mysteries presented are nice and they leave some space for interpretation. After the story is over, there really isn't any replay value. It would be like reading a book for a second time.  There is little to do besides finding clues and putting pieces of the story together. You wander around the house, take items in your hands, turn them around and put them back or toss them away.  Gone Home isn't completely void of positive sides. It excels at creating an atmosphere. This is exactly what it feels like to roam around a strange house. I was constantly waiting for a jumpscare.  If you really enjoy stories or if you have 'played' Dear Esther or Trauma and enjoyed it, Gone Home is a game for you. Otherwise, don't bother.

Dear lord where to start with this behemoth of a game! I suppose we could start by addressing that this game is no where near as mind numbing and vomit inducing and its predecessor Assassins Creed 3. Ubisoft has seen there mistakes and absolutely capitalized on the strongest point that AC3 made, and that my fellow gamers is navel combat. Oh yes! I was very much impressed with the ship combat of AC3 and what they have brought to the table with AC4 is something I am going to remember for a very long time.  We join Edward Kenway, the grandfather of AC3's Connor Kenway, as he makes a name for himself in the Caribbean islands in the early 18th century. He first leaves England for the new world as a privateer, but soon turns to piracy realizing that it is far more profitable. What I like most about Edward Kenway here is they have decided to go back to the witty assassin reminiscent of the young Ezio Auditore. I won't lie. While the story of Connor Kenway is a sad one, he is far too much of a whining ♥♥♥♥♥ and never listens to those with experience. Even in the end he neglects his mentor Achilles. Although Edward is a pirate, he has his band of pirate vagabonds to crack jokes with. Instead of complaining and belittling the man who taught him everything.  I'm sure what everyone is most interested in is the focus of the game being on the vast, blue, and very much alive, Caribbean Sea. The ship operates much the same way that a character would in the Assassins Creeds since the second one, in that weapons, armor and special ammunition can all be bought. Of course you must plunder the materials from enemy ships to build said upgrades. The ship, The Jackdaw, is commanded by Edward and is manned by a crew who actively keeps your ship, erm... ship shape. They will run about tying down masts, preparing/cleaning the cannons and yes, of course, swabbing the poop deck. The transition from ship to land in most instances is literally a matter of jumping off your ship and swimming to shore. There are a few larger areas and 3 cities that require a load in order to reach. Aside from those areas the Sea is the limit. If you see a small island, feel free to drop anchor and swim ashore to check for any treasure (which you will find plenty of). When on land you can acquire treasure maps that lead you to plunders of gold or plans for new weapons for you to equip and use. Ship combat is also something to drop your jaw and drool all over. Once you 'incapacitate' a ship you can pull yours up alongside it and choose to board. Your crew will then toss over the grappling hooks and pull the ships together. Once on board you have to complete certain objectives like 'kill 10 crew members' or 'kill the captain' and even setting off the powder reserves is a fine objective and swift manner of clearing the deck. There is forts for you to assault, whales and sharks to hunt (don't feel bad they aren't real and can't go extinct) animals on land to hunt, assassin missions, and some other side quests I won't go into too much detail about.   The fighting mechanic of the game has you with dual cutlasses, smoke bombs, your hidden blades, rope darts, a few miscellaneous items and I will say again, oh yes, four flintlock pistols. Nothing feels more piratey than swinging over on a rope from your ship to an enemies, slashing down the captain with two cutlasses, and then blasting away four crewmembers with your pistols. The counter attack and sneak attacks are your best weapons as always and now instead of the poison blade we have a poison blow-dart gun. Extremely useful for knocking an enemy musketeer down or sending a brute into a full blown rage on his comrades. The game feels swift and smooth when in combat.  I could go all day praising this game. I really could. 10 out of 10. Really. Not lying. A full 100%. Pros and cons... well, only one con.  As a long time fan of the series, I loved GTA 5 on the PS4. But playing it now on PC, my mind is blown. Basically EVERYTHING looks better, much much better, execpt one fault... the one con... PLAYER MODELS.  Basically with everything else looking almost photorealistic, the stilted player models really stand out as being sub-par. (Sub-par compared to the rest of the graphics... I do understand that rendering faces is one of the hardest things to do in a game).  But everything else looks AMAZING. I mean, I keep losing track of my missions because I get too distracted by driving through this gorgeous town, with seemingly more people to make it lively, and more variety in character models.   As for performance, Rockstar did an awesome job of refining and streamlining.  My rig played the game at fullest settings at 4K resolution pretty darn well. I was lazy and forgot to look at my FPS counter to see my frame rate, but it was fairly smooth with some apparent frame rate drops. I decided to see how smoother it would get if I dropped to 1080 resolution instead (as I feel sometimes too high a resolution can look weird anyhow). At 1080, it was blazingly fast and smooth and I baely noticed any graphical difference.  What's also awesome is that they included a sort of GPU performance meter. So as you select options, the game will tell you how much it thinks your GPU will be taxed.  To compare, my rig: MOBO: ASUS X99-DELUXE Processor: i7-5820K Haswell-E 6-Core 3.3GHz GPU: EVGA GeForce GTX 980  RAM: 16GB DDR4 (crap brand, and slower speed to save $ on my build).  I can't freaking wait until ENB and graphic/gameplay mods start rolling in.  I'llalso finally dip my feet into online play (which I didn't lilke on the console, but want to get into now).  Even though I played the PS4 version probably up to 100 hours, I'll replay the entire campaign and spend another 200 hours on this version, I'm sure. GTA is always one of those games you can just pick up and casually play if you want random fun and chaos.

Right now that I have fixed the glitch where I was getting like 1FPS I can now play the game. Wow 10/10 this game is fantastic and then when you've finished it you have a whole library of mods to play with. Brilliant game.

Contrary to what the majority of online reviews for No Man's Sky would suggest, this game did not shoot down a commercial air liner, invade multiple neighboring states, sanction a dictator's use of nerve gas on his revolting populace, bankrupt one of the world's biggest economies due to sanctions for the aforementioned, run a massive state-sponsored doping program for its entire olympic team, or vociferously support the 2016 presidential campaign of Donald J. Trump. In stark contrast, all No Man's Sky has done is merely charge $59.99 for a game that nerds were apparently overly excited about, which commits the mortal sin of crashing sometimes.  What No Man's Sky provides is an engaging and immersive experience in the form of a top tier walking simulator, in which the player is both free to follow a purportedly engaging storyline, or just do whatever they want, forever (being the sort that has logged well over 2000 hours in KSP, I belong firmly to the latter category, so I can't really comment on the story beyond the fact that it seems like it would be interesting if I cared). Starting worlds are sometimes boring but you can easily spend dozens of hours combing them for whatever resources or making up little missions for yourself; if this game cost $9.99, or had it not forcibly sodomized an entire incoming class of high school freshmen on the first day of school, it surely would have gotten an overwhelmingly positive reception.  Where it ultimately falls short is the same place minecraft does: the inventory system seems like a tacked-on time sink and the combat is terrible. Unlike Minecraft, there are currently no options to disable all this--and furthermore, without these two facets, you would basically just have a poor man's version of Space Engine, which is free. So while I do enjoy the game, and I can rest safely knowing that this game did not actually behead a toddler and post videos of it online, I can't actually recommend it for everyone.   * I edited this review to walk back on some statements about the variability of the worlds--they do vary a good bit--and the size of the game, because honestly who cares. I still stand by it basically being a paid version Space Engine (another 'game' I've logged dozens if not hundreds of hours in), with ♥♥♥♥♥♥ combat and an annoying inventory system

This dev has had over 9000 years to perfect this game, seeing as it's pretty much the exact same game they released in 2009. Still this one is plagued by the same bugs and problems all of the duels games have suffered from since the first one: random crashes, game breaking bugs during gameplay, netcode that barely works, etc. Worst of all is the clunkiness of the battle system, although i understand the challenge of transferring the irl magic experience to an internet one. There are however a lot of easy changes that could be implemented, but the dev would first have to make up their mind as to what direction they want the game to go.  As it stands right now the game is stuck in limbo, somewhere in between Hearthstone and Magic Online - some sacrifices to player control have been made to improve the flow of the game (like holding priority being removed), but far from enough if they want to catch the casual audience. Still, some of the changes are pretty frustrating, and I believe they could've been implemented in a better way.  The F2P aspect of the game is generous. The dream would obviously be a proper magic client like Magic Online, with a bit more eye candy - this is not that. As it stands right now this game is a cheap cash grab, don't fall for it.  Props to the devs for actually listening to some of the critique though, adding back stuff like 2hg. Although i don't see why it was removed in the first place.  TLDR: Try it out if you like mtg. Treat it like the latest angry birds. Don't spend money on it. Prepare to win/lose more than once due to the client.

review_text: Cannot get enough of this game. Played it for endless hours as a kid, and got it again on sale, still just as good as it was when I first got it. Seriously one of the most fun RTS games around (having played several) it has a beautiful simplicity about it not seen in many other games. While some games take hours just to learn and get the hang of, this is a game that takes 20 minutes or less, yet still has almost endless playability. Don't forget the countless mods for this game, too bad they arent on steam though :(  Gameplay 10/10 Graphics 7/10 (it was good in its day though) UI 10/10 (Super easy to use, even if it doesnt look the best) AI 7/10 (again, good for its day, but it can become repetitive) Replayability 11/10  Overall 9.5/10 - If you dont have this game, you need to try it, possibly the most underrated game of all time, and one of my top 5 games

My childhood was   Transformers  .   The shows, the games, the pretending...  Oh, and of course, the many, many toys.  One, missing the projectile that goes with his gun. Another, missing the lower part of his leg. All of them worn and scratched from years of play.  Yes, that was my childhood.  -----------------  Years go by, a new   Transformers   movie is on it's way...  'What?! It's an actual live action movie?! AWESOME!!!!!'  I went to the theatre with excitement in my heart, and a spring in my step.  The movie started, and I watched...  -----------------  Three movies later, my childhood memories crushed by the abominable  Bayformers , I had given up hope.  But then.  In the warm glow of my laptop's screen, I saw it:     Transformers: Devastation     With my optics, I gazed upon its glory. With my audio receptors, I heard their wondrous voices.  Memories flooded my mind, Joy burst into my soul.  Although tears were blinding my vision, I could still see clearly enough to click ' purchase ', and then ' install '.  The game started, and I played...  -----------------    Pros  :  Graphics so smooth,  Jazz  would think it's his reflection.  Gameplay so epic,  Unicron  is dwarfed by comparison.  Audio so amazing,  Soundwave  proclaims it superior.    Cons  :   Decepti cons     Insecti cons      Con cept Art   -----------------  This, my friends, is my childhood.   This, is    Transformers   .

I hated this game until I beat it the first time. I felt like it was forcing bad poetry down my throat, it was over the top and a lot of the conversations felt like useless filler. That said, I kept going because I thought the artwork was very beautiful and I wanted to see them all.  After beating it the extras    explained that the characters were based on literary authors who created classic pieces and it was mirroring their lives but as modern teenage girls. After learning that I was able to see the game through different eyes, all the useless filler conversations were actually references to authors lives. The forced lousy poetry in every sentence was actually a reference to historic literature instead of the game dev's just going over the top. I went through for every ending after reading all the extras about the authors and actually enjoyed the game for a lot more than just the artwork.     I appreciate the time and research the dev's must have put into the characters backstory, and I like how they imagined puting them together as modern teenagers.

Dragonball Xenoverse 1 was an amazing stray from the conventional Dragonball games of the past decade or so. Instead of a straight up fighter, as most of the predecessors have been (excluding Sagas and a few of the portable games), XV1 introduces a slightly more open feeling as well as a refreshing revamp of the Bandai fighting system. The story was linear and understandable, not completely 'non-cannon', the game was solid.  With the announcement of Dragonball Xenoverse 2 needless to say, I was extremely excited. This is the first game I've pre-ordered in since Call of Duty Black Ops 1 when I was a teenager, and the first I've ever pre-purchased on Steam.  However, I cannot in all good faith recommend this game to newcomers or people who aren't completely into DB/DBZ. I'll give a pros/cons list followed by a non-biased conclusion below;  Pros;  +Tons of characters/variations to choose from (aside from your own created character) +Bigger Hub World than XV1 with a lot more to do +Fan-service is amazing +DLC and updates rolling out pretty often +Covers the DBZ story from beginning to end very well for newcomers and fans alike +Tons of customization for your character +Tons of side quests/parallel quests to keep you busy  Cons;  -Essentially XV1 with a fancy coat of paint and a slightly different story -Combat is still just as clunky as is was in XV1 -Gets pretty repetitive after a while -Definitely a 'grind-fest' (not a con for me, but a con for most) -AI is awful when you need it and 'great' when you don't (Team AI will knock opponents out of your combos, while Enemy AI will completely destroy you) -The PVP elitists have found the broken 'builds' for their characters, not a fun time -A couple of new mentors, but not enough to really write home about -Co-op is horrendous, creating a team and getting your friend in is bad enough, but once you finish a parallel quest you have to back out and do it all over or face doing the same quest over and over  My biggest complaint about the game is the lack of new quests and the fact that Bandai used the same story as they did in the first Xenoverse. The story is also very short. I completed it in ~12 hours, and that's with grinding for loot and trying to get the damned online co-op to work. Bandai said they'd be releasing a patch that makes it so you don't have to completely redo the story over when you start a new character, and once that patch rolls out I'll probably pick the game back up and level a few more characters. Until then, even the occasional raids (only on weekends, when I work a ton, thanks) can't convince me to launch this game again.  For what it's worth, I actually love this game. But that's only because I'm extremely biased. I grew up on DB/DBZ/DB:GT. If you're like me, and you love DB no matter what (even through the awful live action movie that we paid to go see in theaters), then pick this game up. You'll get enough nostalgia and 'wow that's really cool' moments to make up for the price tag.  For everyone else, I'd stick with XV1 first (while it's on sale too, since it's not for everyone) and if you like it, you can continue to this game.

The Good:  The gameplay is deep thanks to some simple yet well designed mechanics, the most notable of which is directional blocking and attacking. Ranged combat has mechanics of its own as well. For example, firing too early or keeping the bow drawn will increase the spread of the arrow forcing the player to draw and release at the appropriate time rather the fire arrows like bullets. Mounted combat is also present. The depth of gameplay is not limited to its combat however. The RPG elements and social relationships balanced outside of combat and on the map screen are just as interesting and may even be more important to the long term success of your conquest or vassalage. The depth of the core combat translates very well to multiplayer too by offering a rewarding skill based experience. The Napoleonic Wars multiplayer expansion offers an experience just as fun and unique and there are various mods as well.  The Bad:  The lack of production values are evident in every facet of this game. The graphics are very dated, the UI is serviceable at best, the animations are poor, the music bland outside classical tunes, ect.  Verdict:  What Warband lacks in production values it makes up several times over on a solid core built upon it's gameplay. The only story here is the one you create yourself, but if you want a solid competitive melee combat game or unique experience that will let you lead armies, usurp kings, become a vassal, siege castles, or lead your own ragtag band of mercenaries just to name a few things then this is your game.

Prototype 2 is similar to Prototype 'overall' - ie: an open world sandbox where it's fun just doing whatever you want. The main problem with Prototype was that the first special offensive ability gained was pretty much all you needed to use for the rest of the game, so the other offensive abilities don't get much use. Prototype 2 is much more 'balanced' than the first in this respect, but now the game holds off giving you the (overpowered) offensive ability from the first game, until 'after' the final battle - meaning that there is nobody left worth using it on! That aside, it is a fun sandbox game with just enough to keep people coming back after the campaign is completed...

Max Payne may be old, but it has enough style and flair to keep it going today. What would otherwise be an ordinary third person shooter is made interesting by the addition of bullet time dodging and a just self-aware enough noir narration, turning it into a classic instead. It has some rough edges as a sign of its time, like enemies that begin to soak up bullets as you reach the end, but the rest more than makes up for it.

To register it on Microsoft LIVE you can't use the in-game Microsoft Register Function because it's bugged, you have to create an XBOX Live account with your Microsoft LIVE account only to click yourself through the dubious website Microsoft likes to have and register the game there. But not with Product Key A you purchased from the retailer but with a completely different Code B Steam gave you. Only then you shall be able to sav... Oh, w8, nvm. Now there is a whole lot of blibedibla about how awesome GFWL is and if you scroll past that w/o vomiting over your entire screen you can actually see a small blue line called 'Create local profile'. One of the only F2P shooters I've actually enjoyed. It's not perfect, but it is really a blast to play with its tight gameplay and unique art-style. I love that it isn't shying away from a massive amount of crude content (gross gore effects and nudity), because it really makes it stand out, ultimately, making it even more enjoyable. Best of all, no renting of awesome weapons with a huge amount of in-game cash, or owning a gun through micro-transactions. Once you buy an weapon part with in-game cash, it is yours.  If there are any accusations that this is a Pay to Win game, they are completely wrong and here's why: Blutes, which are used to upgrade your arsenal, can only be earned through playing the game.  Spacebux, however, can only be bought. Spacebux are used for your cosmetic needs. It's as simple as that.  About the cosmetic items. None of them are free, and some of the items cost a RIDICULOUS amount of money.  It's a little overboard, but at least they give you nothing but something to look at. This means you'll end up seeing most people using the same default characters a lot.  I'm a little disappointed with the soundtrack. It's pretty generic and it only exists withing the menu, so I had it turned off pretty quick. A bit disappointed with the overall lack of personality, aswell.  By looking at the screenshots, it looks like it's all over the place, but it feels kinda disjointed and not always present.  Maybe that's just me, though. I feel like it's not quite up to par with TF2 level of personality, yet.  Anyway, in conclusion, play it if you're into arena shooters (for example, Quake) and you don't mind a bit of silly fun.

While I respect this game for trying to be fresh, it ended up feeling clunky and poorly-executed. On the good side, the story can be interesting and it's a fresh change from zombie apocalypse. Confrontations can also sometimes feel rewarding if you outsmart a group. The problem is that these good parts are rare. While the game teaches you the basics of combat early on, it also defies these at times. Also, the ending was horribly underwhelming.  Confrontations: The game does a great job early on of showing you ways of dealing with multiple people and when you are underpowered (lacking ammunition for your gun) but it breaks many of the conventions established early on. For example, the enemy will rarely recognize the bow and arrow as a weapon and so, rather than stopping, they keep charging you. The enemy will also, after a while, charge you when you have a gun out (believing you have no bullets). This breaks the mechanic of shoving the enemy into fire or off cliffs since it takes so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ long to maneuver them into position while keeping your crosshairs on all the enemies. Another issue is enemy spawning. One particular sequence annoyed me to no end. You had to walk forward and get the attention of one group before a second group would open an impenetrable door and attack you from behind. You weren't allowed to sneak past one group or deal with them one at a time; you were forced to let them flank you.  Puzzles: The game has a lot of climbing and maneuvering puzzles. I actually think this is its best quality and wish the game consisted purely of these sorts of puzzles and left out the clunky combat. Your climbing time is restricted by your stamina, which decreases over time as you climb. The first problem with this is that your character is incredibly difficult to control while climbing. I routinely found myself shifting along edges when I was pressing down to slide down the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ pole I was clinging to! Secondly, climbing is introduced in one of the hardest climbing puzzles in the game. With no guide or direction, the player has to complete one of the longest and hardest to deduce climbing paths in the game.  Story: The story is pretty generic, but by the end I had some hope for it. We were saying goodbye to some main characters and the protagonist renewed his effort to reunite with his wife. And then the game ended. The game progressed to a state where, while not great, it was certainly improving. But instead of continuing the game, it just abuptly ends in a vague cutscene. I had only just gotten a second arrow for my bow.  In the end, this game seemed like it was trying to be a lot of things and failing at all of them. It was made by what appears to be a primarily Chinese team, so this may be their first entry into American games, but it felt very lacking in ways that cannot simply be excused by considering other cultures. Get this game if it's on super sale. If for nothing else, the guy who voices Adam Jensen in Deus Ex: HR plays a guy in a wheelchair here. So, if you want to hear the moody gravel voice, this is the game for you.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: I absolutely love this game. If you've played a Total War game before, this is the best. Certainly ups and downs in the series, but TW-R2 makes up for a lot of the mistakes. Not all of the DLC is worth it, but if you purchase via a Steam Sale I'd recommend them. I'd say that this wouldn't be a game you play constantly above all others, rather one that you play in between other blockbuster games. Five out of five stars for this one. Overall it's a great platformer.  Play with a controller for best experience.

This is one of the best games I've ever played. It has great multilayer, fun to play single player, and is all around a great game. It's perfect for a gamer like me who doesn't have easy access to Internet and has to play offline most of the time. Even though my online time isn't high, I have easily played more than 150 hours offline. I wish there wasn't so much negativity being brought to this game because of recent 'micro-transaction issue.' This is a great game that offers such a thing as DLC and micro-transactions. Even if you don't want to participate in that, it's not like you shouldn't buy the game. You don't have to buy everything to enjoy this game. That's unreasonable. You don't have to buy any additions to the game to play it. You're also not missing out if you don't. If you try to play with someone who has bought mission DLC, you won't get kicked out because you don't have it. You can play the mission without committing to any payments. It's not that big of a deal. People are blowing it out of proportion because Overkill said they wouldn't do this. Well, it's a game. It's a business. Almost every game does something like this. No one bats an eye when Call of Duty releases something new and charges you for it. At least here, you don't have to. It's still an amazing game regardless of what DLC you have. The only reason to buy any DLC is if you genuinely enjoy the mission/character and want to play it more. That's why I got them. Please, just ignore the negitive reviews and buy this game.

Less flashy than XCOM but with all the right stuff - I actually prefer it to XCOM, and that's saying a lot. It has all the best parts like the planning, base building, turn based tactics and air combat elements but the best thing for me is the overall feel of the game, its a good take on the alien invasion scenario with enough identity that makes it feel like more than an XCOM clone (like the setting in 1979; seeing the world divided up as Soviet Union, Indochina etc along with troop backstories showing combat experience in Vietnam/Afghanistan and the awesome music makes the game much more immersive). Basically it feels to me like Men In Black 3, MGS3: Snake Eater and XCOM had a love child.  I *would* however reccomend watching a couple letsplay videos on youtube if you arent used to XCOM/turn based squad shooters, as a bad first impression can ruin any decent game. That being said the game is more than fair, one of the reasons I prefer it to XCOM is that strategy actually works, as opposed to experiencing 'that's XCOM, baby' when the RNG decides to f*ck with you. Repeatedly.  Tl;dr - a cold war version of XCOM that doesnt need to be flashy to be awesome.

This game is a gem. It cannot be described otherwise. I found it on sale at a ridiculous price, and decided to give it a try as I was attracted by the premise of a game about 'mastering an ecosystem', and by the reviews talking about quality narration and voice acting. This totally exceeded my expectations. Not only is this game a quite challenging and extremely enjoyable puzzle/explorer, with a set of original mechanics unlike anything I had ever played, but Waking Mars is a little masterpiece of science fiction. The way the developers struggled to create a set of 'rules' according to which the wierd and colorful martian 'plant fauna' functions and thrives, and yet managed to render it - if not entirely likely - at least scientifically plausible, is really something remarkable, and really shows passion and effort - and some good degree of competence in the matter. Story-wise, it's pretty simple and complete within itself, as a good short story should be. A great idea to begin with, that has been chiseled, polished and refined - a gem, as I said. The characters are among the most real and human I've ever found in a game, even if they're just a couple of static drawn faces talking through the radio - and in addition, the voice acting is tremendous. Especially considering this is an indie game. Something I really enjoyed about the story is that there's not something like an immediate danger, or an impending problem that has to be resolved before-it's-too-late. The only part in which you are in some kind of trouble, the only one part in which you are trapped within these caves and have to get out somehow, is not at all pivotal and actually ends before mid-game. After that, what's left to keep you going is just the fact that you're ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ curious. Both yourself, and your asian astronaut counterpart. The things you discover while venturing deeper and deeper inside this huge network of caves as ancient as the solar system, while making it flourish with life, are bound to keep you stuck with this game until everything is made clear. The kind of curiosity, suspence, and marvel that I experienced while playing through the game are the same that I'd feel while reading a well-written novel. In conclusion: this game is great. The gameplay is original and satisfying, and the setting, narration and atmosphere are terrific. And the price is ridiculous. If you like Clarke-tier hard science fiction, you'll love this. Buy it.

8/10 This Metal Gear game features great graphics and a good gameplay. As expected from the gametrailer, it has some button-bashing and some crazy and fun SLICING action, pretty much equal to the amount of slicing compared to the famous fruit ninja game ;P.   From start this game challenges you with pretty hard tasks which usually needs some game knowledge. Although combo's and moves can be practised in the tuturials you can pick up from boxes, I tried to leave them out, because I found it too distractive for the storyline and just take the fun out of the play-learn-apply way I prefer (although, if you don't know how to block, the first two might come in handy). Between levels are a bit much cutscenes, which do in fact add context to the game and demonstrate great graphics, but often have long durations compared to the levels itself.   Upgrades to visual appearances, moves, health or stamina is done quite nicely based on experience point acquired during levels. Of course I HAD to pick the sombrero and poncho uniform :P. Like the latter, the game has more humourous additions, which I will not spoil right away, but do make me smile.  Overall a fun game to play. Time to completion might be pretty fast. Considering the fact that this is the first Metal Gear game in this particular style, its really well made and promising. Can't wait for a sequel to this one. I do wish for a bit more of an open-world sequel though (similar to e.g. Prototype).

This game gives me a lot of mixed feelings. It was the first game I bought on Steam when it was called Railworks, and that was back in 2011. Lots of routes and trains, plenty of scenarios, and decent graphics and controls.   Now, it's an overpriced train sim with almost $5k worth of DLC with ok graphics and little content.  Three routes? Seriously? That's enough already to make people have second thoughts about this game. Even if they do get it, if they want more to play around with they have to buy more routes. At this point I would assume someone would just stop playing overall, because just look at the prices. $39.99 for a mediocre route with a few new engines and stock? Bull****. Utter bull****.   I mean, it would be better off if they lowered the prices for what they're giving you, but obviously they aren't listening after a few years of people telling them the same thing over and over.  Now, there are good things about this sim. It's way better than any other train sim out there, with decent graphics and pretty good controls. The physics can be a bit weird sometimes (cars moving away from the engine when you go to couple up to them, or when a train derails it spasms and has no weight at all), but its usually well done, especially the wheel slip. The engines/stock is pretty well modeled, but bridges, buildings, people, pretty much everything else, is nothing special. Major stations looking kinda nice though, and the routes are pretty accurate to their real-life counterparts.   The workshop is nothing to write home about, but it has some great fan-made scenarios and routes (If you have the DLC to play them). If you want more routes to play with, go to the workshop and download the Hedborough North and other classic routes to mess with.  Overall, this game is worth it if you are a rail fan looking for a good sim to play with, who also has the money for the WAY overpriced DLC they have to offer. If you don't have the money, just wait for the steam sales and you can get a few things for an ok price.  If you are someone just looking for a fun train game to pass the time, don't spend you're money on this. Go buy Mini Metro, it's like $5.00.

app_name: Half Minute Hero: The Second Coming
review_text: Having 100%'d this game after 40+ hours and found myself wanting more, it's safe to say I thoroughly enjoyed this game.  Whereas the first game's RPG mode was only one of several different modes with unique gameplay, Half-Minute Hero: The Second Coming scraps those secondary modes and focuses solely on the high-speed RPG. You spend most of the game doing quests that usually give you a 30 second time limit to complete them. It's hard to impossible to actually complete any quest in that amount of time however, so there are almost always Goddess Statues to reset the timer for a small amount of gold. Each of the 80 or so quests pretty much boils down to going to a town to find out how to solve the gimmick blocking access to the boss, solving the gimmick, then going to beat the boss... resetting the timer whenever necessary. Thankfully, a surprisingly engaging hilarious story and the occasional quest that mixes things up keeps the formula from becoming too grating.  The biggest difference between this game and the first is the world map. Instead of simply picking a quest, you actually explore with your team in true RPG fashion. You can even level up, collect gold to spend on items you may have missed during quests, and go into some dungeons free of a time limit. Best of all, your level will carry over to the quests you play, lessening the amount of grinding necessary to complete each one. Another great thing is that you can use any gear in any quest after you obtain it. The first game locked gear away making it all feel kind of pointless, but in this one, if you want to take your ultimate gear to the first quest and wreck the boss in one hit, feel free!  There's really not much more to say. This baby is well worth the asking price, especially since it comes with the first game AND the entire soundtrack (which has some damn great tunes in it).
