Oh man... This game was terribly disappointing. Do NOT spend your time or money on it. I really can't tell who would be the proper fanbase for this game, or if there even is one.  The whole concept is fun and would be awesome if it was applied well, but right now this game is just pretty poorly done. Sure, the graphics took some time, but they're nothing that drastically special. Other than that, most of the game actually seems pretty slapped together by the time you get through it.  Firstly, I am a really experienced programmer, at least for 7-8 years now. Obviously, I'm no amazing specialist, but I know waaaaaaay more than anything this game goes into. Even so, I thought it would be interesting. Turns out not.  The game has no difficulty as you're basically invincible from the beginning (which is possible through more than just 1 way). Even if you don't make yourself invincible, the fighting really isn't the focus and is pointless anyway.  What's more important is the coding and hacking, which is a flop. The majority of the puzzles require barely any thought, except for one or two which require no real thought in programming but more thought in understanding what in the world you're supposed to be going for. For the former, most puzzles simply require setting a value to X or changing a 'greater than' to a 'less than' and you're done. For the latter, I mean things like what are required from the Library at the endgame--some are easy, some are just unintuitive to even know what you're meant to edit.  Anyway, I beat the game in under 4 hours, probably closer to 3 hours as some of my gametime is spent with the game sitting up. For me, it was boring. For non-programmers, I feel like it would be a pain. The first couple of chapters would be fine, with some general understanding, but once you have non-programmers needing to delve 3+ levels into an object to find some function or variable, they're not really going to understand what this means anymore... they'll just sit with their game broken into 4 screens, confused.  Lastly, the whole story was boring and the dialogue was TERRIBLE. Oh man, I wanted to punch the screen so much, I kept skipping through more dialogue the further I was through the game. It was cheesily stupid to the point where it was annoying. I can deal with cheesiness and love overly-stupid humor, but this game's dialogue was just the worst.  Usually I don't come out this gritty about a game, but it's just not done well. I wanted this game to be great, especially from Double Fine, but nope. I've played many other Double Fine games on console before (and bought them again for the PC to play again), but I still stick by (as many others do) Psychonauts being their best.  Anyway: Just don't buy this game.

app_name: Valiant Hearts: The Great War™ / Soldats Inconnus : Mémoires de la Grande Guerre™
review_text: First things first, before you buy this game know that it doesn't run on Steam. It uses the Uplay program which to me is a negative. When I buy something on Steam, I would like it to be compatable with Steam and not use another program, which I am forced to download (Not counting MMO Launchers, etc.).   This game is about several characters, who must survive the tripulations of the Great War (WW1) and to do so, must solve different puzzles to advance through each stage. Each stage is also filled with facts about the Great War and little knick-nacks to find throughout the stages. There is also a section of brief diaries that you unlock throughout the game, but give no great insight into the game.   The story is predictable and has the same Hollywood Ending that you would come to expect. The characters are archtypes, with little influence on how they are unique. They are standard good-guys & gals in any video game sense and carry the same archtypes as you would think of their character models.   The puzzles are less than challenging, even without the hintbook which the game offers, you simply need to run around the stage for a few minutes to figure out the simple puzzles. The hardest part of the game comes with digging, not to spoil a part of the game, but it is infuriating rather than challenging. There are some sequences where timing is of the essence, but all that requires is a good eye on what is happening, with the objective plainly in sight.   The artwork is pleasant to look at, with the preference to the details of the enviroment and to the sounds that you can hear in the story. It has a cute and tone heavy artstyle, but little deviance from a PG-13 flick (Ex: You are in a trench, there will be no organs , just dead bodies around and lots of mud). Characters also speak four different languages: English, French, German and Canine.   Overall, this game is something that you can play if you have some spare money and a bit of free time in your hand. Do not expect it to be the next great game on your list however and it is certainly not worth the 14.99$ tag on it's packaging. If you do wish to buy it, I would wait for a sale on the game. It will keep you going for awhile, but not with a great excitement of any sort.

The question that I think is important to ask is 'Is it fair to judge this by the standard of Season 1?' I won't waste space debating that answer, but it's something to consider.  Season 2 differs in its style in a big way from Season 1. Which style you prefer will be entirely up to personal preference and what it is you value in a narrative and in characterization. Or, perhaps, just what you enjoy 'seeing' on screen.   Telltale has done a commendable job at trimming the fat of the monotony of some point n' click sequences, but often this comes at the expense of further character development. I appreciate the lack of Myst like 'click here click there' sequences that kept the pacing of the story quick and riveting, but I also found myself pining for the more nuanced and personal relationship to the characters that I was able to develop in Season 1. I think it is telling that Season 1 took me several days to finish, both literally and emotionally, and that Season 2 took me a mere night at the computer.   Season 2 is full of thrills and chills, but they often feel forced, or rushed. They lack the scope of Season 1 in many ways. This makes them no less gruesome or shocking, but it does leave you less impacted as a player, and that was something I cherished about Season 1.  The presence of some characters in Season 2 seemed trivial, and other times downright annoying. There were some characters that shined, but their development seems stunted or worse: nonexistant. Also, the presence of some tropes from the comics and shows felt a little cheesy. Some characters could have been re-writes from Season 1. The same person in a different body.  Don't take this as me saying you shouldn't play this game. If you are a fan of The Walking Dead you should. If you loved Season 1 for all of its flaws as a 'game' but excellent vision as a story-telling device then you might walk away from this one feeling a little jaded like I did.   The voice acting is still superb as are the individual lines of writing. The game looks even better than it did in Season 1 imo. The score is still pretty good and doesn't intrude on the drama. As I said before, Telltale has cleaned up the gameplay of the point n' click considerably here and tried in what little ways you can with this genre to make the 'playing' more interesting. That's appreciated.   Overall this wasn't bad, but it failed to leave me hungry for more. I'm far more excited for the TV show this fall than the next installation of this game series, though I will undoubtedly play it.   Since I can't give this game a real score and can only 'recommend it' or not, then I will go ahead and say, yeah, this is worth a playthrough. Just don't expect Season 1. If you're more into non-stop chills and thrills though then you will probably be striking gold here.

POSSIBLE SPOILERS:  I enjoy this game. But I also attempted to like it a few times. Not until I played a character that doesn't rely on O2, did the game become 'fun'.   Borderlands, for me, has always been about taking my time, scoping out the terrain, searching out a sniping post, exploring and searching for hidden loot, and so forth. But you can't do that comfortably in this game, needing O2. Yes, there are many 'stations' and ways to fill up your O2, but my mind hyper-focused on topping off my O2, so much so, that it ruined the game for me.   So, if you find that you're not enjoying the game due to the O2 restrictions, try CL4P TRAP. You'll be asked multiple times if you want to play as this character. I don't know why the programmers did this. The game glitches a couple times, making you think he's running out of O2, but he's not. Sometimes you'll get a low O2 warning, but ignore it.   It's an whole other game if you play this character. Relax, explore, and don't breath. Enjoy!

10hrs in and enjoying most aspects of the game, although admittedly it DOES seem overpriced. The initial draw of this game was it's wildly ambitious concept and the size of it's 'universe', and from that starting point it feels like they've spent the rest of the time trying to add things to the game to make exploring such a vast universe feel exciting, and they've both hit and missed the mark there.  The good: - Each planet looks and feels very different... The varied sounds, flora, fauna, terrain, and weather really make each new world it's own immersive experience. - Sound design is great (when you climb into your ship during a rain storm, the rain sounds change to mimic the sound of it hitting your cockpit glass almost triggering an actual feeling of shelter) - for other reasons too - Enjoyable gameplay loops so-far, I feel like there is a lot I still need to uncover/learn. - Narrative devices are hidden within found objects. This kind of storytelling always makes me feel like I'm part of a giant mystery.  Cons: - FLIGHT MECHANICS: This is my biggest issue so far.... atmospheric flight is pure garbage... one of the things I was looking forwards to most was canyon flying, swooping in close to the ground and between rock formations, chasing herds of space deer across the plains of space grass. BUT NO! There is a buffer that forces you to a certain distance above the ground and above terrain objects. It feels like being made of styrofoam and trying to reach the bottom of a pool. You cannot fly yourself into the ground. It's so immersion breaking and frustrating. It feels like bowling with the bumpers on. I know some people suck at flying and you didn't want to alienate them. But PLEASE MAKE IT FUN please please please it's all I want. I don't care if I crash my ship and am stuck for 11 weeks trying to find resources to repair it. Those are the kind of stakes that breed good pilots. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ I'm so upset about this. IT COULD HAVE BEEN SO COOL!  - That is the only con.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: #♥♥♥♥onami  This game is ♥♥♥♥ing amazing and Kojima clearly learned his lesson from MGS4 and decided to emphasize dynamic gameplay over excessive cutscenes. The game controls and feels very good and every mechanic has been polished to a mirror sheen while allowing you freedom to approach an infiltration in many different ways. The game is well optimized and great to look at, which is an increasing rarity among ports at day one. The game also oozes style and has plenty of that stupid over the top charm that we all know and love from the series as well as some genuinely unnerving and disturbing segments, which all mix together supringly well into something absolutely surreal. Here's to you Kojima. We will miss you.

I found the premise of The Room very intriguing. The idea of mysterious, intricately built mechanical puzzle boxes hiding unimaginable secrets appeals to me greatly, probably because of my love for Lovecraftian tales of revelation, and my liking for the puzzle box concept in Clive Barker's Hellraiser stories (of course The Room is not a horror per se, but that's okay). The progression gave me a good sense of achievement, although it was a bit linear; then again it would be silly to deviate from linearity given the game style - successive solving of the intricate puzzles which lead you onto the next step of the larger puzzle.  Sometimes the camera angle became frustrating; I was tempted to look around the environment surrounding the puzzle's focus because I was quite eager to soak in as much atmosphere as possible; I wanted to approach those things in the background but couldn't. Perhaps the developers could flesh out a slightly more interactive environment around the puzzle-focus, some sugary fun around the meat of the puzzle itself, for those of us who would like to explore the environment around the puzzle, perhaps even little mini puzzles around the main one for those who like to explore.  I was disappointed when the game ended; it was too soon. I hungered for more. I did obtain the game on special, so I did not feel cheated regarding the price vs length ratio. All in all, this gets a firm thumbs up. I would love to play much more of this sort of thing. I heard there was a chance that sequels would not be distributed via Steam on PC which would be such a terrible shame. I play games on PC and refuse to pay much - if anything - for gaming on mobile devices, since mobile devices cannot offer a fraction of the same quality of atmosphere that a pc or even a laptop can. Please release sequels (all of them) on Steam for PC.   Yours faithfully A fan

I have never been a massive fan of roguelikes as many just push players a bti far in terms of unlocking stuff. But this game takes a amazing mix between prgoression and training that takes a minute to learn and a LIFETIME to master.   This game does one thing really well. Impromptu problem solving and reaction time flow together in a way you can scarcely imagine. Do i flip the table to block the incoming shells? Or do i fire one of my blanks to guarantee my safety? Or do i dodgeroll out of the way and hope there isnt another slug there to say hello...  The sheer volume of gear can maybe trump what The Binding Of Isaac has to offer (I KNOW they are two different games... But in terms of content i think Enter The Gungeon wins here) The gear and guns have some interesting synergies, but the unlike Isaac the synergies dont eventually overload. There are specific ones that can buff the HELL out of your favorite firearm. And some subtle ones that go by unnoticed. And some that dont help your situation at all. Forethought is the key word there. I had a great time when i found The Hexagun (Magic based sniper rifle with only one shot per clip) and i also found a hip-fire holster (Fires a free extra shot from the gun when you reload it) Turning the rifle into a instakill rifle on most minions. And equally nasty against bosses.  All in all. If you want a game that challenges you to a limit, and provides alot of content for a not so expensive amount. I highly recommend this. Fun little puzzle platformer with great artwork! You're commanding two robots with complementing abilities through some kind of futuristic facility. One robot can collect energy from the environment, the other can use that energy to blow stuff up. One can carry and stack boxes to help its heavier companion reach higher platforms.  The puzzles are all fairly straight-forward, and the required platforming skills are pretty moderate. Both won't give a practiced player much of a challenge. Even the optional collectible orbs are usually so obvious that I didn't miss a single one on my first play-through. I wish there would have been some more difficult levels. There are plenty of game elements that could have been used to design some fiendish puzzles.

Personal rating: 8/10 Replayability: Medium Budget: Worth it.  Lenght: A few days to a week.   I'm not sure what to think of Risen. The company behind Risen made Gothic, a series that I loved. Gothic has a tragic story, funding was cut from the expansion pack to the third installment, resulting in a buggy game that felt unfinished, and had the wrong voice-actors. The dreaded fourth installment that the orginal company wasn't even part of, killed the series, and I hear this is why they started Risen on the side.   While I was initially excited about Risen, I feel like Risen is some sort of attempt from the company's side to 'win back' their original ideas for gothic. It feels like they're trying to make the same game, but different.   Still, Risen has a decent story, but Risen is a series, and the third installment just game out. I'll try not to judge Risen 1 on the sequels, but it's hard, because frankly Risen 2 feels like they retconned a lot of Risen 1 in an attempt to reboot their franchise as a sort of 'pirate age' age game, even though it clearly felt like a high-fantasy gothic medieval game in the first installment.   Separately though, the games are quite good, it's just a bit of a snag to get over how poorly they connect, despite several characters making appearances in both games.   In many ways, Risen perfectly embodies the spirit of Gothic and does provide some not-seen-before elements. Where in Gothic, you felt like you were the nameless master-mind behind a gang of anti-heroes who follow you out of some misguided sense of loyalty (honor among thieves, I guess) rather than true friendship, always facing challenges in a hostile world as you go about, in Risen you feel like people actually have a more 'human' relationship towards you. Sure, you're still a nameless guy but apart from a select few individuals, you're truly alone this time, but your friendships in the game are based on actual frienship, meaning the character actually has some relationships with people.   Risen in no way beats or threatens the legacy of the 3 real gothic games, but if you liked gothic, you NEED to play Risen too.   Fun fact: Many fans believe that unofficially, Risen is set several years in the future of the original gothic trilogy. Read more on this theory on the internet.

This game might be one of the greatest disappointments I've had the displeasure of enduring. Naturally I bought the game due to my love for the Ghostbusters franchise, but the gameplay is imbalanced and boring. Between levels you are forced to endure a comic which isn't bad, but it does take too much time. I do say forced, despite the option of skipping the comic, but there is an achievement for sitting though them all.   On the plus side I do like the design of the game. Though the level design was repetitive with you revisiting the same map on a few occasions I still enjoyed the atmosphere the production team created. My main complaint about the game is the movement. The playable characters move very slowly, and the game play consists of you and the other three members of your team being swarmed by fast moving specters. These apparitions are also capable of shooting swarms of projectiles and can cause your movement to be slowed, creating a situation where your death is inevitable. So common is your death in this game it seems to me that the developers tried to compensate by incorporating your innumerable demises into the overall gameplay. This combined with the moronic AI for CPU players makes the game nearly impossible to complete on your own. 'Why don't you get your friends to play with you?' might be the obvious question, to which the answer is, I am the only person I know dumb enough to spend money on Something with as bad a rating as this pile of ♥♥♥♥♥ merely because it's a product license I like.   Bottom line, I would not recommend this game as it is to anyone.

Truth be told I was not sure what to expect with The Binding of Isaac.  Thematically you can push the notion that it's an imaginative biblical study, or one that focuses on mental health crisis's in families, or that it's a reflection on childhood trauma...  And that's fine, story & inferences in games are great but when the gameplay is reminiscent of the frustrating 4 way directional combat of the first Zelda game, and the random spawning of items & creatures isn't so well measured as it is in games such as Diablo, Isaac simply isn't a very fun game.  With that being said, if you are a fan of the first Zelda's combat (which only really improved with A Link to the Past) and are admittedly skilled enough to get through the game without much trouble, then I am certain you'll have a better time with this than I did.

This game does improve on the original but definitely leaves something to be desired.  Positive elements:  The controls have been reworked to allow for a smoother experience on foot, in the air, driving vehicles and in combat. Overall, you'll find yourself much more immersed.  The graphics are fairly decent for a 2011 game with nice lighting, textures and shadows. Reflections however, weren't so good.  Radnet gives you new tasks to do which are much more interesting than the events in the first game and you're also offered skins for James and Alex, some of which can give a good chuckle. You can turn on fun-enhancement bonuses with it which include having a weapon to infect everyone around you or causing damage around you when you jump.  Negative elements:  The story is still not very good. It ends up just being a mediocre revenge tale which turns into an even worse rescue mission. The story plays a lot like Saints Row 3 and 4 in that you simply do the same thing over and over to progress.  The cutscenes work really nicely outside of this game. Black and white with elements of red and orange popping in are great, but contrast way too badly to make use in this game and should have been left out.

I know, it looks like i've only played this game for seven hours, but a few days after I bought it, it went free on xbox (my luck), and I thought I might as well play it there for the achievments (steam achievments are useless). I first thought this game would be mediocre, as I haven't heard anything about it. Luckily, I gave it a shot for $5 on steam, and it turned out to be the best game of 2012 imo. This game is like GTA expect better. Now, before the fanboys come charging in, let me explain. Its melee is far better than GTA, its story is one of the best stories in a game in recent memory, and was good enough to make me actually want to beat it. The driving is at least better than gtaIV (gtaV may be better than SD, never played it), and the gunpay was about the same as gta. I will say the open world is a bit lacking though, and there aren't enough side missions. The setting was better too, it was a nice change from the typical LA or NYC. Now, back to the story. It is good all around, but the last 3 hours or so really blew me away. The character development was so good that *spoilers* I was cheering and clapping at the end when I finally got to grind up (I mean grind up, I threw him in an ice grinder) the main enemy, and then, five minutes later, I was cheering again when *more spoilers* I jailed the ***hole police chief for life. It was very touching and overall a great end to a great game. Please support this game, the publisher think it was a commercial failure even though it sold over 2 million copies. This is one of the best games i've played in a long time, and I can't stress it enough.

If you like the idea of a role playing real time strategy game this one is for you. A real world historical strategy game, where you create a dynasty and then try to survive in till the 1400's. The entire game is based on objectives you set for yourself.. You can try to rule a single Dutchy for the entire game, or try to become an emperor and control vast areas. Restore the holy roman empire, or go on crusades, or expel the infidels from the holy lands, and even raid and pillage as vikings or Mongolians.   I recommend just starting with the base game, and if you like it add Charlemagne, old gods and way of life. That will add a ton to the game for just a few bucks. In my 300+ hours i never have even played in the mid east, so the India, horse gods, and Jerusalem packs don't add much playing in the Western Europe areas. One day though.. The rest of the DLC is portraits, music, and animated armies. Helps with immersion. They have great 80% sales often on this so when they do, haul in the DLC you want. Then all that's left is to complete your goals, you set in this great grand strategy sandbox.

Seriously one of the best games that I have on Steam. If you like history, or grand strategy games, or just the most ridiculous scenarios imaginable in a middle ages setting, this game is for you. Words can't accurately describe the variety of things you get to do, buy it and try for yourself.

It would be nice if Steam asked me, 'What do you think of this game?' FTL is not a thoroughly bad game. I have very mixed feelings about it. I had fun with it, but I also endured a lot of tedium. But Steam doesn't want to know how I feel about the game; it asks me whether I recommend it. Yes or no. And the answer to that question is, unfortunately, undoubtedly, no.  FTL is intended for multiple play-throughs. The randomly generated galaxies mean that each new game is a little different than the one before it. I should emphasize the word 'little,' though, as that is the source of the game's first main problem: its repetitive nature. By the time you have completed three or four (short) games, each likely ending in your ship's early demise, you have seen essentially all the game has to offer.  You might still feel compelled, as I did, to make another run at beating the game. And then another. And another. But by this point the game has ceased to be truly fun. You start with the same ship and the same weak weapons, encounter essentially the same enemies spouting the same lines, and repeat the same tired motions. FTL does a good job, admittedly, of tempting its players to try again each time they fall just short of their goal, but it is due to a compulsion to see the mission through, not because the game itself is that much fun.  And FTL will keep you short of your goal, over and over. FTL is not a hard game. A hard game is a game that requires a high level of skill on the part of its players. FTL doesn't require skill; it requires luck. Since each game is randomly generated, you just have to cross your fingers and hope that, this time, you won't run into a rebel ship with impenetrable shields or space pirates that instantly teleport onto your ship to murder your crew, and that everything falls into place perfectly for your encounter with the game's nigh-unbeatable final boss.  Does this game have its good points? Does it really matter? Despite its positives, FTL quickly devolves into an exercise in tedium and frustration. In this galaxy of 'glory and bitter defeat,' the glory is elusive and the defeat is irritating rather than bitter.

The fact that the game is what it is is fantastic in and of itself. ♥♥♥♥ing miracluous, actually. Honestly, you seem more like a whiney ♥♥♥♥♥ than someone who actually has good taste in games.    All that being said, Gear Up is a really good game. If you like fast paced gameplay (Even faster than a typical FPS), and are satisfied with shallow customizations, definitely check this game out. However, if customizing down to the nitty-gritty, planning your play-style in advance, and dealing with sudden kinks in your stategy seem to be your thing... or if you'd rather play fast and hard... or if you want to go with an in-between.... Reluctance and hesitation were the first feelings out of me when I acquired this game, mainly due to the seemingly never ending popularity in knock-off Minecraft games. However, this game quickly won me over after about an hour or two of game play.  Don't Starve is a survival game as the title suggests, but contrary to the title, the cause of death usually won't be starvation, as nearly everything in the game seems to want you dead. Not much is explained to the player, aside from the fact that you and your character need to find food and shelter before nightfall on your randomly spawned island.  Game play is tedious and crafty and only grows in difficulty as you progress. I find more joy in learning as I play, rather than looking up how to play or how certain elements work. I don't think you can get much enjoyment out of simply playing for an hour or so and then quitting. No, you must be persistent and be prepared to sit on your butt for a few hours in order to become properly and fully immersed into the environment. Each time you die you must start over, unless you figure out some way to re-spawn (don't worry, it's entirely possible). The map also changes every time you start a new game, so there's no worry about knowing where you are or what could possibly lie at the edge of your screen. Something I find special and charming to this game is the characters. As you progress, you can unlock unique characters, who all have their pros and cons and as well as distinctive personalities. Their different abilities can dramatically change how you view the environment given to you, whether it's for the better or worse.  The main focus of this game is survival, so if you're looking to build incredible structures then I suggest you go back to Minecraft or some other such game. With that being said, after I'm able to build a comfortable base, map out the entire island, and survive the dreaded winter, I enjoy starting a new game and tormenting myself all over again.  Of course, this review says nothing about the underlying plot hidden between the cracks of the updates. That's for you to figure out though.

It took me 80 minutes to 'beat' this game. Some might look at that time and scoff and determine that it is not sufficient, when money is involved there needs to be a certain ratio of money to time in order for something to be of worth.  That opinion is fine, and if it is yours, stay away from this. Do not buy.  But, if you care more about the experience, if you go to the movies and pay $10 to sit for 80 minutes and see something that you want to make you think and feel, then this is for you. You don't shoot anything, you don't have enemies, you don't have leveling. What you do have is a story and a setting that seems more realistic than most things you go about your life viewing on a screen.   So I finished the game in 80 minutes. But I will be thinking about it for much longer, hours, days, and in that ratio of time to money it ends up being money I am glad to have spent.

Ricks Review:  Summary:  Gameplay: 8/10 - Simple and Qurky at times which is enjoyable, childlike and frustrating.  Game Making: 9/10 - Researching new topics, engine and engine bits allows for managable customization of your game and with many mods extending the lists of these for more replayability. Pushing sliders up and down to influence how you game looks, feels and plays is key to making your game a success. While matchin the right Topic, Genre and Console is the most frustrating, obvious and simplistic thing ever devised, it sometimes causes frustration in 'How do they not work!' and 'OMG!!! I got a Super-Amazing game!'  Console Making: 9/10 - Although not at all similar to creating a Game and only available in the late game, it is surprisingly enjoyable to create a 15 billion credit machine to compete against Vonnys and Mirconofts superboxes. Compared to game making, the choices are few to graphics, technology and optimization, and the console designs are limited to a select few (Xbox, Wii, PS2, and the Box) with upto 3 different variations depending on the console type.  MMO Making: 8/10 - I listed this different from Game Making as its selling process is totally different compared to the generic game, as it can sell Forever! Unlike basic, generic games, MMOs can sell for intill the player decides to stop selling it (which can be for any reason the player decides on). The best way to keep selling the MMO is to keep advertising it and putting Addons onto it. I rated this lower because it felt limited and cannot be unlocked through standard researching but through a R&D lab (Research and Development Laboratory).  Sequels Making: 7/10 - Again placed these types of games under a different review because they are similar to eachother and different to MMOs and Generic game making. Sequels in the base game are unlocked several years down the timeline but can be modded to be unlocked after you create your first game. Sequels allow for the player to create an entirely new version of a pre-made game, (Real Honour 1, 2 or 3 etc.) the ratings of your sequel will depend on the time since last game was released and if the game was made on a new engine (with other tech changing the ratings as well). However, creating a sequel replaces that game so if your sequel was rated bad and your wanted to start from the begining you cannot do this and you'll need to make a sequel of the sequel.   Addon Making: 5/10 - Similar to sequels but only apply to MMOs, you can create an expansion pack for your MMO game which can encourage more people to buy the game and play it. That is really it. You can however apply Game choices like new graphics or additions, but this is it. Nothing more nothing less.  Research: 10/10 - Nothing really to improve on. You can research Game Topics (Spy, Fantasy etc.), Engines, Engine Components (Animations, cutscenes etc.), Casual genre, new gameplay additions (marketing, target markets) and that is really it. After you reach late-game, you can get a Research and Development lab which is used to unlock Console Making, MMO/Addon making, Company Exhibitions and better hardware.   UI: 5/10 - UI is well designed, it is simple but accessible. However is very quirky and annoying, sometime changing the game title can be awkward. Menu buttons fail to respond or work sometimes, and clicking on a button can make another thing happen. Technical ability of the UI is poor but the display is good.  Audio: 10/10 - Best thing about this game is the relaxing, jazz-style music which plays.  Moddability: 4/10 - I don't understand how to mod this game, so I rely on the community to judge half of the review for this part. Lots of mods available from similar topics and research to gameplay changes and addons. Workshop compatability helps as it installs mods to your files directly and hosts several pages of fun and awesome mods. However I do occasionally run into faults with modding and workshop, from mods not being installed properly and requiring to access the game, turning the mods on and restarting the game for them to work. Although easy to live with, just frustrating and takes time away from playing the game.  Main Points:  Great gameplay, from heavily customizable games to creating amazing supermachines to compete against Vonny or Mirconoft.  Great jazz audio which is relaxing and satisfying.  Modding support greatly extends the shelf life and fun of the game, even with its qurks.  Improvements:   Improve the UI technicality, sometimes buttons fail to respond or work, button pressing may cause another button to be pressed and changing the game title is very awkward.

Well, with the upcoming DLC Bad Blood (I think that's what it's called).. I have to say it's overdue. The last DLC we got was Conspiracy, which wasn't a DLC that included missions. It was simply a new minigame that you could play for about thirty minutes before either getting too angry to play or just finish it, bored out of your mind. Watch_Dogs has solid gameplay that only suffers from a few issues such as not enough bad guys to fight in and out of multiplayer, vehicles that may have difficulty being handled by some players (such as myself), a lack of enemies in missions (When I said a lack of bad guys earlier I meant just while walking around regularly, either on your way to missions or just driving around for fun.), and weapons.   Now, there's probably more I could go to, but my point is, if you're looking for an open world game that has more guns than GTA IV and Saints Row, with a fun twist to it such as being able to blow up random stuff and taking down helicopters with hacks and anti-material rifles, this is a good game for you. If you like all of that, plus having a decent amount of cars to choose from, nicely styled outfits (even though you have... four, I think, standard styles in the outfits and no ability to choose individual pieces) that have really cool designs, and missions that are really interesting, this is a game I'd recommend for you. It has a lot of benefits to it, with losses that can be fixed in patches and DLC, it's just that the game is given DLC in far too slow of a manner, and it only patches bugs instead of giving people stuff.  When I mean DLC, I have a similar game I could look at. Sleeping Dogs has.. a ton of DLC. It's probably one of the most heavily DLC addled games out there. I'd say it has at least thirty different DLC content that you could purchase, and it's all usually very cheap, and actually changes the game up. Gives you new missions, new weapons, new outfits, new cars, and boosts to help you get ahead in the start of the game. All in all, Sleeping Dogs has everything you could want for a game because of of the large amount of DLC and the already amazing game. Watch_Dogs, however, does not have the large amount of DLC to make up for it's losses. It doesn't have patches that make things so much amazingly better. You don't get a rocket launcher in this game (though you also don't get one in Sleeping Dogs [You don't need one in Sleeping Dogs, though] so that means it's unnecessary to add one) even though you have helicopters that chase you, and there just aren't any new missions.  Bad Blood (Still unsure if that's the new DLC name) will be the first REAL mission, and even then it means you play as T-Bone, or at least that's what they get at. That means all of the stuff you bought for Aiden Pearce probably won't be transfered over, which is okay for an expansion DLC... but it means the real cash, dosh, dollars, currency, USD that I spent on this game also doesn't matter for this DLC. I don't know how it will end up playing out, but I hope this DLC is more than just T-Bone, something that includes content not for him, but for Aiden Pearce. I hope, at the very least, that it gives us a new place to explore while using Aiden, and more missions in the new location.  To skip to the important stuff, this game is good. I like it, I would recommend it. I hope anyone who purchases this game feels like it was worth the money.

This type of element to the game that made you care about what was going on isn't in Season 2. I need a reason to care about these people or it's just another B movie horror survival story. Season 1 had me invested in the characters. Kept playing through because I just had to know what happens.  I played Season 2 for Clemetine. Was thoroughly let down, Finished it so I could 'get it over with'. I shouldn't have bothered.  This series went in a different direction that makes TWD world even more evil and cold without likable characters and relationships I can get invested in. I don't care to support this series if they plan on continuing it. Other negative reviews cover in detail many things and I can say that I generally agree with the lot of them.

A few things you need to understand before you pick this up, or even start to play it for that matter. This game is not a series known for holding your hand, and more importantly the developers decided the last reboot was too simplistic and easy for their tastes. They have made the enemy AI slightly better, buffed the opponents, and modified the overview/direction of development/ and tactical behavior. You are simply on the run and on the clock constantly at full tilt. This game does not accept blunders without incredible repcussions, and even with the supporting infrastucture it becomes manageable. The AI is designed to be completely unfair, you are the underdog. Higher levels of play completely encourage you to fight dirty and quickly, limiting engagements to preferable 1 turn to kill the entire opposing party, about 3 to 4 times any given mission. That being said, its a series for masochists, and some of us prefer that. I recommend it, within a few months it will have the modding community release a full conversion im sure.  Pros: Mods are easy to use and fully adjustable without impacts on achivements The is enough time to complete every mission even timed It has improved on the framework by adding in an entirely new level of customization which is completely moddable without penalty Campaigns are notably shorter yet more open in terms of time Interceptor games are removed You now have complete control over missions besides dark events Classes are now far more balanced Endgame still keeps you on your toes While the game is designed to move quickly, you have the full freedom to prolong the game for as long as needed within reason Full stealth is now an acceptable option Maps are non static Legend difficulty redefines the pace of the game completely  Cons: Protection missions late game are the worst besides the final mission. Considering I've beaten the game I'm not joking. Minor glitches and occassional game breaking bugs need working out. AI has some rediculously unfair abilities such as literally dragging you through cover. Hacking system needs some work in terms of rewards. Some class builds are intunitive Avatar Program has some minor flaws with implimentation Without adjustment the rate of loot is too damn low Retaliation missions have several annoyances such as audio and faceless design. There should be another way to reduce progress on the avatar project than story progression and base destruction

An interesting game, it's similar to Amnesia in a lot of ways (enemy mechanics, puzzles, the 'screen sanity meter' etc.) but with a few gameplay changes, such as no inventory/HUD of any kind (except for when you take damage).  Like Amnesia, the gameplay revolves around exploring areas, solving puzzles and evading monsters. Unfortunately I found SOMA's puzzles to be a bit dull, most of them involve flipping various switches, and none of them really give you any feedback on whether or not you're doing it right. I also found the seafloor sections could drag on a bit with nothing to do except run towards your objective, and some of the areas could get repetitive in terms of their design.  However, it compensated for this with its great atmosphere and story. Granted the story is quite far-fetched, but I found it to be more interesting and thought-provoking than Amnesia's. There were a few loose ends that didn't really get tied up, and the ending was left kind of open, but maybe I just didn't find all the audio logs or something. There's lots of attention to detail in the level design, not all of which may be necessary as most of the levels are 'industrial interior' or 'seafloor'. I'm not sure about it's replayability, you do make some choices throughout the game but none of them seem to have any effect on the overall outcome other than changing some dialogue.  I also didn't find it particularly 'scary', it was more of a psychological, tense experience. But it definitely kept me interested to see where the story would end up, and what impact, if any, my choices would have. It took me about 8 hours for my first playthrough, that was with me exploring and listening to audio logs, reading things on computer terminals etc. You could probably do it in less or more time depending on your playstyle, so I'd say it can range anywhere from 6-10 hours. Frictional had the decency not to make it a full price game for this reason, I think.  Even if you don't like horror games, you can enjoy this for its sci-fi tension and story. Check it out.

I should probably start by explaining that I am new and mainly clueless to the ideals of anime. As my first foray into an animation style that apparantly cause dispondant teenagers to disown their own culutre and declare themselves japaneese, I was underwhelmed.  Essentially, my understanding of anime is that busty girls with odd coloured hair and no resemblance to asain or american females, fight monsters while equipped with revealling mech suits. I'm also privy to the knowlege that there is a large amount of dark themes, surpirse twists, impossbly hard to remember names and the ever famous tentacle monsters. This game has none of that. This was 30 minutes of disapointment.  What I got was an adventure that comprised of reading a book worth of dialouge ( I know now that it is something refered to as graphic novel game, but that doens't derive from the fact that it is a game and should at least have some function of interactibillity). If your game is going to comprise mainly of dialouge, atleast make the dialouge...good. It was superficial at best, forced if you will. They just pushed the ideal that our main character, is smart, determined and a strong independant female without giving us an actual reason to believe it.  I counted about 3 options of choice throughtout the game. Do I stay to play basketball, how do I answer a quiz and do I hand him the pan of baked goods. Do I? Do I even care by this point? So far I've read my way through a week of her school life and as far as sexual appeal of the game goes... its lacking. I'm assuming this is an age friendly version of anime, or the rumors I have been hearing about the representation of females in animet have been false.  At the end of the day, it leaves a bitter taste. Missed oppurtunites of choice and interactibillity which would have been welcome in the plethora of text. I'm glad this was purchased for me by a friend as I would be angry that I wasted my money on this 30 minutes of tripe, and while I can't commend the gameplay, I can't really complement the drawing talent either because a) there is next to no animation and b) apparantly most animes look like this so it's clearly not a unique art style, or at least difficult for your average artist to draw in 30 minutes.  All in all,   2 out of 10.    Horrible plot (Apparantly with multiple endings, each 30 minutes long to find.) Uninteresting characters Unsatisfying end  Just another crappy game clogging up the steam archives.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: This game is tough. It reminds me a lot of the old platform games like Mega Man because the monsters are usually in the same hiding places and have the same AI reactions, so even when you (not if) die you at least know what to expect coming. It can be very confusing at first because there is no set path. You can sort of create your own quests because getting nice weapons is in itself a quest. For instance I wanted a particular great sword which required me to get to a specific boss, kill it, then use a bonfire astetic to kill her again and get the sword I wanted.  If you like a challenge, enjoy fantasy games and have an interest in looking for lore instead of it being handed to you then this is an excellent game.

Batman: Arkham Asylum is a pioneer which changed the landscape of superhero genre in the gaming industry.   Story is well written, as you will feel tension in the atmosphere. Joker pulls all the strings and you must fight the odds. It starts off with Joke being escorted to his asylum under Batman and Arkham Asylum's watchful eyes but Joker manages to escape and turns the asylum into his playground. You must save the people in the asylum and stop this madness that Joker had planned all along. The voice acting is top-notch. One can listen to Joker or the Riddler talk all day. The Scarecrow bit in the game is one of the best gaming moments ever. I loved the tone of many super-villians in an asylum with Batman. This game changed the whole landscape for the superhero games.  You will feel like Batman as you will owerpower most of the thugs. Different gadgets are used to solve puzzles and move ahead in the story. The sleath mechanics are well done too and one of the best parts about stealth is how after you take out one of the enemies the fear slowly grips other enemies as they become aggressive but as soon as the numbers start dwindlling they start to panic, this machenic is one of the best in the gaming history. Boss fights on the other hand were a bit too easy and lackluster. Also there is some odd detective work which was nice. Of course, there is the good old fashioned brawling there too.   Graphics and atmosphere are good as they capture the tone of the dark and gritty world. Art-style is also very good. Environmental design is brilliant and there a twisted sense of character in every area you’ll discover. This claustrophobic cove of madness oozing terror has to be one of the best settings in video-games.  It has everything you would like from an immersive world, great storyline, sleath, detective work and old fashioned brawling. What else do you want? So, are you a Batman fan? buy this game, even if you are not one you will still love this game and is inarguably one of the best superhero games ever.

The gameplay is fun, despite somewhat simple. Playing a mage I found the spell crafting system really enjoyable. What really sticks out about this game however is the story. The story is so immense and the dialogues are often funny and interesting (although sometimes repetetive.) The audio is excellent and it really does wonders for the dialogues that have voice acting, which is the majority of it. The visuals were pretty nice too, interesting art style. Not everything I have for this game is praise though. The story although in-depth was a bit short, or felt short. The ability to change the difficulty of the game during a campaign is a negative for me, but it may be positive for others, to which I can understand but will never relate. Those are just minor issues though, what struck me about the game was it left me wanting more. It felt unfinished when I beat the game. Tiers (the area you play in) is just a small part of the world, but it's all you get to see. It's high praise to say that I desperatly want more of this game, the puzzles, the dialogue and the action. But the game itself was so short. It didn't cut any corners or ask you to grind for a long time, which I was very happy about, but many of the trailers sell the game on being replayable and that fault is supposed to make up for this feeling I am left with. You can replay the game, choose different areas and do different quests, but the song remains the same, some minor details change, you go to different areas, but you hit the same checkpoints.  tl;dr - Left me wanting more, didn't deliver. Worth entertainment value if bought on sale.

Why? 7) Hint: Games so simple, just pick up all the suit cases and place them NEAR the box without putting them in. No enemies will spawn to stop you. Brilliant.  8) Take your time using your skills or youll end up activating and IMMIDIEATLY deactivating them. 9) WTF is healing in this game?. Medkits I get but....Green Plus-sign auras that float around? A circle of green you can just walk in ? That revives?( Most of the time, youl find the insta-revive just about useless when solo-ing) 10) EXPECT GLITCHES.....all the time.....with out fail. enemies that get stuck behind the spawing door. gun not to fire when pulling the trigger...skills that blatantly dont work and (My personal favorite) receiving damage during the loading screen when respawning. 11) you will respawn into oncoming fire so find cover 12) and LEARN how to manipulate cover becuase this is literally the worst cover system I have ever seen in a game  13) We have the resources to constantly Hel-evac items but cant clean the items at the mulitple hazard check points that exist in the dark zone? 14) 'Post game' is replaying the same missions with enemies with more health. The game story and bosses are great however I'm gonna give a negative for following reasons:  -There is a bug when you invade, host or phantoms will be just floating in a T-pose without animation so you can't see if they're attacking or rolling and they take no damage. It is seriously game breaking in terms of pvp and it happens a lot. This also occurs during co-op play with phantoms and will completely remove any challenge from boss fights since phantoms just murder the boss without taking any damage.  -Cross Region Matchmaking options don't even work. You'll get matched with people all over the world even though you have it turned off. Enjoy the lag.  -Some weapons are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ly unbalanced : Dark Sword, Estoc   -Invasions are completely ruined, You get a HP penalty when you invade and often fight 3-4 people who just run at you and mash R1.  -Walls and areas in game have broken collision, mobs can hit you through walls in most zones.  -Poise mechanic is completely broken and you'll lose most of the time if you have a slower weapon without hyper armor. Heavy armor is completely useless.  -You can get backstabbed in the middle of a jumping attack animation.   -Recovery time after drinking estus is pretty much none so people just run around and chug estus without getting punished.  -Some weapons have ridiculous phantom range.  Besides these issues game is better than other dark souls games, however don't expect a great Online experience because T-pose bug is ♥♥♥♥ed up and there are no news of any fix. ♥♥♥♥ Fromsoft

I have been playing the original doom games, custom WAD's, Brutal DOOM mod for over half of my life. Hearing about this game and knowing it was on its way is the most i have EVER been hyped for a game in a damn long time. I have to say on the singleplayer side of things, it far exceeded my wildest dreams. The map design is incredible and the playstyle is innovative and something not really seen before in todays game market and it works hella good.  I was pleasantly surprised watching the SnapMap reveal at E3, i had played a lot of HALO Forge back in my console days and was more than welcome to give this extra content an open mind. However im not sure if it was just me or the presentation of snapmap but it really was not to my liking, but im accepting that thats 100% my opinion, yours may be different. I just found it very hard to understand even with youtube tutorials and the end result of custom maps disappointing. Also 95% of the published maps made by other people are much the same, not even coming close to the campaign which i was desperatley looking for a replacement after playing it multiple times.  Now, multiplayer is the only thing that really pisses me off about this game and where i feel they mucked up. It seems that nowadays, all developers and companys care about is money and popularity, where are those two things at right now? Consoles. An inferior platform thats held many titles back on pc from becoming much better than they are. So how does this affect DOOM? Besides Arcade Mode which admittedly is a nice feeature, there has no been no singleplayer content and not a solid hint about any upcoming singlepayer content. All DLC has been bloody multiplayer modes and new maps, because way more people play DOOM multiplayer on consoles than PC, in fact ive struggled to find games online because its that unpopulated.  So please Bethesda more Singleplayer content please, most of the pc community is not interested in the multiplayer, we want to klll new badass demons not whiney little kids.  But all in all a spectacular game that i will continue to come back on now and then when im in the mood to kill some hell spawn!

To address some of the other reviews.  No, it's not the same as the previous Lovely Planet. The 'Arcade' in the title imples that it's something different. And that's not a bad thing. The original Lovely Planet was great! Fantastic! This Lovely Planet is just a little different, but still great.  There's no vertical aim, meaning you only control which horizontal direction you're aiming in. That's one of the biggest changes and it's something that takes a few minutes to get used to.  There's less emphasis on exploration and mapping out your own path through the level to try and be just a little bit faster. There is of course a fastest path through the course, but there's less freedom to make up your own. The speed-running in this installment is more based on skill rather than ingenuity, although it does take some ingenuity to find the quickest, most efficient path though the level. But once you find that, your speed is mostly dependent on how skillfully you can navigate the level and shoot the baddies. I think more so than the original, this game has more puzzle elements to the level design, but don't get me wrong, it's still balls-to-the-wall difficult to execute in the harder levels.   A few cons.  There is currently no level editor. It really seems like a game that would lend itself well to that, so it's a shame that it's not there. The built-in levels are great and all, but replaying the same levels to try and set a new PB can get repetitive.  There are no leaderboards. This one really bugs me. I'd love to see how my times are compared to other people's instead of just trying to beat my own all the time. While the original Lovely Planet had leaderboards, I'm pretty sure the top times were broken or full of people glitching the game is various ways, so that might be why there are no leaderboards this time around. Because he couldn't differentiate cheaters. Who knows. It's just a guess.   TL;DR It's a great game. No vertical aim takes some getting used to. More puzzle-based in the level design but still very mechanically difficult to pull off in the fastest way possible. If you don't care too much about beating your own times, then you might only get a few hours of entertainment of it, but it's not an expensive game. Sadly no leaderboards or level editor :(

If you're any kind of gamer at all, then it probably won't be too hard for you. The fights can be quite tough sometimes, but keep trying and you'll get through soon enough. The fight mechanics are pretty easy to use and make the fights quite fun. The game is not short and took me 20 hours to beat it, but I was also very carefully searching for collectibles. I'd say get it if it goes on sale if you really want to play it.

This game was a nice trip down memory lane. Being an older gamer it reminded me of some of the systems that had came and gone and how far gaming has come in the last 20 years. For a first time or even a second time playthrough this game is fun and can keep you entertained. After the third or fourth time it starts getting boring. I find myself making the same types of games because they yield more money than other experiments. Game time goes too fast to really explore much if you want to try to keep up with the advances in the game world technology to mirror real world advancements.  In all I'd recommend a few playthroughs for anyone wanting a nice casual easy going game. I'd wait for this game to go on sale to purchase as the replayability is there if you want to explore it, but if you're like me you'll take what you know about the game creation process and capitalize on it by recreating it whenever you can.

First of all, let me just say that I haven't writtien a review since the the required review for the Pillar of Community Steam badge. I've always planned to write more, but I wanted to make my next one way more meaningful than the first. So, here I am. This should already say something about how great this game is.  Risk of Rain is one of the best games I've ever played (And I've played on every platform, and over thousands of games). It blends co-op, roguelike elements, challenge, and good old fun in one, amazing, already low-priced package. The soundtrack is great; so much so that I was always stuck listening to it in my head, until I eventually took the files from within the game itself and copied them to my phone to listen to them when away from my pc.  Whether you like playing solo or going through games with a friend, you'll find there's challenge in both, no matter the skill. The game's difficulty slowly increases with time...after the 40 minute mark, you've just about hit the highest difficulty. However, if being challenged isn't your thing, you can always just choose the 'Easy' difficulty at the character screen, and difficulty will climb slower, but you won't be able to earn achievements this way. The challenge is to get farther than you did your last attempt, and to collect as many loot items along the way, to fill your logbook, which in itself I've spent many hours just reading the hidden and not-so-hidden references in the backstory of Risk of Rain. Each item has a unique effect, and different descriptions. Some items can only be found within the game after completing certain parameters shown in the logbook. New classes can also be unlocked this way.  Even if you don't like reading, and prefer just going in and shooting things, Risk of Rain will sate you. After 5 mintues into the game, you're already guarenteed to be running for your life, blasting what you can as you try and evade everything trying to kill you. Each class in the game has unique abilities, and offers different playstyles, suitable for different types of players. Personally, I like being a gunslinger, and sneaking, so naturally, I love Bandit and Commando. Commando is your basic, starting class, and is actually arguably the best class of the game, if you know how to use him to his full extent. However, that doesn't mean that your class can't be the best in your hands. Commando is only the most balanced, and a good starting point for every type of player. But as you get into the unlockable classes, you'll eventually find one that suits you, if the first few aren't your style.   The art in this game was also really outstanding, and inspiring. Pixel art isn't easy to pull off well; yet Risk of Rain's dev team did an amazing job of spinning a story within the art itself. Personally, it inspired me to do more art, and more writing. I don't think the game will ever look dated...This game could easily look as something from the 90's, yet something from any future generation with its fast gameplay, and creativity. If you appreciate any kind of digital art, you'll be lost and immersed in the game's visuals itself. This may be a little unrelated, but the soundtrack just added onto the ambience so well when enjoying this game, and the art.  All in all, the game is easily worth the money invested into it. Every game is different, no two playthroughs are the same, and your luck can be all good or all bad in any playthrough, but ultimately, you can control your destiny in the game with your skills. The game really reminded me of Dark Souls with the challenge it gives you, and the gameplay itself feels like a classic sidescroller, yet with more magic and originality than most. The game is easily a classic of its own, and I believe will withstand the test of time in this coming generation of games, and even afterwards.

There are new floor layouts, larger rooms, new enemies, new items, crazy interactions between items, and tons of hours to be lost in this game. Neverending Nightmares may be a short experience - taking around two hours to finish - but the paper and ink art style and oppressive atmosphere has combined into a very innovative and interesting game. More reminiscent of Limbo than any of the more conventional horror games I've played, the game creates a dark world with only one objective: keep walking.  Though the third person perspective means that it can't do jump scares as well as most horror games, it nonetheless managed to create a feeling of dread throughout the game by focusing more in the psychological part of the horror genre. The slow building gameplay helps contribute to the dark and introverted feel of the game; though I found it wonderfully refreshing, not all gamers may enjoy what is essentially wandering around a dismal world for half an hour.  Though it suffers from a few minor glitches with the lighting, it never really got in the way of gameplay and the branch-off levels add to the otherwise short game. It gets a little repetitive by the end, and the short environments show its indie background, but it's ultimately worth playing and is infinitely preferable to games like Dead Space which overly rely on jump scares.

DDE has gotten a bad rap from previous DD owners because of their idea that this is just a reskin of the game they already own. However, DDE introduces enough new features (armour, amulets, security) that it could really be considered a massive DLC. Especialy since the price was only 5$ for those who already own DD. Moving on from that here are the pros and cons I have noticed.  Cons: Armour is really ugly untill high level, Very mismatched unless you find a complete set, and no option to hide armour as of yet. Cons: The so called rebalance made the summoner near useless( No more Private Defense Pool), Barbarian is still useless, has yet to be balanced. Cons: No steam Market integration? yet? I Hope to see this in future. Cons: You have to make a seperate Playverse account, a small annoyance but it does irk some people. Cons: You have to buy the same game on both mobile and PC in order to play it on both, 2 seperate copies, Perhaps they will offer a code for the playstore sometime in the future?  Pros: Runs buttery smooth from what i've played so far, and I experimented with some high-demand survival maps. Pros: Rebalanced loot system is wonderful, I can actualy use a majority of the items I find now. Pros: ALL DLC INCLUDED. Huge bonus. Pros: People actualy play this one, DD is dead, DDE has a much larger playerbase and thus a lot more public matches. Pros:NO HACKERS! This really should have been in the old one but Since I bought it shortly before DDE came out It never bugged me to much. Pros: Amazing new maps, I have yet to try the Embermount Volcanoe but from the videos it looks amazing. I really hope to see more maps come to this verson. Just keep cranking that level cap up trendy. Pros: It's on android, Prepare to see a huge leap in players once it is realeased on the newer devices, and imagine all the fun you will have showing off your PC exclusive pets and Heroes to the noobies from android.   Overall a really good buy for the 5$ pricetag, If you didn't already buy it for PC seriously consider paying the full 20$

Technobabylon is a throw back to the classic point and click adventure games of the mid 1990s. If you were a fan of the classics like the original Monkey Island or Sam & Max then this game will be for you. In blocky 16 bit graphics the cyberpunk world comes to life with a great story, interesting characters and cool scenes.  The game wisely keeps you locked in 'chapter' areas meaning you dont need to scratch your head picking up a random object in one scene only to use it 10 scenes later, all objects are useable pretty much in the same area you find them.  Dialogue is tight and well written, voice narration is solid and story in general is interesting and engaging.  However, one MAJOR gripe I have with this game, and it REALLY bothered me on too many occasions was how unclear interactable objects were!! The times I got stuck and had to google how to move forward ALWAYS resulted in me having just missed a chest of drawers or a cupboard and had nothing to do with actually getting stuck on a puzzle. It ended up with me on each new scene running the mouse up and down the screen ensuring I had 'scanned' everywhere in fear of missing an interactable object. Some sort of 'highlighting' system is sorely missed.  All in all a solid point & click adventure set in a cool cyper punk world.

Mortal Kombat X is the 10th installment in the iconic Mortal Kombat series, and probably the best game out of them all. It features 26 playable characters (35 if you include all the DLC) with each characters having 3 different variations that are designed to fulfill specific roles (zoning, trapping, rushdown and so on). The variations provide changes in terms of moves and combos, so in reality you're looking at a game with potentially 105 characters (DLC included), which is a lot. The characters are well balanced for the most part, which means whichever character you choose to main will probably also be tournament viable if you get good enough at the game.  The game is also very fast paced for a fighting game, and offers players tons of content, from a (good, but short) story mode; to traditional towers in which the player has to beat a series of AI opponents with a final boss at the top; to living towers (regular towers that get updated with new challenges every once in a while to mix things up) and more casual game modes such as test your might and test your luck. It also features a return of the Krypt, in which you can unlock various costumes, fatalities and other behind the scenes novelties.  This port had a rocky start when it first launched on PC, with people not being able to get past the main menu due to its many issues, but most of the issues it had are now fixed. All in all, the game continues Netherealm Studios' tradition of offering the player great value for money by showering him with tons of quality content - a lot more than what you'll find in any other fighting game out there.  So why am I not recommending this?  As of January 20th, Netherealm had announced that they are dropping support for Mortal Kombat X on the PC. This means that the Kombat Pack 2 DLC, featuring Triborg, Bo Rai Cho, Leatherface and Xenomorph, will not be released on PC at all. This also means that PC users will not get the updated netcode that is currently being developed for the console users, and will have to stick to the inferior (and quite horrible) netcode the game currently has. On top of all that, every new balance patch the game receives from now on will not make it to the PC. In sum, anyone who plays this game on PC is doomed to play an inferior and different game than the console version. You'll have less characters, worse online, and worst of all - the frame data on your moves list will not be compatible to the frame data of your console counterparts, which means that any effort of friendly competitive play between a PC user and a console user will automatically put the PC user at a disadvantage (if you had plans to play this game competitively in casual tournamens like I had, you might as well forget about it).  Now lets be clear: while I do hold Netherealm Studios responsible for this to some extent, in my book the main villain of this is Warner Brothers, who now demonstrate a pattern of releasing shoddy game ports on PC (Batman: Arkham Knight, anyone?) followed by quickly dropping support for said ports. That company proves time and time again that it doesn't care about PC consumers by outsourcing games to bad port developers and quickly dropping support for said ports in favor of making new DLC. This is quite embarrassing for the company, as it turns away from the few people still willing to give it money for its horrible PC releases.  If you're a fan of Mortal Kombat, consider buying it on console as it is indeed a good game. If you're strictly a PC user, however, don't bother. You'll be playing an inferior product that will be vastly different from the main game. Also, in the future, if you encounter a Warners Brothers game that is being ported to the PC - avoid it like the plague. I know I will.

A Bird Story is really at the fringe of what constitutes a video game, as, here, interactions are neither providing you with any kind of choice or even the illusion of freedom, nor ask of you some sort of performance, they are only there to reinforce the identification, to make you enact the story of the character. This approach is not without merit, but it might have been better served by a slightly shorter or better paced game.  That said, A Bird Story is without a doubt a pure product of video game love, in all its 16-bit pixel art glory, its narration through digital puppetry, and its skewing of video game codes.  Sanding out: the originality (at least for a game) and the confident delivery of its wordless story, the fascinating use of space as a dreamscape and the beauty of some of the painted backgrounds.

If your looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if your looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.  Ingredients: 1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter 2. 1/2 cup white sugar 3. 1/2 cup brown sugar 4. 1/4 cup water 5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon 6. 1 pinch salt 7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced 8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie   Directions: 1. Preheat oven to 425 degrees F (220 degrees C). 2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside. 3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices. 4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal. 5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust. 6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C), and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.  Eat.

I never understood why it seems so hard to make a decent cyberpunk(ish) game. Shadowrun Returns is on the right track, definitively.  As a longtime player of the P&P-RPG Shadowrun, I thoroughly enjoyed this latest installment. Of course it has its problems, but on the whole I can recommend it to anyone not too hooked up on graphics or other shinies. This game further improves on the original Shadowrun Returns and its first official successor Dragonfall.  As with Dragonfall in regards to vanilla Shadowrun Returns, Hong Kong makes further small improvements on the gameplay and mechanics that I personally welcomed as good additions (for example having a Mass Effect 3 like skilltree for your team to give them certain powers as they progress in level).  PROs: - Story is very good. In fact I'd say it is the best so far. - Very well written dialogue with interesting special options if you developed the right skills, providing a satisfying RPG experience - Atmosphere is very immersive, the texts (yes reading! see below under CONs) are painting a pretty good picture of the Awakened Hong Kong in all its corrupt glory and misery - Quite a few points in the storyline where you have to make decisions influencing your later game in small ways. - Interesting characters as fellow runners with good backstories and their own missions. I never used hired help.  CONS: - A LOT of reading involved (didn't mind that myself, but it is not for everybody). Nothing (exept pro- and epilouge) is voiced over - Limited possibility to equip your fellow runners. They mostly have their own weapons and equipment and don't need stuff you might want to give them. That is on one hand fine, as you won't earn enough money to equip everybody with the best stuff anyway but it takes away some of the fun (for me at least). - Occasionally ATROCIOUS AI in regards to neutral NPCs in combat (had a physical adept - supposedly helping me - run circles for the whole fight while his mates and my team died) - Same for the odd enemy AI (not to spoil anything but the final boss hardly even attacked my team during the whole fight). - The hacking minigames during matrix runs are an interesting feature but it gets old very quick. - the odd bug (had bought equipment like spells disappear between runs occasionally)  While on first glance the number of cons does seem to outweigh the number of pros a little, this is mitigated by the fact that the cons are - to me - minor annoyances as I play primarily for the story experience not the gaming challenge.  If you played and liked Shadowrun Returns and Shadowrun Returns Dragonfall, Hong Kong is a must have in my opinion. If you didn't, you might want to check them out first, as Hong Kong features improvements over them that you will probably miss when going back and the lack of which might lessen your enjoyment of the earlier installments (especially of the original).  8/10 would shoot plastic man in the face again

The NEXUS! update solved a lot of problems people had, but fails to solve the biggest problem: it's simply not fun.  This game is a mix of <FTL, Fallout, XCOM, you know the drill. Name awesome games to trick people into thinking your game is awesome too>, but simply fails to generate fun.  Do sidequests? Well yes, otherwise you'll run out of stuff even sooner. Which leads to several screens giving you the option of throwing away your previously invested materials or continuing on, with no clue what outcomes continuing could give. 'You see a shack. Investigate (y/n)?' does not suddenly make me want to retreat. I cannot guess the dangers nor the benefits this could give me. There is no choice. Even when encountering a trader, where the choices of trade or don't trade would be an actual choice, it doesn't say the numbers. Trade food for fuel. How much? Not a clue. Just a wild guess whether you'll be happy with the results.  Combat is apparently a lot better in NEXUS(never played before it) but is still quite stale. The enemies simply have better numbers. Health, damage, and amount of troops. The retaliation of gunmen is fun, but they have to live for that to happen. Pretty hard if every enemy one/two-shots you.  But that's okay, there is a rock-paper-scissors thing going on: shotgun melee dudes so they have to walk back to you(and lose 40% damage), snipe gunmen so they can't retaliate(I thought being inside 2 squares would do it too, but when counterattacking they lose their minimum range for some reason), slice up snipers so they can't attack back, and pewpew the close-rangers with gunmen I guess? Except you don't know the enemies' composition when choosing your team. You don't enter a sniper gym and use all your melee pokemon, you simply don't know what will happen. Good luck planning!  I did a quickly failed normal run and a halfway-through easy run, but that was enough to see what the game had to offer. That is, without unlocking. But that won't change much. The starting weapons/equalizers mostly.  Which are about the only way to even the odds. But cost prohibitively much to use on a regular basis. But even in Easy, where I have tons of resources so far, it's not FUN. Sure, I can heal 4+ hit points with every character each turn. And then? They can slice 3 damage off of the 25hp boss for another turn? It's just a battle of numbers.  There is no line of sight, no Overwatch(if you say you're part XCOM, that's a must), no special abilities each character can use only once each battle, no upgrade trees(the 'fallout' means 'lore-wise only')(damage all the way, one more hp won't let you survive another blow, but one more damage might give the enemy one less turn)... Just clicking through screen after screen like a robot. If it would be relatively simple to write a bot that plays exactly like I would, so that I think it through once and then stop thinking, the game fails to be fun.
