On my first playthrough, I had to stop playing repeatedly. I would win a fight and then have to go sit down with my face in my hands. This game is emotionally brutal. I have never played a game that makes you feel like the villain before. I have never cheered on my enemy, hoping for them to kill me. This game has done something many games have tried and failed to do. And that was all on my first playthrough. Playing it again and getting an entirely new experience, it was like I bought two games. And how opposite everything became, how happy it made me. And yet, my previous actions were still lingering here. My actions felt like they mattered.  And that's what makes a game worth playing. It's what makes a game a good game. When what you do feels important. When you're not just hitting the play button to a movie you're watching. When it feels this personal. I just. This is the definition of a good game. It is good game incarnate. It ticks every box. I don't know what else to say. Even with it's simple graphics, no game has ever felt this close to being real before. This is a true masterpiece that will be remembered for all time. A game by which all future games will be judged against.  I've palyed it twice, beaten it twice, but it's not over. I feel like I'm still playing it even after all these months. It's changed my entire perspective on so much, and made my life just that much better. It's a game that stays with you through the rest of your life.

Left 4 Dead 2 is a Zombie Game. There isn't much more to say.  You play as one of the four unluckiest ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥s in Savanna, Late for the last helicopter evac, you have to shoot, slash, bash, and blast your way through hordes of victims of the green flu, a mysterious pathogen that leaves you with desaturated skin, and a ravenous hunger for people who don't have your condition.  The game takes place across the deep south, in locations such as Malls, Amusment Parks, Swamps, and the many streets and houses of disaster stricken America.  The game is a lot like it's predecessor, The original Left 4 Dead. With a few major changes, new infected, and weapons, things like M60 Machineguns, Grenade Launchers, Machetes, Katanas, CHAINSAWS.   New zombies that enter into the picture are the Spitter, who can drive a wedge between tightly grouped survivors, the charger, who can also be disasterous on say... a rooftop, and the jockey, who can manipulate a survivor into a a hazard, like fire, or spitter goo. or a witch.  Wandering Witches have also been added, as if dodging them wasn't a pain in the ♥♥♥♥ already.  Overall, I'd say it's a good game, but it needs more campaigns, or perhaps a new Left 4 Dead game, perhaps set somewhere other than america.

Negatives First off, i'd like to say i love this game but it should never be played by any human with a rational mindor semblence of sanity at all.  The community is so garbage that it will kill this game for you.   No lie, no joke, you will grow to be the most cynical hateful person because everyone 90% of the time in the game forces you to be the only competent one.   The game itself also has quite a few flaws, for instance the pathfinding is absolutely horrendous and in terms of balancing there isn't much.   You may look at the roster and want to pick a cool looking hero, but you shouldn't because ♥♥♥♥ you. Most heroes are either irrelevant to drafters who know what they're doing or are jokes. Each patch has a rotation of heroes that are strongest that will be overplayed in every skill bracket untill they get nerfed to the point of irrelevance. Then there are some heroes that are just absolutely broken and shouldn't be in the game to begin with because no matter how many times they are nerfed or their counters get buffed they manage to find a way in.   Also, the game has an extreme learning curve but no utillities in present to help you learn it. The tutorial shows you the most basic information the could've figured out by just playing the game however it does look nice. The bots aren't programmed particularily well, but they may show you some things that the tutorial did not care to mention.   The most irratating part of the game is that there is no region lock, so in a communcation teambased game (and i've had this happen before) you may get up to 5 different langauges on the same team.   Most of the time its dysfunctional numbing mess that makes you wonder if you have too much free time like me.  Positives  If you have the patience, and the iron will to grind for competent teammates and try to have a positive attitude the game is actually quite fun. It offers a good challenge, and despite win or loose some games really are 'gg' (good game). When both teams have 'meta' heroes that can fight each other equally it becomes a real good time. Working as a team to accomplish a goal, and playing well really has a fulfilling feel to it and if you like that, you may want to try this game.  While the game is scattered interms of balance, each hero can be fun in its own way, and each certainly different to it. Some heroes act fairly simillar, but even then they each have their own feel that makes each worth trying out atleast once.   Individual playstyle in certain heroes is also highlighted by this uniqueness and your personality and how you play often shines through by what you play the most.   For instance i'm a prick so i play heroes that display this through underhanded and dirty tatics. In contrast one of friends who plays mostly nice characters that do simple things is a pretty nice guy.   All in all this is like the cheap hooker of mobas, she may look nasty and she probably got some diseases but shes experienced in the job and no matter how many STD's she contracts she'll be always for a good time every once in a while.

'Crusader Kings II' is a grand strategy game by Paradox Interactive and is one of the best in the genre (see also 'Europa Universalis IV').  Crusader Kings could be described as some kind of 'dynasty simulator', it effectively puts you in the shoes of a member of a family of your choice: Emperor, King, Karling, Duke, Khalif... whatever you want to start with, of course, if you pick a minor title, the challenge of rising to the top will be greater, as will be the reward.  You will have to keep an eye on your neighboring kingdoms while also watching your own relatives and allies so you don't get backstabbed. You will also learn fun history stuff like feudalism and succession laws while you create your own dynasty that will last thru the centuries.  Game of Thrones fans will also like the CKII mod inspired by the books, also recommended.

If you like simulation/strategy games this one is a great addition to your collection. Another huge bonus is a LOT of user generated content. I believe there are full on Game of Thrones conversion mods and things of that nature. My only complaint was playing a nation like England, summoning troops and getting them to on ships its very tedious.

Step into the shoes of Dr.Neil Watts and Dr.Eva Rosalene, two employees of the Sigmund Corporation, whose occupation consists of sending the elderly through self-generated blissful memories before their final moments. This is done through the use of technology developed by the Sigmund Corporation to alter one's memories and allowing them to experience events that may not have actually occurred in their patient's life. Their current patient, an elderly man who goes by the name of Johnny, wishes to go to the moon. However, his reasons are unknown, even to himself, which begins a race against time for our two doctors to determine Johnny's reason for wanting to go to the moon and to do so before his last breath.  'To The Moon' is no technical marvel, running on the 16-bit RPG Maker XP engine, it falls short of most modern games. As such, the controls are quite stiff at times and intrusive when trying to navigate around the game world. The game also offers very little in terms of gameplay, the most being a simple minigame which is done to bridge and traverse across Johnny's memories. The game's length is short but reasonable and can be completed in a few hours at most. With that being the case, the game has minor replay value, except for any replay value imposed by the player themselves.  Even with the aforementioned limitations, 'To The Moon' manages to deliver a heart warming experience. The soundtrack does a wonderful job of setting the atmosphere through Johnny's good and bad memories. The art style is reminiscent of older RPG's from around the SNES to GBA era. The banter between Dr.Watts and Dr.Rosalene is humorous and Dr.Watts sprinkles some pop-culture references throughout the game like the 'Hadoken' and 'Kamehameha' to keep the mood light despite their dire circumstances.   'To The Moon' is far from a perfect game, but what it sets out to do, it does incredibly well. Overall, a highly recommended game if you're in the mood for a quick game with the aesthetics of an old school RPG and a story that manages to tug at the heart in all the right places.

This is the first game in my library I do not recommend.  It is not the game from the advertising video. It doesn't feel like a micromachines / childhood mini cars experience. I rushed to buy the game but then I was dissapointed (there were no steam refunds at the time, 2013).  I have had controller issues since day one. Have tried with different controllers and all of them feels different, work or don't and have to configure them every time. Even when controls are mapped, the game plays awful. Physics will make sure you get in the last place. Forget about using turbo, you'll ruin your hard earned place. If you manage to be in 1st place for the first 15 seconds then you have a chance of winning the race but if your opponent is 10 seconds away from you in a better place then just restart the race, it is impossible to catch up.  I did however managed to run the game when I recently purchased it with one controller (it worked and felt really well) and this is when I could play the game (and used to recommend it as a casual racer) but very little time then the controller died on me and had to rely on mouse or keyboard (mouse is the best alternative so far but keyboard is too laggy). I've been struggling with the game ever since, trying different controllers every now and then but none of them seem to work like the first one, maybe it is the latest patch that brought like a new controller backend but instead it messed it up.  The level editor is really good though, have spent many hours building custom tracks.  In conclusion, it is a good casual racing game if you are tolerant to frustration but it is not a game for you if you do not like to lose.

If you've ever looked to a game for large battles with innumerable players and vehicles, Planetside 2 is right for you. Games like Battlefield boast about their epic 64 player battles running smoothly, but Planetside blows this out of the water.   Before Planetside, I had always looked for an FPS that required teamwork and coordination to succeed. Other games may have had some level of this, but none surpassed the level needed to succeed in Planetside. It is not uncommon to find yourself in a platoon cooridinating with over 100 other players in a firefight against another 100 players.  While Planetside may take some time to build up your character (unlocking equipment and vehicle upgrades etc) the time invested is definitely worth it in the long run if you plan on playing a lot. You will also need a decent computer and solid internet connection as well if you want to compete in the large battles.  Overall, I definitely reccomend this game to anyone looking for an FPS that strays from the typical mutiplayer experience.

This is a great co-op zombie experience. Not the best, or the most fun, but certainly standing out on its own. This game is a slap in the testes for new players and doesn't really get easier for veterans. It's based on the original Night of the Living Dead/Dawn of the Dead/Whatever* of the Dead movies by George A. Romero (the title refers to a quote from Dawn of the Dead, directly, and one survival map is based on the location of the original NOTLD movie).  To start off, the game is primarily co-op. You really can't play this singleplayer like Left 4 Dead (believe me, I tried. It's improbable.) and you will have to rely on your teammates. You will have to stick together and work cooperatively, checking your corners, watching your back, and making sure nobody falls behind.  The game features two modes. Objective; where the players complete objectives to advance while a innumerable amount of zombies stand in their way, and Survival, where players defend safe zones while holding off hordes of undead.  The player is limited on how much they can carry, including firearms, melee weapons, supplies, tools, and ammunition. Often, tools are required in objective modes to advance. Supplies, such as bandages, stop deep wounds. First aid kits heal damage, and Pills stop infection.  Speaking of infection, one feature of the game is the ever-suspenseful infection. On the off-chance a player is bitten, they are infected with the virus and slowly turn into a zombie unless they have pills they can take to kill (or at least slow) the infection. If not, the player changes into a faster, more recently infected zombie, much to the danger of teammates. The question often asks you of whether or not to scour the map to help your teammate, or put him down so not to risk yourself.  Firearms are easy to adapt to, but the player may want to keep track of how much ammunition he has. Ammunition is very scarce, and although enough body shots can (usually) kill a zombie, it is ineffective because of the amount of ammo required and how scarce ammunition is. Headshots are a must.  Melee combat is difficult, and the player will risk themselves getting attacked at close range. Some weapons are more powerful, heavier, than others, but clearly require more strength, which can tire the player out quickly.   Onto design and criticism, the game's environments are linear and and enclosed, but the amount of detail in environments is excellent. The game has a couple bugs (on some occasions, you may get stuck, or an objective will become impossible to complete. A crucial instrument in the level's advancement may not spawn, or may disappear.) The melee combat is flawed. I could attribute it to the difficulty of the game, but it can be frustrating. You might miss a swing if you haven't directly aimed at the zombie. You can't hit with reflexes. Each swing or strike has a 'charge' time, and a recover time. It's generic. And although ammo is very scarce, you can find guns rather often.  some players might criticize the amount of zombies, the scarcity of ammo, the frustration and hopelessness of Infection, or the difficulty of combat, but it contributes to a game like this, where the point is not a generic, chaotic Zombie Slaughter FPS.  Regardless, it stands out as a suspensful, scary, fun, and incredibly difficult game.

Being an older gamer I thought I'd enjoy the challenge of a turn-based RPG, but the game is much weaker than Might and Magic games released 10+ years ago. As an added negative, the game should run/load incredibly fast as it does not have fluid movement from AI programmed nasties like a majority of games nowadays; yet the programmers failed miserably and the game loads/lags well beyond what it should (a rough guess would be it runs at 5% of what it should comparing it to other graphically intense newly released games played on the same system with similiar settings. If you are immensely bored, find it for 99 cents or less, and a glutton for punishment buy it; otherwise get anything else.

You know what's worse than not trying? Succeeding well for 90% of the task and then flopping miserably at the end, and that is sadly what the Director's Cut did. The gameplay is identical in every way to the original version and is enjoyable as always; the only major change (in the story mode) is a refined 'story,' which as been added solely by the addition of voiceovers. What's amazing is that they actually manage to set up a fairly interesting story, rife with contradictions (trying to remain spoiler-free here). Unfortunately, when the entire game builds up a dichotomy of impressions, making you guess what is true and real, at the end, it falls flat. You aren't given a choice, you're force to pick one of the impressions as 'true,' and the game unambiguously shows that you were correct. This isn't even the ILLUSION of choice, this is shoe-horning us in to a meaningless ending. It's like playing a puzzle game and simultaneously watching a completely unrelated movie; on the one hand you're playing a game, and on the other merely along for the ride as a dispassionate observer. The protagonist's doubt isn't your own, because you don't ever act on it. You're forced to just sit back and watch as he picks correctly. As such, I can only say that you should play the non-director's cut version, 'cause honestly the game was better when it lacked story than when it insulted its players with this.

Ever thought, 'Gee, it'd be fun to make my own game!' while playing something in your Steam library?  Game Dev Tycoon will give you all the fun parts of making your own game, without the stress, debt and strain on your relationships! Though you'll still have to deal with criticism from the (in-game) media & fans, watching your favorite idea go down in flames, and watch your plans go completely off the rails.  Honestly, this is just a fantastic little game. There's some nostalgia for older gamers, as you get to create games-that-never-were for your favorite 70s & 80s consoles and computers. You have to balance a desire for the hottest new tech against the realities of the market & your budget. I've honestly never made it past the point of having a small company with three or four employees, but even that early in it's a fantastically fun experience.  Get this if you want a game that's easy enough to drop into for ten or fifteen minutes when you don't have time for other things. Get this if you want a game you'll spend hours playing, trying to build your gaming empire and create the best business you can. Get this just because it's damn fun.

Shadow of Mordor is fun and frustrating both.  At the heart of Monolith's impressive looking action-adventure is the Nemesis System, a unique AI mechanic that has enemies that kills you be promoted to higher ranks (such as captains and war chiefs) and gain more buffs. The more you die, the powerful they get and become harder to kill. Each captain will have their own strengths and weakness, which you must exploit to gain an advantage. For example, a captain could killed by a stealth finisher (which is the best kind of finisher, trust me) but he might be invulnerable to ranged attacks and normal combat attacks. In this case, it would be foolish to confront him upfront and engage in a duel; more likely than not you will be ganged up by 30 enemies pounding you with all kinds of powerful attacks. Instead, it would be wise to track the enemy and then pounce on him from a higher ground or sneak up on him when he is not looking.  Nemesis System is the backbone of Shadow of Mordor, a true example of what sheer outside-of-the-boxing thinking can do and the ability to exploit the more processing power that is now available on consoles and PCs both. However, it can also be frustrating, and to someone like me who is less skilled in these kind of games, a game breaker. At times the captains have mind-boggling weaknesses that cannot be exploited unless you luck out (such as a captain may only be weak to a certain type of creature attack, but where do you find that creature? Who the hell knows?) Other times, when you have worked hard in dwindling down a captain's health, you will suddenly find yourself facing against may be one, or two, or even three other captains, who pop in to say hello before promising to take your head off as a prize. This skews the difficulty to near impossible levels without warning, and then you must either show deft skill in killing nearly 30-40 enemies plus all the captains at once, or run away and do the mission once again.   If you can look past that, Shadow of Mordor is a satisfying action-adventure title although the Assassin's Creed-like controls leave a lot to be desired (Assassin's Creed games already handle poorly!). The Batman-esque melee-based combat is incredibly visceral and engaging too, and when you are not bogged down by clunky controls (it's downright frustrating with the keyboard and mouse), it is an impressive show of gory kills and stylized acrobatics.   I am only half way through the story, so I cannot comment on it that much although my friend tells me it ends well and sets up with a franchise in the making. From what I have played, the story is mildly interesting with impressive performances from Troy Baker and the actor who plays Gollum - absolutely stunning portrayal of the pesky little bugger.  Verdict: Shadow of Mordor is an awesome title only held down by its poor controls and unpredictable difficulty. Beyond that, it is a fun game worth playing.

Game was extremely frustrating at first, only because I'm not used to playing this type of combat style. This game actually got me to play Dark Souls just to see the connection everyone was talking about, and I'll agree it definitely was inspired, but its so different from DS that honestly the only relevant link I can make between them is the combat style. A few key things to note about this game would be:  - Every action you take animation locks you. Light weapons like daggers/fists are very short locks if you don't get snagged in a combo attack, and larger weapons like greatswords and maces have a longer lock (approx. 3-4 seconds per special attack swing). This is also effected by the type of armor you're wearing. Wielding Heirloom (elemental hammer) and wearing all light armor I was able to swing and dodge attacks significantly faster.  - Know your enemies! This game is 90% about knowing your enemies and how to handle them. This isn't the type of game where you get in and steam roll through trash mobs to the boss. You need to wait for your enemies to give you an opening and move in for the attack. The more you play this game & the more you die, the more you'll get better at noting openings in your enemies. Once you get the timing down to make hits in the sweet spots, the enemies drop like flies.  - Leveling. I found it insanely helpful to level grind for a while in order to progress to the area past the 3rd boss easier. The good news is that there's an experience modifier you gain from killing multiple enemies in a zone without going back to the crystal and healing. This modifier makes it insanely easy to level up and I recommend taking full advantage of it. (side note: bank your experience when you get the chance, it won't reset the modifier and you'll be thankful you didn't have it on you when that 1 mob decided to eat your face off in an area way far off from your last crystal touch).  Things I would like to see them improve on with this game:  - Combat system: swing/hit lag. There's been numerous times in a fight where I got hit by a swing that was actually 4 feet away from my face, as if the weapon has an invisible hit box on it that extends past the actual model of the weapon. There's also the enemies that swivel around to cornhole you when they should be stuck in an animation lock.  - Combat system: heaviness. I enjoy how heavy combat feels here, but to an extent. I've fought quite a few enemies wielding a huge great axe with a heavy shield on that come at you swinging a mile a minute AND have time to swivel on a heel turn and block your attack with that big shield that begs you to question why this is kosher when it takes you 3-4 seconds to make that swing and sticks you in a deadly animation lock. If combat is going to be this heavy I would highly recommend making sure all the monsters suffer the same penalty.  - Expansion to multiplayer/co-op. I really, really hope to see this game develop further into a state of being where other players can come in as other characters and assist. I realize at the moment everyone plays as Harkyn, so it's a bit unlikely, but this game has a ton going for it in all the right places and I would love to get the opportunity to play it with friends instead of having them watch me play. This game starts out a little raw in the beginning, so it'd be nice to have the ability to walk through it with a friend that's new to it and encourage them to keep going/show them the ropes.  - Map. This game would have benefitted GREATLY from a map or at least a compass that points you towards lost experience points like the loot compass if you're really against maps for forcing exploration (I'd argue there's no reason you can't just have people unlock the map by exploration as they go though). I had no problem memorizing where to go and how to get there after a few runs of zones, but whenever I died I wasn't particularly paying attention to the exact location I was in and I'd have to run around for a while to figure out where I dropped exp. There was also a time where I died at the entrance and dropped 6k, but when I respawned the experience wasn't there, even though I still had 100% (6,438) on the screen waiting for me to pick it up. I had to just take a dive because I had no clue where it put it.  Comments:  - I would have loved to see the story developed a little further. We know very little about Harkyn, but are continuously reminded of his reputation. This may be intentional, leaving the player to their own imagination, but it makes it feel a bit impersonal. Also, the ending was....disappointing. I just wasn't really ever sold on this game story-wise. I played it for the challenge and the audio clips were intriguing to say the least, but that was about it. It could have benefitted from a little more world immersion, cutscenes, and an ending that's better than just a re-cap of what you accomplished and a snippet of story about Adyr.  The ending feels rushed honestly, or like someone got burnt out and just needed to meet a deadline. It bums me out because of how awesome the game was otherwise -- but perhaps we'll see more great titles that improve upon this? :)  I would love to see some worthy content addition that adds more areas to play in so we're not stuck in just this one castle. While there is one other area you gain access too, most of the game is played just in a single area with multiple zones you're entering into. It's huge, don't get me wrong, but I would love to see more.  TLDR: I highly recommend this game and will be continuing to play with ng+ and ng++. It frustrates the crap out of me when I die but, I'm constantly challenging myself to beat bosses without taking any damage at all or meeting legendary requirements to make it harder on myself and taking advantage of every slow-mo death blow I deal while adding a 'BAM' vocally for enhanced enjoyment, as is mandatory. Game takes a while to warm into if you'r'e new to it's kind, but stick with it and you'll get your monies worth.

EDIT: what I wrote in the 2 first paragraphs was written after about 10 hours in the game. I finally managed to get past the game freezing bug I mention below and finished my first playthrough of the game. As I feel more positive after having completed it for the first time, I though I would add a couple of lines. Basically the part between double brackets can be put a bit aside as it only mentions a negative aspect.  ((I really enjoyed my first ten hours of the game and didn't encounter any game-breaking bugs. And then suddenly for no apparent reason I reached a point of the game where my game keeps crashing. Basically I'm unable to proceed any further and have to give up until it gets a patch I suppose.  Seeing as I am not the only player (far from) that encountered game-breaking bugs, I really wonder if the PC version of this game was correctly tested. What a shame that the developers neglected this, because it is a game with some potential.))  UPDATED REVIEW AFTER 1ST PLAYTHROUGH: The game breaking bug I mentionned above was an encounter with 2 little 'fire guys' where my game always froze. I basically just skipped them in the end and managed to complete the game.  Now what is my final thought? If you remove the multiple crashes I had (some of them at the worst possible moment during a boss fight) I believe Lords of the Fallen is a game with a very solid foundation. While it's gameplay is not as deep as that of its spiritual example (less possible builds, skills, etc...) it allows for a very smooth playing style. When the game didn't crash the action was very fluent and I was able to really time my attacks in a way I feel is satisfactory. Fighting the different mobs brought me a lot of fun and entertainement, which is obviously why I play video games.  While the boss fights were way too easy to complete, I never really disliked a fight. Although I strongly believe Lords of the Fallen is in no way as hardcore and challenging as it would like to present itself. If I remove the time I lost due to crashes I think I have beaten the game in about 13 hours, which isn't that much. Off course there is NG+ and I will also start a fresh character, but I would like it to be more challenging.  As a conclusion I can state that this is a game that does a lot of things very well and I strongly hope it will get a sequel that pays attention to the negative aspects of this game. While I first said I wouldn't recommend it, I now changed this to YES, because I think the game deserves your attention.

Reminds me a bit of Hex-Mines, a hexagonal version of classic Minesweeper. There aren't any mines (you lose points for guessing wrong, but you don't die), the levels are hand-built rather than randomly generated, and there are some very novel mechanics introduced with each group of levels.  Pros:  Good game mechanics Well-made puzzles, all of which are solvable without guesswork  Cons:  Few levels and no player-created or periodic content, and thus little replay value.  Summary:  If you like puzzle games, snag this on a sale for about $1. It'll last you about 2 hours.  After looking around, it turns out the game has two sequels which expand on the game mechanics and add randomly-generated levels.

Dodgeball is fun. Dodgeball whilst controlling unicycle robots and raving simultaneously and having a monstertruck announcer hype up everything you do is insanely fun.  On a more serious (as serious as you can get for this game) note, the game is well built and very well polished overall. There are several game modes ranging from Deathmatches and dodgeball game Elimination to 'Super Ball', where one of you has to keep a golden dodgeball from the other team and keep it away from them as long as possible, whilst your teammates protect you.  There are numerous singleplayer modes as well, despite it being mutiplayer focused (as you would expect) and the bots are surprisingly good (or I just suck :P).  In terms of skill, the balls move quite slow as projectiles (relative to most twitch-reaction FPS games) and so there is a fair bit of skill leading shots. Not only that, in point-based games, extra points are given for ricochet shots, mid-air shots etc. etc. Also, it does take some getting use to the jump (hold down longer for bigger jump) and the fact you are uncontrollable, movementwise, when airborne (I hope you have decent momentum buddy!). But its not that bad to adjust, and the feeling you get when you do something awesome, intended or not, not matter how much it has happened before, is euphoric. And then you get the monstertruck guy massively hyping you up. Because he's awesome too.  There are also powerups, which do various things such as homing shots, jetpacks, fireballs (balls can't be blocked or caught) etc. Because the game wasn't exciting/silly enough already.  Its truly is great. Its silly appeal (robot dodgeball robot rave) doesn't seem to wear off, and regardless, it is a well built FPS game. It can appeal to those who are casual or competitive, because everything is a 1-hit KO.  Also, robots have mustaches and glasses.  tl:dr version: Robots. Dodgeball. Rave. Fun. Monstertruck Announcer. Hype. Awesomeness. Euphoria.  I really really hope this game grows a good multiplayer scene, because it deserves it 100%. The dev recognises this and has already tried to help it along as much as possible, with easy to join multiplayer matches.  RRDDD (Robot Roller-Derby Disco Dodgeball) everyone.  (Side note: I have an Alienware laptop: So my laptop follows the colourscheme of the game. My laptop raves whilst running the game. Completely superfluous, but great nonetheless.)

An absolutely amazing space 4X game that brings some new twists and life to the genre.   Before I go on, know that there are some issues with the game as it is now. Late game ends up a bit lacking, and the interface needs some work. Diplomatic communication and AI needs a small upgrade too.   BUT, don't think the game is unplayable if these things worry you. If you pick up this game, my advice to you, is grab your favorite sci-fi, and roleplay it. This game is unprecedented on the feeling it gives you as the leader of your empire. It IS your empire, and it IS your guys that are being badass.   It's kinda hard to put it in words, but you are going to end up immersed and that familiar 'One more turn' feeling that sucks you down the rabbit hole, will hit you HARD.   As it is now, this game is one of my all time favorites, right up there with the classics, and it's only going to get better. Paradox has always had extensive support with updates and fantastic DLC after launch. And you can mod this game with the same fervor you mod Skyrim, just waiting on those total conversion mods, yum.   So yes. Recommended with high marks.

This is an RTS, but unlike others, it seems to be focused on multi play. there is resource sharing. you can play any 'tech level' right away depending on your style of play, communicate startegies to friendly players, and select starting positions before gameplay. the economy is forgiving. units seem to have little health, but don't take a long time to build. the maps are not huge, so be ready to fight fast. structures do seem to be harder to take down. defences are key. like in other rts's, expand or suficate. get to the the other resource points and don't let them go without a fight. get recon to know your enemies weak points and fly behind their defences for effective airstrikes. like the game description mentions, mixed units are more effective than just one kind.

VC is a great experience, and I'm glad the port to PC was so good because I'd been eyeing it for a long time from afar. Good music, good strategy and to an extent challenge, good character and machine designs, great original fantasy WWI/II setting, gorgeous art direction and style that honestly I'd be okay with other games aping- It's not an accident that this did so well on steam.  There are negatives, too- the tone of the narrative is pretty inconsistent and basically childish, especially in the extra missions, a lot of the later conversations are cringeworthy and the antagonist might as well marker 'evil ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥' on their face. There's not a heck of a lot of subtlety, at any rate. Some of the missions kind of suck or drag (There's a pretty strange difficulty curve here), or aren't clear on what you're doing until you've played it a few times to know. Once you undestand rankings are based almost purely on speed and that spending all of your CP on orders for one person is usually ridiculously viable the game may lose some of that great strategic element. A lot of the upgrade options aren't really options, like the mortar lances that you should pretty much never ever use.  The flaws, however, are minor compared to the novel strategy and action hyrbrid systems, the underused setting and aesthetic, the satisfying headshot 'clunk' noises when you don't screw it up, and the wonderfully exaggerated ragdolling when your characters are shot down. The main characters are probably likable enough, and as offputting as it is it's also kind of great in a corny way to have the main heroine flash a smile and pump her fist each time she ends someone's life- which she will be doing a lot.  Speaking of which, the many characters you control have a pretty good spread of voices and eccentricities. The positive and negative qualities like fear of heights or working better when far from the group help give them personality beyond being Engineer # 6. although most of these being negatives or more trouble than they're worth could have been worked on a bit.  At any rate, Valkyria Chronicles is the kind of game I'd probably try to complete sometime despite the backlog. Good on sega for bringing this to steam- too bad the newest developing title in the series looks like it might kill it.

Up front, I'll acknowledge that I only played the game for less than an hour, but versus MOO2 and other available games I felt I had already found enough to be done with this game.  - Star lanes. The game doesn't let your ships fly just wherever you'll order them to go within a maximum range. Instead, there are definite paths between star systems that the game chooses for you. You must follow them, creating a strategic layout that is remarkably worse than MOO2 offered.  - Simplified interface. The game feels like it's been streamlined excessively. The strategic depth and decision making that you'll find in other 4Xs, like Space Empires IV, Civilization IV & 5 just aren't here.

It's exactly what it looks like. A good idea that's been hammered and melded and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ized by the publisher to resemble the games that were all the rage in 2007. The cover based third person shooter. The modern military shooter. More zombies yay.  The thing is the concept alone was enough to bring this on a slightly higher level. It's a ♥♥♥♥ port, so that brings it back down.  Still, if you play and enjoy warframe, this should shed some light on quite a few things. After all, Warframe is the game Digital Extremes were trying to make when they made this one. I personally LOVE warframe, so seeing little recognizable things here and there keep me playing. It's--It's kinda like the videogame equivalent of Agents of Shield. It's the content you go to the movies for, but filered through layers upon layers of bureaucracy and rushed out the door... But the chunks of the cool stuff the creators wanted to create make the whole thing a bit more special.   What's the moral of the story? Publishers ruin everything.  tl;dr: If you like Warframe, play it. If you like Gears of War, play it. If you have a soft spot for the early last gen titles of 2007, which was arguably the golden year of that gen, give this a shot. If you're not tied to any of these, this game will do absolutely nothing for you.

People either really likes or really dislikes this game, I have never met a person that said it was 'OK'. It's either 'an amazing game' or 'a terrible game, not like Skyrim at all'. And I think that those who dislikes the game was looking for Skyrim with friends, something that this game would never be (they said from the start that it was going to ba an MMO, and that exactly what they gave us). The ones that like the game was looking for an MMO, and this game is a unique type of MMO. I hear people say that this isn't Skyrim online, I'm here to tell you that they are right. It's so much more! This game has the gates of Oblivion with the Sigil Stones ans Scamps that follows with it, Ayleid Ruins with traps and Welkynd Stones and the Shivering Isles with Haskill and Sheogorath. If you ever played Oblivion, then this game holds a bunch of nostalgia for you. I couldn't stop laughing when I fist saw Haskill, all I could think about was the Summon Haskill spell you get from the Shivering Isles quest line in The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion. This game also contains the Dwemer Ruins with the Dwarven Spiders and the Dwarven Spheres, the old dragon burial mounds and the Nordic Ruins with Draugrs and puzzles. Almost everything you can do in The Elders Scrolls V: Skyrim, you can do in this game. And you can do a whole lot that you can't do in Skyrim. This game has content from all of the Elder Scrolls games, but I have only played Oblivion and Skyrim so that's all I know :P If you are looking for Skyrim online, you're not going to find it here. But if you are looking for a unique MMO, with content from all the Elder Scrolls games and endless possibilities in character builds and playing styles, this is the game for you! In my opinion, this game is better than Skyrim. This game is AMAZING!

Admittedly, my opinion of TRON 2.0 is mixed. Part of the reason I give the game a pass is because I'm a huge TRON fanboy. It's a shallow reason certainly, but what can I say? This game knows how to play upon my nostalgia.   The plot is unnecessarily convoluted. There's a hostile corporate takeover, data-wraiths, algorithms, corrupted programs, tons of callbacks to the movie, and a lot of other nonsense. All you really need to know is that you're zapped into the computer world. This is a classic-styled FPS, with multiple stages, some exploration, and various equipment upgrades to collect. In keeping with the original movie's theme, everything is a reference to computers & the internet. One of the villains is known as the Kernel, you use a sniper rifle called an LOL, and...ugh...you get the idea.  As with most other FPS titles, TRON 2.0 provides numerous weapons. However, they give you the best one right at the beginning. The disc works in just about any situation (even boss-fights), and it's entirely possible to beat the game using it. Get the sequencer upgrade and you can throw multiple discs at once, which is practically game-breaking. The rest of the weapons are usually situational, such as using LOL to protect a program from far away. Still, there is some value to trying everything out, because ammo is constantly available.  Aside from the usual 'run around, collect keys, and kill stuff' levels, there are also light-cycles. The light-cycle is based off of one of the mini-games from the arcade version of TRON. The goal is to make your opponents crash into your jetwalls. The differences (aside from better graphics and camera controls) include power-ups and some changes in the mechanics, such as speeding up when you're close to a jetwall. The AI tends to be amazing in their inconsistency. Either they pull off a wicked stunt and blow you away, or they crash into a wall when you're a mile away. All told, it's a good addition to the game. It provides a nice break, and doesn't become overbearing.   For the most part the game is merely alright. The mechanics are solid enough and the levels are reasonably-paced. Unfortunately there are some aspects that don't really work. Take for example, the enemy variety. One constant that I've noticed is everyone attacks in groups. You'll fight some corrupted programs, and then some ICPs. You'll tangle with some ICPs, and then some data-wraiths. You'll attack some resource hogs, and then some ICPs. Each enemy has their own weapon, but they never mix things up. For example: you won't fight a corrupted alongside an ICP. There are story reasons for this I'm aware, but it makes for less intricate battles.   Continuing on the issues with combat, I can't overlook that a large number of enemies, just aren't fun to fight. The corrupted throw around tons of splash-damage weapons that ruin your equipment, forcing you to run anti-virus programs to fix them. Data-wraiths are a nuisance due to their camo and rapid-fire weapons. There are also seekers that can hit you from anywhere, as long as you're visible to them. It's also unfortunate that if you can get the drop on a crowd, the battle is always yours. Prolonged fights are few and far between. This is also why I like using the disc so much. The disc-play is really well-done. You can block enemy discs, exploit openings, and quickly wipe out adversaries. The few boss-fights in the game are pretty weak, especially the kernel.  Despite my issues with the game, I thought it was enjoyable enough. The music is really good, the visuals still hold up rather well, and a remarkable amount of love is shown for fans of the movie. However, you might not be willing to put up with a confusing story, somewhat dull combat, and potential quicksave abuse. The heavy fall damage and platforming can result in a number of cheap deaths. Treat this game as an experience and you'll derive a bit of enjoyment out of it. Just don't go in expecting too much.  Oh and definitely grab the Killer App mod.

Tell you what. Here's a quick(ish) rundown of why this game is good, why you should buy it, and with as utterly few spoilers as humanly possible.  The atmosphere is unreal, as the colors are so vibrant without ♥♥♥♥♥♥ your eyes and the music is so melodic.   The characters have depth and the game manages to put each one into dozens of different emotional states in a small amount of time without ♥♥♥♥ing up the pacing.   The designs of the characters are detailed enough to be differentiated but simple enough to be memorable. The designs of characters often stay in sync with their personality.   Nothing in the game outstays it's welcome Not till you're so late game you're totally invested at least.  Characters genuinely want to be kept around by the player because you want to see more of their writing, not just because you want a good ending.  The story is easy to understand and you're fed info at a consistent rate.  The game isn't particularly repetitive, it has a really high awesome per minute, with new, funny or shocking content being presented constantly, but never shoving a player into anything immediately, always giving them time to settle in.   Characters you're supposed to care about or trust prove their personalities through gameplay instead of words.  Above all of this are tons of far more complex pros that I can't go into without spoiling the splendor of the moments.  Finally, it's just really damn funny.  Not gonna lie, it just follows the rules of design and does everything right, but with its own style and creativity. Building off the shoulders of giants but not directly taking from anything, the game is a loveletter to anybody that's ever enjoyed a single smidgeon of an RPG. Buy it.

At least they fixed the audio in Bioshock 1. Haven't checked 2 yet.  Lazy port. Lazy remaster. The textures are better. It's generally visually improved. What isn't improved?  - FOV is still so low I wouldn't play it on my phone. The hotkey workarounds still work but Flawless Widescreen would be better. I suppose I'll wait to see if someone makes it work for this version of the game.  - Mouse input is still broken.  - Lots of other bugs still present, including some new ones. It keeps setting itself to gamepad mode, for example.  - Few game options.  - Audio balance is bad and it is very quiet.  If you're going to remaster a game, fix the damned game. The FOV and mouse input issues should have been fixed in a patch for the original game many years ago. Why are they still not fixed in a remaster? It's embarrassing that they let this happen.

Big survival horror (RE/SH) fan here. Game has a lot of fundamental issues such as enemy AI (easily killed because they are stuck). Setting up the controller is a challenge as the default settings don't appear to work for anything other than possibly the PS4 controller. If you are using an XB1 controller you can start the game with KB/M and then go into the OPTIONS while IN THE GAME. There you can move around your controller settings. I believe I had the layout of the steam contoller and the Dpad layout of the DS4 and it was working alright. Your notes/info section only holds the info for the last item you inspected (which will include a locked door) and you can't view older files found. Ended up writing down puzzles with pen and paper (by my counter, there were only 2-3 puzzles). Using weapons other than the wrench was difficult in the sense that the function buttons didnt work correctly. The gun would randomly shoot when I only used the aim button, and I had to keep hitting aim on/off to shoot additional shots. Unless there is a hidden ending, I clocked 1 1/2 hours to finish the game. Going to hit my save file to check another few portions of the game out as 2 achievements are still 'hidden'. Really let down so far with this game.

While Spec Ops is a difficult game to recommend based on its mediocre gameplay, performance issues, and frustrating controls the story packs a real punch and is the sole highlight of the game. The 'Do you recommend this game, Yes : No' is very limiting here because it depends. If you are looking for a good game this isn't it. If you are looking for one with a great story that is a military game no less than you found it. In this 'I'm offended' generation where someone always has to be offended by something and demand its removal the devs went all out and didn't care who they offended. They had a message to send and pulled no punches.  Game wise Spec Ops The Line suffers. I was constantly fighting with the controls and couldn't help but be bored to death whenever the shooting started thanks to the mediocre mechanics and bad AI. It came to the point where I was trying to rush through the game just so I could be done with it and that's a shame because it somewhat took away from the impact of the story.   The visuals are average and the aliasing hurt my eyes but it runs worse than Crysis 3. On a rig equipped with a 3770K, 16 Gigs of RAM, and a GTX 780ti I was getting frequent stutters, shaky mouse movements, and framerate drops.   With that said the creators of Spec Ops The Line clearly had a vision and they must have fought tooth and nail to make it happen. What starts off as a cliché military shooter ends up as something far more. The story is grim, depressing, bold, and a big middle finger to military games which rely on unquestioning patriotism and make war seem like it's fun and cool. It is not afraid to tackle the horrors of war and PTSD. It's designed to make you feel uncomfortable and it's about time a military game goes this route.  It's a shame then that there isn't a good game here to compliment the work put in by the writers. I am glad to have experienced Spec Ops The Line but it is not something I can easily recommend.

Presentation  Steam Sales are always a great way to buy more games than you'll ever be able to play, but between all the big purchases you will often find some random games to keep you busy when there's nothing else; Space Trader: Merchant Marine is exactly that kind of game, but unlike most small, indie games this one managed somehow to keep me around more than it should have, just because at its core, it's an addictive experience. The premise of the game is simple: you are a new trader trying to find your way in this business and since you don't own a ship yet you need to find someone who is willing to give you one. What follows is a simple story and you will see the little twists coming from miles away, but even with that in mind, the missing excitement of what's going to happen next isn't the thing that will keep you around. The campaign has only five chapters and once you understand the mechanics of the game you will have no problem making millions from trading food stamps, oil or even organs. This game simulates the stocks trades perfectly, following the same: buy low, sell high premise, and even though at first it may be hard to figure out how to make all those millions, once you get the idea, it will be only profit for you from that point on. There's no real reward for doing this except the fact that once you reach a goal you will advance to the next chapter and start all over, trading between different planets, but that little sense of advancement is what will keep you playing.  Rating: 5.5    Gameplay  The gameplay in Space Trader is pretty straight forward: you start on a planet and you have to buy commodities that will bring a profit and travel to another planet to sell them at a higher price, keeping a constant watch on the time limit that each mission has, calculating the profit per shipment and the travel time in order to choose the best option. Finding good deals is easy since those always have a gold star indicating that you should buy them, so eventually it becomes just a matter of having enough money in your pocket or enough cargo space, and your only enemy becomes the time as you carefully have to choose where to sell the stuff you just bought. You can make money by taking bounty missions from bartenders or by completing small tasks for different people, so it's always a good idea to talk with everybody because you might find out some interesting things or take on some missions that will give you some extra cash. The shooting mechanic feels old, but that's probably why it feels so addictive at the same time, as you will most likely instantly connect the dots between those shooting sequences and games like Doom, and even though all those bounty missions will get boring pretty soon as each map looks and feels the same and there are only two to three different types of enemies plus the target that you're supposed to assassinate, it is a good distraction from the boring buy and sell mechanic that the game generally delivers.  Rating: 6    Graphics  If you look at this game you will assume it was released in the early 2000's because it looks like it was made a generation ago; from the simple and stiff characters to the environments everything looks pretty bad and the repetitive level design doesn't help either. By the end of the game you will know every corner of each planet, you will start to love a certain section for how easy it is to complete a combat mission and you will hate another for how difficult it is.  Rating: 5    Audio  The sound in this game ain't exactly bad, but it's simply mediocre at best. The voice acting is horrible, but the soundtrack manages to save it sometimes, especially in the heat of a battle, but otherwise there is nothing that stands out, it simply gets boring as you will end up ignoring the other characters or laugh at how bad they sound no matter the serious, story essential thing they just said. There are intentional comic lines too, such as the robot that answers by saying WTF, but those only make the entire thing feel worse, as the game is undecided between trying to be serious and making fun of itself.  Rating: 5    Extras  After completing the main campaign you can also try the challenge mode that offers three extra missions that are pretty easy to complete and the only real challenge comes when you try to get the achievements, and if you're into that the game can get interesting and it has even some decent replay value as you can spend up to 18 hours struggling to complete everything. It's also supposed to have a multiplayer mode, which puts you and a friend in a race to get the most cash from those challenge missions, but after an hour of banging my head against the wall I couldn't figure out a way of making it work.  Rating: 5    Verdict  When a game is getting such a low score no one would actually recommend buying it, but in this case I'll make an exception: you should give it a try if it's on sale and you can get it for less than an euro, as for that amount of money it will keep you busy more than enough to justify the purchase. It is in no way a great game, one that you will remember after leaving it aside, but as long as you play it you will be entertained simply because it's so stupid it becomes like a good parody.  Rating Overall: 5.3     Check the full review and a lot more about Space Trader: Merchant Marine at  http://gamedragons.net/viewtopic.php?f=7&t=233

I have played Dead Space across three separate platforms (PS3, XB360 and PC) clocking in over 300+ hours. One might say I have an obsession with the series and I can say with certainty that this game is worth buying, especially during a sale  Positives: - A compelling story which begins the epic franchise that has spanned 5 games (including Extraction and Dead Space IOS), 2 movies, 2 novels and 2 graphic novels - Fully immersive game mechanics. No HUDs, no pause while looking through inventory, all character data is displayed on Isaac Clarke's RIG in real-time, all 'cut scenes' happen in real time - Diverse weapons each with a primary and secondary fire mode, plus two tools to help in combat; Kinesis, which allows you to pick up and hurl objects; and Stasis, which slows enemies and dangerous objects - A terrifying and grotesque foe who are as varied as they are aggressive, who are capable of tactical adaption - Zero Gravity environments that change the dynamics of gameplay and how you fight - Rich and detailed environments with atmospheric ambient noises and music - New Game + which allows you to carry on your fight with all your previous gear and upgrades - The option to run game higher than 30fps on PC - please see below for issues however  Negatives: - The port is not without faults. You are either locked to 30fps or, by disabling Vsync, unlimited fps but with physics issues - Poor M/KB compatibility and what appears to be command delay for controllers - Linear gameplay that begins to stagnate after one or two playthroughs - New Game + does not allow you to change difficulty level - DLC available on console absent from Steam. While not necessary I dislike content exclusivity  Solutions: - Both of the first two negatives can be fixed, please consult the Steam Forum for solutions  Conclusion: An absolute must for fans of great third person action games but especially for fans of Horror. Horror is a niche market in video games these days with fewer and fewer contributions to its genre. This might be from a previous generation of games but it still stands tall with the best of them in my opinion

app_name: Castlevania: Lords of Shadow - Ultimate Edition
review_text: So, what did it think of the game? In short, it... was... beautiful... I was completely taken aback by this game. It blew my expectations out of the water then proceeded to BLOW MY F*****G MIND!  This game is EPIC Beyond Belief! It is not only an AWESOME 3D Castlevania, but it’s an AWESOME game PERIOD! It also has one of, if not THE best stories in SERIES HISTORY. The story is AWESOME beyond description. It also has some of the BEST VOICE ACTING I HAVE EVER HEARD IN A VIDEO GAME EVER!  It’s not perfect though, not by any standard. It has a few minor issues. It’s an incredible game held back by a slight lack of polish. As such I can't give it a perfect score. It’s STILL one of the best games in the series. So I would give this game 8.9 out of 10.   PROS   - Beautiful detailed graphics and visuals. The game is a true piece of eye candy.  - Incredible art design all around  - Some of the best acting to be found in video games.  - BEAUTIFUL and epic original music score  - Epic and sweeping story unmatched in scope or writing within the series  - Identifiable & believable characters  - AWESOME and satisfying combat system  - AWESOME boss battles  - An ending that will tear are your heart open then make you crap your pants in amazement  - Roughly 20 Hours of gameplay with loads of replayability  - Unforgiving but Fair Difficulty  - Linear Gameplay  - PATRICK STEWART, MOTHER F****R!!!      CONS   - Linear gameplay  - TONS of OBVIOUS invisible walls  - Easy to miss environmental exploration cues  - Fixed camera can interfere with control  - Barrows a most of its gameplay elements from other games

Dying Light is truly an enigma.   There are so many large problems with the game that make it feel like progress completely halts. It's got a pretty questionable combat system, the main story is incredibly predictable, and it feels like a good portion of the game contains unfinished mechanics.   I freaking love it.  I've almost 100% the main game, and definitely plan to.   I don't know if it's the skill trees, constantly urging me to continue playing, for just one more level. Or the actually decent parkour mechanics, beckoning me to frolick atop the rooftops, for just 5 minutes more. Maybe I'm still searching for the questgiver that just slowly sunk into the floor, like it was quicksand.  I don't know what it is, but I love this game. Hyper Light Drifter, in a lot of ways is simply a action-platformer.  Although many say it’s a indie Darksouls game, it more in-line with Devil May Cry or Bastion type game, as there is no punishment for dying. The game's combat and special moves resemble Devil May Cry with gun-sword combat, deflecting projectiles, perfect-timed swings and platforming, the game also borrows a lot from Bastion’s top down game mechanics.  The pacing and level design of the game resemble more closely to a Zelda type game, with freedom to go to three available directions; East, North and West. In my playthrough I started with the North. At the North I learn hints of the backstory and lore through its environments and pictograms-dialog from engaging with the NPC. Talking about environments, there are secrets, hidden switches and hidden rooms everywhere, I once also found a hidden switch that lead to a hidden room with more hidden rooms. Which leads me to say “Way too much wall hugging” thankfully there are community guides if you so wish to use them.  In conclusion, Hyper Light Drifter is blend of multiple great games, but does not simply just copy, but make it their own unique design and style. A great recommendation for all!

Endless Legend is a great 4X game, which manages to mix the battle and city building aspects better than any other such game I've seen before. The world map which you build on is the same as the battle map you fight on, and cities get bigger as they build up, taking up more and more physical space in the world.  The tech trees and different gameplay styles all feel somewhat balanced; you aren't allowed to research everything at every level, and hence have to pick which sort of civilization and which sort of victory you're heading towards. However, you never really fall behind in any one resource production, even if you don't focus on it.  The combat balance is interesting. While there is the basis of a great system, units often end up being little more than walking mannequins for your epic armor.  Minor factions replace the barbarians found in other games, and add a lot by their presence. They serve both a combat and a city building role, granting you units to fill the holes in your army composition, as well as a passive bonus to some kind of resource based on how many of their villages you control.  To summarise, this is an excellent entry into the 4x genre, and well worth picking up. A word of warning to those of you who like to turtle: if you're playing multiplayer, you  will  have to build military units.

Hi, Billy Mays here  Are you looking for a realistic, gritty, brutal and immersive world war II multiplayer fps? Have you always wondered what it felt like to have your skin melt off your bones as a stream of fire roasts your body? Do you wish to feel the satisfaction of hitting a running target - 300 meters away - right in the head? Have you always wanted to hold a katana in your hands and charge with your team at an unsuspecting American battalion? Well look no further, because this game answers your call!  Rising storm is a very realistic multiplayer fps, with battles up to 64 people. The game is set in World war II, in the Pacific theatre, involving the United states and the Japanese. Battles are extremely intense, and teamwork and tactics play as a key contributor in winning battles. Most battles in rising storm involve the opposing team starting off at one end of the map and capturing or destorying key points, trying to advance forward, while you hold them back and defend them or vice versa. Teams are normally fixed in these scenarios (meaning one team is always going to be the attacker and the other defender). The balancing in this game is very different from those in other games. Asymmetrical is the term. The Japanese, while having inferior weapons, have much more higher supression resistance and higher morale Aka balls made of titanum or some ♥♥♥♥, compared to the Americans, who have frequent panic attacks. Sly little booby traps can be layed by the Japanese, also and you can get all of your mates to do a banzai charge (good times). However what the Americans DO have is the bigger guns, more load, heavy firepower and Macca's meaning they can use their superior technology to their advantage.   Moving onto maps - Their designed almost perfectly. The maps are large and spacious without having loads of empty space and everything is placed in the right spot without being obvious. Every map is played differently too, for example 'Hanto' which is a dense jungle type area makes it very difficult for the Americans to spot the heavily camouflaged Japanese. Another map called Iwo Jima has an uphill slant with many concealed bunkers to defend from, which can ridden with booby traps. All in all the maps in this game is what balances the teams out the most.  Rising storm has some really decent graphics. The gun models are astounding and realistic, and textures for character models are very good, too. Environments look gritty and worn out without being too dark and unrealistic. Overall its actually very, very decent. Audio in this game is great as well. It really does add suspense and fully immerses. No silly background sound effects, everything is being produced real time. The music is suprisingly very well done, and doesnt get in the way or break the immersion of a battle.  The game may be very, very difficult to get used to for most people. Controls, character movement and coordination may feel clunky for some people as the weapon is not fixed to the center of the screen and the character gains speed instead of instantly moving. Many beginners will also find it difficult to tell the difference between their own fellow team members and the enemy and will shoot without confirming. It takes a while to get to know how each map works out and what the objectives are. In the end though, once you do get used to it you'll be satisfied.  Lastly, communication is key in this game. Although a team may be able to capture a point or even win the round without communicating, it will most likely be very difficult as people will be scattered, unsure of when to attack and generally go off and do their own thing. So whoop out your mic and help out the team or if not, atleast take the time to learn which button you press to chat. If you're looking for a more casual experience, use a general role instead of a special class.    You get 2 games for the price of 19 bucks (USD), which is Rising storm and Red orchestra 2: Heroes of Stalingrad bundled together. I probably shouldnt tell you about the download size. Happy Friday.

I can't fathom how this game has such good review scores. It's a bland, repetitive ARPG, with an uninspired story and clumsy game mechanics (UI, item creation, pawn control, quest system, save file management...). Granted, the ACTION aspect of the ARPG is well represented, and I can only dream of another game that grasps the thrill of a Dragon Dogma's boss fight and applies it thoroughly, with a story and setting to match.  Until then, the game simply isn't worth its full price. If my opinion is any good (and it probably isn't), I would stay away from this game unless it's on a big (50%+) sale.

Monster hunter meets Resident evil meets Skyrim. I remeber when this game came out on console and hit gamers with relative surprise. Most people including myself kind of expected this game to be terrible, and upon starting the game, thats exactly what it looked like. However, something rather brilliant happened. The tutorial   It started like a hack and slash, nothing too special but its not like it was boring. Then the dragon, then the pawns, then the enemy variety, and then, at the very last part of the tutorial, the boss fight. It was this fight that completely hooked me and almost every player that has played this game. An incredibly dynamic and fun as hell boss fight, the thrill of taking down a beast far larger then yourself by climbing on him and taking out his weak points. The stradegy in outfitting your crew with great gear and skill synergies to take down even meaner foes.  It was the action that stopped this game from being forgotten. And if you played it long enough, you will also realize its flaws. Terrible voice acting, tiring side quest, and an illusion of a large open world. (All of you veterans of this game know what im talking about when I say illusion)   If you can manage its flaws, if it even matters to you, you'll find one hell of a great game that you could easily sink thousands of hours into as the rpg elements are incredibly deep.  (Its also a really good port)

I don't exactly know how after all of this time Fromsoft doesn't know how to find the balance between PVE and PVP, and I really don't understand how they could ♥♥♥♥ up worse than Dark Souls 1. Fromsoft seems to be entirely biased in favor towards people who pick up the game, play it once, suck ♥♥♥♥ at it and never play it again. Hosts can summon phantoms almost instantly, Darkmoon Blades come to help fairly quickly, and the odds are always stacked insanely against the invader but we'll get to that later. All of this is extremely weird to me because I think even a blind man could see that Dark Souls has always been a niche series that many diehard fans enjoy, fans who will play the game for years after release.  Dark Souls 1 had an amazing PVE experience, but a lacking PVP experience, at its prime, seeing how there was no region lock, players of all different countries would be paired together and the lag would be immense. There wasn't that much build variety, and covenant and player interaction besides invasions and co-op were lacking.  Dark Souls 2 had a dissapointing PVE experience, but by far the best PVP of the series, while the region lock still didn't work, latency seemed to be at an all time low, there were hundreds of weapons, and most builds were viable to get you kills. Covenants were expanded upon, and if they wouldn't have had stupid things like the Soul memory system, it would've been as perfect as could be.  So Dark Souls 3, 2 previous games of experience, they know what works for PVE, and they know what works for PVP right?  Wrong, apparently. While DS3's PVE is pretty good, DS3's PVP is by far the weakest in the series. As far as dueling is concerned, these games have been out for years and region lock STILL doesn't work (How exactly do you ♥♥♥♥ that up?) but build variety is lacking, every other duel you get paired up with a Carthus Curved Sword weilding R1 spammer, and that works because poise is gone, so all that 'Tech' of taking the first hit to pull off a parry or hell, parrying in general is either impossible or extremely risky due to gettimg 3 hit killed or missing the parry due to latency. When it comes to balancing and fixing bugs, Fromsoft lurches to every update (It took them months to fix a weapon swinging faster than it should. Months), and as the game grows older it seems apparent that many glaring issues will never be fixed. ♥♥♥♥ on DS2 for being made by 'B-Team' but at least they could patch things.  Finally, there's Invasions. Boy did they ♥♥♥♥ up. In DS1, invaders couldn't use Estus. Fair enough. In DS2, Invaders had half of their Estus count. Fair, seeing how there was less Estus in DS2. In DS3, you ask? Invaders have half of their Estus, 30% less health, and, get this, the game prioritizes pairing you up with multiple player hosts, meaning you're almost constantly getting ganked by 2 or more people. What this means is that every Invasion becomes a battle of attrition, where you can't win unless you have an OP meme build that one shots people. Because if you take half the hosts health lets say, theres nothing stopping him from cowering behind his summon and sipping some estus. Repeat that 5 times and you're out of Estus and they still have a lot left. Not to mention if you do happen to kill a phantom the host can just run back to the bonfire and summon them again almost immediately.  Every battle, even if it's a 1 on 1, becomes 'Who has better hyperarmor'. You can literally win any duel as long as you out hyperarmor their attacks, so even if you hit a naked man wielding a giant ♥♥♥♥ing hammer, who has no poise as he's attack, he'll just take the miniscule damage and crush you anyway, which pretty much makes all fights a passive roll fest.  Fromsoft shows no evidence of even wanting to address the exsistence of this clear bias against Invaders, and shows no will to change it. The longevity of the game is damaged severely because of this, as many players are already abandoning the game.

Aside from generic high fantasy and superheroes, pirates seems to be a popular genre for CYOA works from Choice of Games. Several of their offerings already explore life and on the high seas. Not to disparage the other fine offerings already on hand but I hold the opinion that Choice of the Pirate is the best one of them yet.  For one, it presents a wide scope in the narrative with a meaningful choice between civilization and freedom. Neither side is shown to be an absolute good but rather present different views on what people value in life. It also has a strong Carribean feel with native magic being intrinsically tied to nature. The set pieces are fun, especially as you meet creatures like merfolk, sea monsters and ghost ships.   Overall it is well-written and a fun romp. Each playthrough takes approximately 40 minutes which is decent for a CoG title so no fear of a truncated game either. I won't lie, the previous two offerings from CoG did not impress me much. Heroes: Redemption was basically the author strongarming the reader into listening and playing along with the author's political views while Sea Eternal was kinda meh. Choice of Pirates on the other hand is a strong offering that ranks alongside some of their best works.

Most of the time I don't know whether I should skip it because I'm half bored to death or if I should actually watch/listen to it because it is important to the story  Other than the cutscenes the game is pretty good. The characters are pretty memorable and offer some enjoyment with their unique interactions.   Overall Rating 7/10 (Cutscene Simulator 2009)

I don't really know where to start too well because this is the first review I've written up, but I'm going to try to start by saying that there used to be a game called Battlefield Play4Free. Now, this game was a serious gem. It really required skill, tactics, and understanding of the maps and weapons you were dealing with. It wasn't like Call of Duty, where your guns kick like babies and as long as you keep your reflex sight on something you hit it, it had insane recoil, lots of spread, large variations in damage. All the guns were very diverse; none were the same, and excluding a few, they were all very balanced. The map design for the most part was amazing and well thought out. But no one gave this game credit for what it was, which in my opinion was probably one of the best multiplayer exclusive games ever created, just because of small issues like bad starting weapons, a pay to win system, and a few hackers. Eventually, because EA handed the game to a smaller company that co-owned it (EAsy), the community eventually started dying out and the game was shut down even after various offers to purchase it from players and other companies.   This game really does remind me of Battlefield Play4Free. I think it's incredibly fun, and the weapon balance, despite starting with mediocre weapons, is excellent. There are no two guns alike with one better in some way (like BF3, where the M16A3 trumps all) and all of them feel slightly different and behave differently. The map design feels relatively nice and there aren't any of those stupid maps where the center is a chokepoint/slaughterfest for either team. So far I haven't found but a few glitches and none of them have to do with abusing maps; mostly just audio and graphics glitches (i.e. fire a rifle and unscope and it doesn't go through the bolting animation). Please, I just ask of you, that if you happen to come across this review, seriously try the game, even if you don't spend much time on it. This community is slowly dying as well, and I don't want another amazing game to slip between our fingers.  Overall Pros: Very good weapon balance Fun, balanced maps Great gameplay Weapons CAN be bought permanently WITHOUT spending any real money (Daily rewards, WP, crates) Nice map and weapon diversity Great customization system  Overall Cons: Some hackers Enough WP to buy guns is harder to get than it should be Clunky gameplay (but still fun) Occasional glitches Somewhat confusing user interface  Overall, I think it's a great game. Please don't overlook it.

After having played Alan Wake on xbox360 and now on PC I can definitely recommend this game. It does a really good job at emmersing the player into the dark and beautifully designed world, this is one of the most detailed and well crafted game worlds I've seen. The atmosphere, story and pacing is really well done and is probably where the game shines the most. Don't expect a horror game, this is an adventure/thriller that takes the player on an nail-biting adventure that won't be forgotten easily.  The gameplay and mechanics are fun and fluid with some room for improvement for buggy running and jumping controls. Other than that, the gameplay is quite unique and fun making for some intense, interesting and horrifying moments. I've played this with both keyboard/mouse and controller. The game plays easier with keyboard/mouse since it makes aiming and camera controls easier to do. A controller is definitely not required but it does come down to preference.   Definitely a game that must be played if you like Thriller/Adventure story based games with unique 3rd person gameplay.

There's a lot of new content for money, but no engine improvements...  + GTA-like idea, free to play. + Your cars, your outfits, clans. + Amount of Weapons (A lot of people say premium weapons broke the game, but actually only few of them are useful, the rest are just reskins of basic weapons you can buy at vendor... even some medium player with some op weapon can be easily defeated, you just have to use your brain and have better aim, that's all. It is a shooting game guys, you win with your aim, not with accessory) + Events for easter, halloween, christmas.  ------------------------------------------------------------------------  - toxic community - hackers (but you have them everywhere... better to have one in 10 minutes long mission than 45 minutes spent in CS:GO playing against hacker ;-) ) - A lot of cool stuff, like outfits and cars, but for 'VIP' money. You can make that money, but not enough for cool stuff, only for basic things like kneepads or some basic mask.
