Can't think of anything else, but I think it is enough.  In my opinion, it is a very good game to play, you should definitely try it out, maybe you will like it as I did :) I found the best group of people, and even though the game sucked sometimes, we enjoyed the best time. So I just hope you'll find a good community to play with, it will keep you ingame.

Mass Effect is an action role playing third person shooter game, developed by BioWare. Releasing on Xbox 360, PS3 and PC. The game was released in November 2007 as a timed Xbox exclusive, but eventually got ported to PC in May 2008.   The game starts out with you being able to fully customize the appearance of the main character, male or female, abilities, and background story. The game play is based a lot around a squad of three characters including you. There are six difference “classes” that come in the form of important characters that you encounter throughout the game. Each of these classes will level up, and have their own special abilities and talent tree to select from. The talent tree is very detailed, allowing you to spec each class into specialized roles which you will take on different missions depending on what enemies you will be facing.   The game’ combat system is primarily in real time, but you can pause at any time to give orders to your squad. I however found this to be a very negligible feature as I had no use for it all, I don’t think I used even once. The combat is very easy to get to grasps with. A really good cover based system is included, with some really tight controls to boot. Whilst you do have unlimited ammo for your weapons, you do run the risk of overheating your guns if you just continuously fire, meaning you have to take breaks in your onslaught for a few seconds at a time or you can simply switch to one of your many other weapons. I liked the idea behind not having to scavenge around for ammunition, its 2183…it’s all lasers for gods sakes! :) Another key game play element comes in the form of the dialogue and morality system. A really good portion of the game takes place in dialogue scenes with NPCs, your answers really do matter and can sometimes change the course of events which carry out for the rest of the game. I am not normally a fan of games which have too much dialogue, but I was completely intrigued to explore each dialogue option to delve deeper and immerse myself completely in the story. Different actions and different results will affect your morality, completing the main missions don’t really add much to the system, but a lot of the game’s side missions make you choose a “good” or “bad” resolution to them, which really makes you think sometimes.   Mass Effect is almost ten years old now, and whilst it is showing its age, it is certainly still an OK looking game by today’s standards. All of the animations are really crisp. The textures aren’t all high resolution, but you have to remember the age of the game when playing. The facial modelling of all of the characters is done very well too, and even betters some of the recent titles!   I played Mass Effect on the medium difficulty level, and I found the game to be challenging enough so that I wasn’t breezing through the game, but not so hard that I felt I wasn’t able to progress without having the need to grind a few levels before taking on a certain boss or mission. The only times I did find myself getting stuck, is purely a UI issue, rather than the gameplay. The game doesn’t do a fantastic job at telling you where to go next; you have a lot of maps to be able to look through, but no help at all comes for many of the side missions for where you need to go, meaning I had to look up a few of the missions online to be able to work out where to go.   The majority of the game’s levels are really well designed. There is really only one main hub of activity in the game which has an open world feel to it, and that is the Citadel. You will find a good portion of the game’s side quests in this location with plenty of NPCs to interact with as well. Each of the game’s main missions takes place on a different planet, and that planet will also have a handful of side missions for you to complete along with the main story. I would urge people to check them out as they are well worth your time, and I didn’t at any point feel like I was completing the same type of mission over and over again. All of the side missions add a great deal of story which also keeps the lore crew happy as well.   I have recently just finished my second play through of Mass Effect, the first being a few years ago now. The most recent play through of the game took just under 20 hours to complete, this is with me being in OCD mode and completing all of the side missions, exploring all of the levels and interacting with any of the NPCs I came across. There is a deal of replay ability with Mass Effect, you can of course ramp up the difficulty, but you can also play the game in different styles using the morality system, and changing the gender of your character to also effect relationships with your crew members. I would even say I could squeeze another two play throughs out of Mass Effect before I would find it hard to find something new to come across.   Technically, right now I have to say Mass Effect does have a couple of issues running on newer operating systems. Whilst I didn’t have any crashes or major bugs, I did have a few performance problems with the odd stutter every now and again. I am pretty sure it is not hardware related as my PC is more than capable of running current titles on 4k with high settings. Another pet peeve is that I wasn’t even able to run the game on 1080p; you have to put up with the lower Xbox360 resolutions. I didn’t play with a controller, as I am more interested in being more precise with my aim than anything else to be honest.   My conclusion on Mass Effect is, that whilst many of you will have probably already played and finished the game, I would still class this as a must play title for our recent memory. It is the start of a truly fantastic series, and one of the standout titles of the last generation of games. It is showing its age slightly, but Sci-fi fans will want to start here before stepping foot into the Mass Effect world. If you can look past the aging graphics you have an absolutely stellar story and hella fun gameplay with some heavy RPG elements.      Tom's Score Card   1) Stay away 2) Not Recommended 3) Only recommended when on sale 4) Recommended 5) Highly recommended   6) This is a must play    If you found this review helpful, please give it a thumbs up. If you would also like to follow my reviews more easily, be sure to join my group by clicking  here .  You may also be interested in checking out a PC gaming podcast, be sure to head  here.    http://store.steampowered.com/app/17460

If you want a good *adventure* game, this is not the game you are looking for. It has its strengths and especially the first half is mostly very enjoyable, but the second half is pretty much just an interactive Hollywood action movie with a teaspoon of adventuring thrown in. You get more adventure pleasure by orders of magnitudes for the same amount of money if you buy some LoK or older TR titles at once.  The immersion is mostly very good, with gorgeous graphics, well-done level design and a convincing soundscape, sometimes dented by the clichéed characters and story. 'Magic' is a pretty lazy deus ex machina, and it's thrown around very liberally here.  The game seems to have been designed with ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in mind, because activating Lara's 'instinct' negates all need to think about what to do and where to go, and the tombs are mostly a joke. The first three are moderately challenging, the last ones are actually amongst the easiest aprts of the game. Calling them puzzles is an insult to puzzling really. They're really more of an afterthought to justify the title, as the tacky 'Tomb raided!' congratulatory text shows. Looking for the collectibles provides the main bulk of adventuring challenge, because a minority of them are hidden well, but mostly the way to them is just 'complicated' to stall time (run up there, jump down there, slide down there, climb up there) instead of hard. Because of Lara's instinct finding out which is the right way is also a no-brainer. So unless you're very disciplined and never use the instinct function (the hardest difficulty does not deactivate it), this game has next to no adventuring challenge.  The weapon upgrade system is interesting in the beginning, but overall it's pointless and won't noticeably affect your playstyle or combat success. Upgrading for the sake of upgrading.  As mentioned before, the first half is mostly adventuring with a bit of fighting, like you're used to from TR games, but in the second half you will have many moments where enemies just constantly pour down on you like you're playing an old school arcade shooter, and some stationary wave-challenges. The last one in the monastery is the biggest and also the most infuriating one. It was actually the last straw that made me decide to write a negative review.

I think I picked this game up on sale for £5-6 a while back. On the face of it, It's a turn based strategy game and has a number of interesting game mechanics hidden within it.  The tech tree is extensive and the brain power required to decide what order you will need things in is interesting. Picking your colonies, even nice planets, and then wondering why moral hit rock bottom is a fairly big part of the game.   Quickly you learn how to do better - expand, drop the tax to 35% or less to maintain moral for a bit, scout the area and hope for free hive ships and then make your fleets as big as possible.   Would I recommend it... full price? (£20+) Heck no. On sale? (sub-£5) Yes. Especially if you like turn based strategy or want a game to play while you watch TV.

I rarely buy games at launch, but since I loved Braid I had to buy this one. The puzzles are challenging but, when everything finally clicks, so satisfying. I love that the game doesn't have any tutorials or direction, but is made in a way that you never feel like you're lost.  Even though the game is open world, it's organized much like how Braid was. Each 'area' of the world has a puzzle theme that gets more and more complex as you progress through that area. The difference here is you don't really have to do the areas in a specific order.   I hear a lot of people saying there's impossible puzzles, or it's impossibly difficult. I'd say they're probably playing 'wrong.' If you can't figure something out, don't be afraid to leave the puzzle and come back to it later.  Interestingly, this makes for a great group game I've found. Even though it's technically singleplayer, my friends have had a blast adventuring and working out the puzzles together. It's a lot more fun thinking everything through as a team I'd say!  If you liked Braid, you'll love this game. If you aren't a fan of solving puzzles, hated Braid, or get frustrated easily, then you'll probably hate The Witness. This is easily one of my favorite recent games!

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Looking at my steam account you may see that I've played the game for 95 minutes. Well I haven't. I haven't even got to play the game. 95 minutes is the combined time the game was running while I spend hours uninstalling, reinstalling, validating files, downloading fixes, reading forums, watching youtube tutorials, going through all this trying to fix Games for Windows Live associated crashes. Never again will I buy GFWL games.

Edit after a year or so. This sim just keeps getting better. The latest release with the fantastic nordschleife really puts this at the top of the racing hill. They also worked out a few bugs that were sometimes interrupting the fun - no more. What was previously a great game with issues is now starting to really get polished. There is no better sim racing experience that getting into a good online race with this game. Shell out the $20 for some paid content, join some big-boy races with respectful racers, and never look back. The pricing model works well for me because i can buy once (still cheaper than the other sim) and not worry about a subscription. Again, no other racing sim gets my adrenalin flowing like this one - it really conveys the feel of speed and competition. Single player is quite good as well especially for European racing fans, but i'm more of a multiplayer user. Again if you are a sim racer and have not tried this in the last year, do yourself a favor and try it again - then judge for yourself. Even using the free content to get a taste should convince most.   There is still the odd bug or two, but the devs pay attention and always take care of the issues that the community brings up - for instance - currently player names above the cars are located off screen for triple monitor users. I believe this is about to be fixed in the next patch (as of 6/13/16), along with taking care of those with low ping to not ruin races. Check out the forums here  https://forum.sector3studios.com/index.php   Final thoughts - at 450 hours played, i really should be faster! But i'm getting there....  Original Review: Great racing sim. There are sims that do certain things better (AC graphics for instance) but as a whole package, this is the sim that makes me feel the most like i'm in a car driving and gets my heart pounding. You just feel like you are there, which is the whole point of a sim right? The Raceroom track that comes free is great too, and i like being able to pay for what i want, even if it can get pricey if you want a ton of content. Thumbs up!

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Rome II... well, there is a lot to say about this game. Too much to say for a short recommendation like this one. There´s been a lot of hate directed towards this game, and for good reasons. At launch the game had many problems, and was not really any fun to play.   But now, especially after the Emperor edition, the game is worth your time. (should you be a historical strategy fan) Most of the game´s problems are fixed, and many of the things that many people call stupid design decisions, are not that at all in my mind. The game is a good Total War game. That´s my opinion, Not as good as Shogun 2, though. I wouldn´t go that far. So, if you left the game on launch for it´s problems, you could try it now and think again.  There is one thing that i need to mention, though: The DLC is mostly not worth the money, the extra units are not required nor do they bring that much fun compared to their cost. The campaign expansions i have not tried, so i dont really know about them.  This review was not that extensive, so i call it a recommendation. And i do recommend the game in it´s current state. Hopefully this was of use to someone.

Amazing game, but crashes all the time (there is an easy fix, though you have to do it every time before you start the game). First off, let me get this out of the way: Far Cry 3 is an amazing game. Graphics: 9/10 I can't rate this category fairly because in order to get 50-60fps I have to play at 720p medium. The games art style though is great and I am sure it looks amazing at 1080p ultra on a better pc.  Sound:9/10 Sounds great, can't complain.  Gameplay: 10/10 Shooting feels great, flamethrowers and rocket launchers add variety. Lots of vehicles for running over bad guys.  Performance-8/10 My pc is kinda s*** but this game is easy to run. However there is a major issue on Windows 10 which is that it crahses every 5-10 mins. To fix this, open task manager, navigate to services, find TabletInputService, right click on it, and select stop. No idea why it causes the crashes but once I disabled that it ran fine.  Overall-9.5/10  This is the perfect far cry game (at the time of when it came out) in my opinion. Everything great about the first game without the boringness of Far Cry 2.

Here we are with yet another free-to-play survival horror FPS. It may be free, but, is it worth installing to your computer?   'No More Room in Hell' is a standalone mod for the game, Half Life 2. The game provides a simple startup, character selection, and server selection. Before heading into the zombie apocolypse, you need to find the right character that suits you best, right? Unfortunately enough you chose from one of four different avatars to use. They aren't any different aside from looks.   But, let's forget that and talk about the real reason you're reading this:  How is the gameplay? When you finish loading a game session, you and all the other players will start unarmed. The round then begins and you will have to rush to find randomly spawned weapons, medicine, and tools. Once you are all geared up, your team must band together to fight off the appending zombie forces.  There are three game modes. One involves you and your teammates defedning certain area's from being overwhelmed. Secondly, there are mission objectives where you must use problem solving, and sheer wits to make yourself to extraction. Thirdly, there's plain survival.   Each is fun, that is, if you have a decent team around you. There are several locations that suit the tone of the game perfectly. Each is unique, and have there advantages and disadvantages. From croakers, to runners the actual zombies, when at high graphical settings, are quite disturbing :)   From shooting my friend after he got the infection, to reaching the extraction chopper just in time, 'No More Room in Hell' is a good, fun, and satisfying experience.  Final Verdict: 7/10  Pro's:  *Requires a TEAM *Great map variation *Good visuals *Good amount of weapons  Con's:  *No real customization *Lack of lasting content  Cheers, God Bless,  -Furry

SUMMARY: A (free!) third-person sci-fi coop action game. As with many free games are these days, there is A LOT of grind involved. But there's also plenty of content that keeps the game enjoyable. Worth a try, especially if you have friends who are interested.  Pros: +You're a freakin' space ninja with magical powers.  +Extremely fun movement system that allows you to parkour all over the place, much more mobile and fast paced than many other action games +Large variety of weapons and Warframes, fun to collect and switch up your loadout when you get bored. +Not pay-to-win. you can collect blueprints for items from missions or buy them with credits you collect from missions; you can trade for the premium currency (platinum) with other players to get any other things you need +Developers do listen to community — bugs get fixed, submitted content (skins, sometimes weapons) gets implemented +Active with updates that add more content/story to explore  Cons: -If you are new, you will find the in-game documentation sorely lacking. The game explains very little, and you will want to check out the wiki to figure out where certain loot drops, or how the weapon upgrade system works. Without a guide, you will be lost -Clunky UI forces you to click many times to access things like your inventory, friends list, etc. -Various bugs, ranging from mild annoyances (getting stuck, graphical issues) to game-crippling (enemies spawning in impossible-to-reach places, random disconnects that cause you to lose mission rewards) -THE GRIND. Do you mind fighting 20 minutes for a 5% chance for that loot piece to drop? (Hope you switch things up every once in a while to avoid burnout) -Developers have a poor sense of balance. i.e. introducing new weapons with high mastery level requirements and high crafting cost that are useless to players of that level, Warframes whose abilities are objectively inferior to other frames', absurdly lopsided enemy scaling that leaves very little middle ground between slaughtering enemies with a push of a button and constantly having to cast CC to avoid getting one-shotted -Not as fun without friends.

Papers, please. A dystopian document thriller.  Nothing would sound more boring right? Well, you are right. It does sound boring.  But dont let the graphics, the minimalistic gameplay and repetetive music fool you. This game is one of the greatest out there, and easily beats AAA titles. I peaked you interest yet? Good.    You play as a border inspector in the fictional communist country of Arstotzka, just having finished a war against the neighbor country Kolechia that lasted 6 long years. Your job is paperwork. All you do is stamp passports and look through papers. Fisrt day, only citizens of Arstotzka are allowed entry. Next up, foreingers with valid passports are allowed to enter. Same day terrorists attack the border. Next day all foreingers must present a valid entry ticket. It all goes on and on and on. Eventually you will be dealing with a lot of papers. Still, you are expected too keep the same amount of presicion and speed as you had before. Why? At the end of each day you manage your expenses: Heat, food and rent. On top of this, we have your family: Son, wife, mother in law and your uncle. Not paying for food or heat will result in them being sick, which will result in them needing medicine. If you cant pay for medicine, one of your family members die. But on top of that, if you make a mistake at work, you get a citation. First two are warnings, after that you have to pay 5 credits per mistake. Incedentlly, every entrant you procces correctly will give you 5 credits.  Now, with that out of the way, this game has a lot of choices. There is no black and white, there is only gray. This women does not have the right papers, but she will be killed if she returns to her home country. Do you let her in at the expense of your family, or do you deny here and move on? This man carries what he calls 'harmless medicine' and pays you 10 credits to let him pass. Do you detain him, or do you give a stamp?  Do you help a secret group overthrow the proclaimed corrupt goverment? Do you do your thing as a upstanding citizen?  Papers, Please is a work of art. Amazing and fitting gameplay, awesome story and sub stories.  Also, you might wonder what happens when your family dies or you run out of money. You will be replaced and put either into forced labor or ported back to your village.  Glory to Arstotzka.

EDIT: After a long and steep learning curve where I really had big trouble with this game, I've decided to change my review to be positive. When you figure out how things work (the game won't explain even half of it!), it gets a lot more manageable and fun. Usually, I always recommend tackling games without a guide. THIS GAME IS AN EXCEPTION! Read all the guides you can possibly find! If you are willing to do so, you will be greatly rewarded with a cool experience.   ORIGINAL REVIEW   I really like many aspects of the game. The general theme is great, the graphics are really cool, and the narrator's voice is awesome and contributes a lot to the atmosphere. It's dark, relentless and unforgiving.  All that sounds really great. The problem is that the amount of randomness in this game is totally out of control. Need some examples?  - Will an attack hit? Random. - Becoming poisoned, or start to bleed? Random.  - Turn order? Random. - Is the treasure chest a deadly trap? Random.  - Will the hero die when he's hit while deadly injured? Random. - Will there be an act of bravery or a mental illness when stress is at max? Random. - Which party member will fall victim to a trap when you walk into it? Random. - Which new characters will be available after a dungeon run? What are their skills and stats? All random.  ... and worst of all, these random elements absolutely cannot be avoided by the player. The game forces them upon you. There are regular opponents in the game that can inflict permanent (!) random diseases on your characters with their NORMAL ATTACK (for crying out loud), which cost an awful lot of money to cure. So, literally, if your heroes are unlucky, they WILL die. This is NOT under your control, not even in the slightest. What's more, the game also fails to display any kind of percentages with which an action will succeed, it just happens. Not even turn order is properly displayed when you have to take an action and make a choice which enemy to take down.  To summarize, you can be the greatest tactical genius that ever walked this planet earth, but if you are simply not lucky enough with the random numbers, this game will kick your ♥♥♥ without a moment of hesitation. This is just bad game design.

I bought the game while it was on sale for 85 percent off, and having finally beaten the game, I can safely say that while this is a good game, its not worth 13 dollars. It's a puzzle platformer with a charming, simple story leading your character along: your uncle Fred has gone missing during one of his adventures, and you want to know what happened to him.  I will praise the game for its environments. I enjoyed entering a new area and getting to look around at everything. The only downside to this is if you have ♥♥♥♥ reflexes or suck at puzzles (as I do), then looking at these pretty areas can get a bit tedious after twenty deaths, and you'll long to either go forward or back just to get a change of scenery.  The story is, as I said, simple and charming. You meet a couple of characters, but I was disappointed to see a lack of world building. You enter a new area, but no details are given about it. In one area early on, you're given the impression that everyone fears a place called the Chasms, and yet none of the locals sit down and explain why. You couldn't go up to random NPCs and talk about what was going on. You couldn't trigger conversations with Maggie once she was on your back. For me, I love a good story that has a bit of that in it. The lack doesn't make the story bad, its just a personal preference of mine.   I will say that the ending, for all its build up, seems rather abrupt. After chasing Fred all around these fantastical places, and wondering what would lead him so deep into this realm, the answer you get is... lackluster. It makes sense, logically, and it ties the story off, but if you were craving some kind of big dramatic ending to go with the story like I was, it won't give you that.   All in all, I give the game an 8/10. It's got heart, its got charm, its beautiful and it doesn't stick around longer than need be. If you're here for a simple story, no great big strings attached, then that's what you'll get. Enjoy it for what it is, but don't expect much more than that.

Main Story is beatable in 1-2 hours. very easy for a Sudoku game. There's few gamemodes within Sudoku in Story mode:  Clue find - Regular Sudoku without time limit, fill the big square with ? marks.  Inspection - Fill the entire Sudokus(9 big squares, 81 tiles) several times Within time limit.  Lockpick - Fill the center tile with correct number(4 wrong number to fail), Within the time limit.   Chase - Fill the tile with red footprint. Taking more time = more tiles to deal with.  The ball rotation with RMB is hard to control, thus it would frustrate you in timed levels. Other than that, control is quite generic for sudokus(number keys and - key work and recommended)  I've been a fan of connected sudokus(those are connected at corners), but in Main Story mode they haven't fully used its goodness. In Classic mode it's used well, and plays better than 4 minigames Story mode offered.   7/10, Get it only if you like Sudokus. If you're detective fan, there are better games around.

Plagued with the same glaring issues that most every RTS has.   -Your units are stupid and won't do anything effectively without micromanaging the hell out of them using macros and bidning them and babysitting them constantly. You can select a group of units and tell them to walk in a direction. The fast ones won't slow down for the slower ones and end up trickeling into their own demise. They will also walk past enemies that are firing on them and ignore them as they die. If you use attack move, they will stop and fire at any stupid 10billion health wall and never get to their destination. You have to micro manager them like crazy to get anything done. Its just a huge wall of unnessiary ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t to have to learn to get into the genre, and I hate it. There should be many variations of attack move, and ways to command your units to do things more intelligently rather than babysitting them when you want to focus on something else. The mark of a good RTS player is that they can not only do this, but do it in many places at once while still focusing on the big picture (macro) which brings me to my next huge complaint.  The macro is the same old same old remember an exact set of openings and folling them to a T as fast as you possible can.   It pisses me off that a game with STRATEGY in the name of the genre is more about memorizing optimal patterns, repeating them hundreds of times so you can do them faster than the next guy, and honing your ability to click hundreds of times and minute (most of which are just directing your braindead units to doing the most simplest of takss).   I was drawn to this game because it was hyped to fix the issues with the direction RTS games have taken. It was said to put the strategy back in to RTS and take it away from being what the genre really should be named... CPM (Clicks per minute) because that is all this genre is.

Disclaimer: I got this game in a giveaway.  The first thing you notice is the CRT filter. While other games and emulators have attempted such filters before, I've never seen one this elaborate. Unfortunately, the filter gets to be a bit of a headstrain after a while, so I turned it off (although there are LOTS of options for customizing it) Without the filter, you get the typical NES style graphics.  The game is fairly basic. You collect items that allows you to pass further through the game (for example revealing platforms or allowing you to breath underwater). Through the game you can collect both keys and gems. Keys allow you to open locked doors, but if you're missing one you have the options to either buy one with gems or loan one from the key lenders. I never had to loan a key so I can't say if it makes a difference in the end.  Storywise, the king has been kidnapped by a wizard, who took his heart and divided it in six pieces across the land. Finding them allows entrance to the castle, where the wizard holds the king, and where you will restore his heart and win the game.  If the player gets lost and don't know where to go next they can visit the fortune teller, who will direct the player to their next objective. Retrieving some of the hearts required for entry to the final level can be rather frustrating, while an NPC points you in the general direction, finding them can involve a lot of trial and error.  Overall, would I recommend the game? Definitively.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: Recommended... but do not buy this game for the campaign.  Cons of Campaign - - Game loses textures halfway through missions. Guns look bad, faces look plastic.. really takes the immersion away. - Game loses sound assets. I lost gun-sounds, music, explosions.. basically everything except the com link between me and Hendricks. Made me want to just quit out. - Lack of meaningful characters. - FPS is all over the place in cutscenes. Sometimes it looks like a slideshow and others where it will hang up on a still image for a few seconds. Actual gameplay - it usually doesn't drop below 30 with vsync. I have an fx8350 and a 270x.. playing on high settings, no aa.  Pros of Campaign - - Weapon customization. - Great map design.. several ways to complete the objective. - Campaign specific camos to use in multiplayer. - Solid FPS mechanics. Getting headshots feels rewarding.  As with all COD games. The Multiplayer is the only reason to play. But it is a shame that they botched this campaign so hard on PC. It is not that bad on Xbox, but of course on there you are limited to 30fps and lower resolution/texture quality. Overall with the bugs (6 months after release... I figured they'd have sorted it by now) it feels like a bit of a let down.

This is the third installment of the Zero Escape trilogy, starting with  999: 9 Hours, 9 Persons, 9 Doors  on the DS and  Zero Escape: Virtue's Last Reward  on the 3DS and Vita.  While the Creator says that it can be played without prior knowledge of the previous games (999: 9 Hours, 9 Persons, 9 Doors on the DS and Virtue's Last Reward on 3DS and Vita, with both being remastered for Steam), I HIGHLY recommend playing the previous two games to get the complete story and not be spoiled.  The game takes a much darker turn from 999 and VLR as Uchikoshi, the director and producer, was given more creative freedom to make his game, at the cost of a tight budget, which shows.  The animations for the characters are somewhat wooden and odd. The lip-syncing sometimes better suits the English or the Japanese voice acting (which you can change on the fly.)  The graphics aren't too good or too bad but the launcher lets you add AA which gets rid of the jagged edges unlike the Vita and 3DS versions.  The story is just as good as the other games with an even bigger twist. As a closer to the Zero Escape Trilogy, it fills its purpose but still leaves me wanting more since it falls a little bit flat after the big reveals in 999 and VLR.  Participants are split up into 3 Groups, C, Q and D into their respective wards. They are split off and have no way to contact each other except through notes passed on through an old dog who must go through vents to deliver them. They wake up with watches on their wrists. A masked figure appears:   '90 minutes. When next you wake, you will have forgotten everything that has happened here.'   Their watches have drugs that cause them to sleep and to forget everything they have learned, done and accomplished in the last 90 minutes that they've been awake. The only way out is to kill 6 other participants for passwords to the X Door. Their lives, Zero's and the fate of the human race are in the hands of these 9 participants.  This is The Decision Game. This is Zero Time Dilemma.  The game play is made up of Fragments, Quest Rooms and The Decision Game.  Fragments are the visual novel part of the story where you choose a fragment that looks interesting and you play for that team's scenario in an unknown timeline and location in time. Are you playing before or after a character has died? Is everyone else still alive? Did that person die in this timeline or will you cause it later on? You'll have to play to unravel the mysteries that Zero is leaving you with.  Quest Rooms are essentially the puzzle aspect of the game. While they aren't as demonic as Professor Layton's twisted mind, they are much easier than those in VLR and a little easier than the ones in 999.  The Decision Game is where you'll spend your time figuring out how to keep someone or even yourself alive. Will you pull the trigger to save someone from a fiery death at the cost of possibly firing a live bullet at your friend? Will you push a big button that says DO NOT PRESS THIS? Will you escape the Decision Game by guessing the correct flip of the coin and save everyone? Will you be able to make a miracle and roll 3 1's on 3 dice or perish at the odds of 1/216 and cause the death of 3 people?  The game changes with every choice you make. Will you save humanity, destroy it or will you cause something even worse?  A way I can summarise the Zero Escape series is: Who is Zero? What do they want? How did we get here? Why do I know these things? What's the answer to that damn puzzle?    Pros   -The story being set up as fragments without a known timeline or history -The soundtrack -It's made by Uchikoshi -Easy puzzles -60fps with up to 1440 resolution -Anti-Aliasing -Separate sliders for BGM, Voice and SFX -Can switch your VA choice, invert controls and cursor speed in-game -Runs pretty well on my IntelHD 4600 toaster laptop -Darker and edgier than 999 and VLR -Still has that Uchikoshi humour sprinkled throughout the dialogue    Cons   -The story spoils 999 and VLR and expects you to at least recognise some terms such as Morphogenetic Field Theory, Monty Hall and Many Worlds Theory -The motions of the models are sometimes stiff or just plain odd -The voice acting can get odd at times in both the English and Japanese voices -Easy puzzles (VLR had really difficult puzzles and bonus files for completing them a certain way) -Some textures look pretty bad, like on the X-Door's keypad and the butterfly on Zero's cloak -Uses a launcher to change graphics settings

It's billed as a 'Space Trading Adventure', but this buggy game is a poor port of a poor mobile game, with very little adventure and very little trading.  On the surface, it seems interesting. You have to keep your interstellar crew happy while they zip around the galaxy, fighting and trading. So it has a Sims aspect to it. But here, as in so many other areas, the execution is poor. As your 'Sims' level up, they demand more amenities to keep them happy. But once you install the required room on your ship, the problem goes away, forever. There is no tension involved. Crew unhappy, buy room, problem solved.   The whole game is like this. At the beginning of the game, you want a shooter who can also clean or repair, so they always have a job. So this is a bit of leveling up strategy, or someone to look for when hiring crew. But later in the game, you can get robots to do the menial work, removing the need to bother with it. Strategy removed.  As for the trading, it doesn't make sense. Some planets consume, some produce. But there is no overview of this. What's more, it's not very lucrative, compared to...  Combat. Here is where the game is completely broken. Combat involves 'tapping' buttons at the right time. There's not much to it. But compared to trading, it's insanely profitable. There really is no reason to trade. Once you have their hull down to 15%, they will be on the phone to you, offering 40k, 50k, 60k to let them live. Compared to the 7k you can make hauling cigarettes across the map and back. And once you've figured this out, in a matter of hours you'll have the most expensive ship in the game. And then, there is no game anymore.  Yes, there's a story, whatever.  Finally, it crashes. A lot. There are in game bugs as well.  Stay away.

After I finished my first playthrough ever of this game (Never played RE0 on GCN), I think it's time for a review. This is a great game, I'm a long time Resident Evil fan and this game didn't disappoint me at all. While I think it did most things really well, there were some others that I didn't like as much, but I'll get to that later.  Let's start off with the technical stuff. Oh man, this game is a wonderful remaster. The graphics are unbelieveable good, considering that this is originally a game from 2002. The backgrounds, although pre-rendered, have never been so dynamic, it's often hard to distinguish between pre-rendered and actual rendered models because they are both so detailed, pre-rendered backgrounds have never looked so good. Force Feedback/Controller vibration is also a thing, even during the cutscenes, and it underlines some intense moments very well. The controls also received an overhaul. In my review of the HD REmaster I complained about the new control scheme, feeling out of place by making character movement look unnatural and that you need to adjust your stick when the camera changes. While the latter problem presists, the movement feels much more natural now. Definitely an improvement. You can now also set the refresh rate to up to 144 Hz, while the HD REmaster only allowed 60 Hz.   SPOILERS FROM HERE ON IN:   Next up is the game itself. The gameplay is solid, it might feel strange controlling 2 characters at first, but you'll get used to it. You can switch between the characters almost anytime, and they both have different strenghts and weaknesses. A weaker point of the game is, in my opinion, the plot. While I love the RE storyline, this one had a few elements that I didn't like. Don't get me wrong, I know Resident Evil isn't know for it's perfect writing, but this one seemed a bit weaker compared to the other games. First off, Marcus in his young/leech/whatever form. The way he looks is just ridiculous, and that chorus like singing...just no. Another point I didn't like so much was Billy's backstory. The guerilla village thing just seemed too forced and didn't fit into the story at all. The message 'war is bad' dosen't fit into Resident Evil, we have other game series like Metal Gear for that. Where this game really shines is level design. It's so good I could talk about it the whole day, but I'll try to keep it short. First off is are the settings. The train is awesome, the atmosphere of it driving during rain, all the moving objects on the tables...just perfect. The training facility is probably the longest part of the game. One might say it's basically a cheap version of the mansion from RE1, but I would have to disagree. My favoriten part however is the factory. In this part you are seperated from Billy, so you are alone and can only play as Rebecca. Here you will revisit some places from Resident Evil 2, and I would have to lie to say that it didn't make me feel a bit nostalgic. Then there are also the treatment facility and laboratory, which also have great level design. The way you have to interact with your partner over different rooms is just amazingly thought through, no argument there.   tl;dr  +amazing level design and atmosphere +slow paced gameplay, with an overall good character switch system +excellent soundtrack  -plot has it's weakpoints   Overall: 8.0/10   While this REmaster is better from a technical standpoint, I just prefer the core game of the remake/first REmaster.

Having played Tales of Symphonia and enjoying it, and some of the VERY early ones like Tales of Phantasia I was hoping that this would be a more modern take on the JRPG genre. Unfortunately it is not, it is just a lot of bland environments stitched together with some mundane dialogue at best.  The dialogue to one side the combat system is awful, the majority of it can be summed up as 'mash B button'. You can swap mobs and do SLIGHTLY different moves (i.e press A button once then mash B again, or press R1 to become some superman and press B some more), but generally its clunky, its given you a 3d space to run around in, but you always just run in to fight a single monster, it would have felt far better if it just allowed you to run around and attack like an action game given the way the camera and attacks act, you also end up often getting shanked in the back in battles as you are too busy mashing B on the guy in front to notice you have twerked your way into the stragglers.  The story so far is at best bland, at worse boring and mundane. I quite like the idea of these celesial beings humans cannot see, but they just all feel like space cadets. For those of you who could stand enough of FF13 to meet Vanille will know what I am on about. Every time the characters open their mouth its just some random piece of nonsense they throw out and the animations are often clunky, like there is a bit early on where the seraphim with you shouts 'BOO' at someone, and it was just animated in a really odd way and it just felt overly childish.  Anyway I could rant for longer, I am told it gets better after the 10 hour mark, currently I have far better games to play that would give me far more enjoyment and not require 10 hours investment for POSSIBLE good story/characters/fun.  If you like pressing the B button, this is a game for you.  If you do not like pressing B, I would stay away.

Party Hard is a pixel graphic strategy/stealth-em-up game. The idea of the game is you're a guy trying to get some sleep, your neighbours are having a house party, so at 3am you go around there and kill them all, even the DJ. From that you go on an anti-party psychopathic rage around America crashing parties and killing everyone in your path.  There's small (skippable) cut scenes between levels which help set the narritave, but aren't important to watch if you just fancy killing 40 odd people on a rooftop party instead.  The gameplay itself can be quite slow, but not to the point of where it's boring, to me it makes it feel more like the character you're controlling is cool, calm and collected; although it can be annoying when trying to outrun Police/FBI/Bouncers. There's a sprint bar which depletes *very* quickly, a slight shame if you're waiting to speed up your murderous rampage. Different 'events' can and do happen per level which can be a help or hindrance. SWAT might turn up, FBI might turn up, Pest exterminators might even turn up. Level design is nice and the mechanics are good, I think the odd thing could do with a slight tweak, but it still perfoms solidly.  The soundtrack to the game is pretty decent, and will end up staying in your head for hours after you've exited out of the game. The sound effects suit the game to a tee.   At the time of writing this I had completed the 'main' story within 7 hours, that's with several restarts on one level, so I suspect it could be completed within 5-6 hours. There's scope for replayablilty with extra unlockable characters with different abilities and probably even better a level editor and steam workshop thus allowing for arguably endless playability.   All in all, it's a decent, humourous game; a solid little indie title which I would recommend to anyone.

Fast-paced retro arcade bullet-hellesque bullet deflecting goodness.  I initially started out with the controller and eventually moved onto the keyboard+mouse. It's personal preference really as it is very easily playable with either control scheme.  As far as the action goes: it's blindingly fast and crushingly addictive. Different enemies handle different weapons which determine size, speed, and spread of the bullets. To fight them, our means of attack are your gun and sword; the latter can also be used for defense as it can deflect bullets.  With the ability to deflect bullets, there is also the added variation in how bullets are deflected; for example, the large single bullet is deflected into two slower bullets, while the bullets from the game's proclaimed 'best weapon' almost appears to move back at the same number and at the same speed. A nice feature is the ability to control the direction of the deflected projectiles depending on where you have hit them.  There's not too much difference between enemies. There seem to be three total but they really only come in two sizes: humanoid, and large alien size. Despite this all seem to move the same speed. While that may sound boring, I believe it simplifies things and helps add to the twitch-frantic action.  As hard as this game is, you don't ever really feel cheated and are forced to 'git gud'.  As far as visuals go, the masterpiece is the pictured soldier on the menu. The art style and symbols used remind me very much of Tsutomu Nihei - it's good stuff. I also liked the fact that as you survived and progressed and raised your ranking, the soldier's armour would change (both in game and on the menu) and reflect that change in rank; though I wish you could view and choose the different armour variants.  For $4USD, this is a great arcade-style game with an awesome soundtrack too.  PS: The 'Kensai Mastery' is impossible.  28/08/15: Kensai High Score = 114 -_-

Wow. This is one of the very few games that I can say I will probably never forget. POSITIVES: * Easy to grasp, well thought out controls. * Variety of upgradable items and abilities. * Awesome narration detailing your every move. * Choices that effect endings — replay value! * Amazing music — it truly fits the game. * Story — This is a game of the rare group that I can genuinely say elicited an emotional response.  NEGATIVES: umm...  About the story. This is a story-driven game. I see it as less of a game than an interactive storytelling device. And it does that exceedingly well. A story is always more memorable if you're part of it. In Bastion, you're shaping it as it goes along, giving an extremely memorable experience.

Holy ♥♥♥♥. Please don't mind my hours, as I've poured /hundreds/ into the vanilla game on the PS3. I'm really biased towards this game.  New Vegas, with confidence, is one of my favorite games of all time. I've never even heard of fallout when I picked it up. But man, it's a great RPG and an all around amazing game.  Well, for starters, you have a ♥♥♥♥♥on of choice and ways to approach things. Which route do you want to take to vegas? How do you deal with the guy who shot you? What skills would I need in this situation? There's a lot of quests and a lot of replayability. I've made at least 10 characters on the PS3. I think the best word I have for skeptics is that 'It gets better.' There is a lot to do, from questing to bartering to making choices that impact the ending. I think the best way to enjoy this game is to take your time with it, because it is easy to streamline it fairly quickly.  Compared to Fallout 3, it is ultimately the better game. I'm going to get a lot of hate from this, but NV has /way/ more content and more competent writing. FO3 had a great atmosphere and a better world, sure, but Vegas is the superior game. If you're looking for a middle ground, get A Tale of Two Wastelands. Or Fallout 4.  My two major complaints are probably no playing after the game's completion (A la FO3 Broken Steel and Skyrim, although there is a mod) and the pacing. I like it when the pacing is up to the player, but the game is hard to play until you get to vegas, and the game tries to streamline you down a certain path (Goodsprings- Primm - Nipton - Novac -Boulder City - Vegas). Although I've spoiled the game for myself by reading the guide, so maybe this is why I'm going on about this.  Wanna wear a suit and be banned from every casino? Go for it. Want to blow everybody up in those casinos with portable nuclear bombs while wearing a space suit?   ...You can do that too. Oh, and there's mods. Dust is a great game. The whole cutsy animated look isn't really something I'm used to so there's moments where I'm thinking 'Wow that's really overly cutsy borderline furry' lol like the Figet character whos your sidekick in combat throughout the game, but the game has a very deep and rich story with a tremendous amount of voice over content. There's a lot of talks with characters in the game and the way Dust doesn't remember anything makes for a very rich and engaging story where you are constantly discovering in pieces what hes about and what your purpose in the games story is. It's the kind of deep story that most games lack now days.    The learn as you start playing way they teach the mechanics of the game is also very well done. All of the little tutorial tips as I learned new moves actually displayed the name of the button that I had remapped certain actions to instead of a preset default key mapping. I was glad to see that the game knew I'd changed primary and secondary fire and correctly displayed it when guiding me through, most games fall short with this but not Dust. This game really has a very polished and good feel to it.   The combat is great hack and slash fun and the secondary fire with the ability to add in extra damage from Figets ability gives it some fun choices that work better for certain enemies. Overall this is a definite must buy for anyone who likes a good hack and slash game. Cutsy animation that some may hesitate at but if you want a taste of that hack and slash fun and the deep story I mentioned just watch the video trailer on the Steam Store page here and it's clear that this game is standing above most other games in its class.

Best Japanese life sim 11/10 like Skyrim with guns  But seriously, this is an amazing game to pick up and kill some time with your friends. Think Mario Party meets DnD with a bit of Magic the Gathering. You move around a game board, rolling dice each turn. After every roll, you will either pick up (or drop) stars, draw a card, use a card, or face an enemy. The stars serve two main purposes, using a card (many cards require you to sacrifice a certain number of stars to activate them), and reaching goals to win (which i will cover later). Each card you use is taken from a pile, made up of the decks you and your opponents construct before each game. These cards can damage enemies, give you or someone else stars, heal you, give you an extra set of dice to roll, and so much more. All enemy encounters are settled with a literal roll of the dice. The object of the game is to complete five goals before anyone else, either winning a certain number of fights or collecting a certain number of stars.  All in all, I think this is a great game, and I recomend it highly c:

This is the second in the Nightmares from the Deep series, and this episode gives a few nods to the first, but not enough where you can't enjoy this game on its own merits. Beautiful backdrops and objects, passable voice acting, and a moderately entertaining story round out the package. No replayablity really based on the story, unless you have to get those achievements, but still a nice game if you can get it on sale. about 4 hours playtime for the main story and 1 hour for the epilogue.

Reminding more a book than a game Soul Gambler is these titles called visual novels, where options are limited in reading plus decision making which has effects in the story.  So, while the gameplay in Soul Gambler might be thin it is the story which this is should be played for. I won't get into details but the tale is about mystery and romance and the manuscript is quite pleasant. This is not a child feature though because there is some drugs and murders included.   The chances to affect in tale are various and it should take 5 or 6 times to see every possible dialogue and event. The diversity of storyline is achived with the decision crossroads and four different attributes chosen in a beginning. Although 'all roads lead to Rome' - the choices made aren't remarkable. No matter how you played your cards you are more or less chained to follow the main agenda.  The graphically well drown Soul Gambler might not be the long lasting joy taking only 1 hour to complete, but the decision points add some re-play value for as long as player is not annoyed of already seen events.   In my opinion Soul Gambler is good for its price and great when on sale. A good title in evergrowing family of visual-novels.

I don't usually review games, but Attila is a game that I really enjoy and is also a game that has garnered a lot of hate from the Total War community, so I thought I'd give my thoughts on it. This review doesn't deal all that much with the gameplay aspects, although I will touch on that, but is more a defense of the game in general. Firstly, I'm pretty new to Total War. Shogun II was my first Total War, but I kind of just dicked around in it for awhile and never actually learned much. Attila was the first Total War I really sunk my teeth into, and has since become one of my favorite games. The only reason I include this is to let the readers know that I don't have a long relationship with the Total War franchise and this was pretty much the first one I really spent time playing.  Attila is not without its flaws, I'll admit that. However I think people are harder on it than need be. A lot of the negative reviews don't even address the gameplay, they are simply some half-♥♥♥♥♥ protest of DLC. I don't like DLC either, but I don't see it as being that bad in this game. There are quite a few factions to play without the DLC and there is a fair amount of variety between the given factions. Too quickly players forget that Medieval 2 only had like four playable factions. Attila has four European horde factions alone.  Coming to the gameplay, I have really enjoyed my time in Attila. It's a solid game with a breadth of fantastic strategy features. The family tree, building management, tech tree, and military strategy all come together to create a complex and entertaining experience. The battles are a ton of fun and the seiges can be epic as all hell. I think it's definitely worth checking out of you're a fan of strategy, especially with the Age of Charlemagne expansion coming up just around the corner.

Since this is a free and overall fun game i can safely recommend it Give it some time and then decide if it is worth it for you to spend money and look cooler If you are patient and use your ingame currency the right way then there is no real need to spend any money

Awesome throwback to the old school Action RPGs of the past like zelda ocarina of time and super mario 64, while also really paying the most tribute to wind waker! All in all for a game released in 2015 it feels like I'm playing one of the previously mentioned games, but with modern graphics and sleek UI!  I would recommend this game for anyone who appreciates this genre, you will probably put between 10-20 hours into this depending on if you want to 100% it or just finish it, it's quite a fun game to 100% too so if you like to complete games in your steam library this should tick quite a few completionists boxes.  The levels or areas are based around mainly islands that you discover as you visit the various locations in the world and slowly collect items for your character, it's similar in it's inventory to a zelda game were you can get a better sword and shield or unlike the bow.  It's more basic then a fully fleshed out old school nintendo game, but probably as fleshed out as there modern remakes. I will say one more thing though, the story was awesome and the writers did an excellent job of presenting a a story in the simplest fashion they could (so you arn't overloaded with text/information)  This works in it's favour so you are actually playing more of the game and the cutscenes/text you choose to read are just added benefits that can be ignored.  +Simple graphics style that is charming +Music and sound is also top tier +Story is interesting and delivered well for a game +Wind Waker Trbiute!  -Fishing, you arn't missing out on much here pretty rudimentary minigame added to break up gameplay I am guessing -Few bugs here and there, didn't experience anything game breaking in my playthrough. -Backtracking for 100% completion  -Didn't really take advantage of the boat mechanic in the game enough.  I might update this in the future.

Edit: Dey tryin'. Massive is being very up front about issues with this game and is actively listening to community concerns. Whether they are able to deliver meaningful solutions remains to be seen, but they're doing more than many developers would to try to address players' concerns.  Original Review:  I've never seen such poorly tested and poorly thought out content pushed out. This game is ridiculously buggy, Massive makes questionable (and downright poor) design choices, and with 1.1 has made the game so frustrating, tedious, and unrewarding, that I can't make myself log in anymore.  They can't even blame Ubisoft, because the core game sucks (but should be/almost is amazing!). It isn't microtransactions, it's that their PVP is unbalanced latent garbage. If the community finds a way to grind PvE more efficiently, they quickly nerf it, and bug out three more things while they're at it, and then make optimal gear that much more unattainable. It takes about an hour, give or take, to get the mats to craft one item. The chances of you rolling all the right major/minor attributes to complement a specific build sit somewhere near 1-3%. Are you freaking kidding? I don't have a year of playing The Division full time to build one optimized gear set. The chances to get a specific item you want as a loot drop are far worse, since you can't even control the gear slot or primary stat bonus.   The bottom line is that the RNG involved in loot and crafting will cause you to pour hours and hours of work into trying to acquire something, only to get several bad rolls and literally trash everything you made. It would be like if the hardest raid in a traditional MMO had a 97% chance of dropping nothing at all. There's a reason they don't do that. People wouldn't play. Technically you will get an item, guaranteed. Guaranteed to be useless.  Basically, they're continuously moving the finish line farther out, and stabbing you in the side with a stick while you hobble along towards it. Don't plan on having any fun once you hit level 30 in this game. If you do, they'll promptly shut the servers down and patch it.  The game has been out for a month, and they still can't fix the glitch where 10% of the time your group can't start a mission and has to quit. ... ... ...UI doesn't show correct player stats in main menu, you can't shoot random valid spots with healing AoEs, players are unrevivable, dailies don't reset, Xbone player accounts are disappearing, people don't get rewards for completing missions, you'll randomly get kicked out of game for 5-10 minutes due to mysterious errors. Two of four groups earlier today had this happen 30 minutes into a mission. Too bad there are no checkpoints so you're completely SOL at that point. The bugs are as numerous as they are confusing and infuriating. And they seem to never get fixed unless they allow people to get gear faster, then its a big deal. They don't want you getting things you want in this game. That's bad. Go back to grinding for scraps.  I really have no idea where the dark zone thing came from, because it feels like no one told the dev team about it until after the game was made. It basically feels like a straight up PvE TPS, and then they turned on player damage. It's awkward as hell and totally unoptimized for any kind of PvP combat. It'd be like hosting PvP on skyrim with 250 ping, it just feels clunky and wrong, in addition to the aforementioned dramatic latency issues. Also hacking, which they have totally failed to address.  Nice graphics though. Really sweet realistic glass breaking from bullets. Kudos there.

Imagine you are but a pixel, and it is through combining forces with other like-minded pixels that you will be able to take on anything that stands in your way. Well that's basically what Pixel Galaxy is with bright colors, a strong soundtrack, and lots of challenging fun.  You see in this pixelated space world, you start off as a single pixel, and if you get hit, well that single pixel gets hit, its game over, lights out, please try again. Here's the interesting thing though, its not all about dodging all that could destroy that single pixel. You see if you time it right the other pixels that might have appeared as foes can become part of you.  That's right, as you play through the levels you will have the chance to touch pixels that are moving, or perhaps are stationary on the screen, and in this action you can add them to you. Doing this will, hopefully, build a barrier of expendable pixels between damage and your starting, single pixel.  You will be faced with varying levels of difficulty, and of course which each higher difficulty new things will open up for you to play against. New bosses will test your skill, and you will definitely need to be fast at dodging while hopefully holding on to pixels that can shoot so that you can return fire.  I highly recommend that you play this game with a gamepad, there's something about controlling the pixel, or the collection of pixels with the left analog that just feels so natural. There's also the ability to rotate with the triggers, something that you are going to want to master so that you can evenly disperse your collection of pixels, be able to aim a bit better, and of course protect the starting pixel from damage.  This is the type of game that I never had an issue with coming back and playing some more. There's always that chance that I will man up and try to take on the next level of difficulty, and of course after getting my ♥♥♥ kicked I'll jump into a lower difficulty, kick some ♥♥♥, and once again feel really good about myself.  This is a challenging game that has quite a few layers to it, and unique experiences each time as how the pixels come out each time are random, which I have to say I both love and hate. I love because that means no two games are alike, but I hate because I can't learn through repetition to find the best solution. In the end though I think I'd much rather have the unique gameplay than the repetition which I could possibly burn out on.  If you are looking for something a little different, spacey, and pretty easy to pick up, you're going to want to add this game to your collection, there's no question that this should be a game that everyone should take for a spin. This game gets a 9.2 out of 10.

As a point of disclosure: I did not pay for this game, I received it as a gift and was asked to provide a review when I had a chance.   I wanted to like this game more than I actually do. At first, I thought the reviews that mention the US election must be off base, but it really is the basic premise of the game, albeit in an attempt at a humour. The game has you supporting a caricature of one of the candidates for the US election and going to war on their behalf along with some subquests that allude to various rumours that had been made during the election news cycle. The presentation is tongue in cheek, but how amusing you find this will depend on your sensibilities - I had a few laughs, but spent most of the time I've played so far groaning or rolling my eyes when confronted with the storyline and dialogs such as in tavern rumours. The 'Blood in November' alludes to wiping out as much of the opposing side as you can before November 8th, which was the day of the US election this year.   The overall quality of the writing isn't bad, but there are times it's very apparent that this game wasn't written by native English speakers and could likely use an edit.   As far as gameplay goes, it's the same system as Legends of Eisenwald if you've played that. You have a choice of one of three characters to use while you build up an army to fight with. The characters are a ranged attacking baroness, a melee fighting knight or a support mystic.   Combat plays out in a turn based system, but the strategy involved tends to be more about whether or not to get involved in a fight in the first place, which characters to bring with you, or which character to give equipment to more than anything else. Once you're in combat, the system boils down to prioritizing targets out of those characters you're allowed to target. You cannot move your characters independently of attacking, so ranged characters tend to have the option of firing at anyone they can see (assuming there aren't buffs or other factors that prevent a characters from being targeted), while melee characters must attack the closest enemy character and only have options if there is more than one character at the same distance. I find that the system feels fairly simplistic and not strategic which really hurts a game that relies so heavily on combat to attract you if the subject matter has fallen flat for you. I know that some players aren't bothered by the movement being part of the attack though, so your mileage may vary.   Other than that, the graphics are fairly attractive - the screenshots on the product page are pretty indicative of what you'll be looking at. I also enjoyed the music in the game and on the title screen. Unfortunately, the whole package didn't really draw me in.   If you loved Legends of Eisenwald and wanted more whatever the storyline, you'll probably like this. If you love any type of turn based combat, you may like this. If you prefer more complicated strategy or RPG mechanics or want a more involved story, this game will probably leave you feeling similar to me.

Nikopol is a point-and-click puzzle game, taking you to a dystopian world ruled by religion. You play Nikopol, an artist whose father was convicted of a crime before he was born. You have joined a resistance group to try to help, only things aren't always what they seem.  This is a rather short game - I finished it in well under 10 hours. Most of the time, I enjoyed playing, exploring the environment and solving the puzzles. The only parts I did not enjoy were the few times you have to act quickly or get caught/killed, because what you need to do to escape is not obvious. Luckily, the game lets you retry until you figure out the magic solution. Also, towards the end, the puzzles flucuated between solvable and impossible, and I needed to get a clue from an online walkthrough.  Overall, though, I'd give the game 3.5 stars out of five.

Before I get into my review of this game, I'd like to make two things clear:  1.) I love the books of Alice in Wonderland and Through the Looking Glass. Both are fantastic books and I recommend them highly to anyone who loves good literature. 2.) I saw the Tim Burton movie for Alice in Wonderland...and after playing just 5 minutes of this game, I can tell you THIS feels more like a Tim Burton creation than that abomination I saw on the big screen.  Now to talk about the game. I haven't finished it yet, but just from playing 100 minutes of it, here are my impressions: CREATIVE! One of the most fascinating aspects of the books is the sheer creativeness and ideas that flow from them, and this game captures the odd and nonsense designs that I feel the book was trying to create. The graphics, art, designs, and setting, all look like something straight from the mind of Tim Burton and his vision of Wonderland. The character of Alice is an excellent reincarnation of the classic character, despite how I'm used to the blonde wide eyed Disney version, this gothic recreation is a welcome one, and she plays the role well. The story is unique and gets you invested very easily.  As far as the gameplay is concerned, that too is rather unique. It's built as a platformer game and the world is filled with puzzles and riddles you have to slowly work through to make progress. And even when you make it through those, there are various and frightening foes waiting for you to battle. Switching often from a close combat slashing style game to a first person shooter game, there are a lot of ways the battles can shift and turn, making the game just as unbalanced and exciting as the setting.   If I had any faults with this game, I would say that there are a few bugs. Starting out in the game there were several moments where the game would slow down or freeze on me, however, the game quickly would fix itself and jump right back into gear. In truth, as far as I can see, there are no obvious problems with the game.   If you are a fan of Alice in Wonderland and want to see a gothic and darker view of the character and Wonderland, DEFINITELY give this game a try...and if you were like me and saw the Tim Burton movie, PLEASE give this game a try. You'll find this game is FAR MORE Burton than even Burton was. Get it while the gettings good!

The Wolfenstein saga was my first true FPS, my department head at the college I was attending in 1993 passed the shareware version to me and I was hooked. This chapter of the game is the most heart rending story of the FPS world to date. The look at the realities of war and how people are ground up by the ambitions of those who are 'protected' from the violence and make the rules is very true to life in my humble opinion. The action is intense and the choices made during the game play effect the outcome and is in effect 2 different games depending on the choice made in a pivotal point of the game.   In spite of the serious natter of the story line, I really had fun with it. The inclusion of the mini games of the original Wolfenstein 3D were a really fun, and brought back great memories of playing it when it was new. It really shows just how far the game world has progressed since the first 3D shooters began.   The game is not for the squeamish or faint of heart and definitely not for young children. When you go into the game, go in without expectations and above all, don't keep trying to compare it to the previous three. Especially in terms of the physics and control. As long as you stick to this, you'll find a fairly enjoyable game. It's pretty good, but not truly the real Sonic 4 it could have been. The reasons for this notably boils down to three issues in particular: Use nostalgia, progression, and level design.  First off is the appeal of nostalgia, it was unfortunately far too heavily use. With too much borrowing from previous games with the Sonic 1 style bonus stages, and nearly every boss being a rehash with minor tweaks at critical health. The level themes were all very close too in style to previous games, which made it felt more of a reimaging than a sequel.  Hand in hand with the nostalgia overload, the game was extremely conservative in the progression department. The bosses as mentioned above were a certainly a major notable case. However, lots of finer details are also affected by this as well. Gone are items introduced in Sonic 2 and 3, leaving only basic ones introduced in Sonic 1… namely just Shields, Power Sneakers, Ring and 1-Up monitors. Although not exactly bad simplifying things to focus on gameplay, it definitely is odd to have the first three progress in complexity and item count to have it go back to square one… Alongside this, the game doesn’t really introduce anything new to the series to give it that “Sonic 4” feel. The only new aspect added was the homing attack, which certainly met with mixed reactions. Though it does at least give it that new feel and gives it a progression between the classic series and the modern series putting in the homing attack.  Finally, the level design in the game is often quite questionable at parts. The first level is fantastic, along with the third… but just about every level after these two has quirks with them. Often you’ll find horribly booby trapped, cheap tricks, or heavily gimmicked sections RIGHT before the signpost… which means if you need rings for the bonus stage you can pretty much forget it unless you have the level memorized like the back of your hand. Now that’s not bad to put a challenge for the player right before the exit… but there are good ways to do it and then there are bad ways to do it. And if the traps before the signpost weren’t enough to keep your mind busy, you’ll likely have noticed how many times you came across cheaply placed enemies that offer not a challenge, but instead only frustration. Jumping on a spring or running through a speed booster shouldn’t result in an immediate collision with an enemy for instance. Then we have Mad Gear 3… certainly an experience.  However despite these shortcomings, I still find myself coming back to the game from time to time. It’s enjoyable, and is simple fun like a classic game should be. It has enjoyable graphics (though the shading is a bit of an unusual choice for the characters compared to the scenery), is challenging, and is a lot of fun to be Super Sonic again in a game instead of it being a story element. It may take a little to get used to it initially, but definitely worth a look into if you want a classic game experience.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Has taken me more than year to be able to conjure up a review, and I'm still not overly sure. My sessions still often end in 'Rome II has unexpectedly crashed,' I have unmodded legendary saves that won't load, and I cannot play for nearly as long as on the original Rome without getting bored.  My main concern is that my current legendary campaign, although almost complete, is no fun at all. Diplomacy on legendary seems to be something that escapes me: factions half way across the map declare war for no reason when I have good integrity and it is near impossible to sue for peace with anybody but, if you do, they often declare war again a few turns later. The battles are not neccessary challenging but the constant spam of agents and armies from every side make it such a grind to make progress. CA said they were limiting armies to make battles more meaningful with bigger impact; however, with a restricted number of armies, it just turns into mine being holed up, fighting many meaningless battles that take a couple of turns to recover from, with enemy agents constantly limiting their movement!  The modding community is, for me, the only thing that rescues this game as most of the crappy features in the original (save politics, family trees, and awful unit collision/charging) can be altered. Admittedly, a lot has been fixed since release, but there are still many things that seem inherantly broken to many paople; I really think I only play because of my interest in the time period, and due to a huge amount or nostalgia for Rome I.
