A game designed for tabletop and the odd evening with friends and beer that absolutely doesn't work online in an infinite open league; crippled by people powergaming and setting out to ruin your fun whilst a deliberately imbalanced skill system means that only a few teams can compete... combine that with a rule set that is set up to encourage 'fun chaos' but instead ensures you fail constantly even with perfectly calculated dice odds, and that failure can mean your team ends up outright killed, and the game just isn't worth playing.  I tried to love it, I really did; I have fond memories of a few games of the tabletop with my brother long, long ago. But online every single game devolves into the same cycle of Chaos, Dwarves, Chaos, Chaos, or some other brute team which can simply cripple your players and eke out a win... or a few people abusing the Matchmaking by having 5 or so L6 players hidden amongst some throwaway L1s, and then use the overpowered skills stacked on the individuals to humiliate you.  Those masses of positive reviews? They're from people who really don't care about the community, but will do what ever they want online and damn what it feels like to you to try and play for fun; they're the people still here years down the line that didn't care the game never took off, because they're in it for the shallow ego boost that comes from using an easy team to win match after match. Seriously, the game designed teams as Easy and Hard difficulty and then suffocates under finding literal easy mode teams in match after match after match...  I'm told Blood Bowl 2 rebalanced the teams to make them a bit more equal? But after paying nearly £100 to make this one complete, I'm loathe to let the developers nickle and dime me to an equal level for a game they're already witholding teams for DLC from ...  And they never fixed the bugs in this either! Clicking on any player during kick off breaks your ability to swap substitutes out. Players can simply sit on the Intercept roll if you throw the ball and they have a chance to catch it, and force your entire timer to run down. And it's hard to escape the suspicion that people have individual RNG seeds, with some being much worse than others, when you run into bad luck game after game... someone even went as far as telling me my dice were cursed the other day. It's possibly not true, but in a game SO luck dependent, if you're the sort of person who isn't going to abuse the rules for quick overpower, and finds it frustrating to see your strategy fail because someone else did, or the dice went against you, this game will be absolute torture to you.  I've installed it twice now, and it kills me every time because of that. I end up seething at people being gits because the rules allow it, and you can't stop them, and I can't even have fun with my own team. If this sounds like you too, for goodness sake don't go near this game, not even with the lovely Warhammer Lore.

First off, It's not a game. It's an interactive short story. When I say short I mean 1-2 hours depending on if you go threw everything.   Possible Spoilers Below! YOU'VE BEEN WARNED!  The Good:  Story is a decent one. Even though it was dragged on for way longer than it should have been. (Yes I know, the 'Game' is short as hell, but honestly the story could of been told in 30 seconds on one piece of paper from your sister as soon as you walked into the house)  Nostalgia: if you grew up in the 90's you probably can connect to somethings that were laying around the house.  Emotion: I guess if you have experienced some of the things that your 'Game' sister when through. In example: being a teenager who is stuggling with personal things and school, being bullied or growing up gay you may have felt a little more emotional than I have while playing. I however felt happy with how things turned out in the end, even though I felt cheated by the endless rollercoaster the 'Game' made me ride even though I didn't have to, to come to the ultimate ending.   The Bad:  Gameplay: It's not a game. If walking around a virtual world and picking things up counts as a game then I guess you can call it a game. However my idea of game involves a little more interaction than just walking around a house picking up pieces of paper.   Length: Short, not short enough? I guess I'm torn because I feel as if the story could be wrapped up in the first 5 minutes but then it wouldn't be much of a 'Game'. Still all in all its very short for the price tag.  Price: Way to much for the length. If this game was less than 5 dollars I wouldn't have a problem with it. However I paid almost $7 and it was 66% off. $20 full price is something you pay for a game that lasts 10+ hours and has some replay value.  Summary:  The only redeming value I got from buying this game is the story kind of sticks with you. I'm happy the way it ended. I felt good for most of the characters and how they ended up. I wish there was more of a story, maybe shedding more light on Oscar even though seems there's a pretty obvious assumption of why he was the way he was. (google it)  Score: 6/10 (8/10 if the price wasn't so high)

I can't seem to figure out the specifics. I make matches, trying to maximize the gem matches for my creature's affinity - sometimes I win quickly, other times I'm overwhelmed quickly. I know a little more of the mechanics than I'm typing here - their are gem color affinities, and good/bad enemy match ups, etc. - but the flow of 'battle' still seems mostly out of my control.    I'll try playing a little more, with a more serious mindset, to see if it suddenly clicks. But it really doesn't seem like the type of game you _want_ to give that much thought to - I doubt the end payoff is worth the effort. Sad to say I can't recommend it. Anodyne is pretty much Zelda: A Link To The Past. It plays the same and it feels the same. Thoug, it has it's own art style and graphics, it's own battle system and enemies, and another story of course. This makes Anodyne a complete different game from Zelda, while it will guarantee nostalgia and fun.  The screen size is a bit weird in the beginning, because Anodyne is also availabe on Android and iOS and is a simple crossover with no big optimizations, at least not for the user to see. It still plays in full screen and it is easy to get used to the screen format after some time.  The story itself is a creepy pile of nightmares, and I have yet to find out the subtle messege, the game seems to deliver. The surface of the story is easy too follow, so even if you're like me and need some time to understand things (duh!), you will have a good time with it.  7 out of 10 points for this well made game with much love put into it. Dyscourse is a weird game. It tries to force the player to make hard choices about survival, while attempting to be light and easy to play through. Each character is more of a cartoon than an actual fleshed out, relatable person in the story.   Dyscourse felt a bit confused about what it was trying to achieve. A short 'choose your own adventure' with funny dialog? Or a serious, thoughtful narrative? Owlchemy failed at both, which is really unfortunate because I liked the way the game itself was structured. Easy puzzles, lots of dialog and a solid art style.  I will not be recommending Dyscourse at this time, but who knows? Maybe the upcoming Indie Island DLC will be more confident about its identity.

If you are a hardcore Tolkien fan, then this is your game. I cannot stress enough how mnay times I've tried to learn proper Elvish phrases just because of this game. The amount of lore is outstanding for such a title. And there's rewards for gathering this lore. The voice acting in this game is phenomonal, making it feel less like a game and more cinematic the more you play it.  The gameplay is smooth and easy to get a hold of, so even if you are not a huge fan of combat you will still enjoy lopping the heads off a great number of Uruk-hai. Side missions exist to better prepare you for certain encounters, teaching you tactics and allowing you to hone your skills before you slip in and take down Sauron's army.  Oh, and the army. The Nemesis system is what sets this game apart (gameplay/combat-wise) from Assassin's Creed or Arkham. So many ridiculous characters will try to kill you, and you will come to learn their likes, dislikes, strengths and weaknesses all too well. They're not your friends, but you will know them just as well.  Oh, and this game has the best mounted travel and combat of any RPG. Skyrim horses climb vertically? Caragors can do the same AND eat the face off an Uruk.

Want to blow things up? Want to blow things up for profit? Want to blow things up for profit and mission completion? Want to blow things up for profit, mission completion, and loot? Want to blow things up for profit, mission completion, and loot IN SPACE!?  I LOVE BLOWING THINGS UP IN SPACE FOR LOOT AND PROFIT!  Alex Ayers and the crew over at Entheogen have made a great flight sim that works with both mouse and keyboard (personally I use a Nostromo N52, Mouse, and Voice Attack software), and uses slightly modified WASD controls. With the way loot drops, the variety of loot, and the humor in the references (albeit sans speech), it is a lot like Borderlands 2 in space. If you know me - or even if you don't - you will know that I love Borderlands 2 (as evidenced by my 1000+ hours gameplay), and I love this game almost as much in just a few short hours. :D  Does it have problems? Yes, but generally they are minor and do not effect gameplay. Such as accidentally moving a toolbar icon and not knowing where it is stored (there is no ability to lock the toolbar), the huge but unsortable inventory, or flying through another ship. These minor problems are overshadowed by:   - the sheer joy of blowing enemies to bits using one of the hundreds of weapons,  - watching enemies explode - as the game description says - like loot piñatas, - a not uninteresting story mode, - very cool original music - an RPG element including crafting and gathering (skill trees are coming in a future update), - a large and easy to use DB on the  Zigfrak.com  site that lists ALL THE THINGS, - and an awesome price (IT'S ONLY $5 USD!).  Any major problems are often quickly and directly addressed by the developers (as evidenced on their forums), and the forums - both here and on the Zigfrak site - are full of people that are knowledgeable, and information that is helpful.   My only hope is that one day we get co-op. ;)  In short, if you like flight sims or space, and FPS, then this is going to be a fun investment for you. It will provide several hours of fun and explosions, and that is just in the FREE DEMO. ;)

Depth serves as a breath of fresh air, coming out of the depths of the ocean as one of the most unique team based competitive game I've seen in a while. In Depth you take on the role of one of four divers, the rest being your friends, total strangers, or A.I. It's your mission to collect as much gold as possible all while dodging and killing the opposing shark. On the other hand you can play as the shark, and your mission is simply kill kill kill. Personally I found playing as the shark way to easy and as the diver way too hard, especially given the fact that your diver is so unresponsive. Infact I find myself hooking up an Xbox360 controller to my PC in order to get more clean, controllable gameplay. If you don't mind using a controller I'd highly recommend it. All and all Depth feels unique, fun, and above all suspensful. If you're looking for something new, or a place to make new friends Depth is the way to go

The greatness of this game can be easily lost on impatient people, but for those willing to give it some time, this game will not disappoint. I now understand what the creator means when saying it is a game of moments, and will share with you one such moment. The moment that hooked me. A moment in space and time that only I witnessed, that will replay and resonate with me over and again.   It was near the early stages of the game, I was in the midst of exploring some planet in order to find some zinc to use to build my first hyperdrive fuel canister. I do not know what zinc looks like, and haven't the slightest clue where to find it, so I land my ship on some undiscovered planet and start trekking. Scanning everything I see, I ponder what form zinc might actually take in this alien land. Is it an element? a mineral? Its used in pimple creams, might it be white? I think its a metal, maybe its in some kind of rock? Where do you find rocks, in caves? Oh, what ho over yonder, A CAVE!!  Jetpacking towards the mouth of the cave, I spy a sentinel patrolling, but its passive, and I haven't been disturbing the wildlife, so I pay it no mind and begin spelunking. Deeper I penetrate, finding gold and plutonium, carbon and platinum, but no zinc. After scanning and searching for nearly ten minutes, I decide this must not be the cave I was looking for, so I turn to depart. On my way out to the entrance, I decide to scan the local plants, get some info for my logbook, and maybe find some useful something-or-other, when I spot an odd looking bulb.  The bulb could not be scanned, but can be interacted with, how odd. Upon closer inspection, it seems that there must be something inside it. I press the interact button, and lo-and-behold, it splits apart like a giant banana, revealing some sort of intergalactic space pearl!! The space pearl has a value of roughly 27,000 spacebucks, and there are about 5 more bulbs all nearby, SCORE!!! But alas, I have no room in my inventory, so I start clearing items, recharging consumables, transferring, and sorting stacks to make as much room as possible without wasting any of my valuables, and finally have created 6 open inventory slots, so I begin looting.  Upon picking up the first pearl, my computer chimes in with a warning that sentinels have been deployed. That was fast. Wait, what was that, computer? Elite sentinels, you say? Well that escalated quickly, but I've probably still got some time before they appear, right? Wrong. Emerging from the mouth of the cave, I see the normal drones, followed by a few vicious mechanical dogs. No biggie, dogs have melee atacks, I'll be fine, I think. Not these dogs though, they've got dual laser cannons strapped to their heads. They only stop chasing to fire at me, and are very quick to pick up pursuit once again. I have no recourse but to flee, as my sheilds start depleting at a very rapid pace. Luckily I have an extended sprint perk that I picked up a few stops ago, and boy am I glad I had it installed, because I make it back to my ship just as my sprint wears out. Huffing and puffing, I climb into the cockpit, with my sheilds at roughly 10%, I slam on the W key to take off as the cockpit door SLOWLY clicks into place, and its off into the sky with me, laughing the whole way as I point the ship straight up and breach atmosphere.  Crossing into the cold emptyness of space, I see the local space station in the distance. Safe at last, I think; or was I? Excuse me, computer, I didn't quite catch that. Did you just say that my cargo is being scanned? By whom? Hostile, you say? Targetting me? Why? I'm just a lowly space vagabond, trying to get fuel for my hyperdrive. What have I got that is so valuable? Oh, right, the intergalactic space pearls. And thus begins my first space dogfight, I'm pretty sure I'm going down. Bobbing and weaving, I think back to what I know about space dogfighting, and the only voice I can hear is Peppy from Starfox, telling me to do a barrel roll. Frantically, I roll, and aim for an asteroid feild, hoping that the rocks will smash my pursuer's ship to smithereens. I speed up, and flip on him, track him, and let loose with my photon cannons, scoring some hits. I take heavy fire head on as we pass each other, but I am quicker on the turn around this time, catching my assailant mid bank as he is crossing my T. His starboard side is riddled with laser fire, and he spins out of control, only to explode in a fiery death a few seconds later. Acting purely on instinct I point myself at the space staion, and engage impulse speed as fast as I can, watching my arrival time click down. These are the slowest 8 seconds of my life.  I reach the station coming at a slight angle to the entrance, but I make the curve, and coast to a stop in the landing bay. My cockpit opens, and I emerge, victorious. I push my keybard drawer into my desk, spin my office chair around, and fall to the carpeted floor of my apartment, nearly hyperventillating with the adrenaline rush. I crawl on hands and knees into my kitchen, pull myself up with the refridgerator door handle, and drink 16 oz. of water in one gulp. I eat a few cheetos, and replay the events in my mind. I begin adding up the profit I will have made from the sale of the space pearls, it should put my bankroll up to around 500,000 spacebucks, halfway to that new ship I wanted. Would it be worth it to go back to that cave? Oh, no! I have no idea where it is!!!  Yet I will return to the planet's surface. Not for the fame, not for the glory, not for the women, and not for the spacebucks. No, I will return for still another reason. I MUST return, because I never found that zinc I was looking for in the first place.

app_name: Saints Row: Gat out of Hell
review_text: Gat out of Hell is another incredible open world playground with just enough new toys to play with to distract from its content problems. It is true that the game consists solely of side missions, with each set getting you closer to a cut scene that progresses the story along. The main campaign can easily be beaten in 4-5 hours of gameplay, although the game will hit the end credits before you run out of things to do in the city. The game's humor and style is still top notch, so whether you purchase at full price or wait for an inevitable sale depends solely on how much you love Saints Row.   At $10 this would be a 100% must buy. At $20, it's for diehard fans only.  Want an expansion on these thoughts? My full review is now up over at  Geekenstein.com .  www.geekenstein.com/review-saints-row-gat-hell/

many reviews of this game are poor becuase they come in expecting something it is not. This is not a military game, it is an economics game. It does that very well in my opinion. It does have a hell of a learning curve, which most people whine about these days when death comes in negitive numbers, not in battles lost. Having a military is extremely costly, that is truely to deter anyone from trying to turn it into a military rush game. THIS ISN'T A TOTAL WAR GAME, and it has nothing to do with the earlier grand ages rome. If you wanted combat, go else where. the combat model in this game is set up so that you dont have to pay attention to combat, troops are very fire and forget, and no amount of tactics will save an inferior army. The bandits are a little over powered for my tastes, given the massive military costs, but thats it for military complaints.  As towards peoples failures, this game was set up to be like the middle ages, in the middle ages NOT EVERYONE SUCCEDED. The best players of this game will still lose because of a random plague at the beginning or something of that nature.  there are parts of this game that are not intuitive, but anyone who has taken a college economics course will tell you that economics rarely is. And as i said before, this game is true to the economics of the time. Can you have a max size city? Yes, larger is always possible, but you have to be a master at logistics, and almost no one is.  TLDR: This game is fun, but has a harsh learning curve, and is about realistic economics, not warfare.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: This game would be awesome if not for all of the performance issues.   I'm running an i7 4790k with a Superclocked 780Ti and 16GB of ram, and I still see a lot of performance issues.  The Good:  The multiplayer is a pretty enjoyable, though the time to kill is seriously ridiculous. 'Melt' doesn't even describe what happens. It feels like a more in-depth Black Ops 2, as far as customization goes. None of the perks seem to be 'clutch' or 'must-have' as with some of the older CoDs. The Killstreaks are pretty comparable to the Killstreaks in Black Ops 2. The movement is pretty fluid, and it feels like a nice balance between the 2-D movement everyone is used to and the insane 3-D movement of AW without the annoyance of the Exo suits. Also, when it does run smoothly, it's actually a very pretty game. Lots of bright colors, smooth edges/texture resolutions. That's of course, IF it runs smoothly (which only happened the very first time I loaded the game).  The Bad:  Did I mention the time to kill? Because it's ridiculous. There is very little leeway in terms of being caught in a gunfight and being able to get to safety/escape. Some of the guns seem waaaaaay too accurate with too little recoil. Of course, that will inevitably be balanced as the competitive side on consoles takes off (Treyarch liked to balance around competetive a bit too much in Black Ops 2 imo). Also, the 'feel' of looking around whether with mouse/keyboard or with a gamepad does not feel natural. I'd dare to say it can feel awkward at times, but I'm not sure if that's because of the performance issues or whatever. Fruthermore, the maps feel incredibly small. I know Treyarch says that they are larger than most of the Black Ops 2 maps in terms of square feet, but it does not feel like it. Personally, perception is everything.   The Ugly:  The performance issues. There are just so many of them. First, there's the memory leak which will be glaringly obvious to everyone. Even with all the ram I have (more than enough for any game out right now. Hell, 8gb is more than enough tbh), I'm still seeing my memory usage hovering at ~90%. It is definitely NOT optimised for PC. The frame rate loss is abysmal. One second you could be running 60+ fps, and then for the next 5 seconds be at ~5 fps. The stuttering is highly annoying, and not conducive to playing for enjoyment. Furthermore, this stuttering can actually cause your mouse input to lag, which is just stupid. Even when I switched to a gamepad, it felt like the problem still existed though in a slightly less annoying manner.   Rating:  3.5/10  There's a good game in there. It's just buried deep beneath a pile of S**t, and the S**t is 300 ft tall, covered in chainsaws, and on fire.  I would recommend not going beyond your 2 hours of game time or 14 days while waiting to see if they patch anything, then if not, get a refund. Otherwise, I would wait until it goes on sale/gets patched sufficiently before purchasing it.

Hope you don't hate cute, because boy oh boy this game sure is cute, pretty sure my testosterone levels have dropped some by playing it.  Let me start this off by saying this game is a Roguelike, and that I've always liked Roguelikes but they are very hit or miss with me, I can't get into Stone Soup but loved Shiren the Wanderer for example, if this sounds like you, who likes them but finds that Dwarf Fortress is to 'hardcore' for you, then seriously consider this one.  Sproggiwood may be on the casual side of the spectrum of Roguelikes, but don't let that make you think that this one is any less of a Roguelike or that it will be an easy one, you'll definitely need to plan your attacks and with proper movement if you plan to beat this, less you be overrun but duplicating slimes.   I'm not gonna lie, I have no idea what the story's about, some Sprite, some God, Mushrooms, whatever. I look at the screen during the cutscenes and go OMGoud sooooo cute, then before I know it all the talking is over and I'm in a dungeon killing things and getting loot.   Gameplay there's not to much to say, it's a Roguelike.... is simple but still hard to master, you have 6 classes to choose from, but 5 of them are locked and need to be unlock first, they each have 4 very distinct skills and play very differently from one another, this is all great for the replay value BTW! Dungeons are short, which is a good thing! they take about 5 to 10 minutes to beat. By beating dungeons (or even dying in them) earns you gold, and with that you can buy upgrades and better equipment that makes your little guy better for even easier dungeoning, shopping is all done from your little Village or Home Base of sorts, that can be customized to your liking, but customizing things around in your Village like adding sidewalks or making a garden are all purely a cosmetic thing, they have no impact on anything unfortunately.  That's probably the only negative thing I can say about Sproggiwood, is that the 'Village' part seems empty and pointless, they could have made it into something fun, something to do in between dungeon runs, like by having a garden in your Village would let you harvest ingredients on a timer, then those could be made into powerful one time potions, or having the forge/blacksmith building craft powerful equipment from materials you would find in dungeons or maybe from rare drops from the end bosses, I dunno seems like a missed opportunity here.  Anyway let's wrap this up, if you like Roguelikes, Casual Roguelikes, Coffee break Roguelikes, Cute things, Steam Cards, Achievements, then get this game, it's a very polished gem, I highly recommend it.

This is a pretty tough little game, even on the 'easy' difficulty setting. Essentially, you have a bunch of drones at your disposal, six of which you'll pick to go out and do the next mission. Some are 'assault', some are 'heavy', and some are 'precision' (dictating their stats and weapon of choice). Combat is turn-based and is similar to XCOM, albeit a bit more simplified. Some things do bug me about it...one being that the stationary lasers scattered around the levels can shoot through walls and barriers going the opposite direction. I also don't like that right click activates/fires your weapon...it just felt more natural for it to deselect the unit you have selected. I've shot my own drones by accident more than once because of it. Despite these small problems, the game is fairly enjoyable. Missions take 15-30 minutes a piece, longer if you're overly cautious about your moves, so there's a lot of play time here if you stick with it. You can also upgrade your drones via credits and tech points, with each having its own bank of tech points to spend. Give it a look if you're in the mood for a simplified (but still challenging) XCOM-esque game.   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=J38ZjHxAh-s

Downloaded this game on a whim. Really glad I did. I tried a vast variety of MMO's since I burned out of WoW 4 or so years ago. None really sucked me in. In a few hours, I didn't want to stop playing this game. The combat is really well fleshed out and fun. The dungeons are fantastic. Coming from WoW and Tera, they take them to the next level. Everyone needs to pull their weight, and at the end you get your ranking on how you did compared to the others. This helps keep you focused trying to be the best you can be. There is a cash shop, but this is the best kind of cash shop in a F2P game. There is absolutly NO P2W. Mounts, pets, costumes, home decor etc... nothing with stats on it. You can't get armor, gear or anything in regard to stats. The only items might be flasks for increased XP. I don't consider help getting 1-50 being P2W.   Zones are fun. Like WoW, there is no load screens zone to zone. Its all open and immersive. The styalized graphics are fun and look good. The sound is well done and the music really shines. Try the game out. Its free. Nothing to lose!

This game is pretty fantastic. It's got a few quirks here and there, but it's fun, the characters are fun to play, there's build flexibility through talents and items (perfect for the min-maxers out there!), and it's written so, SO well.  Since everyone's biggest questions will be about how Battleborn stacks up against Overwatch, here's a quick rundown:  * Battleborn will require more time investment per game, whether you're playing PvP or PvE. (I haven't played much PvP, but PvE is usually 30–60 minutes.)   * Battleborn has much greater depth in its story, gameplay, characters, and settings right now; I'm not sure how Overwatch's characters will be developed as their other media are released (comics, videos, etc.).  * Battleborn missions give me the sense that the game requires more skill than Overwatch—I haven't had to take a break from Battleborn because I was getting killed by too many lucky or inadvertent shots, unlike Overwatch.  * Performance-wise, Overwatch runs more smoothly than Battleborn. Video can get choppy in Battleborn, particularly in outdoor areas. Right now, this is the single biggest complaint I have about the game.   * Overwatch doesn't have much in the way of player collision, but Battleborn does. You can't shoot or run through teammates. (That's my other complaint.) It takes time to get used to that and gets frustrating sometimes.  Ultimately, the games fill two different niches for me. When I just want something to play for a few minutes, I'll go with Overwatch. There aren't any gear changes or additional talents to have to think about in order to make your character the best at what you're trying to do. You can just jump in and start playing. When I'm ready to roll my sleeves up, push the furniture back, and spend some serious quality time playing a game that requires me to make gear and talent choices that'll affect my ability to fulfill a role, when I want to sink my teeth into a game with substance, Battleborn's the one I play.

I originally played Bioshock a decade ago. I remember running it on my crappy PC and having some issues at the time with occasional bugs. In fact Bioshock 2 was my favorite of the trio and I had intended to buy the remastered version soon.   The game is a bug-ridden nightmare. It is hard for me to even fathom that my souped up new computer, of which I am incredibly proud, of cannot run Bioshock as well as I could run this game 10 years ago on a computer held together by glue and a boys obsession with video games.   I was excited to see a patch came out recently. I shouldn't have been. It actually messed up my settings so badly that for some reason it thought I was using an X-box controller? and then it reset my difficulty without asking so I was no longer on a Hard run. Thus that achievement is no longer attainable until I restart again and I already watched the start of Bioshock like 10 times because my computer kept crashing.  My advice: If you have played Bioshock before then this is nothing new or good. Do not buy. If you have never played Bioshock before, wait to see if they fix the game. If not, buy an older version.

review_text: Out of all the original dawn of war games, this is my favorite. Something very simple makes this particular game stand out: fortifying individual areas on the strategic map. Recettear is an interesting combination of store simulator and dungeon crawl RPG. You're a young girl named Recette. Her father is gone and left her with a massive debt, and Recette runs a store to earn money to make the debt payments. In the main game, you have to get through about a month's time in-game, making payments each week.   If you can't make a payment, you will get game over. PRO-TIP: If you know you can't make a payment, spend all the money you do have on items. The items you have will carry over, but your money will not.   If you make all the payments, you beat the main story of the game, and you will unlock other modes.  Where does the dungeon crawl part come in? Recette can hire an adventurer to go in dungeons. Recette gets to keep any items found, up to the limit of items the adventurer can carry. It's a way to get free things to sell in your shop, but also you will find items that you can use for crafting into other items. You get to control the adventurer while in the dungeon. Each adventurer controls differently, so it's worth trying to use all the characters.  The best part about Recettear is there's tons of replay value, especially if you are one of those people who wants to collect all of the items to fill out the item catalog.  Probably the only bad things about this game is some of the items have extremely low drop rates, which can be frustrating, and the music can get pretty repetitive. But overall I recommend anyone who likes RPGs or simulation games give it a try!

I started playing STO during beta and, like many others, after release soon maxed out a toon and started to wonder what the end-game was, PvP turned out un-balanced and dissapointing, and exploration was repetative, although the episodes (missions) were always rewarding. Being a trekkie I stuck with it as long as I could but managed to get pulled away by friends onto other games and MMO's easily (yes including SWTOR...) but returned occasionally to see what had changed.   Recently I rejoined after about a year's break and am really enjoing it. The various game play changes and additions, season expansions, and the defocus of PvP, have made this into a great game delivering the online Star Trek experience I was expecting originally. The new seasons (to me everything from Solane Dyson Sphere onwards) are great and embody the Star Trek experience with the exception that I'm in the captains chair.  Well turned around devs, can't wait for the New Dawn expansion now, but at least I have some time to catch-up.  For those considering the game: - It's free to play, and playable as free to play, although I have sub'ed for the extra's... - Feels like Star Trek, Looks like Star Trek, Sounds like Star Trek, - Great official storyline (seasons and episodes) and player added content with lots of great episodes, - Extensive customisation (player, ship, bridge officers, duty officers), - Space combat and Ground combat, but you still feel you are in the same game and plying the same mission, - Lots of extended and end game content now, so much so you can easily get distracted from the storyline, - More PvE than PvP,  - PvE - can go it alone and get a full experience, joining a fleet adds to this experience, - A lot to get your head around now though, but you should expect some technical depth in a Star Trek universe, - and it's Free-to-Play...  Still a few things I'd like to see added/changed (I still want to explore the various ships internally rather than the same layout on each other than the bridge), but definately worth a re-installing and spending the effort getting familiar with the changes if you gave it up a while ago.

This game is all right. It's a straight-up tower defense game, but doesn't really bring anything special to the genre. There is unfortunately not a great deal of towers in the game. Once you've seen them in action, you've basically seen the game. I guess this can be seen as a good thing, since it means you must rely on placement and tower knowledge rather than brute force building.  I appreciate the amount of work that went into the game. The stages and gameplay are clean and easy to work with. I wish I could see the damage numbers my towers are doing, though. The stat bars don't really tell me anything useful.  There's quite a bit of content (with DLCs)! It took me long enough to complete every stage at least once without losing cores. I'm not even going to attempt the additional challenges per stage, though. I've seen enough.  To anyone who decides to pick this up, my only advice is to remember that BACKSPACE loads from your last autosave, and you can hit it several times to backtrack further. This is quite useful for attempting perfect playthroughs.  Overall I'd rate this a 7/10. Would I recommend it? Ehhhh. I kind of liked some Flash TD games better.

I must admit, as a long time Batman: Arkham series fan, I couldn’t wait to buy this game but…on first release, I was horrified to read all the negative reviews – and then it was removed from Steam! When it was “fixed”, I still wasn’t sure. Steam reviews were still mixed and after such a bad release, did I really want to part with some hard-earned cash for this?  Well, I waited…bought it for a discount and boy, was it worth the wait!   Arkham Knight's PC version is awesome! It is so much fun, the way this series has combined fighting, stealth, and detective work with kick-a$$ stories and characters has been top notch, and Arkham Knight doesn’t disappoint.  And now…you can drive the Batmobile!  I know lots of gamers have felt the Batmobile element to be not that great – but I absolutely loved it. Tank battles, Riddler Challenges, Boss Fights, even just tearing around the streets of Gotham. It’s an awesome addition to the main elements so popular throughout the series.  Overall, I’m absolutely thrilled with the game – in addition to the main game, which has a really great storyline with a few twists and turns, there are plenty of side missions to keep you busy and which fit in well with the Gotham City atmosphere.  The graphics are stunning and I found no problem for a GTX970 running at 1080p 60Hz. I haven’t tested at higher resolutions though. Gotham City is such a hell hole but, as Batman, you wouldn’t want it any other way!  Overall, I highly recommend, especially for fans of the series – you’ll love it!  8/10

A dating sim for the young witch in all of us! Like Harry Potter, with a liberal dose of anime and without all that uptight britishness that makes me kinda hate it. Also a liberal dose of progressive gender and sexuality, which is always a fun one, and racial politics too. (That said, I was a *little* offput by the 'marriage is kind of a year-long thing' plot point, even if it makes sense in context...)  Don't be fooled - this is mostly a dating/life sim. There are 'dungeons', but they're more obtuse and annoying puzzle roadblocks I could honestly do without. Persona 3/4 this ain't. Also, you have to be a girl, though you have a wide range of options for your love life, three straight and two gay, and a wide variety of other non-romance events. Romance is legitimate and well-handled - the opposite of Bioware's efforts. (Oh god, flashbacks to terrible competing english VN Planet Stronghold DDDDDDD:)  Overall, takes itself just seriously enough not to be a broad parody, but not so much that it's hard to get into or requires heavy investment. (The default character's name is Mary Sue, if that tells you anything.) Highly recommended for anyone who's even a little into the idea, and even better, sequels are on the way!  Sadly, the best character is unromanceable, despite her obviously being open to the idea of a relationship with a student and a lady. :C Oh, Professor Potsdam, how I long for some private lessons with you... ;o; (maybe she's married? i dunno.)

For the most part, I throughly enjoyed this game. Even though I have only actually watched Alien, it was obvious this game was designed to give you an authentic Alien horror expeirence. Personally I didn't find this game to be too scary (but I can definetly see why it would scare others), only a few things made my heart race, but I still throughly enjoyed myself overall.   Without spoiling anything, the game's story is set 15 years after Alien, and is very well written, well worth the time you have to put in. However, I was disappointed by the ending, which was most likley set up to sell a sequel or DLC, which is a shame considering the rest of the game was so good.   While I did enjoy the game play, and thought most of the mechanics were very well done, I did find parts of this game to be either repetative or enraging. It became rather tedious to have to constantly hide for long periods of time while you were only supposed to be moving across small areas. The amount of save points in the game make it obvious that you're going to die a lot, and that's exactly what happens. If it wasn't the Alien it was the damn creepy androids. The sheer amount of deaths, usually in specific areas of the game, did tend to take away from the experience the game was trying to provide.

I'll be honest with you, I forgot I even owned this game. I must of got it in a Humble Bundle or something. Regardless, I finally got a chance to play this game, and was simply  blown away .   This game is a singleplayer/coop classicly themed side-scroller, built in a world of war, espinitoge, cloning and genocide.... featuring chickens, penguins and others. Seriously. The storyline is actually extremely engaging, controls are straight forward and responsive, and there is a good variety of missions and strategy to be employed. I found myself determined to keep going forward and overcoming any chapter I was dying in.   So, fan of action/puzzle type side scrollers? You shouldn't be disappointed with this title.   TLDR; solid fun, surprisingly deep storyline, challenging gameplay - 8.5/10

Though hampered by the occasional bout of unpredictably frustrating physics, Tiny Brains is unique and creative enough that you forget to care. It's clear that the game was designed with multiplayer at the forefront, as playing solo is possible but significantly less rewarding. Multiplayer can be in the form of couch or online co-op or competitive play, and features gameplay so fun that it's difficult to get too worked up over the potential failures of your teammates.  The game never wants you to take it seriously, and that framework is part of what makes the game work so well. It's charming, packs a killer ear-wormy soundtrack, and offers brain teasing gameplay that will no doubt remind you of Portal. Though the main story campaign is pretty short, the package you get from the multiplayer is more than worth the price tag.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: What can I say?  I thought that the combination of Kiefer Sutherland voice acting, Snakes iconic character, and the MGS series would be a dream combination.  I was so wrong.  This game, whilst having a large map, feels barren and lifeless. There is only one main route that you can take whilst travelling, which makes all of the environment feel bland and pointless, since you cannot go over much of the terrain, not that you would want too much anyway, since all you will ever spend your time doing is using the foulton device on troops since there are no real side quests etc, in open world mode. At least, that is how it feels on the Afghanisan map. I have not spent much time in the African Map yet, but it seems to be the same process of knocking out an A.I combatant and Foultoning them, for no apparent reason, other than it being something to do because your bored.   The open world feels pointless, poorly designed, barren, bland, wasted and very poorly thought out. The difficulty of the A.I does not help either and renders stealth almost pointless what with their super human ability to be able to detect snake a hundred meters away.   I don't know much about the online aspect of the game, but from what I understand, it is replete with hackers and the usual complaints.   Furthermore, I was dismayed when I discovered that this game featured an in game, real world money, shop which uses real world money for purchasing FOB related items. I have not used the in game shop, and I have no intention of using it. It has very limited appeal to me, and almost nothing of any value in it, to my mind anyway. I commonly associate in game shops with MMO's and free to play titles. What were Konami thinking, when they made the decision to put an in game, real world money, shop in MGS 5?   An MTS System does not ♥♥♥♥ing belong in an Open World, Stand Alone, Sand Box Game, nor in any MGS game, especially when the game came with a £40 price tag, originally, anyway.  The ONLY area in which the game shines, is in the Customization department, and even then, there are not enough options to customize weapons and equipment.   Part of me wishes that I had not bought this POS to be honest, part of me is glad that I bought it on sale, but all of me is convinced that this was one of the most over hyped games of the year, as well as one of the largest POS games of the year.  The dream that should have been Snake and Sutherland in one character, turned into a nightmare of a completely over hyped, completely over priced game, and if this is a sign of the direction which MGS 6 will go in, then Konami can keep MGS 6, since this is the worst MGS game which I have ever played, and to be frank, I don't even think that you can call it an MGS game, just by putting 'Metal Gear Solid' in the title. Rather than a game, it feels like it has actually been designed to be little more than a rushed, poorly thought out, Konami Cash Grab, using the MGS trademark to do it.  Verdict; Avoid it like it is the plague.

Civilization and the roads we take  this is the best out of box civ experience i have had to date. i started my civ journey with the release of civilization 3 after watching a buddies dad play civ and alpha centuri and was my introduction to strategy games. since then i have logged more hours into the civ games as a series than i care to admit.  civ 6 is beautiful, the map drawing on the fog of war, the animations of the leaders, and the little things like day night cycle and the waves from the ocean crashing on the coast lines. it can take a few hours to understand districts and how vastly important they are to your future. at the begining of my first play through my founding city was treated like i treat them in every game resources and farms however i waited and put off districts thinking meh cant be that important and next thing i knew i was behind and it took me the entire renassance era aka(7 gameplay hrs) to get my footing and lead. districts are a nice feature and will keep you focused on placement and priority on your civs end game goals, it is not forgiving if you do not plan for the far future.   the addition to creating armies and corps is nice but the boost in my mind is really not that big, dont get me wrong it takes away the 1000s of units scattered around but also feels like you are just deleting an unit not creating a vastly stronger one. the AI leaves something to be desired, their combat effectiveness is poor not knowing how to attack a city or defend one, if they had an understanding of unit movements and placement i would have easily lost half my empire and would have been a limping dog for most of the game.   now for the last bit, i have always loved the nuke explosion and with this game when you drop the thermo-nuclear warhead on a city the resulting explosion and devastation will make your nipples hard.  to wrap up i strongly recommend this to civ players old and new.  4.5 out of 5 nukes

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Story's ok so far, characters are ok as well. That would be great if this game was just a choose your own adventure book with graphics, but it isn't. In some dumbass attempt to justify calling these interactive storytelling things 'video games,' Telltale shoves quicktime events into everything, which luckily, are still broken.  Fix/remove the ♥♥♥♥ing quicktime events, or at least add gamepad support. Whenever a QTE occurs, rather than having the mouse start at the center of the screen (which would kinda make sense), it starts at whatever point it was moved at prior. A point which is obscured whenever you aren't prompted to use the mouse. The same is true when a dialogue choice pops up. Did people not playtest this? Its mechanics are ♥♥♥♥ing garbage. When dialogue pops up, just put some numbers next to the choices and let us hit the corresponding number key. That way we wont have to find where the ♥♥♥♥ing mouse is everytime they pop up.  Also, one of the QTEs was telling me to tap Q (how intuitive, tap the buttons that your fingers dont rest at), and the input wasn't registering. I thought, 'maybe I'm tapping TAB by mistake,' but I looked and nope, I was tapping Q. Thankfully (should I even be thankful for this?) the game allowed me to survive that failure, but another 'tap Q or die!' QTE shows up and I ♥♥♥♥ing died. Game over. 'Maybe it's my keyboard,' I thought as I tabbed out, but nope! Keyboard is working ♥♥♥♥ing fine. So the game broke because it couldn't register that I was tapping a key. YAY!  Maybe this ♥♥♥♥ makes more sense (or just isn't bugged to hell) on consoles with a controller or something, but I definitely DO NOT recommend picking this heap of ♥♥♥♥ on PC.  EDIT: lmao at the idiots who disliked this review. What are you saying, that you LIKE these ♥♥♥♥ mechanics? Are you saying that you would recommend a buggy game to people? Or are you assuming that I'm lying about my experience for no reason? Damn get your heads checked.

It's 'fun' the way a Skinner box is 'fun.'  The goal of the game is to rach level 101. You do this by managing sliders that control the percentage of effort going into resources, recruitment, construction, and army. The game itself consists of that plus clicking Start Day/End Day ad nauseum. Occasionally there are fights where you get attacked and can choose to defend yourself or retreat. This seems entertaining at first but the fights are heavily RNG based to the point that around Lvl 20, ca rounding error WILL get you one-shotted on a regular basis. If you win the fight you get a small amount of gold for buying upgrades. If you lose your population resets to 0 and you go down a level. If you retreat, which you can ALWAYS do, there are really no tangible consequences.  To beat the game: Set builders/army to 0, put everything into resources/population development, retreat from every battle, and dump all of the gold you get from leveling into Town bonuses. Hit Start Day -> Retreat -> End Day on repeat for 10-20min. Be greeted by bland 'Congratulations! You won!' screen. There, I just saved you $0.50 and an hour of your life.   ♥♥♥♥ this 'game.'

A little more simple than most other Paradox games, however it has made the Hearts of Iron series alot more accesible. The trade off is a little less depth than say the Europe Universalis or Crusader Kings franchises, but the streamlined feel gives this title a nice polish. It is also less sandbox than the other franchises and you will have less freedom than in the last Hearts of Iron entry, due to the focus tree concepts which direct the major nations down very specific paths. The variation is in the timing when nations will trigger events, but they will all end up playing a very structured and rigid way; something which goes against what I find the most appealing aspects of Paradox grand strategy games. Taking that into consideration I cannot give this title more than an 8/10, but the newfound accesibility of this franchising is very encouraging.

10/10 will create European Fascist Alliance as Germany, storm Britain, France and the USA with help from all of Europe and create a new world order again.  No seriously this game is amazingly fun to play, compared to previous HOI titles this is the most easiest and user friendly one to date. It does have its problems though, the AI is still as dumb as a pile of bricks and playing as any nation other than the major powers is a bit lackluster (seriously the tech tree is completely bland and the focus tree can be blazed through before 1940, leaving you a bit overpowered when you go against the major powers), but with the right mods and a bit of imagination this game can be really fun to play.  If you've got an entire week free or don't mind not socialising with your friends and having a stable relationship, this game is for you, if not then I'd stay away and go back to call of duty 2 and WAW for your WW2 fix.

The key to good horror is immersion as it's only really frightening if you feel it's happening to you. Amnesia accomplishes this sort of immersion quite neatly through the use of simple light mechanics. There is nearly constant darkness, so it's impossible to guess what's around the corner. You're never allowed a real rest or relief as there's no certainty to what comes next. The player himself can't really see through the darkness either which left me with my nose an inch or two away from the screen, straining to see. Even in the middle of night with all the lights out, I couldn't see well. This is both a blessing and a curse as it does add to the overall experience, perhaps even makes the experience, but it really is a strain to play and that does limit each session. Amnesia doesn't rely on jump scares, just an ever-present physical threat. There is no combat mechanic, no way of fighting back, and anything that catches you will kill you quickly. This has the player entering a mad dash anytime an enemy is spotted with the knowledge that they'll be dead if they stop or hit a dead end before losing it. The player ends up, unintentionally of course, recreating the classic Blair Witch-esque visual as they sprint through similar looking tunnels with almost no light at all. Amnesia continues to build atmosphere through the slowly unraveling story which can only be pieced together by meager bits of information found throughout the house. The player must uncover what would make a man erase his memory and why he would be so desperate to kill a man that seems to want to help him before being killed himself by whatever it is that makes this house so dangerous. There is a mystery to solve and no telling what's lurking in the distance. The lamp's oil will run out shortly, and with it the ability to see the path ahead, can you afford to stand around and wait?

The first question that comes to mind when most people see some screens from Borderlands or watch a trailer for the game is, 'Is Borderlands an RPG or a shooter?' Well, that's not necessarily an easy question to answer. On the one hand, it's far more an RPG than a shooter with its classes, experience levels, skill trees, quests, and all of the usual trappings of an RPG. On the other hand, combat is not a slow-paced methodical affair, being neither turn-based nor a simple matter of selecting the attack button and letting the CPU roll the dice. You need to aim, move, strafe, and everything else that you need to do to survive in a first-person shooter. So what we have here may be one of the finest shooter-RPGs. A hardcore RPG gamer may not appreciate the twitch-based combat, and a hardcore shooter gamer may be a bit put off by the fact that there's some dice-rolling going on behind the scenes (what do you mean I'm too low level to hit that monster???), but everyone else should have a great time with the game.  Borderlands takes place on the planet of Pandora, a backwater planet with a character that's a bit Mojave Desert and a lot Road Warrior. Pandora is the kind of planet that wouldn't garner a second thought from anyone anywhere else in the galaxy except that it's also home to The Vault. The Vault is an ancient structure left behind by some long-gone alien race, but it's rumored to contain treasure and artifacts of vast power. The presence of The Vault has attracted the attention of fortune seekers from all corners of the galaxy, in spite of all of the dangers on Pandora from the testy local fauna and aggressive bandit groups. You are one of those fortune seekers, and while it may seem that exploring The Vault would form the bulk of your adventure most of your efforts will be directed at just getting there - but that's alright because Pandora is a pretty interesting place in its own right.  Borderlands gives you the choice of one of four different characters, and unlike a full RPG there's not really much available in the way of character customization. There are four classes available, a soldier who uses all variety of weapons, a berserker who's basically a tank and can cause some serious damage with his fists, a hunter who specializes in sniper rifles and has control of an attack bird, and a siren who's a stealth-based character with the ability to turn invisible for short stretches of time. Each character provides a different experience, and that experience can be further tweaked by specializing in one of the three skills trees available to each character. While you can certainly complete the game as any of these four classes, it's when you play the game in co-op mode - either split-screen or on Xbox Live - that you'll realize how well-balanced a team these four characters make. And when you do play online, the story progress will be set to that on the host's machine but the level of the enemies will be adjusted to reflect the mix of levels of the characters taking part in the game.  One of the great things about Borderlands is that there is always plenty to do. In addition to the story missions there are always a number of other missions available, either from characters that you meet in the game or from job boards in the friendly settlements. Even after you finish with The Vault, you can return to the wastelands of Pandora and continue taking on side quests.  The developers of Borderlands must have spent at least a little time playing World of Warcraft, because they understand how much fun (and how addicting) it is to find a huge variety of loot. There are an amazing number of weapons in the game to be scavenged from defeated enemies, discovered in foot lockers, or pulled from corpses discovered in the wastelands. There are several classes of weapons such as pistols, assault rifles, shotguns, and sniper rifles, and they come in standard and rarer varieties. Each is rated on damage dealt, range, and ammo capacity, but some of the better ones will include the ability to use special ammunition like incendiary rounds, fire grenades, or even come with blade attachments to increase melee damage. You'll spend a fare amount of time in your inventory screen, deciding which combination of weapons to have equipped, which to save for later or to sell, and which to drop to make room for something else, but it's more a labor of love than a chore. The game is even kind enough to mark loot in the area with a small beam of light, and you'll more often than not decide it's worth the risk to fight through a group of enemies just to see what's sitting under that little beam of light in the distance.  Borderlands has a great look to it - it's not quite a fully cel-shaded game, but it has a distinct comic book look to it. The violence also tends toward a comic book style, and although it would be pretty graphic in a game with realistic graphics it comes across as slightly humorous in Borderlands and fits the game's dark, subtle, and subversive sense of humor perfectly. Pandora certainly has its own unique look and feel to it, and it stands out from the myriad of alien worlds that you've visited in countless other games before. And it's not just the look of planet that makes Pandora, Pandora - everything from your chipper robot guide to the colorful locals to the mysterious woman who appears to you in brief visions, makes exploring Pandora a very interesting experience.    Highly Recommended!

TL;DR - Fantastic game, great gameplay and AI, but can be a major pain in the glutaeus when using a keyboard  GRID is a diverse motorsport/racing game. While the graphics aren't that spectacular, the gameplay is well worth the price. This game has been overlooked by many who play Forza and Gran Turismo, but GRID surpasses the previous two in many respects.  Gameplay: The gameplay is solid. The physics are fairly realistic, the vehicles behave like you would expect, and the AI is much more natural than other games. They often quarrel among themselves, sometimes getting into large accidents and spinning off the track. The 24 Hours of Le Mans is a fantastic race, and I'm surprised not many other games contain it. The game plays fantastically on a controller, but is a major pain in the glutaeus if you have only a keyboard. I haven't been able to test out a steering wheel yet, but I heard it plays even better than a controller. The damage system is well implemented. Hitting a wall at an angle will damage your wheel, suspension, steering, possibly the engine, depending on your speed. With a damaged steering, your car will pull to the side, sometimes to the point where you have to quit the race.  Vehicles: GRID has a large selection of vehicles to choose from. Between tail-happy classic muscle cars, Touring cars, high-performance supercars, purpose-built drift machines, and many other categories, there is a car for every type of racer.  Tracks: I haven't gotten extremely far into the game yet, but of what I've seen, the track selection is fairly large. You have Yokohama, the Le Mans circuit, Circuit de Spa-Francorchamps, a track in san Fransisco, a Michigan demolition derby track, and a host of other tracks.  Race types: Good variety in GRID. Your average circuits are to be expected, but GRID also includes downhill drifting, freestyle drift, touge/midnight touge racing, demolition derbies, open-wheel racing, GT championships, and my favorite of all, the 24 Hours of Le Mans.  Graphics: I haven't been able to test it fully, since my computer is as advanced as a toaster, but the graphics in full are nothing too special by default, but if you modify one of the files, you can enable much better graphics.  Overall, I highly recommend this game to anyone wanting a different racing game and has a computer more advanced than a 2006 toaster.

app_name: Realm of the Mad God Exalt
review_text: With so many moving parts to learn and a seemingly infinite breadth of options to explore, diving into any new MMO is a major investment of time and energy. It's exhausting, really, which is why I appreciate a game like the free-to-play Realm of the Mad God. This fantasy-themed, co-operative bullet-hell MMORPG is beautifully simple, yet deep enough to keep you chained to your PC for days on end. It takes only a minute or so to get right into the action, and, once you get a taste for running around with a gaggle of your fellow pixelated-warriors raising hell and slaying beasts across the online realm, it's hard to stop.

The result is creepy... and unsettling.   ACHIEVEMENTS: If you do attempt this game because it's already in your Steam library from a Humble Bundle you bought 3 years ago, the achievements are easy but at the same time frustrating because is game is so slow paced. The achievements are largely clicking on every creature in the whole game. Look it up before proceeding if your main interest is Achievements.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Instantly hooked. If you are a fan of strategy, the time period, or any other Total War series games then you will most surely love this game. Ignore earlier negative reviews for I can tell you as an experienced Total War connoisseur that this game as far as graphics, realism, and sheer awesome make it a 10/10. For those that have played previous Total War games you are in for a BIG suprise. There is now more faction specific dialogue and artificial intelligence improvements than ever. Expect realistic human like responses in the field of battle! Unfavorable diplomatic relations and sucidal AI are a thing of the past. This game truely tests you like none before! The best part for me is just how cool the units are. You will hear the horns and unified shouts of your troops from rallies! With blood and gore you will see leveys slash through your opponents with massive intensity and realism. Wounded troops will be left behind -- which truely adds to the realism. I can describe in my short review just how great this game is, but it is not for everyone. Ask you yourself now these questions: 1. Do you like previous Total Wars? 2. Do you like the time period? 3. Do you like skill testing strategy games? 4. Do you like being a conqueor? 5. Do you thirst for blood? If you answered yes to any of these questions, I strongly recommend this game to you. If you answered yes to two or more, you will not regret buying this game guarnteed!

Hoo boy... What can I say about this game? First of all, the graphics are really nice, much better than in the Adventure Games. The character animations are okay, even though at some points they tend to skip a bit - at least for me - (most notably when you have to boost one of the two up a ledge). The controls are okay, even though there are some points where it can be a bit unresponsive, but usually it's no problem. One problem however is, that there are a few bugs within the game which can occur at random, sadly some of them can hinder your progress and supposedly can be fixed by restarting the game. I have yet to try out the co-op but I have to admitt that it's dissapointing that it only features a local one.   All in all I would give this game a 8 out of 10.

It doesn't feel pay to win because with a good few weeks effort, the best guns and equipment can be unlocked. Its made as mostly a progression game, where you start off with the minimum and upgrade how you want unlike BF3/4. The maps are much larger and offer an infinite amount of different fights.  One of my few complaints is that the download is large and the game doesn't run the best on entry level systems (It's both CPU and GPU intensive durring large fights).

This game is just awesome: you've got blood, fire, noise and metal with a soft touch of Aetulia's singing. The game really makes you believe that there was an age of metal (which would be awesome, though) with demons, titans, Ormagoden himself and humans. If anyone would take this seriously, it might even become a religion (or at least another evolution theory) for some.  It's amazing that this game has been released with all the good ideas, I'd really want to see a sequel to it with the ability to make Eddie fly when roaming all around the map, although it would be easier to find secrets that way. The limits of the map aren't natural when talking about exploring the game, though: you hit an invisible wall at some point, it feels like the map is made out of many open-sided boxes. But all of these improvements would work even better with separate campaigns for every main character: Eddie, normal/drowned Ophelia(s, spoilers, sorry), Doviculus, that glam hairstyle wearing Lionwhyte, maybe even Rignarok with Succoria, and the future characters, if any. Even though it's far fetched, it would make a great addition to the game. And, of course, a longer story - I've beaten this game in 8 hours straight. Must. Have. More.  So in short, the game is already epic, some improvements would work in favor of the game (unless it becomes worse like what happens with some game series - the writers get carried away), the story pulls you into the game and holds you tight until you ultimately finish the game with every achievement unlocked. The songs are picked pretty well, too. In the name of every metalhead in the world, Tim Schafer, please release a sequel!

How the game works  1. When you clear one side of pellets, a fruit spawns on the opposite. When you collect that fruit, it not only puts a new set of pellets, but redesigns the map of that side and pellet design. 2. When you pass by the green ghosts, when ghosts behind you pass them they follow you. This results in some long chains of ghost in which you can devour when you get the power pellet. 3. Once you get the hang of things you'll notice that the way everything is set up is so that there is a 'victory' trail. It is a path you can take that all the pellets lead to, it is the only path you should be on. If you play on expert, you have to deal with four of the ghosts keeping you from staying on that path. You'll understand when you play.

Although this game is no longer on sale, the Year One Survival Edition is. However, I would recommend buying neither. First off, the concept has been done TO DEATH at this point, so if you're looking for a unqiue survival-action game, you shall find none here. Let's take a look at this game objectively.   - The characters are horrendous, underdeveloped and display a single personality trait - in short they don't feel like real people.  - The voice acting is a mixed bag. The worst performance comes from the character of Lilly Ritter, and of course you hear her shrill voice every three seconds over the radio.  - After a couple of hours you realize the game is very repetitive. The story simply isn't good enough to make you care, but building up your base simply doesn't offer enough to be substantial. All that is left is the game's loot system, which more or less plays out exactly the same every time, with some small variations here and there.   I gave this game an honest college try, and I went into it, really hoping that I had found a diamond in the rough. It's clear to me now however that this game has little to offer considering the price tag.\\  Would NOT recommend to anyone other than those who just want to play another by-the-numbers zombie game.

It’s concerning how eagerly the blind follow the blind with games. Someone with little to no gameplay experience can dissuade many people from trying it for themselves. The reviewers with more gameplay hours who left negative reviews all seem to be playing the game regardless this seems pretty contradictory.  While the gameplay is quite grind-like, as many reviews say, the same can be said of most games. It is difficult to put into words why I like this game so much it can be incredibly relaxing when you’re strolling amongst the lizard bird bears and then on a different world you’ll find you’ve strayed too far from your ship and you’re navigating a fire storm on foot while being pursued by sentinels because you stole the treasures they protect.   No Man’s Sky is less like the conventional game it’s thought to be, where it’s more about immersion, puzzles, exploration than large amounts of combat or a detailed story. These elements are present but they are more subtle, I played 10 hours before I encountered pirates or hostile sentinels. If you play this looking for a complex story you’ll be disappointed, that’s not what the game is about. The prerogative is more about personal advancement in diplomacy with the various alien colonists and technology (albeit the diplomacy is very basic and linear)  A large part of the game, as in Minecraft, is gathering resources however there is a greater stress on choosing what to carry as storage is very limited. You may find a large amount of an otherwise rare resource but to fill up your ship with it would be pointless.  I do have issues with the game however the difficulty is not well balanced; the sentinels and predators pose no threat but in space battles with 5 -25 ships it can be remarkably hard to survive since there is no easy way to flee. Also the flight while on planets is restrictive; crashing is pretty much impossible and even if you collide with the ground the damage is negligible, there is a minimum distance you can be from the ground so landing where you actually want to is awkward.  At the end of the day this is an indie game not some mass produced ubisoft game where a new game is releases 6-12 month later. It will continue to be developed as long as there is a player base so even if you don’t buy the game now give it a follow and check out what’s being added in the months to come.

Is Civ6 a perfect game? No.  Is it a fun game? Yes  I have played every iteration of Civilization and with every change over there are complaints and issues. These are all fair enough, as things we have loved are changing. But, over time, they will sort themselves out as get used to them.   Of all the vanilla releases of Civ this is, by far, the deepest. There are new mechanics and concepts that really take the series in a good direction, such as districts and AI agendas. However, they really need to polish the game a bit more, as some of these great ideas have issues (eg it can be very hard to keep anyone liking you, and there may be no way to prevent it. The warmonger penalty may be excessive. etc).  But there is one GLARING negative. The AI. Civ4 and 5 both suffered with horrendous AI upon release, and 6 continues this trend. The AI is PATHETIC. They often refuse to attack, instead shuffling their units about infront of your army while being massacred by ranged attacks. They don't seem to use districts overly well... it's not thought out to flow across the empire. Instead being a random mix that doesn't necessarily help them. And it's plain too easy to win, even on the hardest difficulties.  Complaints about the art style will likely fade. People hated Civ4's art initially, but now love it. It is just jarring when you look at something you are not used to. Yes, it could use some touch ups as it can be hard to distinguish hills, but overall it looks amazing.   Will this get better? Yes. Civ 4 and 5 had similar issues and they were ironed out. Civ6 is still very playable and very fun. It's not up to the standards of Civ4 BTS, or Civ5 BNW, but let's not forget they had 2 expansions and then almost a year of patches to become the masterpieces they ended up being.

One of the most original and innovative 4x games to come out in years. Eight wildly varied factions (each with their own unique, expansive plotlines), a host of really interesting, unique mechanics (only one city per district, no tech tree). Most of the mechanics are very intuitive, but offer some pretty significant depth.  This came out at the same time as Beyond Earth, but it's been much better received, and the reason for that is simple- it has personality. Playing with each of the factions is like starting a different singleplayer game, and the faction creator is very flexible. Like its predecessor, Endless Space, the game also has fantastic, understated art and music.   The only problems are that some of the more advanced faction-specific mechanics are poorly explained, and that the AI can sometimes be poor at defending their cities. Amplitude have patched this, but some more work remains to be done.  Overall, the most fun I've had with a 4x since Alpha Centauri.

Few games are as important to me as Napoleon: Total War. This video game represents some of my most favourite hours spent playing video games. Every battle I fought in this game felt realistic and intense, capped off with an insane artillery mechanic that will leave you with your heart stopping every time your enemy fires a barrage. This is truly a fantastic game.  As far as critical concerns, this is unlike most Total War games. If you have just started with the series, you might be very happy with this game and Empire: Total War. Both are solid games, but unrepresentative of the Total War series as a whole. If you are a diehard Total War fan, with your Medieval II: Total War poster on a shrine above your dresser, then you probably will dispise this and Empire very much. It is very much a conscious decision that your units walk towards an enemy, stop, stare, and then fire at quizzically odd rates.  I'm not going to argue the attention to detail the developers had for history here, only say that this is about as perfect a representation of 18th-19th Century warfare as we can get with our slow processing power. If it is on sale, I highly recommend this game. If not, consider how frequently things appear on sale, and debate whether that money burning a hole in your wallet can be used for more constructive purposes. Either way, I believe this is a sensible purchase. Who knows, you might just like it as much as I do...

At its simplest Beyond Eyes is what people sometimes cynically refer to as a 'Walking Simulator', but the game deals with exploration in a creative and beautiful way. As the main character is blind she uses her other senses to 'fill in' information about the world around her. She can hear and smell things before they are 'filled in' by touch. The game mechanics and the concept puzzles themselves are generally simple: Rae needs to get from point A to point B and you must either use her senses to find the right path, or find an object that will allow you to remove an obstacle.  Word of warning: This game is a story about dealing with loss and it will emotionally destroy you towards the end.

I love Dragon's Dogma. It's one of my favorite games from the last console generation. It's something akin to Monster Hunter, but a little less... bizarre. Like Monster Hunter you create a person and play with them, but unlike Monster Hunter there's levels, classes, magic, skills and an assortment of quests to complete in a (mostly) open world. Going to split this into two parts: port report and game review.  Port Report:  One of the best ports I've ever played, especially for a Japanese developer. 144 hertz support right out of the box, stable, runs well. I tried mouse and keyboard for a bit, but I played the game orginally with a controller and it still feels best for being a melee fighter. The game looks LOADS better than it did on the last generation and thankful isn't capped at 15 or 20 FPS or whatever it was last time. Some of the textures are a little muddy, but otherwise the game looks rather good for a last gen title.  Game: Dragon's Dogma is one of the coolest one-offs Capcom ever did. The story is rather decent (expecially once you get through multiple playthroughs for the 'meta' aspect) for a fantasy game, and the gameplay top notch. You play as a person whom is felled by a dragon and forced into a pact - your fate is bound to this dragon after they take your heart, forcing you to figure out why you're still alive and what exactly you need to do.  Gameplay is something like a hack and slash with skills, gameplay varying a bit between melee, ranged and magic, with a few multi-class hybrids of those archtypes, and you fight monsters of all sizes with the aid of pawns, which are AI controlled companions. The game is not multiplayer, but you're able to hire the 'pawns' or hirlings of other players or friends to help you battle these monsters, though you can also ignore them. The mounting mechanic that was in Monster Hunter 4 was probably inspired by the grappling mechanics in Dragon's Dogma; entities can interact with each other. A lot. Harpies can pick you up or your team, dropping them from above, you can jump on to ogres and gryphons and hold on for dear life, and you can hold down goblins and other smaller creatures so that your allies can get dirty blows in before releasing them. Warriors can launcher others into the air with their shields to attack things from above while mages blast things out of the air. It's rather spectacular gameplay, to say the least.  The animations are a little wonky for facial stuff, but it's not terrible and is more of a side effect to the amount of customization allowed in the game and was present in the original. That's the only 'con' to the game I can think of aside from left over tidbits from the last console age, but for a port it's actually rather minimal (it's not like this is a remake). There's a ton of content in the base game, and I've never even played the Dark Arisen expansion. If you're into ARPGs, $30 on this game is a steal, and below is an instabuy.
