I'm writing this review for one of my favorite games ever. I own the game on every possible device it has been released on. I own the GC, PS2, PS3 and now the PC version of the game and have poured probably thousands of hours into this title. You would think that as the time passes and technology progresses that these old games would only get better when they are remade for newer console. But that's not the case with Tales of Symphonia. To put it shortly the PC version is just one ugly mess.  Pros:  - Dual audio - PS2 version extra content, few skins and bunch added hi-ougis (or mystic artes as they like to call them)  Cons: - Locked 30 FPS even if the very first version on the Gamecube was solid and smooth 60 FPS - Capped internal resolution of 720p (the year is 2016 btw and this is PC we are talking about) - Crashes at startup, crashes when trying to access the customize menu, crashes alt+tabbing, crashes whenever it feels like - Horrible translation errors in coding, letters missing or generally completely bugged menus - Some kind of VMProtect to act as an additional DRM (yay) which changes the running .exe name and prevents stuff form Nvidia cp AND makes it even harder for modders to fix the damn game - No support for mouse and keyboard  I would NOT recommend this version of the game to anyone. It's just a shameless act from Namco and no one should give them money for this kind of blatant robbery. I love the game itself, I really do. As I mentioned in the beginning I have it for every system and if you truly want the DEFINITE version of the game then the original Gamecube version if the way to go. It does lack a tiny amount of content (not a big deal, few skins and few mystic artes), but it's simply the most solid and smoothest experience of the game. Dolphin with the grapchical enhancements has been the most wonderful experience I've had with this game.  tl;dr: The game itself is one of the best games I've ever played, but the PC port is one of the worst ports I've ever played. DO NOT BUY!  ~Refunding the game ASAP

Allright. this first bit is my honest review for Planetary Annihilation TITANS itself. I love it. Everything from sheer choice of units to the ways you can end a match is amazing. It takes out what I don't really like about RTS which is micromanaging units to 'upgrade them' and limited resources in a map. the camera work is amazing and the Music is just.. bliss. its great.  Some downsides that I don't like are the META people use in pvp which is.. find commander, orbital laser platform x20, and rush. Orbital in this game is too quick to get and easy to mass produce to the point any Ground orbital defense is useless if you can just choose where the orbital units arrive from (right over the commander's head) Unless there's only one planet or that you keep your commander on the move. you have no chance of retaliating. Even with your commander surrounded in catapults and umbrellas.  Basically, I don't like how Tanking/defensive strategies are void to such powerful divebomb units.  But. if you play with friends who doesn't want to cheese a game, its a blast. You have so many ways to play and to win that you can easely invest hours upon hours in it.  Here is my second bit. MODS.  The default AI difficulties in the game become easely exploitable when you first start to get good grip of the game. So with that said. I switched to Queller AI, which claims to have more human like behavior. which is great since.. default AI keeps attacking your metal extractors and being a general annoyance over Duels of massive armies. Queller AI worked for the first few rounds. climbing from gold to platinum AI. until.. Uber.  So the game time counter says 40 minutes into a match, but.. the AI is building useless air units on a moon and continuously build millions of frigates in a tiny lake make the game run in slowmotion in the first 20 minutes. To the point where a minute in the game counter took over 3 minutes in real time. being a real pain to even try to shave the sheer number of units to fix the game. but the time it takes is too long so you end it by giving up.  The game Should be optimized for massive armies and its not my memory capacity that is the problem. the game itself Is fine but that mod makes AI build larger ammounts of units until it slows the game and even crashes sometimes.  (Queller AI) so dont bother doing anything else but 1v1s with bots or 2v2 with human players. because.. only humans know how to build units in the right places without making a million of them. and more than two bots makes the game lag after 20 minutes.  (the previous review was me ranting only on the game's bad performance at handling a mod and I was tired so my head wasn't right. my apologies. Its now a reccomended game for me and not the other way around)

Trigonarium  is a very solid twin-stick shooter that has never received the recognition and love it well deserves. If you find the  Geometry Wars  games to be too fast for you, but you enjoy the mechanics of  Super Stardust HD , then this is the game you want to have a go at. Unlike many other titles in the same price bracket, this one appears to have been tested thoroughly: I don't recall experiencing any bugs whatsoever.  This title is critically underrated and at only three euros it's an absolute steal. I urge everyone to give it a whirl!

I started out playing Helldivers on PS4, and this game was honestly one of the reasons I picked up the console. I was thrilled to see it released on PC and couldn't resist grabbing it here too—and at full price, which is a rarety for me and that alone should speak volumes as high praise.  Know that this game really shines when playing with others. It works as a single player game but can be very challening. However, when you have a full complement of players it's at its best. However, nothing holds a candle to being knee-deep in bug guts with close friends. Helldivers is a shining example of the reason you play games with friends. Everything in the game is leathal to everyone else I've laughed plenty of times when a friend's drop pod lands right on me squashing me instantly like a bug, or he accidently steps on me in a mech leaving me a stain on his big metal foot.  The game harkens back to one my favorite games, Alien Swarm. Alien Swarm was a free game released years ago here on Steam (and is still available). Helldivers is a true spirital successor that game and incorporates the chase for new cool gear and leveling while battling hordes of rentless enemies.  The missions have diverse and creative objectives that don't easily tire. The arrow seqeunces used to call in support objects and interact with missions are a neat innovation and trying to correctly enter them to diffuse a bomb while enemies are bearing down on you is a fantastic thrill. Tension rides high as you are the last one standing, running crazy knowing that the sucess of the mission rides on you finding a spare five seconds to enter in a key combo to respawn your teammates and save the mission.  Helldivers has a great Starship Troopers and Warhammer 40K vibe with a healthy dose of humor. The art is wonderful too, with diverse planets and enjoyable vehicles, weaponary, and enemies.  I highly respect the approach to selling the game too. If you're uncertain, you can lay down $20 and easily get your money's worth with the game. All of the base content will make you a solid contribution to a team. Paid DLC unlocks more cool options for the game but isn't strictly better than the content that comes in the base game. The DLC can be picked up later if you feel you are enjoying the game. But it's always extra icing.  I also am thrilled that it's relatively easy for players of different levels to play together. I've not felt players massively higher or lower than me were either underpowered or overpowered.  If this game looks and sounds fun I highly encourage you to strap on your cape, prepare a piping hot pot of liber-tea, and get ready to dive! I'll see you out there, soldier!

One of the best games I've ever played. Mostly in multiplayer. I remember the old times with timeout issues. They are mostly gone, but there are till some bugs. I'm glad developers are still working on it. It even has a fairly big modding community. I've been playing it for a long time, got it in a computer magazine, and I've decided to support the developers once more, so I've bought it on steam. I realise that there were lot of different companies running The Guild since Europa 1400.  This game has a great potential. It's one of the most complex games, but yet it is easy to play, but difficult to master.  It's an economic simulator. At the beginning you create your character, there are not many options in design, but this is not The Sims. The more important things are the stats and class. You can even chose between 2 religions, protestant and catholic, the only difference in my opinion is that you can only build church of your religion. You can change it in a church.  So to the classes: there are four of them: Patron, Craftsman, Rogue and Scholar. They are not balanced. The devs and modders are trying to fix it, but it's still not it. I hope guild 3 will be better balanced. My first try was Rogue (obviously). It was the easiest way to accumulate a lot of money, get rid of concurrence and hold the highest position in the council. In fact, in about 30 years, I've had a huge mansion on a hill and I stopped playing. That's the biggest problem of the game. In singleplayer, it gets repetitive.  Scholar is second easier, maybe even easiest, because you don't break the law for your job. But you should. Where is the fun? :D You just build a church and people pay you just to go in there. You can easily make friends and make everybody hate your enemies. I personally like the cemetery, because of all the satanists praying there. Even more than in church.  Craftsman is my favorite, because it has a lot of challenge, mostly in big cities flooded with concurence. The job of a craftsman is to make something from something else. That's different from patron and scholar who just collects their materials, and rogue who steals it.   I don't play for patron much, because I find it boring. So no review of that profession.   Every profession is necesarry in a town, maybe except of the rogue. Without patron, craftsman cannot produce clothes and so on, and scholar cannot make medicine or other stuff. It is probably the most frequent reason of dying or bankrupting. No scholar means no hospital, what almost certainly means death. I hate that AI does'n really have a stock of more expensive medicine.   Overall AI isn't so Intelligent, but in recent updates and modpacks it is more agressive and more complex. I hope that it will be better in The Guild 3.  So I think that's enough for now, nobody is reading it anyway, I will maybe write another one regarding some other features, like marriage, children, burning down your neighbours house or kidnapping your mother in law.   I'm really looking forward to The Guild 3, in fact, I would gladly do alpha/beta testing.

Bioshock Infinite gives players numerous creative ways to eliminate their foes. I can’t stress enough on how fun it is to hear your enemies scream in pain after you unleash killer crows on them while setting them on fire right after you’ve possessed them and let them do your bidding for a few seconds.   And the gameplay gets even more compelling when you add such a rich, multi-layered story behind it which tackles themes like organized religion, the hypocrisy of revolutionary movements, racism, American exceptionalism (Something I had a clearer idea of when I played this game), quantum mechanics and the multiverse theory among others.  This is a remarkable achievement of a game that has to be experienced even if you are not a FPS fan. I strongly believe that games like this make you think about and question the institutions that pervade the fabric of our lives. But even if you couldn’t care less about its story or themes, the gameplay and aesthetic elements make it worth the price of admission alone.

Since I liked FC3, FC3: Blood Dragon and FC4 so much, I thought I should try this game out. I heard that it was supposed to be more difficult too, which made it all the more exciting. Truth be told, this game didn't live up to my expectations. Almost every single element of this game was refined and polished in it's sequels and that is no joke. I wanted to make a list of all the improvements FC3 brought to the series, but had to stop because the improvements are way too many for a short review.   I will allow myself to elaborate on some of the things that bothered me with a concrete example of FC:2:s gameplay: Say I want to travel to a safehouse and enjoy the facilities of it.   There is no fast way to travel to safehouses, in fact, contrary to what it's name suggests, the road to your safehouse is often very perilous, which defeats the purpose of getting there in the first place.  Now, there is a way to fast-travel in this game: it takes the form of a bus. The bus can take you to a different region on the map, but almost none of the bus stations are located close to a safehouse, to which you can travel, without hitting an OUTPOST. The outposts are always populated by enemies that shoot you on sight. You can either slowly sneak past the outpost, try to dash through it with a vehicle and hope they won't pursue you in one of their own, or you can, of course, wipe them out. Shooting things is fun, but one would think that killing everyone in an outpost might secure passage through later on. This is not the case though since enemies respawn every time you revisit the outpost.  You make it past anyway and carry on all the way to the safehouse. Here you can sleep and save the game! Yay! Saving the game in a safehouse is completely meaningless, since you can save the game anytime anywhere conditionless of your situation. Sleeping makes time pass by, so you can choose to play at morning, day, evening and night. This seems to be little more than esthetic. I can also choose to store a weapon here. I can take one of the weapons I have on my person and store it in one of the weapon boxes, provided I've bought one of those boxes. Now I have an empty weapon slot, but, should I int he future have wrong weapon for the occasion, I might travel here and pick up whatever weapon I stored here instead! Since I visit the weapon store frequently, buying improved weapons constantly AND since all my previous purchased weapons spawn here infinitly, this safehouse-weapon-storage feels like a self-defeating purpose. Wouldn't it be better to just have a caché or a safe in your safehouse with all the weapons you have acquired so far?   It might be fair to say that this game doesn't go easy on you and it doesn't give you anything (ANYTHING) without effort from the gamer. Maybe it's not particularily bad, just detrimentally cruel.

This game is all kinds of amazing!  I bought it this morning at about 6am and have only just managed to quit having died for the nth time at 10am! I have been a fan of RTS and TBS strategy games for over 20 years and this is one of the finest and most addictive I have ever played.   I started out very gung-ho on Normal difficulty expecting that this would be no problem for a seasoned RTS veteran. I was very wrong. Two battles into the game I was boarded and my whole crew was wiped out in under a minute. The next run fared little better with the ship catching fire and the crew burning to death while I watched helplessly when I should have been venting the air to quell the fire.  So with a heavy heart I began again on Easy difficulty. Shame! The battes were easier and the upgrades came thick and fast. I finally reached sector 5 and my first achievement. Happy days. Then Sector 6. In a dark nebula I was visciously attacked without provocation by a ship that could shut down the door control to my ship. I was on fire from two seperate sources with no way to vent oxygen to stop it and still under attack from a ship that I could barely scratch the shields of. Two crew died failing to put out the fires despite running back and forth to the med bay to be healed. The enemy finally vanquished I regained control of the doors and hid in the cockpit while the fire was starved and died.   After this epic encounter I became more cautious and managed to limp to the Boss level only to be mercilessly stomped. ON EASY LEVEL. I haven't beaten it yet but i think if I just............  To sum up:  Pros:-  Fantastic feeling of control :- From the power management to the ordering of crew and aiming of weapons the control of the game is easy to pick up. You feel like you are the captain of a starship. 'More power to the shields! Fire in Med Bay! Open the airlock and suck the buggers out!'  Fantastic feeling of being out of control :- Despite the above and the ability to pause time you are rarely in total command of an encounter, grinding through opponent after opponent. You have to adapt and play on the fly as well as using well practised strategies.  Pitch perfect graphisc and sound :- The art style is basic but stylish and often amusing. Yes, that is a worm creature running the shield console and yes, that pirate in the engine room was just eaten by your praying mantis chief engineer. To be honest 3d graphics and sparkly effects would add nothing to the experience as you really don't have time to wow at the lens flare. The game music is in an 8bit retro style but is acomplished and catchy. I was humming the tunes in a bathroom break earlier.  Awesome, Outstanding, Original Gameplay :- I won't go on and on and spoil the surprises but just imagine a kind of RTS, TBS, RPG, with a hint of TD with a load of other cool acronyms thrown in for good measure and you still will be way short of how complex the game is.  Cons:-  I should have bought this game 6 months ago when I first spotted it. Oh the wasted time...    Got to go.  Got to make another run...

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: It is fair to say that things did not go well for Rome II on release - I played it then, and it was a stodgy turgid mess of a game. Hopefully CA and Sega have learnt from this for forthcoming releases, as where Rome II is now is a good place.   The recent Emperor Edition introduced an overhauled building tree and politics system, making the latter more understandable and the former more strategic (balancing food demands and public order Vs current resources and requirements). Both are simple enough to get on with once you learn how it works. Additionally, there was a swathe of graphical tweaks as well. Colours are now brighter with a wider colour spectrum which makes things easier to review at a glance and the units/maps/buildings look much better than before. The AI has also been tweaked, and will now fight back. It still throws Elephant units away as if they were nothing, but other units will try and outflank, offer up tempting targets, seek out weak points, defend strong points etc etc. It won't ever match a human opponent, but it can give a good account for itself. Sieges aren't all about assaulting a city as soon as the army gets there either, relying on torches to burn the gates down: often the AI will camp outside and build siege equipment to assault with, and/or use ladders effectively.  Rome II has come a long way and is now a very good addition to the Total War stable. If you're an RTS fan, you can do worse than pick this up.

(I mostly played this game offline, that's why it looks like I only have a few minutes recorded on it)  Transistor wins my 'Biggest Video Game Purchase Disappointment of 2016' award.  This game was advertised as an ARPG. In practice, it handles poorly in real-time. Regular dodging isn't viable, healing in combat is near nonexistent, and there are few defensive skills. The only feasible way to avoid most incoming damage is Matrix-dodging through pause-and-plan. Most attacks force your character to stand still or root you in place, leaving you unable to dodge. If you were looking for the next Diablo, Torchlight, or Sacred, you aren't going to find it here.  Transistor utterly fails as an RPG. There are no meaningful player choices in the storyline and the game world is completely linear. Most zones actually block you from backtracking. The entire game railroads your character through a linear path, and the only choices you make are your skillbar and how you handle fights. There are no explorable side areas. Might I suggest the (somewhat derogatory) description 'rail-playing game'? The story is confusing, vague, incoherent, and makes no sense in some places. The ending was so bad that I actually hammered on my right mouse button hoping for a Mass Effect 2 style Paragon interrupt. Leveling and experience is highly arbitrary per fight and made almost no sense either - instead of rewarding the player for each enemy defeated, the experience system is more like a bunch of checkpoints and you level up after clearing a fixed number of checkpoints. The only replay value is the 'New Game Plus' option - start a new game and keep your levels and progress. Even worse: after starting a New Game Plus, the game actually lost all my levels and character profile.  The soundtrack was largely disappointing, sometimes bordering on background noise. I have no idea why it was tagged with 'Great Soundtrack' - Supergiant's previous game 'Bastion' had a much better soundtrack. Plenty of classic games from the mid to late 90's had much better soundtracks that took up 1/10th of the disk space.  Transistor also lacks a proper main menu and I found the UI to be confusing at first and unwieldy after getting used to it. when you start the game, you get dumped to the intro with no clue about what happened. The skillbar UI needs a lot of work - you have to select each skill and choose a slot when the obvious proper solution would have been a drag-and-drop interface.  The general lack of content is mind boggling, especially for 3 GB of storage space on disk. I was able to finish the game in 6 hours (the same day I got it), including figuring out the UI and testing various ability upgrades.  The good: Most fair negative reviews will find at least something positive. The combat system's time-stop mechanics and the function upgrading are innovative (if you enjoyed the function upgrading, look for Magicmaker - another indie game designed around similar ability-mechanic combining). Most fights were well-balanced (although there were a few unreasonable ones).  Summary: Disappointing trainwreck of fail. No idea why Transistor consistently gets 95% positive reviews - if that's what people like, then I have serious concerns about the future of indie games. I got this game on -80% sale ($4) and IMO didn't get my money's worth. Bastion was better in almost every way (and the price is actually lower too). The $20 base price is ridiculous considering that Skyrim costs the same and has 100-200x more content (not including mods). If you like games with tactical pause, you're better off with Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic, Aarklash: Legacy, Dragon Age, or Mass Effect. If you want a regular ARPG, there are tons of those out there.

An emotional rollercoaster worth buying if you like building, planning or simulation. At first I was like 'Why are all my peeps dying' and '♥♥♥♥! Ill admit it has a bit of a learning curve but its well worth it. As for the emotional rollercoaster part... My village had about 30 people living in it, everything was going nice and food was in surplus. I could live through a few years without being forced to divert my labourers into farmers. When my first villager died of old age I wasnt sad, but happy that he had made it to old age. Then it hit me as I looked at the villager count... there were no children to replace my aging population. With haste I built houses to provide privacy for the growth of my people. But Im afraid its too late. Everybody is old as dirt and as of now I have 7 adults who are mostly old or only female and 1 child. My hopes of reigniting my village are growing dimmer by the season. Ive never wanted old people to ♥♥♥♥ as much as I do now. And so my once stonepaved and glittering village will fade into the foliage as nature overtakes us all in the end.

Massuve Chalice is like a big plate of french fries. When it first arrives piping hot, it's delicious and enjoyable while you juggle the first few hot bites in your mouth but as you continue eating, it soon gets cold and you're left with a mouthful of staleness and regret as you stare at half the plate sitting there in front of you knowing that no matter how you reheat the fries, it'll never be the same.  The game plays around with some interesting concepts, but the execution ends up flat. The idea of bloodlines is interesting and how you manage heroes throughout the years but once you peel that first layer away, there's not much to it. The combat is simplistic without much depth to the strategy and soon gets dull. the characters both heroes and monsters lack variety and the story is pretty linear and bare. The game eventually ends up more of a chore and you feel it even more once you hit the halfway mark.  Buy if only heavily discounted and if you don't mind highly repetitive games, else go for the XCom series instead.

app_name: Dysfunctional Systems: Learning to Manage Chaos
review_text: Dysfunctional Systems is a visual novel that tells of a story about individuals dealing with problems of other worlds. The protagonist, Winter, is one of such individuals - a mediator - who tries to keep a world in proper order. As with most visual novels, you direct Winter to decide on different choices presented throughout the game, ultimately deciding the story's ending.  The story itself is a quick one, and I do wish it was a bit longer (a playthrough can be done within an hour or so, if you read through it quickly), though it's a bit understandable since this is the first episode in the series - there're a couple more episodes after this one, and I believe there's a prequel in the works as well (you can check out their website to find out more about them). Given the short story, there weren't really a lot of choices. I liked the plot and I'm quite eager to know how it turns out.  The artwork and soundtrack are pretty good too - the visuals are drawn beautifully, and the soundtrack complements the various events in the game. Kudos for including the soundtrack along with the game and not as a separate purchase (I have it on my playlist to listen to on the go, loved the title theme song). It'd be a nice touch to have some character voices though, which should make it even more engaging.  Overall, it's a pretty good title - it has an good and interesting storyline, but is rather short that it's like a teaser for the succeeding episodes. A bit pricey for the length of the story, though the inclusion of the soundtrack more or less offsets this point. Everything else is pretty good - 3.5 out of 5 stars for me.  Postscript: Considering the story and all  (and the fact that it has some kind of an anime opening sequence!)  , they should try producing an animation for this one after all the episodes have been released. It certainly has potential in my opinion - hope you can make it happen Dischan! =)

Chuck’s Challenge 3D Review – “Woop! Woop!”  Today, GHT’s Indie corner brings to you a puzzle game called Chuck’s Challenge 3D. The developer is Niffler Ltd. and it was published by Nkidu Games. My first thought when seeing the pictures for this game was “cute, an alien named Chuck.” It reminded me of a cat we used to have named Steve… I love “people names” for cute creatures. However, to my disappointment, the purple alien’s name is not Chuck, it’s Woop. Fun to say, but not as fun as if he’d been named Chuck.  Now you’re probably thinking “who’s the Chuck in Chuck’s Challenge 3D!?” Well, my friends, he happens to be a game creator that Woop has summoned (abducted) from what looks like his holiday and forced to make puzzles. I feel it should be called something like “Woop’s Wonderland”. Sure, in the end Chuck is happy and wants to make more puzzles for Woop, but I would suspect he was brainwashed into it. Vacation on a beach, in the sun and sand, or doing your job?… It really does seem like a difficult decision...  To read the rest of this review go to,  http://www.gamershonesttruth.com/chucks-challenge-3d-woop-woop/

Well done Rockstar on an amazing game!

Visit planets. See alien life. Mine stuff. Good fun. It does get repetitive after about 20 hours. Just not enough variety. It feels like the developers made sure that the planets were not too extreme, and by doing so made it feel similar for every planet. So you would probably get 20 hours of fun out of this - don't expect more until they udate this. I did have a fun time with this game. Not sure I would recommend it at full price, but if you can get at a discount...   To improve this game the developers need to make more extreme variations on planets. They need ones with no life, ice, gas giants. They need ones with no outposts. More height variance. Oceans of acid. Thick dense jungles. Even the film Interstellar had more planet variety. Forget base building, multiplayer - make it a race to the centre of the universe where you have to survive by exploiting the resources of the planets along the way.

Overhyped endless possibilities to get bored for $35.  - Repetitive content: Imagine you like cats. Cats are procedurally generated. No the same cat over the world, but you are not enjoying much of observing new cat pictures over the internet? Same thing here. You see planets --- yeah, they're different but very similar to each other. Building all the same even trade posts for different races are the same. Stations on planes are all the same. - No story. - Stupid NPCs: You can only trade with them or ask for a random technology. - No multiplayer: You won't encounter a player even if you will be in the same location. Incredibly lonely game. - Casual combats: pew pew pew - dead. - Console gameplay on PC: FOV is very low, to make it playable you must adjust it in config files. 30 fps locked out of box on a PC? Is it a joke? Weird controls. You cannot turn off mouse acceleration - Graphics looks broken anyway: antialiasing not working on my setup.   So, in general you have a lot of repetitive constant with what you can do no really much. You just mine resources and go next planet. Craft some ♥♥♥♥ (e.g. new warp engine) and go further. And again trade and mine resources. That's all for now you can do in No Man's Sky.  No Man's Sky is more like tech demo, but not the actual game.

I put everything on the lowest settings, still nothing (and this game is old unreal engine) I exited the settings, and suddenly my mousepointer of the menu was still in the game. Flower Shop: Summer in Fairbrook is a Visual Novel with an more or less properly integrated farming minigame (that actually does no/barely impact to the game... sadly)   To begin: IT IS TOO EXPENSIVE! You should buy it on sale or in bundle or on other sites, 15€ is simply too much, the game itself is a solid novel.  Story:  You play Steve, college Boy who looks after cute girls - thats how his girlfriend Jill gets ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off from him and brakes up. To all unfairness his Father sends him to his uncle to work as a Farmer for him as Steve lacks character and needs to be taught the hard way.  There he meets the 2 girls Clara and Susana as well as the women Marian. He can get a love relationship with them, or phone his ex Jill and try to reunite with her. Each run takes about 45-60 minutes to complete if you rush (and with skip function after 1st run) the first one took ~80 minutes. Every day you start with the farming game, then you can choose what you want to do, either farm more (as said it does no impact to the game ... : ( ), go walking with Clara, visit the Flowershop to buy seeds and visit Susana, visit the Library to read books and visit Marian, phoning Jill or sleeping to cure fatigue.  Pro: - Story, although not the best, you actually can enjoy and get to like the story once you played a bit of it. - Farm minigame (although kinda tirering after a while) - Many choices - Characters have a nice personality - Music - Background - Trading cards - Achievements  Con: (somehow steam doesnt accept the formation in the end... -.-) - Price... 15€ is definetly over the top, this might be worth 5€, maximum of 7€, so buy only on sale or on bundle sites - More or less unpolished characterdesign, still not as bad as in the Heileen series. (Susana actually looks buitiful) - Repeatable dialogues when you visit the girls without having an event triggered - Farming minigame has no impact to the plot, not even an achievement, you more or less can skip it all day [only impact it has: you get special ending if you have ~500$.. that is earned in the 1st 2 weeks alone, after u can skip it all day)  - Often scenes without sound at all - Not 100% appropiate for small children (might be okay for 14+? All how the parents may see it themselves) as the Marian route has a wet shirt szene where you can see her bra. Overprotective adults should therefore not let their smaller kids play that route ^^. This is stated as con only for parents buying this for their smaller children. It is not that of an 'extreme' scene.  Hard for me to rate and not to misrate it... either 6/10 or 7/10 so ill stick with 6.5/10 for now I enjoyed it, but i would never pay 15 € for it, bought it in a bundle or on sale, i dont remember anymore its been a while hanging untouched in my steamlogg.

Hatred is a top-down twin stick shooter where your goal is to kill everybody that moves. The game takes place over 7 distinct areas where you can free-roam (for the most part) and do objectives as you wish. The game does have requirements to move on to the next stage, mainly 'Kill X amount of people' or 'Get out of X'. There are several side objectives that mostly involve killing the majority of people in a given area. Completing these side objectives gives you respawn tokens to use if you die. If you die and you're out of respawns, you have to restart the level.  You yourself play as an edgy misanthrope who has long hair, wears a black trench coat, and spouts one-liners. You are controlled with WASD by default, and there is a sprint mechanic that you can use to vault over small walls, a crouch mechanic that makes you harder to hit and increases your aim, an aiming mechanic that allows you to precisely aim as well as aim higher or lower to compensate for the top-down nature, and this spidey-sense type mechanic that highlights living enemies on your screen as well as equipment pickups. You can carry up to three weapons at a time, from about 10 weapons There is a health system, where you can only regenerate a bit of health by executing 'not-quite-dead' enemies. It sounds all horrible, but trust me, you get used to the same 'stomp the guys head in' or 'shoot the guy in the head' animation after a hundred times. The player character does sometimes get caught on hard-to-see level geometry or a tiny chip of a wall still standing which can be pretty irksome if you're trying to get away from a firefight.  You aren't just shooting innocent civilians, you'll be fighting police, weapons dealers, vigilantes, hunters, SWAT, and the army. Even unarmed civilians will sometimes pick up a gun and start unloading on you. They aren't pushovers, either. On higher difficulties, SWAT can take three shots with a shotgun and still be standing, and you'll often be forced to retreat and find some civvies to execute. The AI has a lot of room for improvement, but I let it slide because it's not really something you ask for in an indie game. An annoying features is that enemies can shoot you from beyond your maximum view range, forcing you to rely on your (inconsistent) minimap to find your shooter.  The gunplay is solid and even the 9mm pistol manages to actually do something rather just being the equivalent of spitballs against tougher enemies. The physics engine is great and it's a lot of fun to play around with, though it can be pretty CPU-intensive. Speaking of graphics, I'm a fan of the style, where everything is grayscale except red, blue, and explosions. It can give me a headache playing too long, though.  It's a pretty short game, I beat it on hard in 3 hours, but like I said, they're free-roam areas and I was mostly just rushing objectives. Unless you want to screw around with the levels you liked later or get achievements, it's not a game that you'll replay a lot. There is workshop support and multiplayer/coop planned down the road, which would add a breath of fresh air to the game.  Finally, the moral panic. The game is not a fourth as bad as the trailers made it out to be. It's actually pretty tame. Like I said, you get jaded to the executions, and there's not really a lot of over-the-top gore except for bloodstains, blood pools, and some limb removal. I honestly don't see how this got an AO rating, it's M at best. For the tone, it gets pretty clear the game is parodying itself. The over-the-top one-liners by the player character (who is called the Antagonist by the developers) includes a line from the POSTAL DUDE. An important code for something later in the game is 666, for god's sake. Don't be offended.  Overall, I'd give it a 6.5/10, above average. Make sure you buy the game if you like it, not just to stick it to the people who don't like it.

After finishing the game, I have some mixed feelings about it. It's not the best of the series, especially the story, but it does an excellent job compensating for the generic plot line with some top notch character interactions and comedic relief. Let's break it down all spoiler-free like for you baby.   Plot  As I mentioned in my sterling prelude, the plot line was very generic. It's your typical boy meets world, world needs saving btw boy is the chosen one. It seemed to lack a lot of the twists and turns that typical Tales games are known for, not that it didn't have some moments, both of the serious and of the laugh-so-hard-the-optional-boss-destroys-you-while-you-wipe-your-tears-away variety. Side note: Some blood in cutscenes when people get stabbed would be nice, just what are you trying to teach these kids Bandai? The characters really do save the story from being abysmally cliche, or at least distract you from that fact. And to reiterate, it's not that the game didn't have some gut-wrenching all up in your 'feels' moments, you just saw them coming from like the 5th dungeon away.   Gameplay  The more action oriented Tales battle systems have never been a selling point to me, this one was no different. You will certainly swell with accomplishment as the pieces to this woman composed entirely of rubik's cubes slowly fall in to place. It felt like the tutorial didn't end for me until I was around 30 hours in, and I've played several other Tales games. It is not quite as convoluted as my previous statements make it out to be, but as somebody who likes to understand every aspect of gameplay, it took a while to pick up on all the nuances and complexities of the numerous mechanics.   Overall  ♥♥♥♥ yeah I'm recommending this. If for no other reason than that optional boss killed me a second time before I finally stopped laughing. If you're into JRPGs none of the cons to this game should bother you. Cliche plot, intricate gameplay are all pretty standard for the genre.   And if you aren't into JRPGs, stay away. It'll make a weeb out of you. Seriously. You have no idea how hard it's been for me not to claim Edna as my waifu while writing this, and I pretty much just did anyways. 10/10 might have become a lolicon

The YouTube person named 'Jingles' first mentioned this game a while ago and since then I somewhat lost track, even though it looked amazing.   A few weeks ago I then saw it here on steam, saw the vids, watched a bit gameplay and then bought it.   The price is high, yes, but justified.   First off, I never played any of the other Elite games nor Freelancer or whatever. The game has barely to no story whatsoever, you start up the game, you watch the tutorial videos (what I really really reocommend you to do) and start a new game.  The game then throws you into your cockpit in a dock at a station and...that's it. You're free to do whatever the f*** you want.  And that's what I like about this game. No boundaries. You could go out bounty hunting, you could go courier style, mine asteroids, trade various goods or explore the vast, seriously  freaking huge  space and sell the exploration data. I will admit, that a story mode or story missions would be a great addition to the game. Like one major quest and maybe some bigger sidequest revolving around the three main factions in the game, a bit like the guilds in Oblivion and Skyrim. But it's still in its baby shoes and they'll add that, but even now the game is entertaining as hell.  The controls are, I admit, a bit hard to grasp at first and there's a lot of them and you might need some time to set them up properly so it fits whatever you use. I use a keyboard and a joystick and it works great, but also tried an Xbox controller, which also worked good. People said that even mouse and keyboard only work fine. This is a space sim, where you control all the functions of a damned spaceship and not a bobby car.  This is not, install, start up and jump into the action game. It needs some time and its complex, but once you worked out the majority of the stuff you'll understand.  The graphics are, well, gorgeous. It looks highly polished and smooth. No graphic glitches or similiar. The The effects are amazing and the HUD design (or really, the cockpit of the vessel you control) is nicely done. The first time I jumped into hyperspace to travel to a new system...I just sat there in awe and all I could say was 'wow'. It's really well done. The stars are well animated and are nice to look at. There's currently only a handfull of different space station designs...so it's not all that diversified in that regard. The ship designs are...well. The following is only my own opinion, in question, just look up the different ships online and judge yourself. Some of the ships look really really cool. But those are mostly the end game ships. The early ones are derived from the original Elite games...from the 80ies. Some ships just look like geometrical shapes with cockpits smacked on them. Cobra, Sidewinder, namely. They have appeal, and I understand the nostalgia factor for some of the players, but that's not the majority. But I hope they'll add some more nice early to mid game ships in the future.   The gameplay itself is smooth and as polished as the graphics. Very well made. The combat is awesome and challenging. Trading, courier stuff and mining is for me personally too boring and has too less PEW PEW LAZORS, but some will surely like that.  You can play the game in a MMO enviroment with other players, alone (but you need to be online) or in a solo world, but with a friend(s). The MMO version is barely any different from the solo one and players are friendly and don't just KOS everything that moves. But docking at some stations is annoying, since there's lots of players who want to dock at the same time. But then switching to solo quickly is easy and happens smooth.  The soundtrack is also well made and not too obnoxious. But it should be noted, that this game is very well suited to let your own music play while gaming.    As a side note, my internet at home died two days after playing, because our provider had issues, so I played solo only and used USB tethering with my phone and it's data plan. It worked very well and used up after 10h playing only 22MB.    If you like games like Skyrim and Oblivion and you like Space and ♥♥♥♥ and don't mind a somewhat lacking story mode alltogether, then you won't regret buying this.   My rating:  2001/10, 'An Der Schönen Blauen Donau' plays while docking ;)

I'm a space man. I've played space sims since Tie-Fighter for PC. I played so much Freespace 1 & 2 my eyes bled. I've played Freelancer's campaign several times over simply for the glory of earning a Titan by the end of each one- Which is ONLY what this game's giving me a thurst to do any more-. I LOVE space fighter sims and space sims in general, Love'm to death. I have about 40+ hours playing this game, with three seperate profiles (or redo's). And so that's why I'm giving this a 'No' and if you're really wondering why I've not enjoyed this product;  This game is beautiful, atomspheric, the DLC does add the ability to land and rover around on planets (I've never owned the DLC so I can't speak at all for that), the stations are beautifully hand crafted and well designed, thought out and totally seem alive. The game is also sort of 'choose your own adventure', although for the majority of thril seekers in space combat, well it comes down to getting 'dangerous'.   Just as rewarding as old space fighter sims were when you pretty much single handedly brought down the enemy capital ships (like the Suicide King's of Freespace 2) or you smuggled a lot of contraban into the heart of colonized space, sneaking right past countless boarder guards (which is something in Elite Dangerous you actually end up doing a lot), you will find here in this game-   But to get to the top of that mountain is a hard climb, one that at any point could find you dead, caught and fined or simply banned and attacked/chased out of a system, make you regret feeling the urge to upgrade, make you even regret going online to read 'the guides', as all they've seem to have done is gotten you ripped apart countless times in places 'they promise fortune!---- If you're 'tough' enough'(Which is totally random, some systems can have harder goons then others). The title is no joke, this is simply 'Elite Dangerous', where the sacrifice is many of your hours, and while there are definetly some good rides to this game, it has some incredible moments for sure, it has just as many thrills as it does chills, spills, and bills- and not good Bills, the bills you don't enjoy seeing over and over again ever 25 minutes.  And IMO that isn't a good game, at least the aspects of combat and bounty which obviously I'm more intrested in. I know they'res a ton of players reading this who have had fantastic luck and fun and actually enjoyed there time spent in this game- but those of you I speak of have a totally different perspective of this game based on what it inittially threw at you and what you wanted in your space game.  Those of us looking for some thing to replace titles like the ones I've mentioned, I really don't think you'll find what you're looking for in this. I did not.

'No man in the history of the world has felt tough with his pants around his ankles...' -Henry   Update 02/10/2016 :  'Bad things happen, and you have to find a way to contain the damage, a good way.' - Delilah  My wife got the game as a gift from a friend and she played it. We compared notes and she did the complete opposite of all the things I did during my gameplay, and she got the same result and same abrupt end. We discovered a part of the story where involving Henry's wedding ring, and doing a different action with it did not garner a different ending that we both thought it would have.  I am sorry  Campo Santo , I have to change my review and suggest people to wait till the game is $10 or less. The fact that it doesn't matter you made a game that has no possible condition of change regardless of which answer you choose, meh and boring.  The time lapse of the game didn't bother me. The graphics were great the sound was really good (specially ambience) and the voice acting superb. But the price and discovering the game has no alternate result from what is scripted to do and end, just killed it for me.  This type of game was ripe to do a multivolume episodic game, because the game's graphics is just perfect for it and the atmosphere, but I have to agree with everyone else who has given you a thumbs down that the game you offered vs the price, just doesn't add up.  ~~~ o ~~~   Launch Review   I went into the game a little bit nervous that the developers had confessed on some previous threads that this game was going to be around 6-8hrs long and that it was not going to be enough time to craft the game around it. Boy was I wrong.  Bra-Fuc-king-vo!!!  It feels like they made the story first and build the entire experience around it. You wont need 20hrs for this game to make you feel satisfied... I plan to replay it again soon and make some different choices, if only to see different dialogs or see if there is even a different endings... I play it from beginning to end in 5.1 hours. I did a lot of exploring. I will probably play it again to see if I missed something and see if the changes in story choices change the end factor of the game.  First I want to say that I love the art style of this game. This neo cartoony look with some instances of photorealistics events just made the game to me far more immersive than I have experience on a game of this genre. There are no weapons in the game (well there is an Axe) only tools that you use to perform certain tasks. There is no blood or violence of any kind with the exception of one scene that was presented rather well considering everything else in the game.  The sound in the game was cute. Music presented itself in the form of crescendo's to accentuate moments in the game where there is emotional or mental intensity. It wasn't boring nor repetitive.  The story, quite honestly was pretty simple, but the way you are brought into the fold was pretty unique and interesting. There is a moment in the game that you don't really know what to expect, and how the story unfolds into this thrilling mystery... The presence of Delilah, a character in the game brought to life by voice actress Cissy Jones is indeed Henry's (and the player's) emotional lifeline in the game. From beginning to end, it was just amazing how it all meshed together.  There are a lot of things to do in the game despite the linear line of events. Something really cool is that there is a camera that is found eventually and the photos you take can be seen from a webrowser and its pretty cool to see the type of things people will photograph (someone should make a forums post with all the photo links).  There were two things I didn't like, one of them is that it only has 5 cheevos (achievements) and for people like me that like some exploration and validation of that exploration, they could have added more achievements because there are a lot of opportunities in the game for them.  The second item I didn't like was the price. I can agree to disagree with people on this basis. The game is priced at $17.99 during the Lunar Special, but its base price on Steam will be $20 smackaroos (well 19.99 for those of you with perfect decimal disorders). I would have valued this game at $16.99 for base sale value, because regardless of the good plot of the game, and the fluidity of its gameplay $20 for a linear 6-8hr game is just a tad too much. Like I said we can go back and forth in this observation but I don't mind as much because this game was really good to play in the time it lasted for me.  Pros:  + Voice Acting + Gorgeous Visuals + Drama (story based) + Amazing Ambient Sound  - Price - No choice you make has a change on the end result. - It's not an adventure game, its a walking sim with storytelling.

Its a challenging game (try setting the difficulty down from the start), in depth, simple graphics, and can be very fun. Get it when its 5 bucks or less.

I didn't enjoy this game as much as I hoped I would. The whole water gimmick was cool at first, but eventually just became annoying.  Graphics: The water graphics, for the most part, look decent. The graphics design is pretty cool, although I'm a sucker for the whole cyberpunk futuristic look. Other than that it's nothing too special.  Story: The voice acting is pretty bad, which makes it hard to care about the characters. The story itself is relatively straightforward and cliched, and is a whopping 3 hours long, so that is definitely not a selling point of this game. The writing is uninspired, although there was one line that made me laugh: 'I've got two balls and neither one is crystal.'  Gameplay: The gimmick of this game is the use of large amounts of water to your advantage, whether it be to access high areas, put out fires, or sweep enemies off of their feet. That feature itself was pretty well done, but it was not utilized very often, and when it was, it was laid out step by step. There was absolutely no intuition required, which made the mechanic pretty boring. Other than that, the cover system is frustratingly bad, the gunplay is unsatisfying, and the controls are stiff.  Overall, don't spend your time or money with this game, unless you're a...uh...water fanatic? It's really nothing special and I found myself frustrated more often than entertained. 3/10

A surprising little game. It plays and looks like a souped up micromachines game from days of yore. It has lots of nice little touches like oil slicks, sand, exploding barrels. And tons of unlockable paint jobs. Not to mention quite a few well designed tracks with the standard elimination, race, time trial options. The multiplayer bonus and shortcuts add to this and it's a shame more people aren't playing it.  The downside is the standard rubberband AI that you get with racers, but if you've played racing games this is kinda goes with the territory. It's not terrible with it, but it is noticeable. Especially in elimination races. The other downside is multiplayer is limited to unlocks, so if you plan to play with friends you may want to spend some time unlocking at least the extra tracks, modes, and cars.   I recommend this as a fun little party racer with a lot of nice little touches that nobody should be disappointed with. If you like unlockables, all the skins/paintjobs should keep you going for quite some time.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Knights of the Old Republic™ II: The Sith Lords™
review_text: [NOTE: this review assumes that the player will use the The Sith Lords Restored Content Mod (TSLRCM) from the game's workshop. This reviewer think that said mod is a necessary part of the game at this point and can't be separated from the vanilla game itself]  Star Wars Knights of the Old Republic II - The Sith Lords is a sequel to the original roleplaying game by BioWare by Obsidian Entertainment. Set once again centuries before the Star Wars' movies timeline, The Sith Lords' story picks up immediately where the story of the first game ended: the galactic Republic is now in disarray due to the Jedi civil war and Sith forces still run rampart through its planets, with the Jedi order in ruin and no Force practicioneers in sight. You take the role of a Jedi who has been exiled from his/her order before the civil war and your objective is to survive the dark forces coming after you and discover the true reasons behind your punishment and the origin of your peculiar connection to the Force.  As far the gameplay goes, the Sith Lords manages to be a better roleplaying game than its predecessor. Several gameplay elements have been overhauled or change to support different styles of play and to truly allow you to build a character following your tastes AND be able to overcome the game's challenges. While in the first Knights of the Old Republic the balance favored pure melee Jedi characters, now other specializations and characters have a chance too: enemies are less prone to resist Force powers, making them viable for pure damage or CC, and both melee weapons and blasters can now benefit from unique upgrades that really give lightsabers a run for their money. It's worth mentioning however that the Sith Lords' endgame somehow favors caster characters: by that point in the game the Force points (your mana, if you will) regen rateo has become gargantuan, allowing you to spam damaging spells or incapacitating effects ad nauseam (something that becomes really useful, since the last portion of the game are a series of straight fights against crowds of opponents).  The Sith Lords' main new gameplay gimmick is the influence system. The companions you travel with will react to your actions and choices, approving them or else following their own moral compass and objectives: therefore, your influence with them will increase or decrease accordingly. Having an high influence with a character means unlocking new conversation options and even the possibility to turn him/her into a Jedi. The main downside of the influence system is however that the parameter is increased mainly during world events, so you'll manage to get a good influence with the characters you'll use most only. Another gimmick the game likes to throw at you is the sudden playable characters changes: at many points during the game you will be forced to put aside your main character to play in the shoes of one or more companions. While these sequences are interesting in their own right and have their place in the main story, they can easily becomes frustrating if you are forced to play a character you are not familiar with or whom you neglected beforehead.  Knights of the Old Republic II is also infamous for the somehow shifting quality of its levels. There are some levels that are universally loathed by the playerbase, with the two main examples being Peragus (which is quite long for being your first proper level after the tutorial prologue, with the story going on at a snail's pace) and Nar Shaddaa (which is another planet that takes far too long as far as the main quest goes, requires a lot of backtracking and has a brutal endgame). Also, the final area of the game, Malachor V, hasn't many fans around due to having been hit the worst by the rushed development cycle and consisting in a series of straight fights against several opponents with little to none interaction or exploration involved.  Star Wars Knights of the Old Republic II - The Sith Lords remains a very divisive game in pretty much ever aspect, even the story and the setting. Some people like the deconstruction the writers wanted to pull off on the Star Wars canon as a whole and the main morality system of the series, while others find it pretentious and too much shoehorned. Some people see the game as an incomplete, rushed mess, while others can somehow get past the blatantly missing part and enjoy what they got their hands on. Personally, while the Sith Lords could had been a far stronger sequel and a Star Wars RPG in its own right, I found myself enjoying it more often than not due to the high level of the writing, the refined and more custom gameplay and its central interesting themes

app_name: The Lord of the Rings Online™
review_text: good game overall

Well, it sucks to 'be there.' :P  I am a fan of the Alien franchise, and so far I probably quite underrated the first movie, as a slow one compared to the others. Also, I found most of the Aliens and AvP games not to grow up to the movies (although Monolith's AvP2 was very enjoyable.) And now, Alien: Isolation managed to do justice to the first movie, and also prove that you can make an Alien game that is so good that I don't wanna play it any longer. And that is a huge praise. ;)  What I took away from the first few minutes of the game (up to an hour or so, taking it very slowly), is that it is very atmospheric, uses lights and shadows very well, and does not have too many cheap jump scares, luckily. The story plays out in a crumbling, mostly abandoned space station, that looks very very much like the Nostromo from Alien. You are all on your own, and in the first hour or so, not much happened. The game looks great, they could have made humans a bit more realistic, but it's not disturbing, but these will not matter in five minutes because of the situation that you are in. ;)  After one hour, during which I explored about 2 corridors and a room, crawling my way through the shadows just in case, I felt that I have to stop. I realized that sitting in a dark room, I am very badly scared. The game does its job so well, that with minimal exposition of the story so far, just the ominous setting gave me a surprisingly strong feeling of dread. I was seriously concerned how much worse this would turn if the monster showed up. ;) (I might update this review if I find it. :)) )  In my opinion, this game really does the Alien universe justice. It brings you there, where the crew of the Nostromo was, and teaches you a lesson on your own skin on how much it sucked to be them, something that probably no movie could do. Extremely well done, although I might have to take it in small portions, in a well-lit room... :P I very highly recommend this to anyone who likes the atmosphere of the Alien stories, and is not (medically) faint of heart. ;) (Actually this may not be a joke... :P)

Pros: Dynamic combat  Lots of Loot to be had  Large world to explore  Decent mix of puzzles and combat  Cons: Rock, Paper Scissors driven conversation  Little questing direction  Crafting is hidden  Middle Ground: Story is serviceable  Camera can cause issues from time to time    Divinity: Original Sin is an isometric view turn based RPG. The game features two main characters that are customizable in regards to appearance, stat distribution, and abilities. Though I played the game solo, it can be played co-op with a friend with each person controlling one of the main characters (I believe).   The game forces you to use the two main characters, and you can have up to two more companions at anyone time. There is voice acted banter between the two leads and you can even set their personalities at character creation so that the A.I. determines their choices during some of these times. But, I would say there isn’t enough scripted interaction between the two. And generally the choices are black and white: a yes or moral option and no immoral option. If the two of you disagree a fight ensues...rock paper scissors style (literally). This method is used to solve all conversational arguments. At first it was some what funny, but eventually gets annoying as sometimes you win no problem, other times you lose horribly even though you were suppose to have an advantage. It’s just dang difficult to “read” a computer opponent. The other companions you find (you have to find them the game doesn’t force them into your path) have a quest and at times they will comment on actions that you have done, but overall they remain silent. If you don’t like those companions, eventually the game allows you to hire henchmen who start at a predetermined level and with predetermined stats. After you hire them, they level with you and you can make leveling choices. The game though is perfectly beatable with just your two main characters though (Lone Wolf perk helps this).  Combat is this game’s strength. While some people may sneer at it having a true turn based system, everything about the combat synergizes with itself. Oh, it’s raining, well my fire spells will do less damage, and my electric ones will do more. There’s poison on the ground, my fire spell just blew it up! There are buffs and debuffs that can be managed, positioning and healing can be important, but the true beauty is environmental control. Water on the ground freezing, setting the ground ablaze, creating an electrifying mist. Each one of these can be created by various combinations and they can affect both enemies and your team. There are various different weapons and armor, but it really boils down to int based, strength based, and agility based gear. While the combat is great, initially setting your character up for combat (specing) was not fun. “How do I distribute my points, there is so much...?” The game does not help you in this respect. You are on your own on how to create your character...but the beauty of this...if you started a caster, you can change him into a meaty warrior or a dexterous archer pretty easy. Any combination can yield good results, but it might not seem that way at first.  The world is huge (and hardly any loading screens...looking at you PoE), and filled with lots to do...almost too much to do. The game overall is long. I have over 90 hours logged on my first playthrough (though I would say at least 10-15 from me leaving it running in the background...yes I know I’m terrible). There are plenty of quests, monsters to kill, secrets to find. There are riddles and puzzles to solve, choices to kill or help creatures, intrigue. There are a few different environments to explore and tons of loot to find. Enemies have enough variety especially bosses to make encounters unique and intriguing. But with all this greatness, the game give you very little direction. Who do I need to talk to, where do I go next what do I need to do. Crafting is almost completely hidden. Sometimes there are hints and you stumble in the right direction eventually in your exploring but other time I had to backtrack talking to everyone in a town trying to figure out how to trigger the next leg of a quest.  The story is very generic. Your heros (source hunters...a little background is given) are headed to solve a murder which is believed to have been caused by dark powers During your investigation you and your partner open one heck of a can of worms (end of the world, the doom of all you know standard fantasy stuff). While not everything is predictable, you do get a sense of “Sorry, Mario. The princess is in another castle” feeling. I wish more emphasis had been put on fleshing out some of the main characters.  Overall the game was enjoyable easily recommendable but with the warning that the game takes a lot of time to finnish.

This... Game... Is... Amazing...  I don't need to tell you (the reader) to download this game. Just do it!  Ok, Now to write a serious review kind of thingy.  The gameplay is fluid and smooth, it feels to me (being predominantly a Xbox player) like a mixture of Halo and Titanfall, but in third-person and a lot more fast paced and energetic than both of those games. Either way, the variety between frames and weapons is great, but there are some exceptions of weapons that are just weaker versions of other weapon *cough* Akzani and Akstilettos, but other than those there isn't much in the way of simular weaponry. The gunplay is brilliant, all the guns (that i've tried anyway) feel great to use and have the 'oomph' that really make them feel effective aswell as being effective. The game looks great from a graphical standpoint, the music is spectacular and the sound effects make the weapons sound 'beefy' and feel great to use (aforementioned above). Since the title update 14 came out not long back, the game is more beginner friendly and there isn't a better time to start playing.  Now the Bad points that need to be present in every review.  The business model for the game is pretty bad: You as the player will require to buy slots for warframes and weapons to be able to build half of the things that you would want. Say that you had a set of weapons that you really like and you threw an Orokin catalyst, maybe a Forma or two on each of them and then you see another weapon that seems interesting to you or want to try it and see what the fuss is all about, but you have no slots and you can't claim the weapon until you sell one of you hard earned or you buy some slots to get more, there is always the option not to but if you don't then you will be limited to a small amount of warframes and weapons. It's the same situation with warframes and in my opinion it subtracts from the overall enjoyment of the game if you have this limitation of having two warframes and eight weapons. My next point is the waiting time for building weapons, frames and some materials like forma and fieldrons (fieldrons can also be obtained from invasions as with detonite injectors), but since it is a free-to-play game this sort of thing is inevitable simular to the buying of slots, weapons and frames. On the subject of buying weapons and frames and other accessories that can be bought with the paid for currency (platinum), most weapons and frames can be bought with platinum and this sort of thing can lead to a 'pay to win' basis, but some of the best gear in the game (primes) can only be found in the higher tier missions in the Orokin void. But they can also be bought with 'Prime Access' which involves the buyer spending a large amound of money for the latest primes that have been released and some other exclusive accessories that can only be obtained with the prime access. Yes I agree that this is either pay to win or breeching what is or isn't pay to win, but this is the sort of game where equipment isn't everything and skill can play a big factor in who would be the highest performing player in the mission.  Some more good points and a general rap up of the review (you must be a champ to get this far)  A good majority of the games weapons and warframes can be bought with platinum, but if you have mods and prime parts to sell, people in the trade chat will gladly buy those off of you for platinum or high tier mods and or prime parts. so arguably the game can be effectively played with spending any money on it at all. Having started my own clan in the game, the clan tech research is a fun thing which can be done on top of all the other things that are done in the game, also there is customizing the clan dojo itself which can take a very long time to achieve anything.  In conclusion i'm 327 hours in to the game and I have enjoyed every minute of it. I have spent £30 ($50) on the game, that was enough for me to get all the weapons and frames that I wanted and some materials on top of that to help with the dojo and the construction of my Zephyr warframe. My philosophy is that if you got at least 1 hour of entertainment for for £1 you spent on the game then you have got you money's worth out of the game. Lastly I fully recommend this game to anyone out there who has the Pc to run the game (which I may add doesn't require a great pc to run).  So... come join us Tenno.   For further information visit:   http://warframe.wikia.com/wiki/WARFRAME_Wiki  or  https://forums.warframe.com/

I have to admit I was a little late to the game when it came to the first game. For those that don't know, the first Lords of Shadow - and by extension this game as well - is a reboot of the franchise as opposed to a continuation of the original canon.  That being said, I have been greatly anticipating this sequel since finally playing and beating LoS and man was it ever worth all that waiting and anticipation! LoS 2 has you playing as the Prince of Darkness, Dracula (Gabriel Belmont in this universe) himself which really needs no explanation in regards to it's freaking cool factor.  Patrick Stuart reprises his role as Zobek (aka Death) and Robert Carlyle is back (and better than ever!) as Gabriel/Dracula, which alone are two good reasons why this game rocks! Also, Richard Madden as Alucard was a pretty splendid choice in my opinion. The sound and music - and the other voice work too - is all quite excellent as well.  I have been absolutely loving the gameplay so far, especially the combat which I feel has recieved a nice overhaul. There is still enough of the old system there that it should feel familiar and comfortable, but they also improved nicely upon some of the smaller snags I felt the first game suffered from. AI is a bit more agressive and (only slightly) less predictable this time, which is a bit refreshing. Also, for all intents and purposes - at least as far as gameplay is concerned - Gabriel and Dracula are two entirely different characters, despite actually being the same person.  Gone is the Combat Cross, Gabriel's signature weapon from LoS; instead Dracula most commonly wields the Shadow Whip which is a manifestation of his own blood that he can summon and control. Instead of Gabriel's Light and Shadow Magic, you now have the life-leeching Void Sword and the armor-piercing Chaos Claws which each offer their own form and style in combat. You can also feed on stunned opponents to recover your health in a pinch. Oh and instead of the consumables Gabriel used to carry around, Dracula wields powerful vampire abilities and other dark magics - Shadow Daggers, Void Projections, Bat Swarms and more; instead of being consumable they all have their own cool-down timers and thus behave more like spells would.  The game is much bigger and better all around for the most part, though there a few minor things that I personally find annoying. For instance exploration is way more 'hand holdy' than it was in LoS... to the point that ledges you can grapple and climb now glow faintly and are surrounded by a small swarm of bats, objects you can bash with your throwing daggers are outlined by a glowing red ring of what appears to be blood, and the addition of an actual function (hold LT) while platforming that outlines possible moves 2-3 steps ahead of where you're currently climbing/hanging. Not really a deal breaker, per say, but definitely takes a lot of the fun out of it. In LoS I actually missed quite a few collectibles due to not being able to find them or simply not being able to reach them once I did locate them - this in turn drove me to explore further and look all the harder for what I may have missed; the possibility for this level of challenge is all but impossible in LoS 2 it would seem.  I won't give away too much of the plot, for two reasons: one I have yet to beat the game and two it would just be a damn shame to spoil something so good. That being said the basic premise is this: a thousand years (and a 3DS game) have passed since the events of LoS, Dracula has been defeated and weakened - by his own blood no less - and thus left vulnerable to an attack from Satan, whom Gabriels seriously ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off the last time they met. Zobek makes a timely arrival to warn his 'old friend' of course and offers him both a way to regain his powers - to defeat Satan - and a way to free him from his curse - death at the hands of Zobek, who has supposedly located the Combat Cross. Thus the battle begins between the Prince of Darkness and the agents of Satan.

I would like to firstly disclose that I at the time of this review have only played around 30 minutes of this game so take what I say lightly as I feel that this is more of a first impressions rather than a full review.  The aim of this game is quite basic, stop aliens who are on your lawn from getting to your porch. The same continuous level just with more and more enemies.  The controls for the game are quite simple. Them being “A” to go move left and “D” to move right, 1-3 to select weapons, “Q” to use shockwave (which sends all enemies back to the beginning of the lawn), “W” to use Cane time (slows down time) and Infuriate (no idea what it does other than creates a fiery circle around the player).   The options menu is terrible!! One switch for Music (on/off), one for SFX (on/off) and one for tutorials (on/off), that’s it, no sliders, nothing. And that music is just irritating, the same song over and over again.  When I saw the shed I saw a glimmer of hope for the game, a way for the player to upgrade his/her weapons and defend their porch, but wait… “Get orbs!” No… don’t tell me, you can buy orbs with real money… Honestly, I thought we were past micro transactions in PC games (we wont talk about games like TF2). I went into Gray Matter expecting an 'alright' point-and-click adventure with decent puzzles and mechanics; what I got instead was an incredibly well written storytelling experience, albeit with gameplay one would still deem 'alright'. The atmosphere created was very mysterious, with heavy dashes of both gothic and paranormal sensibilities, so fans of those conventions should be quite pleased with the presentation. Because the genre of the game is point-and-click, one shouldn't go in expecting heavy action- although there were some great build ups to dramatic revelations, and tension in the style of a true tale of mystery, which keeps the player hooked until the end.  Pros: + Quality narrative with an interesting mix of characters + Excellent voice acting and attention to detail + Puzzles in my opinion not overly difficult or easy, but might require a glance or two at a guide + In-game graphics were okay, but the cutscene/hand-drawn art were quite nice + Great Soundtrack, both ambient and vocalized + Samantha Everett and David Styles as dual protagonists were well developed and had intriguing interactions with both each other & side characters + You learn a little something about magic tricks and neurology. Think about the last time this happened in a game you played!  Neutrals/Cons: - Certain areas of the game experienced a HUGE degree of slow down (Ie. Christ Church Exterior) - No where does it mention (from what I recall?) that you can double click on an area to have the character you are controlling JOG to the spot instead of casually strolling - Subjectively there was some potential wasted with regards to character development, and the 'building up' so to speak did not always feel like it flowed smoothly - The ending felt a bit abrupt, and again there was a lot of stuff from the story which could have been wrapped up in a more complete manner. Was hoping for a more detailed epilogue due to all the time and effort invested in developing the cast, but alas many questions went unanswered  Overall: 8.5 out of 10  If you're looking for a slower paced game with a page-turner of a story, and either don't mind or enjoy point-and-click mechanics, 'Gray Matter' is the game for you.
