It's a flawed game - in part because it's hard not to compare it to others made using the same game engine and by the same studio. Paradox Interactive grand strategy games are second to none. Unfortunately Stellaris lacks some of the same finish as their other games. While there's substantial depth in mechanics, there's a notable lack of flavor in the mid-game as well as occassional flaws in the game logic. For example, if I've developed the technology to settle 'desert worlds' why do I need to separately research 'arid worlds?' While the start of the game is a lot of fun, there's also a notable lack of mid-game flavor - think random events, event chains, exploration quests, and anomolous planets to discover with strange natural histories. Other features that make sense for a 4X game like this one like espionage and civilian trade routes are non-existant.  Why am I recommending it? - it benefits from the core design paradigms that have made other Paradox Interactive games so good and addicting. There's already a lot of depth to it when it comes to government management, planet management, resource management, technologies, and fleet design, and it's for the most part well executed. However, one thing one may either love or hate about Paradox Interactive is their expansion policy. There will be a lot of free patches that will often add new mechanics even. However, those are paid for by the plethora of associated DLCs that go with them.   Finally there're a lot of little things that they just seem to get right - the art style, the music, the character designs, and, well even the naming scripts the game uses are good enough that I never feel the urge to rename anything myself, but in most cases, it gives you the option to do just that.

There are a lot of small problems in this game. But that is this game. Every time you look into something you can find some part of the game you like but also another 4x space game that did that better.  This game doesn't do anything well.

What I liked about the game   • The gameplay can be very addictive and fun, especially when you're running over pedestrians and destroying other drivers.  • I liked the idea that you can see the driver in the top left-hand corner, seeing his facial expressions, when something happens during the level.  • You have three different ways of winning each level. My favourite was destroying other drivers.  • I liked the arcade style of the game. • The vehicles in the game are awsome, You can also unlock other driver's vehicles and use them in other levels.  • You can upgrade your car.    What I disliked or was disappointed with   • Can be frustrating sometimes, police can be a pain in the ♥♥♥♥. Sometimes you don't get credits when you should. Sometimes the last driver can prove tricky to kill.  • The vehicles in the game can be hard to control. The vehicle brakes aren't very good and sometimes turning a corner is like turning a ship. • Out of the three different ways of beating the level, destroying each driver is probably the best way to beat the level, because killing a certain number of pedestrians takes too long and doing the laps isn't very rewarding. • The soundtrack does get repetitive and so does the gameplay, especially towards the end.  • It's a shame you can't visually see the upgrades you made to the car.     Conclusion    A fun and addictive game, which is different compared to over racing games. It can get repetitive, but I still recommend it to any retro gaming fan.  70/100

Paying over $10 for this game will leave you with severe buyer's remorse. If you just want facts, skip the next 2 paragraphs.  To begin, I'll state that if you've played X3: Reunion/Terran Conflict/Albion Prelude and loved them, this is not a sequel, spiritual successor or anything along those lines. X Rebirth falls under the 'inbread abomination' category. It borrows heavily from the lore, but not from any of the concepts that made the previous titles fun to play.  Don't be fooled by the positive reviews, half of them aren't even consistently deluded. With comments like '10/10' then individually listing the areas of the game as followed 'Sound: 6/10, Graphics: 8/10, Gameplay: 4/10. Totally loved it' it's as if the positive reviews came from people either associated with the company or from people so dissapointed that they decided a drunken review to sucker more people out of their money was the best alternative to admitting this game was a flop.   Graphics: Nothing special here and if it runs on your computer properly it can be very pretty at times, about 50% of the uses are consistantly reporting massive framerate issues even after 6+ months of release. It uses directx 9, which is several generations old at this point and most believe this was due to it's, all but confirmed by the devs, origins as a console port.   Sound: The voice acting is mediocre to bad, it's repetitive too so there is really no way around listening to it.    Controls: Severly limiting, more complicated than previous 'X' titles due to a complete failure to streamline them into a simpler form. No initial support for flightsticks on a space sim, the menus you use to navigate and control your *cough* 'empire' *cough* are obviously designed around the limitations of a primarily controler driven interface, they took the menus from previous titles, removed them, then added in even more awkward and convoluted replacements that are twice as hard to navigate and produce half (if even that) the intended results.    Gameplay: If you like ultra repetitive minigames where you ping the map then blow up space debris for money, you'll love this game. If you like reflex minigames where you get to hear horrible voice acting, repeated over, and over, and over etc. you'll love this game. Your ship, The Albion Skunk, is essentially invincible and overtuned to the point that it's almost not fun. The ships in your fleet can't be directly controlled, they can only be given very, very basic orders and they often decide the best thing to do is follow you instead of completing those orders. These ships die or just dissapear because they're probably as sick of this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t game as you are at this point.   Don't waste your money on this game, the only thing they did right was hyping the hell out of it to sell copies and even then it's arguable if that truly was the right thing. After this stinker, I'd be hard pressed to see how Egosoft expects to sell another game in this franchise to anyone.

Toybox Turbos is a very fun, but short racing game. As others have said, it is the spiritual successor to the old Micro Machines games. I loved the original NES Mirco Machines so I was excited to try this one. The game has good graphics, fairly interesting vehicles, and bright and clever course design. The campaign starts out quite easy but gets challenging, especially for the last two cups. Unlike the original Micro Machines, there are some power ups scattered around the track. Luckily, they are not so overpowered as to take over the racing / skill element, ala Mario Kart. The campaign has a variety of different challenges, so it's not just racing. There are time trials, picking up clocks, and an interesting mode where you try to pass as many slow cars as possible in 3 laps. Each level also features a boss level where you must win a series of short, head to head sprints vs. the boss. The game features bright, cheery graphics and the music and sound effects are appropriate for this style of game. The game also runs very well on my rather older pc, which is a plus.  There is also multiplayer. Depending on the time of day, it can be hit or miss finding a match. The game also features local multiplayer which might be very fun on big picture etc, but to have more than one player requires Xbox 360 controllers. Multiplayer races are short sprints run on a single screen, with the slowest cars falling off the screen. It plays a bit like a party game, not a serious racer in MP. This game's campaign is pretty short. I finished it in 4.2 hours, getting 100% achivements, except for local multiplayer. Achievement hunters beware, you need an Xbox 360 controller to get the local multiplayer achievement, which kind of sucks.   Overall, I liked the game a lot, but I do have some criticisms. My biggest complaint would be the lack of customization. Races are always 3 laps, which is quite short. Courses themselves are really short as well. You must always choose a 'class' of racecars, which is preset. There is no option to use all of the cars or custom classes which would add variety. The game is missing a lot of the classic car types from the original. Powerboats, Road Warriors, helicopters, etc. are all absent. Overall, most of these issues could be easily fixed with some DLC or the inclusion of Steam Workshop. It's a solid game with a lot of potential and offers a lot of racing fun. It's the type of game that you can play for 10 minutes after a long day at work and just relax. It might even cheer you up! For 4ish hours of gaming without counting multiplayer, you may want to wait for a sale, however.

Rule one - buy it ONLY on sale. Not worth 24,99€, all and all 4,99€ is about right. How would I describe this? If EA would make a tycoon game this would be it. Details are lovely but the cost...  I would say the game is trying too hard even - yes you can check every single pedestrian who has an ID on him, know his job, home, relaxation and shopping habits. So you know where to transport him/her. You can also find the details of the locomotives, carriages, trams and buses are lovely and not just that the cities and cars too. Also the game never crashed on me (running windows 10 64 bit - respect!) Those would be pros. So what are the cons?  No ships, aircraft, monorails, maglevs, subways, city management, AI, multiplayer. The construction of railways can be a real pain in the butt, endless collisions with terrain, improper angle for road crossings and no matter how much money I invested to a straight line across the map I could never get 300 km/h from the fastest train. And the game feels kind of limited in a sense. Why for example can´t I combine a tram and a train station into one ?  Overall I realize that this game is from a new developer therfore I am not as critical, I believe (rather hope) new features will appear eventually. The game is fun for quite a while if you learn the basics and have a big enough map (reminds me can we have a map editor pretty please?). So if you want to support an indie game wait for a sale(!) and go for it. If you want to have similar amount of fun for free go to google and type in openttd.

Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon is an excellent port of good ol' panzer general mechanics to WH40K universe. Do not expect flashy animations just a hex grid, lot's of units and the tactical fight to secure key objectives.  I'm no WH40k expert, never played the tabletop. I just happen to like the computer games based on this universe, so I have general idea about the units, but didn't know there were dozens of tank variants available. It's great, but at the same time can be very confusing for 'normal' (see not WHk40 fanboys), because we have hard time figuring out what does what.  The problem is, the units have no descriptions. Just 2 or 3 sentences to describe the units. Because I'm pretty sure us 'normal' people have no idea what's a nova cannon and why should I use a tank with it. Why should I take baneblade instead stormsword?  Second thing that did surprise me a little, is that several models use Cadian troopers instead the featured Steel Legion. I mean sure, it not a game breaking thing. But considering how much the game costs, I fail to see why there are no proper models for all units. It's like you order a pear pie and get apple pie. Doesn't matter if both taste great.  Now I come to the thing that bothered me probably the most during my gameplay. I always felt that in WH40k there are milions of imperial guardsmen fighting and that single space marine is worth ten thousands of them. Now I did not have that feeling in this game. There is no unit caps for elite units, the costs of better units is not that higher, so eventually you just field a bunch of titans, along heavy tanks and artillery and complement that with space marines.  Something inside me was really bothered by this. Same goes for the enemy as the campaign progresses. Instead milions of orcs, you meet a bunch of elite orcs, tank busta spam (probably because the designer though player will use mostly vehicles) and orcish vehicles. No orcish horde of sluggas and low orcs that would swarm you and give that 'orky thing' feeling.  I personally would prefer higher total unit limit and way higher costs for vehicles and especially spacemarines. Together with caps on elite units and way lower restriction on number of basic units. For example that 3 units of sluggas would take just 1 slot etc. Some change that would make low 'tech' units viable later in game. Also the important melee combat aspect of WH40k felt a little underwhelming to me. Even assault marines felt like fodder to me.   Speaking about units, it is really nice you can rename and check history of your units. But for some reason I didn't feel much attached to them. Even in ol' panzer general, units got medals. I noticed that Panzer Corps game has heroes in units, so why not here? No elite replacement as well, so really hard to keep 'elite' force with so much dmg going on. All in all, Instead some sort of attachment, they were just fodder to me. No reason to keep them alive, when they lose most experience anyway and I can always buy new unit.  My last 'complain' is about the scenario design. Maps are nice, objectives are fine, I dislike the fact that low tier units vanish after certain point, making your low tier units useless and the enemy force less diverse. What I missed most, was some kind of secondary objective that would make the missions much more interesting. For example rescue surrounded guardsmen, or save lone baneblade which would get you a core unit faster. Or simple objectives like capture this secondary point (warehouse) and get extra requistion points. Something which would introduce some kind of risk vs. reward element.  Patching seems to be at good pace and a lot of stuff was adden since launch, but I had to wonder about some balancing changes (1.04) . For example some of the Salamander vehicles got support trait, whilst the very same vehicles in other chapters do not have it. Now If I want Whirlwind tank for support, I have to take the green one, instead blue one... Are only Salamanders bright enough to support their men?  I do not wish to sound that I do not like the game. In fact I love the game, I really liked the narrative campaign and I see a lot of potential in this series - that's why I have bought it, despise it's higher price tag. The potential for new factions, units and campaigns is tremendous.  I would recommend this game to everyone that likes WH40k and Panzer General games.

Don't let Locoland's (originally Steamland) dated graphics deceive you, this classic RTS's gameplay holds up very well for a 12 year old relic only now making its way to Steam. The charming steampunk setting and cute story will entertain you through tough as nails gameplay that is sure to satisfy the most dedicated strategy enthusiast.   Take note that I am generally not one for strategy games, so my opinion may be a bit noobish compared to a genre aficionado. That said, the gameplay consists of classic strategy fair with the added twist of unit movement being relegated to set rail paths. Keeping this mechanic in mind, you must capture and construct buildings, build your force (in this case trains), complete a variety of objectives, and maneuver your trains in and out of on-rail battles against enemy units that are altogether unforgiving. Make no mistake, the game is hard, super hard. Although getting a hang of the controls and hotkeys helps immensely, this isn't a casual title in any sense of the word.  My biggest complaint is a lack of tutorial. I spent a TON of time figuring out how things worked and what exactly I needed to do, but perhaps I've been spoiled by the casualness of newer games, even hardcore titles give more instruction than we receive here. But to its credit, Locoland is from an era when hand holding and player swaddling wasn't a thought in the developer's mind. Learning to play the game was as much of the gameplay as anything else.   Despite my frustrations, Locoland is a very fun strategy game. I only wish we were given more graphical options, perhaps windowed mode, but considering its age I imagine it would be difficult to implement or that it's simply not possible. Either way, strategy fans take heed, you'll want to check this one out. If you often find yourself reminiscing on the gaming days of old, this is your chance to relive a unique and challenging RTS at a very reasonable price.   Bottom Line: Recommended for strategy game enthusiasts looking for something unique and challenging, although a bit dated.    Note - This game was provided by the publisher with the request to write an informative review. No other consideration, compensation or instruction was received.

One of the best games I have ever played.  The artstyle of the game is wonderful, As I like to walk around for hours just looking at how beautiful the game is.  There are six characters. (Two of them are DLC) All with their own playstyles and personalities. The six Include: Axton The Commando (He's like Roland in the first game with his turrets and stuff), Maya The Siren, Salvador The Gunzerker, Zer0 The Assassin, Gaige The Mechromancer (DLC), and Krieg the Psycho (DLC).  With 3 skilltrees with each character, your playstyles for the characters can be limitless (Probably not).  There is a looting system where you have to fight a boss for a unique weapon/item or legendary weapon/item. They will have a 1/30 chance of dropping the items. (They did make drops more frequent so I don't really know what the chance of an item dropping is now)   The weapons and items all do different things, with different stats, so you would like to get the best one to become a true Badass.  The leveling system: In The third playthrough (Ultimate Vault Hunter Mode) will have all items and enemies scale to your level, There are two DLCs that increase the level cap: Those 2 Vault Hunter upgrade packs (I don't remember what they're called, so sorry), The first one will increas your level cap from 50 to 61, and the second one (You need the first one for this) Increases it to 61 to 72.   There is also something called Overpowered Levels: After you reach level 72, you can go into a place called 'Digistruct Peak' in which you can increase your level even further, as the enemies are tougher, and you can level up to Overpowered Level 8 (Level 80).  I really do reccomend this game.

Spec Ops the Line is an interesting game. On one hand its a refreshingly different from other shooters but on the other hand it is frustratingly the same and often feels its missing out on its full potential.   Spec Ops the Line is a 3rd person shooter that is inspired by Joseph Conrad's novel 'Heart of Darkness' which also servered as the inspiration for the film 'Apocoalypse Now.' You play as Captain Walker as him and his troops Lugo and Adams work their way through the ruins of Dubai which has been ravaged by sand storms, and there is lots of speculation as to why the U.S. Army's attempted evacuation of Dubai failed. The plot to the game is filled with interesting insights into the characters of the game and it has some surprises in store for the player.   Let's start with the games strengths. Spec Ops the Line does for sure succeed in most of the aspects. The gameplay is good, if a little generic for a 3rd person shooter. The story is great for sure, it delves into the mind of Captain Walker as these horrors occur and the game presents moral choices which are genuinly really difficult to decide. I'll talk more about this later in the review. The voice acting in the game is top notch (Walker is voiced by the always excellent Nolan North) and the character writing is great here. The game features a great soundtrack and solid sound design. The graphics are good and surprisingly despite the whole taking place in the same desert destroyed city the setting is never boring and actually one of the games strongest points. The game also features some more sureal moments that are some of my favourite parts of the game. The work effectivly and are a smart way to mix things up.   Now, Spec Ops the Line does have some slip ups for sure. The game first of all is short, I beat it on normal in 5 hours which ever for shooter standards is pretty short. It features options for stealth gameplay but i found my AI team mates would often screw up my attempts at stealth by running around like they're in open combat as well as sometimes even opening fire with unsilenced weapons. I also found it a little ridiculous that an entire infantry batallion would stay behind in a ruined No Mans Land of a city without trying to get any kind of help when they started running into trouble. But that's mostly just me nitpicking, it probably won't bother most people. There's also a plot point involving Walker that I can't get into due to spoiler reasons but it really makes some things that happen in the game make no sense.   One thing with the game that lowered my opinion of it the most is actually how in a lot of ways I feel it didn't live up to its potential. First off is the interaction's with your AI team mates during combat, you can give them a couple orders but with the exception of telling Lugo to snipe somebody i rarely used these which didn't make me feel like much of a team. I found they also were kind of idiots in combat occasionally, i saw Adams and Lugo shooting walls sometimes and running in an open area with heavy enemy fire which didn't really make me feel that they were well trained soldiers. I mentioned earlier in the review that the sound design is solid and that's true, but that's it. It's just solid sound design. It feels so cookie cutter and generic, modern military shooters have extremely high standards for sound design in my opinion because so much of the sounds in this game feel artificial and soft. Battlefield is the best reference point i can think of for amazing sound design in a military shooter game, Battlefield sound design really highlights what's possible for shooters like this.    So, I saved my favourite thing about this game for last. Spec Ops the Line has some choices you have to make in the game. The way these choices are presented to the player is possibly the most well done I have ever seen in a video game. This isn't like a Bioware game where your choices are clearly laid out for you and you have to chose. In Spec Ops you simply find yourself in a situation and it leaves it up to you what to do. It doesn't give you any hints and it doesn't tell you your options, you simply have to actually think about it and make a choice. You also have a time limit on all these choices which adds to the tention but you have no idea how much time you have left. You have to look at the situation and see if you think time is running out or not. It's realistic and it makes some of these situations really intense. It also shows how easy it can be for things to go bad when you have to make a tough choice in the heat of the moment. In my opinion this is the absolute best way a game can present the player with complicated choices and I really hope more developers use this design philosophy.   Overall its a pretty solid game that I can recommend for sure, especially if its on sale. 7/10

Corvo Attano, The Bodyguard of Her Majesty, Empress Jessamine Kaldwin, Framed for her planned out Murder.  Dishonored, an incredible Journey The Shows Corvo exacting revenge on The Lord Regent, Hiram Burrows.  The Game has a unique way of storytelling and RPG-based Gameplay. Upgrade your powers and weapons or just kick the game with you starting equipment. Upgrade at your leisure, gather bonecharms for various buffs, and ultimately the game allows you to choose your play style. Stealthily kill enemies, Get into massive and wild fights, or simply work behind the scenes to give your target a fate worse than death. The Story is altered by your kill count, ultimatly making the world itself and story darker, or, if you use non-lethal methods, you'll find the ending to have a much lighter and easier tone.  This game is worth it, and so are the DLC. A must-play if you havent already.

Very much like Diablo and Torchlight, but with Marvel characters. It's free to play so its definitely easy to get into. However, it's expensive as hell to get new characters. They say that you can get all the characters free from within the game. This is true but I'm pretty sure it would take years of playing to unlock half of them. You have to buy them with points that drop off of enemies randomly and infrequently. So if you want more than one toon it will definitely cost you.  I like the game though. I picked The Punisher as my character and I've really enjoyed it. I'm blowing stuff up and mowing down minions with almost constant gunfire. You get to feel really powerful where it counts and it does get challenging where it should.   Now the bosses could use some work. They try to make you be tactical but with a health gauge that is constantly refilling and the ability to run from them to catch a couple HPs and then jump back in, it feels like you really don't have to be tactical at all. Just don't stand there and you will be fine. Of course, it has been getting progressively harder so this may change. Maybe it would be better if the bosses didn't telegraph what they were going to do. Jugernaut, for example, has a dash move that he rams you with. The game puts up a red arrow showing what he is doing and where he is going with it. When you are not in an instance and playing with others itt helps because there is so much going on on the screen that you wouldn't be able to see it coming. Unfortunately, it definitely takes a lot of the challenge away when you are one-on-one.  The loot is almost always relevant to your hero. I very rarely pickup something for another. I honestly think that there is too much loot though. Since it is all geared towards you, it's either going to be better than what you have or it's not. There really doesn't seem to be any reason why I would feel I should keep it for certain situations. It's either better or its garbage. I really wish they would have added an auto-sort for your inventory. If there is I would like to know how.

The game is an old school point and click adventure game that we really haven't seen much since the end of the Sierra days on PC. Tim Schafer was heavily involved in this genre during the early years creating hits such as The Secret of Monkey Island, Monkey Island 2: LeChuck's Revenge, Full Throttle and his last title being Grim Fandango in 1998. A lot has changed since then in video games, but luckily the soul of adventure games still remain. Double Fine kept the mechanics of this title very simple while still breathing fresh air in the story, visuals and music of this beautiful world they have created. Every scene looks hand painted. The voice acting and dialog are top notch and the music fits perfectly within the story of the game. This game isn't for everyone, but any old school PC gamer who grew up with Sierra and old LucasArts games will have a blast playing Broken Age. I would say this was so far one of the best purchases I have made in a while. Just having the chance to watch Double Fine create this game from the bottom up was worth the price.

They really have the presentation locked in tight. It's also runs extremely well compared to other PC ports.  But its diversity vs quantity of content peaks at about 15-20 hours. Before I hit that mark I enjoyed the grind. And for me that's enough value. For others maybe not. I would put it in the same enjoyment category as Far Cry, Assassins Creed, Shadow of Mordor, etc. While different games in many ways (with some being better than others), they are still exciting open-world adventures with a limited lifespan.

Assault Android Cactus is a twin stick shooter that sees you progress through an overtaken space ship destroying hordes of enemies and bullet hell style bosses.  There is a selection of robots to choose between each with a very different set of available weapons ranging from the fairly standard gun of Cactus to the more bizarre bladed helicopter of Aubergine. Each character also has a more powerful weapon that can be used to destroy large enemies more quickly but this weapon can only be used on a rechargeable basis. Each level is completed while constantly trying to look to recharge your battery which constantly depletes. This means that each level is essentially completed against the clock and death acts as a time delay rather than an all-out game ender. I love this mechanic and think it is the best idea to happen to the shooter or bullet hell genre in recent memory and I hope more developers take this idea on board as it makes death… something that happens frequently… into a managed penalty rather than the end criteria.  The game is extremely accessible to casual players and can be blown through in a couple of hours but achieving high scores can easily become an obsession as you look for the best strategies and best androids to complete each stage.  The level design is superb with platforms, conveyor belts and rearranging floors making the games level unique and engaging in what could otherwise be a repetitive game. The enemy design is equally as interesting with particular stand out types being the dog type androids who ground you in place and the swarms of insect androids that appear from the destroyed android hives.  The endgame in AAC sees you push for increasingly higher and high scores to gain better rankings. Gaining an S rank in a level, involves completing it without a single knock-down. S+ involves no knock-downs but also killing every enemy in the level in one long successful chain. This is equivalent to getting perfect in a guitar hero game or never missing a beat.  Overall the game is a joy to play and definitely one of my favourite twin stick shooters of all time. An extremely varied roster and wonderful charming presentation makes this a must buy for fans of the genre.  For our video review check out the link below:  http://www.thegametutor.co.uk/videos/game-tutor-grades/151017_game-tutor-grades-assault-android-cactus

Lili: Child of Geos was originally a mobile game featuring cinematic graphics, a female protagonist, and light rpg elements. It was successfully ported to steam and is developed and published by BitMonster, inc.  You play as Lili, a young protégé of her father (who is a professor), who is sent to a mysterious island thought to be abandoned long ago in order to collect rare flowers for a project. It turns out, the island isn't abandoned as she had thought. Throughout the island are tree spirits, who have taken over the ruins and installed themselves as the leading caste of the place. Beneath them are the wooden constructs they have built to live in servitude. app_name: The Secret of Monkey Island: Special Edition
review_text: Okay: there's a lot to be said for the Monkey Island games, but I'll just leave this little bit here. Your main character's name is 'Guybrush Threepwood,' and you're fighting undead pirates. It's an awesome game in the tradition of point-and-click adventure, which is awesome, and the puzzles are challenging while not being too obtuse.

After two hours, three missions I dare to say it's worth the 5€.  Basically it's a real-time strategy where you're not playing a cigar-smoking general who commands armies of tanks, instead your units are medics, firefighters, policemen and other people who have anything to do with emergencies; maybe only Batman is missing. The game has a sandbox-like campaign, where you are assigned as the man directing the city's first line of defense during calamities, and you will get - I presume - randomly generated emergencies including extinguishing fires of various sizes - from a few smoking bushes to blazing multistory buildings, catching pickpockets and restarting failing hearts. Time to time, however, a pregenerated larger scale operation will pop up which will certainly require a combined operation. For example in the first and second one you will need both medics and firefighters - with gasmasks in the second, while you need medics, criminal profilers and marksmen in the third. All in all, I have no problems with the variety of missions so far. But the game has problems. The most important - for me - is that it lacks active pause; when you stop the time, you cannot issue orders. Many would say 'it's called real-time for a reason', but I would answer: 'If it was real-time strategy then an ambulance would reach its destination in 30 minutes or so, and not within seconds. So let's call these accelerated time strategy, and as such, they need active pause. Especially when you have to act for all your men and women, because they will apathetically suffocate in a smoke-filled building should you not order them outside.' And with that last sentence we reached the topic of AI. Well, it's simple, and not without problems. For example the firemen will target the nearest fire within a few a meters when they have a hose in their hands, but will gladly try to take the longer route to reach their assigned target, even if that way the hose will be too short. So don't try to send them on longer journeys unattended, which means a lot of micromanagement when your hands would be full even with a more proactive crew. Another problem is that they just don't seem to comprehend the gravity of these emergencies. For example, in the third mission I had six marksmen ordered to riddle two mobsters with bullets. The culprits were of course running towards the edge of the map, and my unit of cold professionals, the tip of the spear, the edge of the knife, the crack of my ... eh... were walking after them. And I don't mean they did not take a vehicle or something, I mean that they simply did not feel like running. I had to order a donut-eating patrolman into the criminals path, and - luckily - they immediately opened fire on him, giving enough time to my SWAT team to finish sightseeing and catch up with them. My condolences to the widow, her husband died a heroic, although otherwise completely avoidable death. Bleh.  I think that's all I could say about the game. The sounds are okay and the graphics are, well, there. I mean you won't drop your chin, unless the most recent game you played is Defender of the Crown, but it's not bad at all. Long story short, as I mentioned before the game has its own share of sometimes hair-thinning problems, and I would hesitate to call it a good buy should I have paid 20€ or so for it, but for 5€ it's a bargain.

Hm, what's to say? Well... it's okay at best. I got this on sale for $2 and I can't say it's worth more than that. The game is all of 30 minutes long maybe, assuming you play the zombie mini-game. Basically, Gynophobia is about a young male of indeterminate age with a paralyzing phobia of spiders and females. His mother slips him some strange potion and he goes on a mental bender within a dream, fighting his fears to overcome them. That's basically Gynophobia in a nutshell. While I feel like there is an idea of a better story to be had here, the work simply hasn't been put into it.  To start, Gynophobia has some buggy walking detection. The difference between a street and a curb can cause your character to bounce up and down while sometimes loading in from a continue screen leaves your movement (and controls frozen). Additionally, you can surf atop of enemies! Land squarely on an enemy's head and you'll be taken for a ride wherever they go. Dead foes also present walking obstacles until they fade into the ground.   Concerning the art, outside of the last boss, it's generally very low. The female zombie-like enemies resemble something from a cruder version of the first Timesplitters with poorly stretched out textures and barely recognizable humanoid shapes. The spiders are somewhat better off, but I'm not certain as to why. According to the developer, these are stock assets. Understood, but they really don't make things look all that great.  This is the kind of title that's bad enough to be amusing and I suspect it capitalizes on that concept so people like Jim Quisition (who gets referenced in this game) will give it free advertising and cash in value. Gynophobia DOES have the potential to be an interesting game, but it's so short and cheaply done that all the tongue in cheek references can't make it genuinely good. I honestly wish someone more competent could have helmed this titled because the concept is there, but the execution is barely worth the penny. The game is so-so at best.

STOP! Don't look at the screenshots. Just give it a shot and you'll fall in love ~~for those who played, see what I did there ;)~~  'Emily is Away' is a short but surprisingly well-made game, contrary to what the screenshots might suggest. The ingenious use of the IM window coupled with a very relatable script encourages you to become completely immersed in the game. The game then exploits your immersion to evoke and manipulate emotions with extraordinary finesse, taking you on an emotional rollercoaster which actually leaves you feeling slightly drained at the end. Magicka is a game where you are a wizard, trying to conjure spells using elements. The catch is that multiple combinations yield multiple spells, most of which will severely injure yourself and your teammates.   The good  You are given from start, after a quick tutorial on the basics, all the elements. This allows you to beat the game with anything. Combining elements is the key factor of the game, where you can cast a stone armor to protect yourself and lay healing mines for your teammate who crushes enemies using a steam-electric-arcane beam. Without a mana or cooldown system. there are some hidden goodies to look for, some only for the sake of it, like the mooses, or magicks, a spell that has to be entered in the right order. Magicks are unlockable combinations that are far more powerful and/or unique than your normal spells. From slowing time to freezing the entire screen, or as simple as a teleport of flammable oil. They serve the purpose of progression, and one is even a plot point of the game. The game is also a living pile of references, ranging to movies to games. If you like to see that kind of stuff, it's plenty in this game.   The bad  The plot is simple: Your goal is to defeat the main wizard baddie. No heavy plot twists or anything. The game is also filled with bugs, bust most of them are fixed within seconds. Gamebreaking bugs can be fixed with an easy restart, which will take you a few screens back. The netcode tend to be laggy for the clients, even with low ping. Most player characters are DLC, but most of them are simple reskins with no unbalanced stats. Some extra content are also DLC-only.   The ugly    Sometimes the physics are wonky, with the knockback being powerful enough to knock your wizard several meters in the air. You can even reach spots you weren't meant to this way. Friendly-fire is also a key factor in the game, which can get problematic in certain situations, such as in tight spaces or a miscast. Hilarity can ensue if the wonky physics kick in. There may or may not be some plot holes around. Not that relevant, since the plot is already an excuse to cast stuff around. Some bosses can be one-shotted. While some can provide a worthwhile fight, some can be downed in seconds. However, one-shotting a boss requires some skill (and luck), and can be satisfying if you did it right.  Nutshell  Grab this game if you got the coin and friends to play with. I still haven't played through all the chapters and DLC-content, but the fun-factor I had playing with my friend is undeniable. Yes, there will be rage over that single miscast, yes, there will be random deathmatches when someone's ♥♥♥♥♥♥, and yes, there will be fooling around whenever there's the chance to. And bugs. And that's it's charm. There's no problem with those, since players can be revived in a matter of seconds.  The fun from this game came from laughing about failures of each other and the good times you spent with them, and when seriousness kicks in when a horde of NPCS appear and you enter a tactical approach to them. If you just care about goofing with your friends in a co-op game, Magicka is there.
Remember: when you see an anomaly, don't move.

TL;DR I would not buy this game if I hadn't already. But I would advise people playing it. Make of that what you will. Simply because of the creators who actually put their heart into aspects of this game and because of modders who really make it shine; this is a fun game to play and worth the time. It is NOT worth ANY money.   'But why?' You might ask. The extremely long rant to follow are *some* (not all, I am just tired of writing) my reasons:  This is a love hate thing with the Sims 3. I was so addicted to Sims 2 I lost whole days sitting in front of my computer planning out their day to day routines and hoping that they actually did well. (I know a lot of simmers just want the absolutely worst outcomes and love either killing and/or torturing their poor Sims)   But this game changed that a little. I still don't have a bunch of murder, sex or terror mods that make their lives truly miserable, but I don't care near as much for the characters I make or choose to play, I don't have much of a connection to them and sometimes I just stop caring and walk away leaving them to do their own thing until I just save and exit. Sims 3 lost a lot of charm Sims 2 had and honestly, I blame this entirely on EA's way of thinking which seriously tanked in the customer help department about the same time. It also began to be called the worst company every year and so on and so on. The game is pushed more and more for microtransations and not much else now. Especially with the upcoming Sims 4.   Microtransactions are a good thing, do not get me wrong please. These are very small purchases someone can make with real money online to pay for all different types of digital goodies to use in game. For most things this is a term MMORPGs use and they sell cosmetic things. Outfits, pets, mounts, hair, etc. etc.   With games like Team Fortress 2 this has not only kept the game alive, but brought forth an entirely new type of community where artists and creators come together to make outstanding creations which the community can then vote on and the original creators can make real money from their serious effort and hard work; hand in hand with the original game's creators to make something entirely new. This idea has even started to be embraced by MMORPGs such as EQ2 which now has their own type of workshop where players can now create things for that game. It goes on and on and on how awesome microtransactions can be for a game, bring it back to life and keep it ticking with new content and eye candy for everyone. It's even been how some companies now fund the next expansion, start new servers or how they are able to further polish their games.  Sims 3 though went and tore this concept apart (as far as I am concerned) and with Sim Points introduced a new low in this field. Sim points are nothing new, the way they are handled in Sims 3 though is. If you want to buy a certain type of object that has new animations and/or has an actual use in the game that would change it at all; then you cannot just buy that item but actually have to spend more money than you would just buying a stuff pack alone. These things are usually leaning towards harassing the player even in their game to buy the items. Everything with Sims 3 is an add to buy something else in Sims 3 and it never stops even when the player is playing. You can turn these options off but let's look at the key point for EA: Sell items to little girls playing. Mostly these little girls do not understand the value of a dollar, do not understand what EA is about or how little they could care. I'm not saying a lot of companis are bleeding hearts but it's more than obvious that EA takes the cake when it comes to giving their customers the finger.   On top of all of this, there are the bug and game stopping glitches. I want to state first of all, ALL GAMES WILL HAVE BUGS AND GLITCHES ALL OF THE TIME ON ANY CONSOLE OR PC NO MATTER WHAT. Even Mario still has glitches! It doesn't matter, games cannot be 100% polished at all times, it is not possible. But game breaking glitches that either force the player to stop playing, do not allow the player to continue to play or break the game entirely, ruin the save or even break itself; are out of the question! Especially for such a large company! Every time a Stuff or Expansion pac has come out, there is always a game breaking glitch that does one or more of these things.   Late Night is a great example. Mysteriously players suddenly had a horrible time trying to please or even sometimes control their Sims. Sims would be unhappy no matter what the players did. They would stay bright red day and night, become incresibly ticked off at all times, divorce one another without the player being able to stop it, kids would be taken away. No one knew what was happening or how to stop it. As it turns out, it was the beds. For some unknown reason beds weren't working correctly in games. They would cause the characters to become more and more upset until someone finally made a mod fixing this glitch. The game breaking glitch was fixed well *after* Christmas, months after the game came out and the glitch was made aware by players on the forums of Sims 3. Simply because the game wasn't tested enough nor were the customers heard.   Game breaking glitches and huge annoyances adding to lag and unplayable situations are still in the game today even though Sims 4 has been announced and you can even pre-order it though they only have a handful of screenshots and a video that they have also stated will change.   They make it so obvious that everything is only about money that they forgot the simple truth. This is a game for kids and adults alike. Great game, even better with friends.  The first review I wrote was terrible (see above), so I wanted to write something a little more substantial.   L4D2 is a wonderful game with friends. It's a classic as far as zombie killing goes in this modern day and age.  The whole point is to get from point a to point b, while keeping everyone alive. At face value, it sound simple, but it's really not. Each chapter has five levels, and they go from being short to longer by the end. The easiest way to stay alive is move quickly, stay alert, and stay together; I can't stress that last one enough.   When racing through levels, you have to worry about not only normal infected (think generic zombies), but also special infected. Each special infected can do a certain thing, and they all have specific noises to alert you one is nearby. Hunters let out a shriek, smokers cough a lot, boomers gurgle a bunch, etc. It's all really well designed.  The level design is great. They are designed in such a way that most of the time, you won't need to look hard to figure out where to go (there are a few exceptions). The levels are the same every time, but they don't play the same every time. Every run has ammo, health, and grenades in different locations. You might find a laser sight one time going through a level, but not the next.   The characters you can choose basically don't matter, it's just a matter of personal preference. This fits in well with the design of the game, because the only thing they want influencing the current round is what is happening in the current round.   There are mutators you can play with. At one point, I believe they cycled by week, but I haven't really checked in a while. It'd be things like everyone has an M60 (machine gun), or every starts with a katana. Those are two really basic ones, but there were a lot for fun variety.   The difficulty settings add a good experience to the game. I played a lot on normal, but then it got too easy for me, so I started playing on Expert with Realism. Expert made it so normal zombies hit real hard, and realism made it so you wouldn't see highlights of allies, people wouldn't come back to life in closets, and a few minor other changes. It makes it much more difficult, but more enjoyable, because you have to communicate with your teammates.   The Versus mode is a pretty awesome mode. It's a team of 4 versus another team of 4. When you are the humans, you try to get as far as you can in a level. When you are the infected, you try to stop them from getting far in the level. Whichever has the most points by the end wins. Pretty simple, but really enjoyable.   This game has really solid mod support. When my friend and I finally got bored with all the content in the game, we went and found mod maps. There are some really quality maps out there, but I'd still recommend you spend a good 20-40 hours playing the vanilla stuff that comes with the game, because it's that good.   For $20, this is a very solid game. It's a steal for that price. If you find it for less, it's definitely worth the price. I'd highly recommend the game because it's quite amazing

This game is beyond reproach. Everything from the opening minutes of the cutscenes to the final minutes of the soundtrack are perfectly polished. Every punch and kick thrown in combat is choreographed flawlessly and the sounds and sights of Arkham City create an impressive world for Batman to survey and protect from itself. The story takes so many twists and turns and even if you think you'll know who the next villain is going to be, you're going to have no idea what you're getting yourself into. Batman's got to use every tool at his disposal not just to survive, but to learn the secrets of Arkham City and stop the dangerous path that awaits Gotham's mega-prison on this fateful night.  You can look at other reviews, they'll describe technically better what you can expect out of this game. But if you really think you're up for the challenge of being Batman during one of his darkest nights, fighting one of his most climactic fights, pick up this game now. It's time to don the cowl, for better or worse.

app_name: Hegemony Gold: Wars of Ancient Greece
review_text: This game is SICK! At ten dollars, this is the steal of the summer sale thus far. Combining elements of Grand Ages: Rome and Rome: Total War with some realistic tactical micro-management that really makes you plan ahead and not get too sloppy, Hegemony Gold is a game that will make anyone who always gauges the quality of a solid classical Empire-building Sim between the City micro-management and the combat system set this game as the new benchmark for utility.  It's just deep enough to be historically relevant and to make you think a bit before you act, but not so deep that you get lost in the details or want to bash the keyboard in frustration as there is just too much to be handled at once.  Hegemony Gold gives you just enough combat challenge to keep you on your toes while keeping a steady flow of the cycle of conquer, expand, and maintain element that keeps things realistic and challenging.This game is both challenging and fun, a real bargain and true gem.

After spending 16 hours of playing this game, I finished it with 83%. And one thing has to be said. This is the most actionpacked, emotionfilled and amazing Tomb Raider Part, I have played so far (and I played them ALL! From the beginning to the end.)   Still there were some points I really missed in this part. The puzzle in the tombs were a little bit too easy for me. Most of the solutions could be seen in an instant, since the survival instinct showed the important parts. This made the game a little less tricky to finish. That sucked a little, since tombs are meant to be filled with dangerous traps, that are thought through. Seeing everything highlighted, made this game like a childsplay. At some points of the game I got bored, because the game just felt like Assassins Creed and Assassins Creed is boring! (except the ship fights :) ).   Still the graphics, the controls and the sound were awesome in this game. The action parts were amazing. You fly through the air, gunshots passing by, reaching for a little corner in the rocks and missing it a little just to get on a rope, where you slide down into a group of enemies. Stuff like that happens a lot :D  I really did miss the amazing boss fights from the first Tomb Raider, also I would like to see a little more tricky parts, where you really have to think it through, before making a step. Like fake buttons, that activate traps, wrong ways to go (mazes) and stuff, that is typical for a tomb.   Still this game rocks! It runs fluently, had no crash at all. Less Sam Fischer parts and more puzzles would make this game more Tomb Raider like and perfect.   The DLCs for the are not really necessary. There is one DLC, that should be checked out 'Tomb of the Lost Adventurer', but the others are just Boost Ups, skins and guns, that are not really needed.    So, if you like action packed games, than buy this awesome piece of software.

app_name: The Lord of the Rings Online™
review_text: Free to play done right (and no it's not pay to win either :D ). Don't listen to folks who have one or two hours logged, you really can get a lot out of this game if you can put in some time and a little money. After all we all put time into games and some are bloody expensive!  LoTRO is the least frustrating free to play experience I've had as Turbine gives you a taste of everything. They don't lock out 99% of the game. Completing 'deeds' (challenges like kill X amount of goblins) will earn you Turbine points for micro transactions in the LoTRO store. With enough time and patience its possible to buy several store items from in game rewards.  If you spend even the minimum on Turbine points (£5) you get upgraded to premium account status. That gives you two more character slots per server and removes the gold cap.  However if you're aiming not to spend anything at all you can't have a job or life and do that. That is some serious grinding. You'll also HAVE to spend time grinding the Turbine points if you don't even want to spend a fiver to remove the gold cap, as two gold is not nearly enough in game currency later on. Good thing is that before you remove the cap all money you earn gets saved in an overflow wallet ready for when you do gain access to it.  WHAT FREE TO PLAYER'S GET  The epic quests that make up the main story line are mostly free (to continue it you need to buy the expansions).  Player housing is free. Some items used to decorate can be bought in the store but most you get for free.  Some trait slots are free. These are different to trait trees (the skill trees you spec into to get your abilities). You gain most of these by deeds and they can be slotted to give either passive buffs or more skills.  Steeds. Getting your horse/pony/goat (can only ride goats in Moria) is a free option but the riding skill itself is not free. Don't throw your arms up in protest yet. With small effort you can get the 95 Turbine points you need to purchase this skill. Most deeds that award Turbine points (and most of them do) are worth between 5-10 points. Some deeds are worth more. It's easy to get (VIP's get a free quest that grants the skill). Some steeds are available in the store. With the Rohan expansion you can ride a war steed for mounted combat and customize it's appearance but that can get bloody expensive.  The Shire, Bree, Ered Luin, Lone Lands and Celondim areas are free. Free, Premium and VIP players all have to buy the expansions, there are five in total. There are quest packs for other regions you can buy in game from about 595 Turbine points. You can travel to the areas you don't have the quest packs for, you just can't do quests there. It's important to note that free content (excluding skirmishes) drys up around level 35-38. Moria, the first expansion is best entered around level 50 so you'll be needing a quest pack to fill in the gap.  Some skirmishes and repeatable instances are free.  Joining a crafting guild is free but you need to pay for full access to it. You don't need be in a guild but it gives you access to far more recipes than you'd have otherwise. Having full access is a distinct advantage as there is far more choice.  All but the Warden (one of the tanks) and Rune-Keeper (DPS and healing, not the only DPS or healing class in game) classes are free. The Rune-Keeper is well worth it, lots of ranged DPS magic fun. The Warden is more of a secondary tank with combos (called gambits) and other tricks thrown in.  The fishing hobby (the only hobby) is free. You can catch fish to display in your home or to cook if you or any of your alts have the right crafting profession.  The in game mailing system is free and is very useful to post items to your alts, not just other players.  Creating a guild (known as a kinship here) is free (well it would be stupid otherwise lets face it).  Free daily silver hobbit present.  WHAT VIP's GET  If you go the subscription route you become a VIP. VIP's pay the following:  $15 a month $29.97 for 3 months $59.94 for 6 months $99.99 for 12 months  For your chosen cost/sub length you get:  Mailbox field access  Access all character trait slots 500 Turbine points per month Access all quest packs and skirmishes that are not a part of expansions Unlimited chat 20 Slots of shared wardrobe space (this enables you to cosmetically equip gear on any character) Additional inventory slots Custom character portrait frame Daily +100% XP (for 30% of a level) Free weekly gold hobbit present Access all monster classes (Monster play is mostly PvP) Access all crafting guilds No currency cap Maximum auction house listings  The most major benefits are the quest packs, skirmishes, 500 free Turbine points, crafting guilds and monster classes. That gives you a ton of extra content.  PLAYING THE GAME  Choose race of men, hobbit, dwarf or elves, then pick your class. The usual DPS, tank and healer classes are present, along with the squishy pet class (Lore Master, that does more than just use pets). Next is the choice of where your character comes from. Choosing this region doesn't determine more than a preset skin tone and hair colour (which you can change). Choose your hair style, facial features etc, pick a suitable name and away you go. The level cap for characters is 100.  The tutorial is easy and makes it clear how to use your abilities. A nice touch is the tutorial skip option available at character creation if you've already done it before.  The usual MMO type features are here. Choose a crafting profession to make useful items along the way, level up and upgrade your weapons and armour, collect type quests, kill type quests, quests in instances as well as those in the open world. Most are set in the open world. There are also repeatable daily quests in certain areas. The interface makes it easy to see who is in your area if you want to start a fellowship (team). There are different instance quests called skirmishes. These can be repeated and are a bit more entertaining than the usual quests as they have a few different objectives and ambushes. You gain 'marks' for successful objectives as well as completing the skirmish itself. These can be completed solo or in a fellowship. Turn the marks in for rewards.  When you start to level up you'll be getting trait points. Each class has three trait trees that will determine specifically how your chosen class plays and what skills you gain. Some skills however are not determined by which trees or how far into them you spec as they are standard skills. The Guardian (one of two tank classes) for example has a tanking, DPS and two handed tanking tree choices. You can choose to specialize in one tree or spread yourself a bit thinner into others as well.  Being an MMO the graphics are not top notch but they are beautiful. Water effects are nice.  USEFUL INFO ON CRASHES  Many players have suffered from constant crashes. Check out post #11 in this thread;  https://www.lotro.com/forums/showthread.php?521607-Lotro-keeps-crashing  That may solve your issues. Also if you have Pando Media Booster installed get rid of it! Not only is it pointless it can slow LoTRO to the point it crashes. Both these solutions worked for me and my wife.  Yet again this is one of the vast amount of games these days that has annoying bugs. Hopefully you won't suffer them or at least be able to sort them with the above solutions. Also some of the store items are far too pricey (extra bags and anything steed related in particular).  There's a lot of nice places to explore and plenty to do once you have a stable game. There are many more features I've not covered such as raids, PvP and monster play, not to mention the legendary weapon system and your free hobbit present you get each day. Have a bit of patience and you'll love Middle Earth. :)

This was a fun point-and-click with enough challenging puzzles to keep me interested, but a bit lacklustre in other areas.   I liked the premise - a world built on a junk yard - and I likedthat many of the puzzles required you to get creative and build things out of unconventional materials. I did get stuck a few times and had to look up prompts, which I hate doing, but it helped me think about future puzzles differently so I didn't need help towards the end.   While the voice acting was decent enough, I found the characters to be quite generic, and I did not care for Rufus's weird creepy possessiveness of Goal as some kind of prize (even her name alludes to this).   My main annoyance was that this game has way too much unnecessary dialogue; I put the subtitles on so I could quickly skip through the lines unless they had some useful information. The dialogue was often trying to be funny but much of it was fluff. I like this kind of Monkey Island style game with quirky humour, and there was some of that, but a lot of it could have been cut.   The ending was a bit abrupt. I didn't really care for the storyline towards the end; it felt rushed and the puzzles became fewer and further between to make way for overindulgent cut scenes. Overall I enjoyed it, but I'm glad I got it at the sale price.

This game...  For years, I'd been waiting--just as many others had been--for a worthy successor to the original few games in the Aliens/Predator franchise. After being so disappointed with Colonial Marines, Alien: Isolation hit me out of the park.  Great visuals, astonishing sound effects and mastering, and a great storyline. The main character, whom the player controls, is Amanda Ripley, daughter of our androgenous queen, Ellen Ripley. There a ton of callbacks to the first Alien movie, complete with newly recorded stuff from the actors which, to be honest, I was only really interested in Sigourney Weaver's newly recorded lines, which were great.  Haven't tried any of the DLC yet, but the vanilla game was well worth full price. It's a different take on the FPS, shying away from combat and more relying on sneaking tactics, distraction, and sometimes straight up running (which works very very rarely).  Old Alien/s fans out there will be pleased with this game. I loved it and it had my heart racing many times.

review_text: Brothers is a great example for gameplay syncing with storytelling. Path of Exile is a Free to Play game. Most often people cringe when they hear this, thinking there's a Pay To Win element - that's not the case here. 99% of the things you can purchase are cosmetic - the only things not that I can think of is extra storage space, which is hardly gamebreaking.  The game was made in the same vein as Diablo, Baldur's Gate, Neverwinter Nights - lots of clicking and making monsters explode. There is an astonishingly large Skill Tree to apply to your character with no restrictions except how many points you can get as you level. You can have a big barbarian spellcaster, or a 'witch' who can lay a major beatdown with a big club.  The game is designed to make it fairly easy to re-roll your character and try new builds because there's no easy way to remove more than a couple mistakenly placed upon the skill tree.   The skills for the game are based in Gems which are socketed into your gear, meaning as long as you have the right color socket you can put any skill you're high enough to use into it. There are even gems that modify what the skills linked to them do, making it even further customizable.  To put it simply: This is an awesome game. You should give it at least a few hours to try it out. I doubt you'll regret it.

app_name: Detective Case and Clown Bot in: Murder in the Hotel Lisbon
review_text: Relatively short, silly point-and-click adventure game. Graphics are decent (pixel-cartoony), story is okay, but it's really the banter and humor that carries the game. You talk to people, collect items, then do a fun but simple 'interrogation' where you ask the right question along with the right item (and then repeat). It's not hard to get through the game because there are no puzzles (you just need to collect items for interrogations and speak to the right people to progress). Separate from the main mode, there's also a minigame mode where you complete lots of puns (timed multiple choice), if that's your type of thing.  Most of the game is light-hearted and relatively child-friendly but later on there are some words and name-calling that you might not want children to learn, and some sexual implications you might not want to explain if they're not old enough.  If you like quick and simple adventure games with humor, this might give you a couple hours of entertainment.

app_name: STAR WARS™: Knights of the Old Republic™
review_text: Must play. Definitely a classic for all ages!

I really wish there was a 'neutral' option on Steam reviews for this one.  The melee combat and movement is amazing and really fun. However, the guns feel pretty useless compared to melee, damage is lacking, the ammo is sparse, ammo efficiency is pretty low, etc. and the ki powers are all geared towards close combat, so there's pretty much no reason to use them other than sniping and/or kiting the more resilient and powerful mobs until you find your melee flow.  The Borderlands elements are done pretty poorly, the elemental effects have no purpose, other than the occassional 'x element immune' which sometimes can bring back those Diablo-esque rage-inducing frustrating moments of unfairness. The randomization does not work, because the maps themselves never change, only the location of mobs and loot can, but those are pointless. The loot itself is also pretty pointless, even on the highest difficulty, the game is easily beatable without using any of the gem slots.  The humor is very 80's toilet jokes heavy, which I personally found pretty great. However, there's far too little real banter in the game, because all in all, there's not much story or cutscene content there. The game's pretty short and it's entirely possible to finish in a couple hours if you don't bother with any exploration (which is pretty dull and pointless) or fiddling around.  Haven't tried any multiplayer, but I can't imagine it would make the game any better, or worse for that matter.  Overall, the game's design feels rushed and not thought through at all, however, just for the melee combat and the few truly funny cutscenes and banter lines, it's still worth it on a sale.

Very satisfying and fairly simple class-based FPS game, taking inspiration from games like Call of Duty and TF2. The upgrades system is based upon easily accumulated credits earned from ingame missions, so while you can buy classes and loadouts with real money, it's all very much achievable ingame. Dirty Bomb isn't particularly groundbreaking, but it's a solid and enjoyable experience in my opinion. It sticks to what it knows best, and keeps the focus on the core aspects of the game instead of gimmicky add-ons to try and differentiate it from the pack. Addictive, fun and solid. Would very much reccomend.

So where do I start? There is so much good to say about this game.  Let's start with the realism: Cars handle very nicely and realisticly. The handling kinda reminds me off iRacing, which in my mind is a good thing as iRacing's physics is 10/10. I would actually say that rFactor 2 has slightly better physics, no doubt. They pushed it to 11/10, and how? Well one example that blew my mind happened in a 3 hour race I did in a GT2 Corvette. I was battling with the AI (we will look at the AI later) and in the exciting battle I got a small hit, went sideways and stood on the brakes while the cars was going sideways on asphalt or concrete. When I continued everything was fine until I reached higher speed or high speed corners and experienced lots of vibrations in my FFB wheel. Why? Because the game simulates flat spots really well. You might say that Assetto Corsa also simulates this, but not as well as rFactor 2.  Then we have the AI. Let me first talk quickly about Assetto Corsa. Assetto Corsa is, at least for me, a game where you do hotlapping, often around the Nurburgring. It is a very fun game, but the level of simulation, the way the cars feel, its not really a racing game, more like a hotlapping game where you drive different cars around different tracks either online of offline for fun. The AI isn't that enjoyable to race with in Assetto Corsa, but when it comes to rFactor 2 they AI is just amazing! If you are close to them they will, not always but often, move to the inside line and give up the ideal racing line to defend their position. They will also dive bomb you if you are going slow into a corner. The AI can also do things wrong, just like a normal person, like get one wheel on the grass and spinout. The AI is also extremely fun to battle with in rFactor 2. I did a 3 hour race in GT2 Corvettes mixed with LMP2 cars. I was in a Corvette. The faster LMP2 AI cars would respect you and find a safe way past you, but they could also cut somewhat close in front of you once they pass you, it just feels so realistic.  There are lots of leagues for this game and race series for the fast and good sim racers out there. Like VEC, Virtual Endurance Championship. It really is a game for racing unlike Assetto Corsa, and obviously you have day night cycles, rain, etc. Not that many cars or tracks included, some cars and tracks are also fictional, but as this is a game for league racing you will find LOTS of HIGH QUALITY cars and tracks. I downloaded a Sebring 12hour track with a league configuration. It is just so good, with the bumps, etc. This is a MUST have game if you're into sim racing. It is kinda like iRacing but with singleplayer and extremely good AI. Obviously you have online racing as there is league racing, but this costs money. You can buy lifetime online service or a subscription.  Solid 11/10 sim racing game.

Let me just start off by saying, I've been following this game since early development and couldn't wait to play it after playing it and completing in in 4 hours I have some things to say.  Firewatch is a beuatiful game, it looks amazing and it's atmosphere is unique and you really appreciate how much thought went into the design of everything. There isn't much music in the game but when it is there it sets the mood really well and it fits perfectly with what's happening.   The voice acting is great, for a game that's story driven the voice acting had to deliver and it did, you really feel for the main character and their conversations aren't as cheesy as a lot of other games.  I really enjoyed my playthrough of Firewatch however it does have it down sides.  I do think it costs a bit too much, $24 Australian for me and it equals to 4 hours, while those 4 hours were amazing, that's a lot of money to ask for a 4 hour game and if I hadn't have been waiting for this game for as long as I did, I wouldn't have bought it, which is where I think a lot of people would make a similar decision, I would've gladly played this game for 2 or 3 more hours and itwould've been justified for the price, if the story kept evolving, it truly would've been an amazing game. Okay, before I start... SquareCells Plus and SquareCells Infinite, please? Take my money.  So this game is another gem created by Matthew Brown. It has different elements than Hexcells, and they are more difficult to get a grasp on. But by no means does that make this a bad game. I can't put into words how much I love this game, and how much I love the Hexcells trilogy, and how much I love Matthew Brown...okay, is that too weird? Sorry.  Anyways, there are three main 'rules' to this game. 1: Squares do not connect diagonally. 2: Numbers without brackets indicate the order blocks that are part of the pattern are in. 3: Numbers with brackets indicate only the number of blocks in the row, not the order they are in. 4: Numbers on blocks indicate that that block is a part of a group of blocks the size of the number shown.   I guess that was four rules...so yeah. Seems simple, right? WRONG! These puzzles are delightfully challenging, especially the second half. The thing is, there's no guesswork involved. You just have to use pure logic to solve them. And logic is similar to common sense, of which I have none, so I'm not the greatest at the game. But I'm okay with that, I still love it to death.  I can't wait for a sequel or two of this series, hence my question at the beginning. Matthew Brown is, in my opinion, one of the best puzzle game developers of all time.

I have mixed feelings about this game. I played it on free weekend and have to say I enjoyed it a lot. Though I can forgive a lot from the game, if it has a scifi setting.  First I must tell, that I never played Alpha Centauri, so I can't tell you how bad this urinates on AC's grave. Even though I've been a huge fan of strategy games since childhood, I never really touched the Civilization games until Civ V few years ago. And then it was too late to revert back to outdated mechanics of AC.  However it is painfully obvious Firaxis intentionally left major gameplay elements (only 8 factions, none of which have alternative leaders!!) out of the game they intend to release later as an 'expansion pack', like they did with XCOM: Enemy Unknown and Civ V and IV. The difference is that those games have enough content in them so that they can function perfectly on their own without the DLC.  The starting opinions is a nice new feature. You are no longer tied to just the faction specific perks.  The victories, aside from domination, are way too similar. Build a teleporter platform, march your troops through it and you win. And the affinities are way too tightly tied to the tech tree.  Come back, when there are actual expansion packs out and the price has dropped. In the meanwhile, you could try Pandora: First Contact for your hunger for Alpha Centauri. I hear it is pretty decent.

As a huge fan of Civ 5 (If you are thinking of buying it then buy the complete edition!), I was immensly excited for this game. But on launch on my great laptop, I was on about 5 FPS from the awful performance. Now It is ok after the major patch but it it by no means smooth. The affinities are an interesting mechanic to play with, but paired with the tech web it just makes for a jumbled mess racing to get as many affinity points as you can. The aliens are a pain in the ♥♥♥ and are always there throught the game, unlike babarians. The unit upgrade system is a great idea, but really isn't as non linear as it seems. The game just seems empty and nowhere near as bright and exciting as Civ 5. However, as DLC gets released, I hope it will follow Civ 5 and turn into an outstanding game because of more time, added interesting and helpful mechanics, changes to old mechanics and MORE AWESOME FACTIONS !  5/10

As I work full time, I'm not able to have camped on this title for forty hours in the first two days, but nine hours is probably just more than enough to chip in my two cents.  The graphics -are- good, but the art direction is bad. And I think that same pattern resonates through the rest of this title. All the game's advances from Civilization V are somehow compromised by negligent design. For every step forward we've fallen back two.   The Tech Web notion wasn't a bad one - other 4X have done this to addictive success - but in BE it seems disjointed and uninteresting. I found myself wanting a tree list of techs to order them in terms of 'earliest benefit' but, alas, I had to roll around the tech map each time to find my way. For a Sci-Fi title I was hoping they'd really stretch their creative limits but everything in the tech is completely uninspired.  'Unit Customization' is another major letdown (but not a major surprise). Your units basically get morphed by whatever ideology you've espoused earlier on, and you can choose to boost their defense or offense. Each singular unit can still level, but they only get to choose between 'Heal up by 50' or 'Boost attack + defense +10%'. Every time. Been a while since 'customization' has been this narrow.  If you were looking forward to quests - yep they're kinda neat but you also have to go hunting for them in the UI and scroll your way through the jist of things. They'd be much more compelling if they weren't hamstrung by the game's lacking (but likely to improve) UI. They'd also be more compelling if they were given due emphasis, instead of being almost exclusively text blurbs, or regular choices of benefits on a new building.  The biggest letdown to me - the biggest obstacle between myself and Beyond Earth's fun - is its lack of rationality. The Health quotient of your civ (an inexplicable replacement of Happiness) is completely out of whack - nearly impossible to master, and a burden that far outweighs Civ5's 'Courthouse Problems'. Money is silly in that you can earn it easily but your various improvements (tile and city) will gobble it up in roller-coaster patterns the more you expand. Build times (unit and structure both) are frustratingly -off- as well. The worst offender of this irrationality is actually Miasma. Miasma looks vaguely like pollution but it's just a damage cloud that sits on the map tiles indefinitely. You can't ever seem to get rid of it. You can spawn an orbital vacuum cleaner to temporarily neuter it (10 turns, then the unit dies, by default).  If they fixed the Miasma, Health, and Building Times in the game - adjusted the ratios at least - I'd say this game is as good as Civ5 at launch. (In particular I had a lot more stability issues with Civ5 on launch than with this one.) The game would still be uncharacterized and a little too clunky in the UI, but it would at least be more comfortable of an experience.  I don't recommend it.
