WARPATH: There are a tremendous amount of quirks to this game that make it a pain in the neck. Right when you start to have fun the game throws you a curveball such as: if you have an army that can't attack a city because it ran out of moves but you send a supporting army to join them, that part of the army WILL AUTOMATICALLY attack the city. What the hell?   You still get all your local forces in the battle but they join piecemeal and the enemy can cut you down before reinforcements come. Nuts! Just give me the option to hold position without attacking like in every other total war game!  Other quirks: cavalry will charge anti-cavalry barricades and all die even if there is a safe path several feet away, archers start with skirmish mode off, fire at will turns off unpredictably so your ranged units will just be standing there idly right as your big, well-planned engagement starts, sometimes archers have moved without orders so that they will be facing left, right, or rearward when the big engagement starts. If you accidentally send orders to the wrong unit right before the big engagement, instead of halting when you press backspace, they will continue to turn around 180 degrees before resuming their original position, totally ruining your plans.  These wouldn't be big issues except 1) they weren't present in Medieval 2 and Rome 1 which were both not broken so why fix them? and 2) battles in Empire are decided much quicker and are much more decisive so that a chance right click which causes your archers to about-face will ruin not just your battle but your entire campaign! Totally nuts! Save a lot and be prepared not to have fun.  VANILLA GAME: The problems aren't so prominent in vanilla. I'm actually having a blast playing as Maratha so I'm going to make this edit and say, yes, I recommend the game despite skewering it above. Just be prepared to save a lot because you will lose due to strange new game mechanics and it will ruin your vibe.

One of the best games I played in 2013. Great cinematic and character development (Lara).  Graphics: Textures and lighting are absolutely gorgeous. Lara's animations during action moves (jumping, climbing, falling, fighting, etc.) are outstanding. The only problem I had in the graphics department was running TressFX on my Nvidia Geforce 560M -- Lara's hair effects absolutely crippled my PC performance.  Gameplay: Mix of exploration, survival, and combat. The game offers plenty of collectibles for achievement hunters. Combat relies on stealth tactics (similar to the Uncharted series on Playstation 3) and resources management. Throughout the game, the player collects various tools that Lara can use to open new doors, climb new walls, etc. -- it's a great mechanic that makes the exploration component of the gameplay feel fresh every now and then.  Sound: Great soundtrack that merges well with the survival theme of the game. Sound effects are excellent, specially in dynamic areas (e.g. some parts of the game feature buildings falling apart) -- you will want to play this with a surround sound headset.  Play this game if you like: > Games that mix gameplay and cinematic (e.g. quick time events) > Survival-themed story > Stealth combat  Don't play this game if you: > Hate having limited resources. You will have to be looking for ammo all the time.

An incredibly simple game, unfortunately to its detriment. What Pineapple Smash Crew does boast, is a great retro shoot 'em up feel, with appropriately themed graphics and music, which are both fantastic. The use of a four person team and the unique grenade abilities is pretty nifty, as are the randomly generated levels, but that's really all there is to say about the gameplay.  It's fun for brief plays every once in a while, but the total lack of variety or depth leave it feeling half-finished. If the aesthetic really appeals to you as it did for myself, pick it up on the cheap (even $10 is asking a bit much for what you get), but otherwise I regret having to recommend a pass on this one.

If you DO use them though they're pretty fun to watch (I once accidentally turned a group of monsters into chickens. NICE). Since every stage allows you to move up/down you can miss enemies that appear to be right in front of you, which is annoying but thankfully not as often as you'd think. Overall fun for what it is. The voice acting is excellent, the writing is good enough to carry the barely existent plot and the graphics look great.

How to Have Fun In This Game  It's not about winning. It's about your team. If you want to be a captain, but you're afraid you won't be any good, talk to your team. Let them know you're anxious or new. The community in this game never ceases to amaze me; just be serious about it, and others will respond. I'm not the greatest captain ever, yet somehow I managed to retain three Russian speaking teammates despite the inability to speak to each other, except for them to be able to say 'You are terrible captain', to which we all laughed hysterically. The only English they knew and it was enough to tell me I sucked.  Have fun. That's what it is. There will be matches you just always win; there will be matches you lose historically. You may level up, you may not; some people grind for levels, some people- like me- forget achievements exist and stay level 7 for two years.   Also, this is a FANTASTIC game to livestream. Almost ALL of my best livestreams are because of this game. When you can announce to a match that you're livestreaming and the worst thing that happens is one guy squealing 'I'M FAMOUS' and everyone else screaming in high pitched voices, you know it's a good community. And community is EVERYTHING with games like these.

To describe my excursion with Proteus and attempt to use it as a cause for you to play or avoid it would be a rather disingenuous waste of time. This is a title that exists outside the spectrum of a traditional critique, and is not something that is easy to recommend or dismiss as the experience will differ vastly for different players.  Like a piece of abstract art there will be those that fall in love with it and are able to recall in vivid detail just what they found so amazing, and an equal or greater amount that shrug it off as pretentious and insubstantial. Regardless of which side you fall on, Proteus is almost certain to draw a strong emotional response, from infatuation to downright anger.  Dropped onto an otherworldly island, your only objective is to take in your surroundings through four seasons. The primitive graphics and bizarre soundscapes are in no way ideal for first impressions, but ultimately are integral pieces of the game that wouldn't be at all the same without them, that if you allow them can be surprisingly engrossing. The developers clearly had a vision how Proteus looks and sounds, and there's no doubt in my mind they managed to achieve it.  If I was to level a complaint at Proteus, it's that in the end it wound up feeling like something that belonged in a gallery, not in my steam library. I want others to experience what I did, but also expect most would feel cheated to have paid what it currently costs, or even anything at all. Thus is the case with most things that aspire to be considered art, which for better and for worse, is the only way to describe Proteus, a love it or hate it affair if ever I saw one. I'm sure this hasn't helped in the slightest for you to decide if this is worth your time or money, but no matter how many words I type I couldn't tell you. If you have access to a copy then I wouldn't hesitate to tell you to play it, but otherwise I'm afraid it is a risk you have to take for yourself.

Fantastic fun! Saw this game on sale, which prompted me to watch the show, which hooked me and got me to buy the game for my girlfriend and I, and we have been playing non stop for the week since we got it.  First, the separate stories for the show and the game are interesting on their own. Then you add episodic missions with some of the same decent characters from the show and events that explain other events, such as where Nolan gets a particular artifact, and in one mission you technically save the city of Defiance. The game also has a nice long story line mostly independent of the show, fascinating in its own right. The game and show stand on their own no question, but if you follow both they cross over in fantastic ways and further illuminate the world.  Controls and combat are solid, what you would expect from a typical 3rd person action shooter, notably with unlimited dodge rolls but without a cover mechanic. The progression is also very nice, with a wide variety of weapons with not much difference between them, instead progressing by skill and based on how you can mod the weapons and how the weapons and mods synergize. There are also 4 core abilities and a plethora of 'perks' to unlock, upgrade, and slot. You choose 1 scifi ability and 3+ perks, unlocking slots over time. One of the best features is you can set up different loadouts, with weapon, ability, perk, and even outfit and vehicle choices set per loadout. It is fairly quick to switch loadouts, and you can in combat (pve), though there is no hotkey to switch yet.  The game is an mmo and has the necessary elements, clans, chat, friends, etc, but is light on other elements. You can only have a single mission at a time, though they are more engrossing than typical mmo quests by far; it will not cross your mind a second time. The chat is minimalist, but they have announced an overhaul. It is, in a way, a barebones mmo, but a very excellent coop 3rd person action shooter. Yes, there are also some bugs, but none of them terrible now, they are almost always easily ignored.

I'll skip the whole novel-stating-why-I-♥♥♥♥ing-love-Tomb-Raider, and instead give you just a brief rundown of what you should expect from Tomb Raider I.  Backstory: Tomb Raider was a PS1 launch title that BLEW UP because of how amazing it was- no joke. Lara Croft became infamous for her 'triangle ♥♥♥♥♥♥s' because her breasts were mere triangles instead of the round jiggly thingies we have today. Tomb Raider is an ongoing franchise that features Lara Croft as the protagonist who either struggles to survive, or go on a massive search for some artifact.   Storyline: You are Lara Croft- a world known archeologist on a search for something called 'The Scion' which a mysterious person named 'Natla' has informed you about. Lara Croft is a bad-♥♥♥, dual pistol wielding girl who lives for adventure.  Gameplay: Okay, now you have to take into account that this game was made in 1996- a PS1 launch title to be fair, so the gameplay functionality is going to be on-par with these next generation titles. Tomb Raider is a 3D platform-adventure game with gun mechanics that actually work. Tomb Raider also integrates puzzles into this platforming-adventure experience, so not only do you have a compelling story, but also a great genre scheme.   Sound: Since this is an outdated game, the sound effects can come out quite poor at some times due to the lower files not being supported by newer tech. If you have high teir headphones, you will know what i'm talking about.   Graphics: I keep going back to the topic of it being from the 90s, but it's true and it's age really puts a show on it's graphics, sounds, and just gameplay in general. The textures can be a bit bad and look unrendered to it's full potential at times, but it still works and looks... ok.. for it's time. Lots of elements found on the ground are sprites including the pickups like health packs, ammo packs, and artifacts. Overall, it's not TOO BAD, but it's nowhere as good as the sequals.   Conclusion: If you haven't found out already- Tomb Raider is AMAZING. If you have not played it, what is wrong with you? Stop reading this, scroll up, and click 'Add to Cart'.

Chip is an isometric puzzle game where you are guiding an energy ball from its source to destination through the use of mirrors, portals, magnets, and various other contraptions. Mechanically simple, there is a surprising amount of depth and variety to be found within the puzzles.  Every level has an additional goal number of moves/time to meet (not necessarily at the same time). Oftentimes these are relatively straightforward, but in some cases they involve meticulous timing in the movement of pieces, which can vary from very satisfying to quite frustrating depending on your preferences.  I was able to beat every level (although not every time/move challenge) within roughly 6 hours, so consider that a reasonable ballpark for amount of gameplay available. More level packs are supposed to be incoming, so that will likely grow.  The graphics are relatively simple (it feels a bit like a phone game), and there is character customization items you can purchase, but they're all superficial and have no impact on gameplay.  Ultimately, Chip is a nice little puzzle game. A little rough around the edges, but there's some good fun and challenge to be found here. In future updates, it could really benefit from the addition of Steam Workshop support to allow for user-submitted levels.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: I have 800+ hours in Peace Walker, game I love and hate deeply and shares a lot of similiarities.  The game is good and all, it's a shame the rating system is still based pretty much only on time (as carried from Peace Walker). I got S rank in basically every single mission by just skipping everything. Not doing stuff is once again the best way to stack up heroism. By the end I had 270k heroism.  One thing that's cool is how side ops are not rated so you feel you can actually enjoy yourself without being punished... but then again those are not that good either... (they are not repeateable though)  I loved this game, having about 140h on it in total... I still feel and know it could've been much better.  The AI is stupid easy with tools such as reflex too you can basically run around everywhere (with infiltration suit at least, which is as usual broken) unnoticed...   This review is clearly for people who like studying AIs in games and being 'the best' ... which is probably not many people. The game is good, just don't expect it to be like other metal gears.

While I commend the developers for flexibility with construction in this game, it's as if it was never play-tested. It's impossible to use. There are no 'standard' game features/controls I would expect. Such as ESCAPE to bring up the menu and/or quit. I used the 'Windows Key' to bring up the desktop then force quit with task manager. The menu for the game is completely non-functional while in-game, clicking settings highlights the button and does nothing. The menus are constantly in the way and disable the mouse scroll so if I move the mouse over a menu while trying to scroll the board, it doesn't move the map, I have to move the mouse around and in-between the menus to scroll - super frustrating. The tutorials do little to help you get started and are constantly in the way. The placement of all the menus is terrible and they cannot be moved around. When I finally did get something working, I somehow managed to use up all $3M with a train depot and a single straight piece of track, left unable to purchase cars or figure out how to get citizens to use the newly built lines. The load this game puts on my computer is extreme and causes lag and this is a gaming PC with dual graphics cards, SSDs, fully loaded. I'm extremely frustrated and probably won't play this game again. Good thing I got it on sale.  ---- NEW BELOW ----  After quite a lot of tinkering around, I've finally got 3 trains up and running, and lines for them to follow. I determined the menus don't work on a multi-monitor desktop PC, when I loaded this game on my laptop, the menus showed up on-screen. I've also figured out a work around for the scrolling problem, don't put the menus up against the edge of the screen and your arrow has room for scroll - big oversight on the developers if you ask me. I'm still not very pleased with the interface, it's incredibly clunky, and I'm not getting used to it. As for game play, I did manage to figure out the loan program to get enough funds to complete rail between 2 cities, and add some bus stops within the cities to get people to my stations, then paid off my loans so I can just build now in the black.  Due to the enormous amount of problems and glitches with this game, I still have to rate it a thumbs down. Here's hoping for significant updates at some point.

This is not a strategy game for casuals nor the faint of heart. If you by some crazy notion think the Europa series, Crusader Kings, or Hearts of Iron is complex you have not played an AgeOD game yet!   This feels like the spiritual sequel to WWI: Gold, and it has definitely improved on previous games quite a bit. In all honesty, it's a blend of WWI: Gold and ACW, which means it's flat out awesome. You can start off in 3 different scenarios: the East Prussian Campaign (which is essentially the battles of Tannenburg and Marsuian Lakes), the historical WWI start in August of 1914, or an earlier one which give you more options for managing your nations. You get to choose from 3 sets of powers: the Western Entene powers, Central Powers, or the Eastern Entente powers. Neutral nations can be persuaded to join either side, and some can have a serious effect on the outcome of the war.  The start of the campaign is where the complexity and steep learning curve kicks in immediately. This game goes right down to the minutia. You will need to take your time and view the many overlays in game before even thinking of any actions. Individual regions have a certain amount of space for troop deployment, known as 'frontage'. Worse terrain like forests, mountains and marshes severely limit this. Also, what's the weather like in said region? Is it raining? Foggy? Bad weather can be a blessing (providing cover for an advance) or a curse (bogging down tanks in the later years).   Supplies & unit cohesion are absolutely crucial too. At the start you'll need to inspect your nation, find your supply depots, where your supplies are produced, and figure out where to build infrastrucure to funnel them efficiently to the front. Logistics and cutting enemy supply lines can literally win the war as an army without supplies can starve to death if cut off. There's also hundreds of other factors all taking effect as well. Each unit has different stats & abilites, as do your generals.   The biggest improvement by far is the addition of battle plans. You can finally decide what your troops do in individual battles, so the war isn't just won solely on the campaign map anymore. Sometimes a human wave assault might be just what the marshal ordered. Or maybe a double envelopment or crossfire is needed. You really feel like a general trying to guess what the enemy is doing and the best course of action to take. The game also uses the Army & Corps system from ACW, which functions beautifully for a WWI setting. Having your corps' properly place in a line of trenches is awesome to witness, as they will intelligently 'march to the sound of the guns' and reinforce other units of the same army that are under attack.  The biggest flaw in my opinion, is that the 'off-map boxes' from WWI Gold are still in. It's still confusing trying to figure out how some of them intersect, and I can't quite fathom why something better hasn't been put in place yet. I understand AgeOD doesn't want to go into the excruciating detail they went into with Europe, but something like the Africa box would have been just fine for other sectors too.  In conclusion: if you're ready to break out the manual, maybe even some pen and paper, and dive into a seriously complex WWI game that encompasses the whole globe: To End All Wars is for you! Once you start getting a handle of the game, you'll get a real sense of accomplishment and only get hooked that much more!

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II
review_text: The game fast forwards you a few years into the future from the previous game (Dark Forces), and it now takes place after the events of Episode VI: The Return of the Jedi. The game puts you right back into the shoes of Kyle Katarn, but this time, with a twist. After the death of the Emperor, a new threat is trying to take throne. A Dark Jedi named Jerec is not only trying to become the Emperor, but he has also killed your father, and because of this, you are out for revenge. After receiving your first lightsaber, you get a choice: Do you follow the path of the Light Side of the force, like your father always wanted you to, or do you subcumb to the Dark Side?    (You also get a different ending depending on which side you take) The game also features some small RPG elements with features like Skill-trees.  This is where some problems arise however.....  The game has many flaws: A lot of resolution issues, frequent crashes, corrupted save games and much more make for a very buggy experience. Yet, the game still does feel very rewarding at times, and if you (frequently) turn a blind eye to these issues, the game can be very enjoyable. The game has some very memorable characters and the live-action cutscenes do give these characters some more.... character! Nonetheless, this to me does not completely make up for the fact that the game runs very poorly on a Windows 10 PC. Besides this however, I do think that you should give this game a try, especially if you have already played Dark Forces.  All in all, STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II is a very memorable game, allbeit in some good and bad way, Nonetheless, this game is a classic and any fan of the Jedi Knight series should definitely pick this game up.

Starts off slow but... it's kinda good. Took a while to grown on me.  It's a slower paced platformer compared to other 3D Sonic games. It focuses more on controllability over a purely wack sense of speed, which I liked.  The levels are colorful and well designed, and there's great variety in both gameplay and graphics.  I'd say that the game's pretty tight once you master its mechanics, but you can play for a while without knowing about some of them (it actually took me a while to notice that there was a run button and that I could kick enemies or charge Sonic's homing attack). It was one of the very few cases in recent memory where I'd have enjoyed a simple, quick tutorial.  Sometimes the physics feel sluggish and annoying on 2D mode, so be warned.  Kind of a 7/10 game overall that I highly recommend if you're a sucker for Sonic.

In a nutshell, this game is 75% enjoyment, 25% frustration. The combat is incredibly fast, fluid, addictive, and frankly, makes this game. Unfortunately there are a few flaws that mar the experience.  - Traveling downward in a level leaves you completely blind. With split-second decisions needing to be made in combat, you're left at a disadvantage.  - Suffers from visual overload. This game is overtly visual, with little attention paid to the audio apart from gunfire, explosions and generic demon grunts. The game expects you to see literally everything coming at you in order to react to it, but the overly vibrant light effects are distracting during combat. Every pickup in the game has a bright glow effect, in addition to all enemy projectiles, lights in the background and some of your abilities. When you're killing multiple enemies at once which spawn pickups, dodging projectiles and using Miracles, there are frequent 'I can't even see wtf is going on' moments, which usually results in taking damage or death.  - Problematic RNG. The game is marketed as a skill-based shooter, and this is true, but it has many Roguelike elements, such as procedually generated levels, randomized weapon and armor drops, and elite enemies that take a page from Diablo III. All of these combined occasionally result in simply unfair situations. You'll be stuck in a narrow corridor with elite enemies who can spawn giant area-denial circles that heavily damage and slow you, launch homing orbs that track you for days, reflect your bullets, or who are simply fast and launch shotgun-style energy bursts at you with no warning. This issue also compounds with the aforementioned visual overload, and is especially noticeable at 6.0 and higher difficulties.  - Excessively grindy progression elements. Crafting in this game is simple if a bit bland, and too focused on long-term instead of short-term impact. You collect components from enemies you kill, and can unlock/upgrade your weapons, Wards and Miracles after each level, or take Oaths, aka your talent tree. Problem is everything is too incremental. You found a shard? You get 10 more HP, or 2% more Pistol damage, or 3% Blink recharge time, or 1% chance enemies will drop Health. You found crafting materials? You can upgrade 1 of 7 Wards to get 5% more resistance on that one item, which competes with all the other Wards that could randomly spawn in a level.  And here's the real catch. You can spend a dozen hours or more and upgrade everything you have, only to spend a number of extremely rare shards to undergo a Rebirth, if you so choose. This gives you a permanent 3% HP and Damage, and a small increase in shard drop rate. But you lose EVERYTHING you upgraded. You start completely from scratch. All weapons, miracles, Oaths and Wards are locked again. And you can Rebirth up to 4 more times, each requiring more high-level shards. Rebirth is optional, but it feels a bit... Sisyphean.  - The game's identity as skill-based conflicts with its RPG progression. This means that you can only progress so far in the game before you start hitting a brick wall in difficulty, die an abrupt death, and are forced to start over. It's where you start emptying 150 rounds of your SMG into one elite enemy and he loses maybe 30% of his HP. Or you start noticing where the most basic enemy in the game is hitting you for half your health. It's where the difficulty becomes more about enemy stat inflation than your lack of skill. To be fair, this only happens at very high difficulties (8.0+), but it's annoying when you feel your mastery of the game is enough to go further, but the game says, 'nope, you don't have Mk III weapons yet, time to die and grind some more.'  - Heavy screen tearing on a standard monitor with 60Hz refresh rate. Developer is responsive and has been timely with patches, so hoping for a fix.  Now, after all that, why do I keep playing this game? The combat is just that good. It has its problems, but what it does right is well worth experiencing.

Overall, the game deserves an 7/10. The game seems to have very little to tie in to the sick and twisted city of Rapture we've all come to know and love. The only sort of tie-in has to be bought for $30, forcing any player to have to pay at least $35 to get the full experience (not including the other dlc only including the Burial at Sea episodes). Anyways, the tears and such are great and fabulous yaddayadda but it's all very open. Basically the series goes from tight spaces, nooks, and crannies, to open, fresh air and not a drop of water that could come crashing through glass at any second. I get it's supposed to be a prequel but come on, it has very little to do with the first 2 games. No tight spaces, no freakishly weird maniacs to fight. The game's new functions are fun and helpful, but it still has nothing brought from the first and second except maybe a few select mechanics. The grabber/melee weapon (whatever you want to call it) helps a bit in battle but soon after beginning the game, that thing basically becomes useless as a melee weapon. Being forced to beat some guy with that thing 7-15 times is just boring and wastes time. The game was overall loads of fun, and the storyline didn't seem to have any sort of inconsistensies, probably because of the fact that you entered a tear every few hours of gameplay. Anyways, the story is very well made, however the ending is very rushed, it's almost like you're forced to try and understand 32 things at once. The ending only really makes sense after thinking about it quite a bit. Also, there are no choices to make, the game is extremely linear. The game really falls through with all the lovable things about the other 2 Bioshock games, basically throwing the mechanics and storyline from those 2 out of a window and into a dumpster. If the 2 Burial at Sea episodes weren't so dam expensive I wouldn't even be talking about this right now. Overall: 7/10 due to the lack of anything Bioshock, Bioshock 2, and Rapture.

I don't normally review games unless I wanna give my two cents but this game? I have to, it's mixed review rate just isn't right. That being said, let's actually review this game and the whole one hour I put into it.  I know one hour doesn't seem like much but to me it was enough to review most of this game.  This game is like a weird mix of Burnout and GTA without being able to get out of your vehicles, it's a lot like Starsky and Hutch for the PS2 minus the guns, that game was a blast with a steering wheel and light gun. c:  I bought this game for $0.99 and just recently saw it was a giveaway by Bundle Stars so I put it on my family account as well so my family could play it and it also reminded me that I should give it a try and I did.  The actual review:   Pros: +Semi-realistic vehicle handling and physics. +Ability to use warning indicators and turn signals as well as your siren and lights and silent police lights(No siren.). +Decent damage system. +You can pretty much destroy everything on a city wide rampage in the semi with a trailer. +Cheesy voice acting. +Great looking for being 6-7 years old. +Tons of video options and resolutions. +Great input support, works with all my gamepads and of course an X360 pad and I believe it has steering wheel support. +Stable, never crashed on me once and it runs great. +Splitscreen racing. +Manual and Automatic settings, gear shifting and more. +Fun to slam AI around during missions that warrant it.  Cons: -Sometimes terrible AI and the AI spawn system is completely borked, AI will spawn miles away from you sometimes in a mission where you have to be within a few metres of them and thus causes you to fail and must restart the mission from the beginning, there are no checkpoints but most missions are pretty short. So try not to flip AI vehicles as a sort of fix. -Cheesy voice acting, not completely a negative, it's sometimes pretty hilarious listening to the voice actors putting on fake accents and faking feeling and integrity in their lines. -Not much depth to the game or story, but this game isn't really about story as it is gameplay. -Some AI is incredibly difficult to take down and may take up to twenty minutes to take down a single car smaller and slower than your vehicle which is kind of realistic but also kind of annoying if you just want to be done with the mission. -No online multiplayer and no splitscreen/online free roam/co-op.  Overall, my final verdict would be 7/10, the game has bad voice acting and poopy AI at times but the game is still rather fun especially to go on a rampage and destroy anything and everything. This game has been so far extremely solid for me, no FPS drops, no crashing and everything is maxed on my system while maintaining at least 60FPS at all times.  My suggestion: Wait until this game is like $3 or cheaper to purchase. It's mostly just one of those fun games to go back to and basically drive around destroying everything, taking out bad guys or leisurely strolling around and going with the flow of traffic and following road signs, etc.
This is now a classic so must buy.

F.E.A.R.: First Encounter Assault Recon is a first-person shooter psychological horror video game developed by Monolith Productions and published by Sierra Entertainment and the first game in the F.E.A.R. series. It was released on October 18, 2005.  F.E.A.R. puts you in the shoes of 'The Point man', a secret special ops group of the U.S. government specialized in dealing with paranormal threats, When the game begins, the player witnesses a man named Paxton Fettel taking command of a battalion of telepathically controlled clone supersoldiers, seizing control of Armacham Technology Corporation (ATC) headquarters, and killing all its occupants. A strong atmospheric shooter, FEAR does so many things right, but fails in some categories drastically. While the shooting is top notch, dynamic lighting and a solid soundtrack makes this an enjoyable shooter, the lack of enemy variety and repetitive nature of the leves might turn off some players. You are equiped with a fairly diverse arsenal from the basic pistol to more advanced weapons like the Type-7 Particle weapon and many other. The bullet time is superb, the amount of enviromental destruction is here aswel, as particles and walls shatter after you shoot them. It's a briliant showcase of how a shooting something should behave. Not many games offer this level of detailt, to be honest, and this one is quite old. The story is quite nice, a bit generic with the whole *experiment gone wrong theme* and some of the plot elements are delivered via phone calls or debriefed during loadtimes. Nothing too over the top, it gets the job done and keeps the player interested. Overall this game has much to offer, and falls into the category of 'old-school PC shooting games', reminescent of the goldern era of shooters (1994-2004) with health pick-ups, no cover system, just plain balls to the walls action. Lastly, I'd like to talk about the AI, which has received much praise, and it deserves it. Your enemies use tactics and commands to adapt to certain situations you put them into, responding adequately, making them feel more human than just lines of code. When you shoot them, they react in a realistic manner, thanks to some great physics in the game, overall they perform well enough.   I'll list specifically what I loved about the game and disliked about it  The good:   Solid gunplay   Great amosphere   Good sound/music design   Challenging enemy AI   Destructive enviroments, awesome lighting effects, cool death animations   Sense of dread and solitude throughout the campaign   Exploration is rewarded  The bad:   Repetitive level design   Lack of enemy variety   Story is delivered poorly and not developed enough   Short lenght for the campaign   Too many health pick-ups and ammo, not too challenging   Dead multiplayer  This is a 7/10 for me, short of a 9 because of the level design being so primitive at times. A must own.

This is a great simulator-game. I really like the realistic physics; if you's expect it in the real world, you can probably expect it here in terms of the physics department. Center of mass, center of lift, and center of weight are all rather important issues for planes and large rockets. Piloting the ships and collecting data is pretty fun. Ship building is a rather complex process, but that's a good thing. It really makes you think about the design of your ship. Sure, you can add a lab for research, but you better figure out how to provide enough thrust to lift it, and enough fuel for the thrust. And you always tell yourself, 'one more launch,' or, 'one more tweak,' but... Well, it just sucks you in.  I'd recommend this to any space or physics nerd (both of which I am).   PS: If you do get this game, try science mode to start out with. You don't have the nagging constraints of monetary restriction and public relations in it, but you are required to collect data to research new parts. This will help pace you by starting you off with basic parts and adding more complex stuff later as opposed to being overwhelmed by all of the parts in creative that you don't know what they do.

Papers, Please is a hard sell, after all the sentence 'Eastern Bloc immigration official simulator' sounds anything but fun, let alone something that will maintain interest. Yet despite this apparent handicap, the game is totally engrossing, as you decide who can enter Arstotzka - and it really is a matter of life or death in some cases - and it poses so many questions about the gamers' ethics and morals.  Even though it seems to be a drudgery sim, with you doing little more than stamping passports for the duration, there's several storylines that run through the game and a sly streak of humour amidst the oppression and the dystopian greyness that stops the game feeling grim.  It may not be for everyone, as the game requires you to concentrate on the papers in front of you as well as keep a good memory of the nations and cities around you, but this gives Papers, Please an old school charm from the era where taking notes would get you through a game successfully, so if this is up your proverbial street this is a game for you. Be warned, though: you will be humming the theme tune for days.

app_name: Injustice: Gods Among Us Ultimate Edition
review_text: Don't get me wrong, I absolutely LOVE this game! The story is amazing, and the little easter eggs that are throughout the campaign and stages are rather pleasing to the DC comics fan such as myself.   However, the online multiplayer is completely broken... None of the servers work, and nobody is ever online becuase of that. I bought this game to play with other people online - to show off my Injustice skills (which, although are lacking, are pretty good) , but I am unable to do so because the online is broken.  On the contrary, the graphics are amazing, the gameplay is really fun, and the characters are from all corners of the DC universe. I would recommend this game to people who buy fighting games for the story; an audience that is severly lacking...

It's ugly, the control scheme is bad, and the quests are all backtrack-everywhere fetch-quest nonsense. It's an old, somewhat experimental game, so I can deal with all that.  What killed it for me was the interminable, unskippable, painfully boring combat. Imagine the worst possible version of Final Fantasy style combat and you're probably close. Then add a half-baked, grid-based positioning system that tries to be tactical, but really only exists to waste your time. Even the fast-forward function isn't enough to save it.

For $33 this is a solid game. Any person who expected a AAA game at that price is naive. The game does lack in areas such as combat which isn't terrible but feel slow and clunky. The dialogue and scrip seems subpar. But the crafting seems deep and interesting. Overall i'd say if you're looking for something to keep you busy till Watch_Dog / Wolfenstein launches then this is a good choice.

app_name: Sid Meier's Ace Patrol: Pacific Skies
review_text: I just cannot start the game. On my Windows 7 Dell laptop w/Sandy Bridge, the games starts, and when it looks like it's going to run.. it closes on its own. Then, I get 'Failed to start game app already running' error message. On Windows XP desktop, I just get 'Failed to start game. unknown error'.  I googled and tried many different remedies, and none of them are working.  Verifying integrity, installing \\Steam\\steamapps\\common\\Pacific Skies\\_CommonRedist\\OpenAL or vcredist, unchecking Enable steam community in-game option, etc.  Same thing goes for both Ace Patrol and Ace Patrol Pacific Skies. As a Sonic fan and a fan of other platformers, this game is a true masterpiece. Fantastic artwork, level design and music. The bosses are spectacular and extremely difficult to beat.   My only minor complaint is that the large and difficult levels can make your playtime of a single level take 20-30 minutes, but once you've mastered it, it won't take that long.   I would recommend that in the (glorious) event of a Freedom Planet 2 being created, that the levels are split into Acts so that individual levels don't stretch out to over fifteen minutes. This helps out in regards to wanting to play a few levels and then having to go out somewhere and lose all your progress. So a level like Dragon Valley (which can take up to around 10 minutes when you're screwing around a little) would be split into Dragon Valley Act 1 and Act 2, with the miniboss of the level being at the end of Act 1, Sonic 3 & Knuckles style.  Also, not a Sonic 'rip-off', but Sonic 'inspired'. Amazing game in general, would love a sequel. Also, HD sprites would be absoulutely glorious. Go hire the artists of Sonic 2 HD or something.

A fantastic fighting game with a combat similar to the super smash bros. games. You can customize your character with various parts to change your abilities, passives, and appearance. The campaign is a bit of a weakness in my opinion, and the regular tournament mode is better, but you have to play the campain to get parts to customize your character, which is kind of annoying. There is no online multiplayer, only local, so it could be a fun party game.

Highly addicting platfomer roguelike game !  The attention to smalest pixel details put into this game is awesome. You see from the animation of it especially the characters.  *Character Options There are different options of character that you can play and unlock along with your progress in game. Different character has different style of owning the game.  *Looting Loot is the main element in this game, as you progress you can unlock even more loot. More and more until you become a demi-god , or not at all and you will die is shame. You die once and you start over from zero, while actualy you only need to finish 6 level ,which you will only be able to get through by dying over and over again until you can beat the game. And you can never have enough of it. Trust me.  *Atmosphere The music is good . It suits well with the game experience and overall character. The atmosphere of foreign planet and the mood of playing space-alien like game.  *Dificulties Another main aspect of this game is also that the dificulties will increase along with the time you play the game from the begining. So if you are not fast enough to gain level or loot, and the dificulties increase , be ready to have a really hard time.  *Co-op The good thing from Co-op is that you can owned the game with your friends. As much friend as you can , but the cons is that you have to share the amount of loot you get and that means you have to share your strength that you usually get when you play SOLO (where you can greedily have your own loot). But anyway playing this game with friends is really addicting.  I am really hoping to see another sequel of this game if it were to be made . And i believe everybody will feel the same too.

This is a puzzle game with a really bad twin stick beat-em up mechanic. No checkpoints in levels. A Twin Stick with no dodge roll, or block button. The twin stick is unnecessary and adds nothing but to make the game harder since it’s hard to see where you are facing. The game is unnecessarily hard and you can get surrounded by enemies and die easily.   It's designed to be four players and the same amount of enemies will spawn regardless if it’s just two players or four. There is no way to complete all the challenges single player.

In my dreams, I'm on a beach, it's not a humid day, just right, the wind is blowing through my hair, and I can taste the salt from the sea as it goes by. I have a dog by my side, a small Jack Russel, he's playing with a toy he got from someone. I'm relaxed, calm, I've never felt this way before. I can say for the first time In my life I'm actually happy. As I spend my time idly, I see a black dot in the distance, I stand up and it appears to be getting closer, the sky turns blood red, only the dot isn't a dot, it's a blackhole, the hole is growing, soon everything around me is swallowed up, i'm in limbo, silence all around me, decaying building,humans without faces, all this ♥♥♥♥ing silence. I just float there, I wonder if I'll ever leave from here. Than I wake up, I see the morning sun, it's 6:00am, I'm exhausted, like everyday. That's it, the point isn't the dream itself, the point is I'm a living corpse. How do you live when you don't want to live? You drag on. :^)

I cannot recommend this to everybody.  This game is very cheesy, stupid, and beautiful. To put it bluntly, if it looks like fun, it just might be your thing. If it looks stupid to you and unattractive, it will be. I squeezed out around 20 hours on this low-price game, and I'd say that it was worth it. The game has a lot of problems in terms of semi-mediocre execution of certain mechanics and a lot of bugs (some of which will ruin a playthrough, enemies stuck outside the map, etc.), but it has the right touch of personality and what I would call a cute mix of things I like.  Play the demo, make up your mind, and buy/leave it.
Early Access Review

TLDR; Look past the dated graphics for a highly recommended game.  A great game reminiscent of others like Fallen Earth and even perhaps Runescape, this rpg has a bit of everything with the great bonus of having a very friendly community. You'll see other comments stating the game has died, or that the player base is empty, but the few people around are the pinacle of a great MMO experience.  Within the first few hours of playing, I stumbled upon a few other people, all who came up to me and donated gold and gifts without even knowing who I was. There are no bots, it is not pay to win and the story is enjoyable and reasonably unique.  Definitely give this game a try (it's only a matter of MB to install!) if you are willing to look past some dated graphics and a small playerbase. Besides, each new player allows the game to grow, and the currently small online group means there is never any lag related to server-side issues.
